title: They used to play on astroturf and terrestrial television
bodyText: In football it is unwise to presume too much. The end of the 20th century finds the game in a state of rude global health but the shape of its future as a mass spectator sport in the 21st is hard to predict. Football worldwide has never been so popular. More countries than ever before officially play the game as members of Fifa. Satellite television has created millions of new fans. Manchester United's triumph in the European Cup final last May was, quite literally, cheered to the ends of the earth. There would appear to be no tangible reason why this happy state of affairs should not continue for a long while yet. Fresh fields are about to open up in Asia, which in two years' time will stage its first World Cup. How long will China remain a sleeping giant? Africa, already a rich source of talent, is bound to provide an increasing number of gifted players for European clubs. South Africa appears increasingly likely to win the Fifa vote for hosting the next World Cup but only provided it can begin to get to grips with the country's crime wave. In those areas where football has been established much longer, optimism needs to tread more warily. South America continues to produce some of the world's greatest players but for the past three decades economic factors have dictated that virtually all of them will end up earning hard currency in western Europe. This seems to be the footballing equivalent of cutting down the rainforests. Should the flow of South American talent ever slow to a trickle the effect on global football would be alarming. Brazil and Argentina remain as precious to the future of the game as any European nation. Association football has been around for a mere 137 years, a relatively short time in millennium terms, yet still manages to behave as if it had a divine right to exist for ever more. That is a lot to take for granted. The appeal of the sport has always been its simplicity and continuity. Football is not a stop-start game. In theory there should be no reason to halt play other than for goals, assuming there are any, and half-time. The laws have survived the past 100 years largely unaltered. The only radical change came in 1925 when the number of opponents required to keep an attacker onside was reduced from three to two. At present the interpretation of the offside law, which has almost always been the biggest bone of contention between referees and players, is about right. Goals can quite properly be allowed if attackers, while in offside positions, are obviously not interfering with play. This, after all, is the original spirit in which the law was framed. No more tinkering with the way the game is played should be necessary. Fifa has surely gone as far as it can in restricting methods of tackling without ruling out tackling altogether, as was seriously proposed at one point. Fortunately this wheeze, like the nonsense about enlarging the goals, came to nothing. However, the 21st century may soon see drastic developments in the way matches are controlled. Refereeing controversies have become endemic because the ways television can expose and analyse decisions are becoming increasingly sophisticated. An already strong temptation to make TV the referee's friend rather than his enemy may prove impossible to resist. A fourth official studying incidents on closed-circuit television and giving his decision through an ear-piece may eventually become as much a part of football as cricket, although spectators might quickly tire of the disruptions to matches this would cause. Either way television, or rather television money, remains central to football's future as an entertainment. In England the hard bargaining over the next TV contract is about to begin in earnest with a figure of £1bn already common parlance. The 2002 and 2006 World Cups will not be completely available to terrestrial viewers and Fifa's insistence that in each country every match played by the national team, along with the opening match, semi-finals, third-place game and final, must not go to pay-TV cannot hide the fact that under such a system one of the greatest matches ever played, the 1986 quarter-final between France and Brazil, would in Britain only have been seen live on Sky. The Premier League's breaking of the BBC-ITV cartel released extra TV millions into the English game and helped, with the Football Trust, to underwrite the cost of building some of the world's safest and most comfortable stadiums. It also attracted the sort of foreign player who previously would never have dreamed of passing up the fortunes being made in continental Europe in order to take a wage cut for the dubious pleasure of being kicked from behind in England. The new century urgently needs to witness genuine attempts by the clubs to restore an acceptable balance between overseas imports and home-grown products at present in danger of being squeezed out by short-term solutions from abroad. Inflated domestic transfer fees used to be the reason quoted for buying foreign. Now, post-Bosman, the greater problem concerns salaries that continue on an ever-upward spiral. Far less fuss was made about Roy Keane's recent £50,000-a-week contract at Manchester United than on the day in 1961 when Johnny Haynes became the first footballer to earn a weekly wage of £100. Yet the amount United have had to pay to keep Keane at home, combined with the even greater sums he could still earn in Italy or Spain, will surely usher in the 21st century against a cacophonous background of players' agents trying to obtain proportionately increased deals for their own stars. For the moment, talk of wage-capping is just that but a showdown with the Professional Footballers' Association cannot be far off. At the beginning of the 60s the £20-a-week ceiling was abolished. Now most clubs would regard a limit of £20,000-a-week as a not unreasonable basis for negotiation even though this would risk losing the best English talents abroad, not to mention a sudden outbreak of homesickness among the Premier League's foreign legion . Football should be entering the new century aware of its limitations. There has to be a limit to what players can earn and the extra amounts spectators can be charged in order to finance higher wage bills. Television coverage may not be at saturation point because most of the live stuff is exclusive to Sky's relatively small audience but how much more can viewers be asked to cough up - and is digital pay-per-view going to be that popular anyway? The worst prospect for football in the new century would be a global satellite TV cartel telling clubs that if they wanted a better deal they had better sound out the Martians. By that time, no doubt, Dan Dare will be in charge of the England team for the Inter-World Cup on Venus, where there has never been a shortage of left-headed midfield players.

title: Outlook brightens for euro
bodyText: Economic growth in the 11-nation euro zone will outstrip that of the US over the next two years, according to Wim Duisenberg, president of the European Central Bank. That should benefit the euro, the single currency which made its debut on the foreign exchanges a year ago and has since lost about 13% of its value against the US dollar. "We expect during the course of this year the euro will go in the opposite direction... The euro has strong upward potential," the ECB president said in an interview on Dutch television last night. Looking ahead to economic prospects for Euroland in 2000, Mr Duisenberg said: "We now expect growth in Europe during the course of this year to be faster than growth in America and that will remain in 2001. "That is the basic background why we say the euro now has upward potential." The fall in the euro from its launch rate of around $1.17 to near parity has proved a mixed blessing for the euro zone. So far the region has been able to enjoy the benefits of a competitive exchange rate without inflation taking off but the decline in the euro's international value has done nothing to enhance its prestige as a reserve currency. Part of the reason for the decline has been the relatively sluggish performance of leading Euroland economies compared to the US but the currency has also been bedevilled by rows between the Frankfurt-based ECB and the German government over the need for Europe's biggest economy to restructure. Such openly conflicting views, coupled with the ECB's studied avoidance of intervention in the foreign exchanges, helped maintain the downward pressure on the fledgling currency. Although Mr Duisenberg and his colleagues are keen to see the euro recover at least some of the ground lost over the past year, the ECB president sought to play down the importance that tended to be attached to the exchange rate. "Europe needs to learn a little bit, I think, that the effect of a change in the level of the currency on the national economy... is much less for the whole single Europe than it was earlier on an individual member state." During the interview Mr Duisenberg risked reopening the controversy over how long he will stay at the helm of the ECB. The former Dutch central bank governor was appointed to the ECB for an eight-year term but a behind-the-scenes row was settled by an informal agreement that Mr Duisenberg would serve only part of his term before handing over to the governor of the Bank of France, Jean-Claude Trichet. The squabble caused con cern in the financial markets where there was concern that the compromise over the presidency could be a sign that the ECB would not be as independent of political interference as had been expected. In last night's interview Mr Duisenberg refused to be drawn on when he would quit but appeared to insist that he would be the one to determine when he would stand down. "I'll be 65 this year. Certainly I'll still do a number of years, but whether I'll stay the full eight years, I haven't taken that decision yet. That will come later."

title: Europe finally files beef ban suit
bodyText: The European commission will file its European court of justice case against France's continued ban on British beef today, a day later than promised and nearly a week after Paris lodged its countersuit against the commission. The court in Luxembourg confirmed yesterday that France had filed a counteraction last Wednesday asking it to overturn the commission's decision to lift the EU embargo on British beef imports. A court spokesman said the suit argued that the commission had failed to take into account new French evidence on the possible health risks of British beef and had broken the EU's principle of safeguarding human health even when evidence of risk was not clear cut. In Brussels a commission spokesman said the launch of the EU's proceedings against France, expected yesterday, had been delayed because the complicated legal documentation had to be approved by the legal services. The documents were expected to be delivered to Luxembourg tomorrow, he said. 'That is when the action will begin.' The embarrassing tit-for-tat, which could see France in the dock at the same time as it assumes the rotating EU presidency in July, follows the lifting of the EU's three-year ban on sales of British beef last August. Member states declared themselves satisfied with the strict safety measures put in place by British farmers after the government's admission of a possible link between bovine spongiform encephalopathy and a new form of the human brain disorder Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, which is so far believed to be responsible for the deaths of 48 people. Despite protracted negotiations, France has refused to lift the ban, citing 'significant if unquantifiable risks' to consumers from eating British beef. The spokesman also said the commission would decide in the next few days whether to try to speed up the European court proceedings against France - which could well take up to two years - by asking for interim measures. He said this was a complex process which would involve the commission giving up some of its procedural rights. 'There are extremely stringent rules, and the commission would request interim measures only if it believed it had a serious chance of obtaining them.'

title: Hi-tech stocks take a bruising
bodyText: Investors scrambled to unload hi-tech shares yesterday as worries about rising global interest rates continued to unnerve the markets. After Tuesday's record fall, the FTSE 100 dropped a further 130 points yesterday to finish at 6535.9, its lowest close since November 23. The London market has shed 5.7% over the last two days. The European Central Bank's decision to leave interest rates on hold failed to comfort continental exchanges, which suffered heavy losses. In France, the CAC-40 index finished down 192 points - or 3.4% - while in Germany, the DAX closed down 85 points at 6502.07, or 1.29%. Technology and telecoms stocks were hardest hit in Europe. The FTSE's two high-tech newcomers, ARM and CMG holdings - both stars of the year-end rally - ended the day as the session's worst performers. CMG was down 471 at £38.50 while ARM Holdings was off 340, at £36.69. Techmark, the London index for technology stocks, suffered as the sector fell from grace, finishing the day down 173 points at 3503 - a 4.72% fall. Its French counterpart, the Nouveau Marché, fell 8.3% its sharpest ever one-day fall, while the German Neuer Markt fell 6.35%. Even private investors - who have been the backbone of the dot.com bandwagon - appeared to have had second thoughts about the giddy heights relatively unknown companies had reached. Justin Urquhart-Stewart of Barclays Stockbrokers, which had several hundred customers trying to deal on Christmas day, said the small investors who had fuelled the end of year rally in hi-tech stocks were taking profits. "People have been suffering from internet vertigo. They are taking some quite healthy profits instead of getting burnt," he said. Many analysts see this week's falls in share prices as simply blowing the froth off an overheated market, but the danger of a full-scale rout is not discounted. "Everybody is trying to whistle in the dark and pretend it is just a healthy correction," said Joe Hall, head of European equity marketing at Deutsche Bank. "The risk is that we see another sharp fall in the tech stocks, and that may start to unleash a bit of panic among private investors." In the US big stocks staged a rally after Tuesday's collapse, with the Dow Jones bouncing back by 164 points an hour before the close. The tech-heavy Nasdaq continued to dive during the day and was down 91.00 at 3810.69 as London closed, after falling more than 4% at its weakest point. It rallied in later trading "The market went up so fast and so far that it had to correct itself a little bit," said Jean-Paul Pierret, director of strategy at Natexis Capital. "We had to have a pretext - and higher interest rates was it." Brokers expect US markets to remain volatile until next week after Friday's publication of the latest payroll figures - the next indicator of inflationary pressures which could influence whether US rates rise. The ECB indicated that its rates decision had not been influenced by falling shares. "At this stage the situation was not affected with any state of fear. An orderly correcting move is not a problem," said Matti Vanhala, governor of the Bank of Finland and a member of the ECB's rate-setting council.

title: Video releases
bodyText: It All Starts Today... Retail £15.99 Artificial Eye, Cert 12 **** It starts like this: a desperate mother with no electricity and broken windows dumps her kids on the conscientious kindergarten teacher who has enough problems of his own with burglary, vandalism, obdurate child welfare people and parents too poor to pay for their school meals. It sounds like Ken Loach and hits you with the same worn-out end-of-the-tether raging realism, but the unemployment black spot here is in northern France and the director is Bertrand Tavernier, another expert on social realism. The genesis of the film came from discussions with Tavernier's new son-in-law, a primary school head; the script shares the credit between the director and the younger couple, and provides the film with immediacy and anger shared with the fictional head teacher, played by Philippe Torreton, a Tavernier regular keeping it in the family. The film hurtles along, throwing insoluble problems at its teachers and leaving the viewer almost as breathless as its protagonists. Like Loach, it has unfussy documentary-style direction and a mixture of professional and non-professional acting. But there's nothing amateurish about it and for all its hardships and the characters' failures, it doesn't share Loach's sense of helplessness. There's a sense of joy at the achievements too, and the children involved look as if they were having a whale of a time. If there's a criticism, giving the teacher in a mining area of high unemployment an ex-miner father with bad lungs and a girlfriend with a delinquent son may have been overegging the pudding a bit dramatically. But the film, which won two prizes at the Berlin film festival, should be of wider interest than teachers and social workers and it looks fine in widescreen. Artificial Eye's clear subtitling is a bonus too in a talky film that crackles with exasperation and determination to improve things. Darkness Falls Rental Fox Pathé Cert 15 * In a busy year for Ray Winstone, this was the role that got away in a film that crept briefly and hesitantly into the cinema and feels more like straight-to-video fare. Winstone appears uninvited in a swish pad inhabited by Sherilyn Fenn, once in Twin Peaks as TV's most improbable schoolgirl since Please Sir and here playing the bored wife of a dodgy financier with a £10m debt (Tim Dutton). Adapted from NJ Crisp's play Dangerous Obsession, Gerry Lively's film struggles to avoid staginess. An interesting premise soon becomes a ponderous exposition in which you tend to guess the plot twists in advance (though not the ludicrous ending). Winstone looks ill at ease here: he's given shocking facial hair and isn't allowed to wear a West Ham shirt even once. Surprisingly, he opts to play a menacing character very quietly when a bit more threat might have livened things up. Vendetta Rental Mosaic Movies, Cert 15 ** Very decent drama from HBO based on a shocking true story. It's set in turn-of-the-century New Orleans where workers fleeing famine in Sicily run fruit and vegetable stalls on the waterfront. When the local police chief gets murdered, they provide convenient suspects and the word Mafia (pronounced Mar-Feea in this locality) is bandied about for the first time in the deep south. As a result, the video box labels Vendetta a "Mafia thriller" but the film is a thoughtful piece about prejudice, corruption and racism. There's a clumsily tacked-on romance but as a courtroom drama, it does a good job. The resultant travesty of justice was apparently condoned by Theodore Roosevelt, history's great proponent of fair trade. Christopher Walken, the cast's only star name, appears in a supporting role as a behind-the-scenes fixer. Nick Pileggi, co-writer of GoodFellas, is credited as an executive producer, and the director is Nicholas Meyer.

title: Brown and EU still divided on withholding tax
bodyText: European Union finance ministers agreed yesterday to explore ways to end the row over a withholding tax on savings, but differences emerged about who would sit on a new high-level group. Gordon Brown, the chancellor, pledged after meeting colleagues in Brussels that Britain would continue to take measures to combat tax evasion and would "take the work of the group seriously". Portugal and France announced that they would be appointing the equivalent of a junior minister to the group, while British officials indicated they would prefer technical-level representation to keep the issue low-key. Mr Brown said it had not yet been decided who would represent the UK on the group, which is supposed to find a solution to the issue by the end of June. The chancellor insists that the withholding tax, to be levied on all savings income within the EU, would cost thousands of jobs in the City of London and damage the lucrative eurobond market. Britain says the way forward is for an exchange of information between member states rather than the levying of a tax. Last year the chancellor rejected a compromise put forward by the European Commission that would require names and addresses of bond holders to be released to other states' tax authorities. Yesterday, however, Mr Brown's tone was markedly more conciliatory than during the ructions over the issue at December's Helsinki summit. British officials want the problem settled before the French presidency of the EU in the second half of the year - if only because with the beef issue still unresolved it would be domestically impossible to make concessions then. Commission proposals would end a system whereby savers resident in one member state can salt away cash elsewhere in the EU - particularly in "havens" like Gibraltar - and pocket the interest tax-free.

title: Time to plan for being outside the zone
bodyText: Tony Blair is Britain's most pro-European prime minister since Ted Heath. Instinctively, he would like to be at the heart of decision-making in Europe rather than stuck on the periphery. Yet here he is, almost three years into his premiership, presiding over possibly the biggest crisis in Anglo-European relations for 30 years. The word crisis is overused, especially in newspapers. Yet there is a growing sense that Britain's entire approach to Europe is being coloured by the debate about the pros and cons of joining monetary union. The feeling that Britain and its European partners have been drifting further apart has been highlighted this weekend by the CBI's reluctance to go out and campaign for the single currency, the comments from Wim Duisenberg, president of the European Cental Bank, that Britain's entry into the single currency is years away at best, the ongoing weakness of the euro against the pound and the dollar, and the accusations of sleaze aimed at Helmut Kohl, the father of the euro project. The latest opinion polls show almost seven out of 10 people are against Britain joining the single currency and more than a third would support leaving the EU altogether. This is serious stuff, yet at this stage of the political cycle there is not a lot the government can do. Supporters of the euro say Mr Blair missed a golden opportunity in the immediate aftermath of his landslide victory, when he could have won a personal mandate for the principle of entry into monetary union. Now, with the next election probably no more than 18 months away, the prime minister and his advisers know that to go against the tide of public opinion on the single currency issue would hand the opposition the one issue with which it could deny Labour a second term. Nor has Mr Blair's policy of constructive engagement in Europe brought much in the way of dividends. The withholding tax row rumbles on, amid mutterings that the veto over tax matters must go. Britain played by the EU book on BSE, only to see the French thumb their noses at the rules. The costs of kicking the euro debate into the long grass until after the election should not be underestimated. Uncertainty over the government's attitude is actually contributing to the strength of sterling because London is seen as a safe haven from an ailing euro. There is a risk that inward investors might start to lose their enthusiasm for the UK if the likely timescale for entry is delayed much longer, although anybody for whom the exchange rate is such a fundamental factor in profitability should be having second thoughts about their entire strategy. However, this is the wrong debate and it is being carried out on the basis of questionable assumptions. Unlike many other actual and putative members of the EU, Britain has never really bought into the political arguments for a united Europe. Rightly or wrongly, its view has always focused on economics - whether the UK would be better off in terms of growth, jobs, investment, living standards as part of a bigger economic unit. In the 1960s there was no question that Britain thought it had much to learn from the economic success being enjoyed by the likes of Germany and France. Having ended the war as the world's second most important economy, the UK rapidly fell down the league table. Even as late as the early 90s, when John Major signed up to the Maastricht treaty, albeit with a British opt-out, the continent could still offer lower interest rates and lower inflation. At the start of the 21st century, the economics are far less clear cut. Since Britain left the ERM it has been the second fastest growing economy in the G7 and has the lowest inflation rate on a harmonised basis. Unemployment is considerably lower than in Germany and France, and, although short-term interest rates are double those in the euro zone, long-term rates are broadly the same. Across Euroland there are calls for the sort of re forms to labour markets and welfare states that Britain went through under Mrs Thatcher and is still enduring today. The combination of short-term political expediency and the medium-term economic outlook makes it difficult to see who could mount an effective campaign against the prevailing tide of euroscepticism. A mixture of growth and reform in Euroland might close the gap in economic performance but is hardly likely to provide a dramatic shift in opinion. Recession in the UK, particularly if accompanied by withdrawal of investment by high-profile companies, might turn the tide. But it is hard to see any combination of factors likely to cause a fundamental swing in public opinion in the next five years. Any organisation worth its salt should start to include long-term British exclusion from the euro zone in its contingency planning. And that includes the government.

title: Welcome to the Knowledge Society
bodyText: Three forces are driving modern economies - finance, knowledge and social capital. It is no coincidence that all are intangible: they cannot be weighed or touched, they do not travel in railway wagons and cannot be stockpiled in ports. The critical factors of production of this new economy are not oil, raw materials, armies of cheap labour or physical plant and equipment. These traditional assets still matter, but they are a source of competitive advantage only when they are vehicles for ideas and intelligence which give them value. When the three forces of modern economic growth work together the economy hums, and society seems strong and creative. When they are at odds, as they have seemed to be for much of the last twenty years, society seems in danger of fragmenting and becoming more volatile. The task is to combine finance, knowledge and social capital in a virtuous circle of innovation, growth and social progress. There are three ways this could be done: by organizing society around the leadership of the market, the community or knowledge and creativity. Those who believe that self-interest and the search for profit are the main motive force for economic growth argue that the market and private companies should primarily organise the economy. That was the argument of the new right for much of the 1980s and 1990s: that society should be rationalised, restructured and ultimately revitalised by unleashing self-interest and extending the market. The more people looked after their income, housing welfare, education, health, the better-off we would all be. This free-market argument is still influential but has run its course. A free-market society would put us at the mercy of the impersonal, capricious forces of the financial markets, widen inequalities under-invest in the long term and the public goods on which we all rely. The rise of the knowledge economy will force us to revise many of the claims of the new right which have passed into conventional wisdom, for example that something's value can be read from its price, as set by an open market. In the 1990s, most critics of free markets have chosen to prioritise social capital, often with the ambiguous but superficially appealing rallying cry that we should strengthen our sense of 'community'. The argument that society should be organised to maximize a sense unity comes in many guises. Stakeholder economists, such as Will Hutton, argue that we need to regulate market capitalism by enforcing upon companies obligations to communities and employees as well as shareholders. Communitarians, such as John Gray and Amitai Etzioni, argue that individuals can only realise themselves within a strong, supportive community. They share the belief that global capitalism is the enemy of community and that we need to make our societies more caring and compassionate. The communitarian critique of market capitalism is superficially appealing but eventually disappointing. Strong communities can be pockets of intolerance and prejudice. Settled, stable communities are the enemies of innovation, talent, creativity, diversity and experimentation. They are often hostile to outsiders, dissenters, young upstarts and immigrants. Community can too quickly become a rallying cry for nostalgia; that kind of community is the enemy of knowledge creation, which is the well-spring of economic growth. This battle between market and community has been central to the politics of the 1990s. The clash between market and community encouraged a string of attempts, none entirely convincing, to reconcile them: Tony Blair's and Bill Clinton's Third Way, Gerhard Schroeder's radical centre in Germany, Lionel Jospin's hope to create a market economy but not a market society in France, George Bush Jnr's compassionate conservatism, were all attempts to marry the market and community, efficiency and social justice. Governments of the right and the left have continued with broadly pro-market policies, while also strengthening social institutions. This middle way is better than the market extremism which went before. But too often this course, tacking between the demands of market and community, is reduced to a balancing act. Politics with a compromise built into its core unsurprisingly leads to piecemeal, cautious reform: one step forward, half a step back. It does not produce a new vision of how society should be organised, nor a radically new kind of politics, with a new uplifting, inspirational goal and new means to achieve it. The emergence of the Third Way and its continental variants marked the end of free-market dominance. But the way ahead is not to navigate a middle course between the old left and the new right, the community and the market. The way ahead is to adopt a different destination altogether. The goal of politics in the twenty-first century should be to create societies which maximise knowledge, the wellspring of economic growth and democratic self-governance. Markets and communities, companies and social institutions should be devoted to that larger goal. Finance and social capital should be harnessed to the goal of advancing and spreading knowledge. That will make us better off, put us more in charge of our lives and make us better able to look after ourselves. The free-market agenda has run out of steam. Communitarianism is fraught with difficulty: when it is not vague it sounds authoritarian. The goal of becoming a knowledge-driven society, however, is radical and emancipatory. It has far-reaching implications for how companies are owned, organised and managed; the ways in which rewards are distributed to match talent, creativity and contribution; how education, learning and research is organised; the constitution of the welfare state and the political system. Knowledge is our most precious resource: we should organise society to maximise its creation and use. Our aim should not be a Third Way to balance the demands of the market against those of the community. Our aim should be to harness the power of markets and community to the more fundamental goal of creating and spreading knowledge. Copyright © Charles Leadbetter, 1999, 2000

title: A rich state riddled with hatreds
bodyText: For the first time since the 30s a party of the far right is on the brink of power in the heart of Europe. By the end of this week Jorg Haider's Freedom party is likely to have joined the conservative People's party in forming a government in Austria. If the coalition goes ahead it will have an impact that goes far beyond central Europe. A far-right coalition in Austria will put in question the belief that liberal values are safe in Europe. Since the second world war conventional wisdom has held that prosperity and respect for human rights go hand in hand. Haider's surging popularity has overturned that comforting belief. Austria is one of Europe's richest countries: it has very low rates of unemployment and a flourishing hi-tech economy. It faces nothing that remotely resembles an economic crisis. Yet, according to one recent poll, a third of Austria's voters intend to vote for Haider's party in coming elections. Has Austria become once again a testing ground for the politics of xenophobia and hatred of minorities? At the start of the 20th century Vienna was dominated by Karl Lueger, its charismatic mayor, who combined a bold experiment in municipal socialism with virulent anti-semitism. A century later, are we witnessing a trial run for a new kind of fascism? It could be argued that the alarm felt throughout the international community at the prospect of a government containing Haider's Freedom party is exaggerated. In part, Haider's popularity can be accounted for by the unravelling of the post-war political settlement in Europe that has destroyed the Italian Christian Democrats and which underlies some of the troubles of former Chancellor Kohl. The Freedom party is benefiting from the volatile political environment that has developed over the past decade. The end of the cold war has inaugurated a period of crumbling parties, fickle electorates and, in some countries, renascent extremism. The Freedom party is doing all it can to persuade the world that its policies have nothing in common with the Nazi era. During the past week or so, Haider - who notoriously praised Hitler's employment policies - has gone to ground in the province of Carinthia. In the event of his party entering government there are no plans for him to take a seat in the Austrian cabinet: he will remain governor of Carinthia, though it is rumoured that his sister may be offered a post. Officially, Haider is not even taking part in coalition negotiations. Everything is being done to represent the party as being of the moderate right and its leader as just another populist politician. There has been no talk - in public - of "rootless cosmopolitans". Not yet. But there are far too many ominous echoes in the rise of Haider for the concerns that are being expressed to be in any way overblown. In its strategy of demonising outsiders, the Freedom party is no different from parties of the hard right in other European countries. In France, Denmark, Switzerland and Italy, far-right parties have achieved a high degree of political visibility by using much the same rhetoric. But none has the pivotal importance of Haider's Freedom party. A government in which Haider held the balance of power could put at risk the progress that has been achieved in the most successful of the post communist states. Austria's neighbours have based all their policies on the expectation that they will soon be fully accepted into the European fold. Poland, Hungary and the Czech Republic look to EU membership as an essential step in becoming normal European countries like any other. With a far-right coalition in power in Austria the political consensus on speedy admission of these countries can no longer be taken for granted. A government in which Haider and his party have a powerful voice could well seek to block EU enlargement to the east. Part of being a full member of the EU is having an open labour market with other EU countries. It is hard to see the Freedom party - which has come so close to power by exploiting hostility to foreigners - accepting open borders with Austria's poorer neighbours. There can be few parts of the world where the belief that history has ended is more ludicrous, or more pernicious, than it is in central Europe. Yet Europe's leaders have been lulled into complacency by the faith that growing wealth is enough to make Europe safe for liberal values. Haider's rise shows that this is an illusion. Human rights do not come in a package deal along with rising incomes, low unemployment and mobile phones. Despite its growing prosperity, Europe's capacity for bigotry, hatred and contempt for minorities is far from being exhausted. The European Union was founded in order to ensure that liberal values are secure. What hope is there for it if it cannot protect human rights? • John Gray is professor of European thought at the LSE Useful links Freedom Party People's Party Republic of Austria Statement from the Portuguese Presidency of the EU

title: You've never had it so good
bodyText: Elateral, a fast-growing software company in Farnham, Surrey, advertised for a secretary/personal assistant in January in the local newspaper. The company received only four replies, and when it came to interview them the following week, three had already obtained jobs elsewhere. When Elateral tried to re-advertise, the local paper said no - its pages were so stuffed with job adverts it didn't have space for more. Is the UK heading back to full employment? BT grabbed the headlines this week with the grim news that it is to slash 3,000 managerial jobs, M&S is culling UK suppliers, threatening thousands of jobs losses, and British Airways is in a programme to cut 6,000 jobs. Yet the other headline which will scream out later this year is when unemployment (judged by the claimant count measure) falls below one million. Several City economists, such as Dresdner, Salomon and Charterhouse, see unemployment falling below 1m by the fourth quarter of this year and as low as 800,000 next year. The UK may be a job-destroying economy, they say, but it is also undeniably a highly-successful job-creating economy too. Evidence of a remarkable surge of jobs, even since the beginning of this year, is building up. Job advertising in the national broadsheet newspapers, after dipping at the start of 1999, rebounded in September, and is now climbing at a rate of 10-15% per month according to Media Monitoring Services. And that's not counting the growth in jobs advertised on internet sites such as the Guardian's own jobsunlimited.co.uk. The number of vacancies posted up in Job Centres across the UK has shot to record levels, with many low-paid jobs almost impossible to fill. On average, adverts for jobs in what the employment service calls "protective service occupations", or what the rest of us call security guards, sit on Job Centre display boards in the south east for seven-and-a-half weeks before they get filled. Demand far outstrips supply. It's not just a south east phenomenon. In the north of England, employers have to wait nearly seven weeks to find a candidate for a clerical job, twice as long as employers in the south, according to the employment service. A survey of Jobs Centres by Jobs & Money this week found positions such as bus drivers and chefs that have been left unfilled for more than six months. Across the country the greatest demand is for "health associate professionals" such as nurses and midwives. Job centres in the south west - where the problem is worst - report average waits of almost 12 weeks before jobs cards can be taken off the board. Essex county council hit the headlines last month when it was revealed that it is hiring social workers from South Africa to fill empty posts. Schools in West London are bussing in supply teachers daily from South Wales and hospitals are recruiting nurses from the Phillipines. Much of this is directly attributable to low pay - unqualified social care staff typically pick up around £5 an hour, compared with £6.25 an hour sitting at a Tesco's till. Even four-year qualified social workers start on not much more than £17,000, while low-pay in nursing has landed the NHS in a near permanent recruitment crisis. Yet staff shortages are not just a feature of low-paid jobs. An analysis of the last three months' job adverts in the national broadsheet press reveals that the fastest growing area is finance and accountancy. Paul Taylor is a manager of the financial division of HW Group, one of the UK's top three temping agencies by market share. He says there are now 40,000 professional temps working in the City of which 65% are in the banking industry. Around one in seven are antipodean, says Mr Taylor, and without them he says the City would grind to a halt. There are not enough qualified locals who are prepared to do the work. "Only six months ago in London we had candidates taking anything," says Mr Taylor of UK finance professionals. "Now they say what they want to do, they are becoming much more focused - rather than saying 'I'm an accountant, is there anything I can do?' " Demand for finance professionals stretches far outside the City. Last year Bradford & Bingley said it wanted to recruit 500 financial advisers across the UK, but in recognition of the booming market for high-calibre advisers, has now scaled its plans back to a more modest 150. After finance and accountancy, the second most in-demand jobs category is managerial posts, followed by marketing, sales and creative and media. Richard Watney of Elateral says that with unemployment in the Farnham area below 1%, it has had to cast its net wide for the 70 jobs it expects to create this year, and has just taken on a marketing manager from France. His only comfort is that it's even worse in the USA, where he has to pay software professionals 40-50% more than in the UK. The volume of jobs is allowing many applicants to pick and choose, says James Reed, chief executive of recruiters Reed Executive. "If you've got the qualifications you can always be choosy, but particularly now," he says. In his experience if the job on offer is not attached to a prestigious brand such as Virgin or Sainsbury, it is unlikely to attract many candidates. It is not just corporates that are feeling the squeeze. The Armed Forces are suffering as highly-qualified personnel quit the service for highly-paid jobs elsewhere. Two weeks ago the Royal Air Force revealed that it is 20% short of its full complement of front-line fighter pilots. The army says the biggest gap in its recruitment is not for squaddies but for nurses, particularly in the Territorial Army, where it is short of 2,500 medics. As in accountancy, antipodeans are filling the gaps. Recruitment Solutions specialises in recruiting for the medical sector through its Paramedics Workline and Medics Incorporated subsidiaries. Michael Adams, a marketing director with the firm, says if you are a qualified physiotherapist or occupational therapist, there are hospitals crying out for your services. "They are extremely placeable. There is a general shortage of candidates. If you look at the occupational therapy market, at one time there were only two or three people qualifying each year but the demand for them is far greater than that," he says. Other areas short of qualified people in the health service are psychiatry, anestheology and pediatrics. Figures from the employment service also suggest a UK-wide shortage of drivers. The internet is producing a growing demand for the delivery of all the goods being bought on-line, but finding the van drivers is proving difficult. There is one area where demand for workers has plummeted. Computer Weekly magazine this week reported that the number of adverts in the national and trade press for IT experts fell by half in the three months to December 1999, taking it back to 1995 levels. But there will be few tears shed for the now workless Y2K specialists who were picking up £10,000 per week in the run up to the biggest non-event of the millennium. The jobs they can't fill Jobs & Money contacted Job Centres around the UK this week to identify the jobs no one wants. Here's a selection: Area: Central Southampton Job: Plumbing and heating engineer First displayed: August 2 1999 Salary: £6.50-9.00 per hour Area: St Albans Job: Bus driver First displayed: May 17 1999 Salary: £195 per week to start rising to £250 Area: Broadmead (Bristol) Job: Retail security officer First displayed: September 24 1999 Salary: £5.60 per hour Area: Southampton Job: Part time catering assistant First displayed: September 14 1999 Salary: Not given Area: Broadmead (Bristol) Job: Part time sales adviser First displayed: October 28 1999 Salary: £4.10 per hour Area: Harpenden (Hertfordshire) Job: Experienced site technician First displayed: May 14 1999 Salary: Not given Area: Central Bristol Job: Commis chef First displayed: September 3 1999 Salary: £5 per hour depending on experience Area: Ambleside (Cumbria) Job: Live-in assistant hotel manager First displayed: November 1 1999 Salary: : £11,500 Area: Crook near Windermere Job: Restaurant manager First displayed: November 2 1999 Salary: : £11,000 Area: Burton in Kendal Job: Commis chef First displayed: August 22 1999 Salary: £700-800 per calendar month

title: Wiltord willing and more than able
bodyText: If Elie Baup is invited to Sir Alex Ferguson's office after tonight's match, the two managers may be there until the early hours swapping hard-luck stories. While United's love affair with David Beckham teeters on the brink of divorce, Bordeaux were left in little doubt that their main striker Sylvain Wiltord considers himself too big a fish for the French pond. Displaying impeccable timing, the 25-year-old Parisian ended a self-imposed ban on talking to the media to declare an interest in moving to Old Trafford. "It has always been my dream to play in England and I'm using this as my chance to show the Premiership clubs what I'm capable of," said the striker. "When I was younger I used to support Liverpool but now my ambition is to play for United. There is only so far you can go in the French League; it is more exciting in England." Wiltord's curriculum vitae is impressive: France's footballer of the year and the Golden Boot winner, he averages a goal every three games. Now regarded as an automatic choice for the national team, he also insists on being called "Mr W". Pretentious, moi? However, it has been Bordeaux's defenders who have impressed in Europe this season. The French champions were involved in three scoreless draws to progress as Group G runners-up, although their league form is patchy; they are sixth in the table, 17 points behind Monaco. "We have found it hard," Baup said. "But we're not coming here to be spectators." Close inspection of the pictures of Beckham in the stand at Elland Road 10 days ago reveals Marius Trésor a few places away. Now on Bordeaux's backroom staff, the former France captain was not impressed by United. "Jaap Stam is a great defender but he lacks mobility and Wiltord can make the difference with his pace."

title: A des res with contrasting views
bodyText: The average UK home shot up in value by more than £2,000 last month as the booming housing market motored into top gear with the biggest monthly surge in prices since just before the early-90s property slump. Alternatively, the average UK home fell in value by £795 last month in what appears to be the first sign that the housing market is coming off the boil. These were the two wildly differing scenarios presented this week by Nationwide building society and Halifax respectively, both of which claim to operate the comprehensive, authoritative barometer of monthly house price movement. So which one got it right and which one is wrong? That is not so easy to answer, partly because the methodology each uses is so complex. "Prices are disaggregated into their constituent parts using multivariate regression analysis," explains Halifax's website helpfully. On the face of it, Nationwide's prognosis seems the more believable: a fairly chunky fall in prices last month just does not ring true when even the notoriously down-playing officials at the land registry are talking of annual house price inflation exceeding 15%. And a glance at Halifax's data for recent months shows some alarming lurches - prices flat in September, up dramatically in October, a fall in November, up sharply again in December. But Halifax's stance is backed up to an extent by recent Bank of England figures showing that the number of loans for new house purchases falling, and the economists in the City are certainly taking its words of wisdom seriously. This isn't the first time the two have differed fundamentally over where they see house prices going. Three months ago, Halifax said the price of a typical house fell by 0.5% in November 1999, while Nationwide said it leapt by 1.9%. Neither side was prepared to give ground yesterday, both declaring that the other was at fault. What is certain is that any more differences of opinion on this absurd scale and both lenders' indexes risk being left discredited. Phone numbers Yesterday was that Friday in the month when the US rolls out the non-farm payroll figures. Usually it is a market-sensitive number, scanned for clues about whether or not the apparently ever growing US economy is at last showing signs of overheating. Traditionally Europe's markets hold their breath for much of the morning, wait for Wall Street to react to the figures and follow suit. Not yesterday. Though Wall Street delivered a predictably knee jerk reaction to the better (ie, lower) than expected new jobs number, Europe had made its own decision about the day's market mover: the telecommunications sector. From Madrid to Helsinki, telecoms and related stocks were rising. In some cases the phone connection was a bit crackly. Spanish construction group Actividades de Construccion y Servicios, for example, rose 11% on the back of its involvement in a consortium which might (not will, might) win a mobile phone licence in the latest tender. In London, BT and Vodafone were moving upwards. In Paris it was Vivendi (part telco, part utility) and Bouygues; France Telecom dipped a touch but after a 25% rise on Thursday who is counting? Deutsche Telekom, SwissCom, Dutch operator KPN and mobile phone maker Nokia showed sharp rises. Does such a flurry of activity make sense? At the wilder end of the spectrum, almost certainly not. The flurry sparked by the Spanish tender looks more like a lottery than a dispassionate assessment of the likely licence winner. The rest, however, is tricky to disentangle. France Telecom shot up on Thursday because it is thinking of floating the internet arm Wannadoo. Deutsche Telekom has already played the dot.com card but its reported pursuit of Qwest in the US provided motive force for the shares yesterday. Their prospects certainly look sounder than many of the e-stocks being offered at fabulous prices. Look at some of the projections for the growth of mobile phone use (say 3bn subscribers by 2005) and Nokia hardly looks overrated. Takeover speculation runs beyond Deutsche Telekom and Qwest. Telcos are looking for partners, allies or acquisitions to give them global reach. The German company tried to buy Telecom Italia last year. Vivendi was prepared to go all the way with Mannesmann well before the Vodafone bid but was rebuffed. Today it is Bouygues, Telefonica, Cable & Wireless, Qwest, Bermuda-based Global Crossing, even BT which find themselves in the bid spotlight. In the short term, prices can be justified by the premiums demanded in the sector. In the longer term, however, they can only be sustained by heady revenue and profit growth. Rapid growth looks achievable, especially in the mobile sector. Even so, the speed with which prices have risen suggests that expectations are outstripping reality.

title: Cyberbanks plan 2.4bn merger with First-e
bodyText: Two European internet banks announced a €2.4bn (£1.5bn) merger yesterday, claiming it would result in the world's first global bank in cyberspace. Under the terms of the deal Uno-e, owned by Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria (BBVA) of Spain and Terra Networks, will buy First-e, the Dublin-based internet bank. The combined group will be known as unofirst and use the First-e brand in northern Europe, Asia and the US, and the Uno-e brand in the Spanish bank's strongholds of southern Europe and Latin America. The bank is not expected to break even until long after a planned flotation which could unlock riches for the young management of First-e who own 16% of the combined group, worth £240m at yesterday's price. It could also mean gains for customers, who are expected to be given a chance to buy any shares in the flotation. BBVA and Terra Networks are paying €291m to current First-e shareholders and contributing €362m in cash to raise capital for the combined group which will have €500m to fund expansion plans. Manuel Galatas, a BBVA executive who will be chairman of the combined group, said the new internet bank would be floated on two stock exchanges in about a year's time. Its target is for 3m customers by 2003 with a possible break-even shortly afterwards. First-e, which was launched in the UK in November by Banque d'Escompte of France and Ebna, a Dublin-based holding company, has 50,000 account holders and another 60,000 customers who have registered an interest in the high-rate interest-bearing account. Xavier Azalbert, 35, who runs First-e, said that the merger would allow the e-bank to offer better deals to customers and put the bank at the forefront of the move from the internet to mobile phones. "As this trend continues, dot.com financial services will make demutualisation carpet-bagging seem like small change," said Mr Azalbert, who is part of the First-e management team which owns 16% of the combined entity. He said the bank was on track to meet its pan-European target of 220,000 customers. First of many • first-e started marketing in the UK in Novermber 1999 • It has 50,000 customers, with average deposits of €17,000 (£10,500) each • It has begun trials in Germany • Uno-e to launch in Spain this month • Soon opening in Portugal • Combined group will spend £325m to expand into the US, Latin America and Asia

title: Colt's £716m to fire European growth
bodyText: A pan-European appetite for the new economy yesterday helped Colt Telecom raise £716m on the markets to fund its aggressive expansion plans. The group, which placed 12.9m new shares, raised £116m more than it anticipated as institutions voted in favour of its growth strategy, enabling Colt to issue a further 3m new shares. The new shares, priced at £38.52, brought in £497m while the group raised a further £219m through the issue of euro-denominated bonds which can be converted into stock. These notes have a cash coupon of 2% a year, mature in seven years but cannot be called during the first three years. They will be convertible into ordinary shares initially at £50.076 a share. Colt, which provides high bandwith data, internet and voice services to business customers, seized the opportunity to unveil a faster roll-out of its European networks than revealed just two weeks ago. The group now intends to expand its web-hosting centres to between 12 and 20 by the end of next year, with plans to raise this to 24 soon after. It is stepping up its investment in internet enabling and content distribution services. By the end of this year Colt plans to launch digital subscriber line services in the UK, Germany, France and Holland but, given the pace of liberalisation in European networks (unbundling the local loop), now intends to extend these to Austria, Belgium, Italy, Spain and Switzerland. Similarly, plans for an inter-city network of some 8,000km this year have been enhanced by new targets to 14,000km, bringing in parts of France, Spain and Scandinavia, by mid-2001. Jim Curvey, Colt chairman, said: "We are sensing an accelerating pace of growth and development in the European market and have therefore decided to accelerate and expand our own plans to ensure that we are positioned to take advantage of these growth opportunities." Paul Chisholm, group president and chief executive, added: "Our aim is to be the leading e-commerce distribution company in Europe." Colt shares fell 72p to close at £39.76.

title: Tony Blair's full speech
bodyText: Today's conference is an important initiative. Not just because it is government, business and employees coming together in partnership to look at a key economic issue. But because of what we're here to look at - the new, knowledge-based economy, and its importance to Britain's future. I strongly believe that the knowledge economy is our best route for success and prosperity. But we must be careful not to make a fundamental mistake. We mustn't think that because the knowledge economy is the future, it will happen only in the future. The new knowledge economy is here, and it is now. This new, knowledge-driven economy is a major change. I believe it is the equivalent of the machine-driven economy of the industrial revolution. But our job is to help people with that change. Not to resist it, and so suffocate opportunity. But not just to let change happen, regardless of the consequence. Our approach, what I call the Third Way, is to manage that process of change to extend opportunity and prosperity for all. To find a way which provides for efficiency in the knowledge economy, and ensuring that everyone feels its benefit. Enterprise and fairness. That is our goal. For some people, the advent of the new economy may be a matter of nervousness - even fear. Whole countries may be staring at the prospect of being outpaced, and left behind. For Britain, the new economy is unquestionably a challenge. But it is a challenge we are now better equipped to face with confidence because of the tough decisions we have already taken to secure a sound economic base - an economy based on a platform of stability, and steady growth. Because macroeconomic stability is the first key element of the new economy. Without stability in the economy, businesses - particularly businesses in rapidly-moving areas like E-commerce - can't plan and can't invest and can't grow. Stability is not an optional extra. It is central to the new economy. The second element is the revolution in technology - and especially information technology. Britain has long been a pioneer in advanced forefront of technology and scientific development. But science and technology is advancing faster than ever before. For business the key to success is how fast they can adopt and adapt these new technologies. IT and communications are vital parts of this. Businesses based on or using IT are at the cutting edge of the new economy. E-commerce and the Internet are already changing business fast. Transaction costs are tumbling. One US bank estimates that the effect of e-commerce is to reduce its cost per transaction from $1 to 1 cent. Astonishing. The Internet is dissolving physical barriers, and levelling the business playing field. It doesn't matter how small a company you are. You have the choice now of defining your business as global. Standard business thinking used to see big as inevitably beating small. Now fast beats slow. The opportunity is there for business to take. E-mail and the Internet may still be a closed world to some people. But especially for the new band of young e-entrepreneurs, and indeed for many young people generally, the Internet is a normal part of life. The e-generation is with us. Unleashing the full potential of people - our most valuable resource - is the third element of the new, modern economy. In one respect, that means reforming welfare, making work pay, establishing the work ethic for the whole of society. Tackling exclusion. In another, it means an endless emphasis on education, and on skills - a ceaseless focus on driving up standards in order to ensure that opportunity for all is real. I want Britain to be at the forefront of the knowledge economy. Europe too. Britain was the first industrial power. Our role as the workshop of the world stemmed from our character as a creative and hard-working people. The combination of adventure and application was a potent mix for Britain. And it still is. For businesses, the challenge they face is widening the reach of the new economy. Many of the British companies already involved in E-commerce are eagerly embracing the opportunities the new economy holds out. But we know we have a good way to go. The new knowledge-driven economy is not just about the new, high-tech industries like biotechnology or software development - companies which have built directly on the UK's university and science base. The new economy isn't either just the new technologies like IT and the Internet. It is instead about new sources of competitive advantage. The ability to innovate. To create new products. To exploit new markets. Using whatever means which are appropriate, including IT and E-commerce. And that applies to all businesses, in all sectors. High-tech and low-tech. Manufacturing and services. Businesses in long-established sectors can find ways of using these new approaches to refresh and revitalise their operations. The knowledge economy can become an indivisible part of the way all businesses work. For individuals, the opportunities and benefits the new economy offers must not be the sole domain of elite knowledge workers. Learning is the key to individuals succeeding in the new economy. At school, in further education, throughout people's working lives. The key capability for people to survive and thrive in the new economy is their capacity to learn, and then to apply that learning. So we have to make sure that the new economy is not just for a privileged few - for the relatively small number of companies working in the world of E-commerce, for example, and for the relatively tightly-drawn social grouping which tends to use the Internet today. Looking just at the Internet, currently half the people in Britain who are online come from the very top social groups. Almost forty per cent are in a single age band of 15-24. We have to make sure people are not excluded from this revolutionary technology. Over the last few days, we have seen a number of important announcements about how people can best use the Internet. My point is this: this technology is revolutionising the way we work, the way we do business - the way we live our lives. Our job is to make sure it is not the preserve of an elite - but an Internet for the people. We have to democratise the new economy. We must ensure that it is open to all. But it's not only Britain which needs to embrace the new economy fully. Europe needs to as well. We're privileged to have Antonio Guterres with us here today. He will address you shortly. He and I and other European leaders will be meeting in Lisbon in a few weeks' time for a European Council. A decade ago, a European Council on economic and social policy would have been all about regulation. Now, there is a transformation. We are meeting in Lisbon with a clear strategic goal in mind: to make the European Union the most dynamic and competitive area in the world. We want to see a Europe of full employment in modern labour market conditions. A Europe based on an innovative and knowledge-driven economy. We want a Europe with macro-economic policies which will give sustainable growth. We want to ensure people have the skills they need. And we want to see a reformed economy which will promote competitiveness. I know this is a challenging agenda. But I know if we can achieve it, the Lisbon summit should come to be seen as a landmark in modern economic and social policies for Europe for the next decade. The prize is great: better growth, better employment and better social cohesion. We want to see as well the remaining barriers to e-commerce removed. We want to see fast and cheap access to the Internet. Britain is now leading Europe in the new Internet economy. Looking at E-commerce trading, we are ahead of Germany and ahead of France. Four times the European average. Over one in six homes in Britain now have access to the Internet. A quarter of our businesses are online. Other areas, like Scandinavia, have seen remarkable growth too in communications technology and e-business. But Britain is undoubtedly doing well in the European E-league. But that is no reason for complacency. With a technological revolution on this scale, we have to try harder. We have to maintain and enhance our performance. That's why I've set the goal of Britain being the best place for e-commerce by 2002. We already have a bold programme for Internet access: • We will connect all our libraries and schools to the Internet by 2002 • We will have 1000 IT learning centres open by next year • We are leasing inexpensive refurbished computers to 100,000 of our poorest families • We are giving through learning accounts an 80% discount on the cost of basic IT courses But we want to do more. Because, the one clear lesson of the Internet is you can't stand still. We will get costs down, through competition. When we published our strategy for e-commerce in the autumn, we were told the key barrier was the cost of Internet calls. Americans used the Internet for longer, mainly because most of them don't pay a per-minute charge. Gordon Brown has challenged the industry to get internet access prices down. Ministers have been working with Oftel and the telecommunications industry to overcome that barrier. And we're seeing change. New proposals for unmetered access have been announced by two major companies - BT and Telewest. As we've seen this week, Altavista is now offering Internet access on the basis of relatively low subscriptions. I'm pleased to see this progress. Our goal must be to see prices comparable with anywhere in the world - including the USA. And today there is a new and further step forward. Another major cable company, NTL, is announcing a new offer - the chance for their customers to use the Internet entirely for free. I'm sure that others, including BT, are now ready to break new ground themselves. Obviously, I am not endorsing any of these companies' products. Different products will suit different customers. But the key point is that we are pursuing policies to get prices down. Giving people a choice. Putting consumers first. And I'm delighted to say this. The Americans have been predicting entirely free Internet access for a while. But Britain has got there first. Today I want to raise our aspirations again. Today I want to announce a new goal. I want Britain to aim for universal access to the Internet by 2005. Making sure everyone has access to the Internet will both improve our competitiveness and reduce social exclusion. Policies of enterprise and fairness - working together. I am today publishing a report by the management consultants Booz Allen which explains the case for this policy. But there is no one-technology solution to deliver it. Some people will access the Internet, as now, through a PC. Others through a mobile phone or digital television. Some will do so at work, others at home. For those who can't afford any of these technologies, we will ensure there is a nearby public access point. Universal Internet access is vital if we are not only to avoid social divisions over the new economy but to create a knowledge economy of the future which is for everyone. Because it's likely that the internet will be as ubiquitous and as normal as electricity is today. We cannot accept a digital divide. For business. Or for individuals. Knowledge and skills, creativity and innovation, adaptability and entrepreneurship are the ways by which the winners will win in the new economy. We all have a responsibility to ensure that we are all equipped to succeed in it. That way we can all prosper. All our people. And all our businesses. For the benefit of Britain.

title: Nuclear flasks fail safety test in new blow for BNFL
bodyText: Flasks used by British Nuclear Fuels to transport dangerous radioactive material can resist fire for less than three minutes, newly released research reveals. The revelation will provide fresh headaches for the crisis-ridden BNFL, which runs a major business using the flasks. New safety regulations demanded by the United Nations mean that all the flasks will now have to be replaced. The tests by France's nuclear safety agency showed that the flasks would rupture within 175 seconds in a fire. The flasks are used to transport 20,000 tonnes of uranium hexafluoride or "hex" - the raw material to make fuel for nuclear power stations - around the world every year, much of it for BNFL. Hex is particularly dangerous because as well as being radioactive it reacts with air to produce hydrofluoric acid, a gas which destroys the lungs. Using the suspect flasks BNFL exports hex to Russia, US and Europe from its Springfields fuel fabrication plant in Preston. Internationally, the nuclear industry makes 2,300 shipments of hex every year by road, rail and sea. The study was by the Institut de Protection et de Sreté Nucléaire (IPSN) in Paris, which advises the French government on nuclear safety. IPSN scientists led by Gilles Sert baked flasks in an oven and ran computer simulations to see if the hex container most widely used, a type 48Y, would survive the kind of fire that could erupt after an accident. "Rupture of the 48Y container in a fire seems likely and its resistance to fire should be improved to guarantee safety," they said. Their study is published in the latest issue of the International Journal of Radioactive Materials Transport. From next year hex flasks will be required by the UN International Atomic Energy Agency to resist fire for 30 minutes at 800C. IPSN's experiments suggest current flasks would explode after 175 seconds "in the most favourable case". But defects found by x-raying welds on the flasks, of which there are 100,000 worldwide, mean that the explosion could come even sooner. "Large quantities of uranium hexafluoride might be released in a transport accident involving a fire," the scientists said. The IPSN study was launched in the wake of the sinking of the French cargo ship, Mont Louis, on August 25 1984 after a collision with a ferry in the English Channel. It went down off Ostend, Belgium, with 350 tonnes of hex destined for Russia on board. There was considerable alarm about the danger and all of it was salvaged from the seabed. There have been several other accidents involving hex, including a train derailment in the US and a seven-tonne leak from a French nuclear facility at Pierrelatte, both in 1977. Edwin Lyman, scientific director of the Nuclear Control Institute in Washington, said: "This [report] raises disturbing questions about the credibility of industry safety assurances, not only for hex flasks but for all the radioactive material transport flasks in service today." BNFL confirmed yesterday that it used 48Y containers for shipping hex in Britain and abroad. It was transported from Springfields to Capenhurst near Chester for enrichment, as well as to enrichment plants in Russia, the US, France, Germany and Holland. "Under the new proposed transport regulations there are some additional requirements, one of which is the fire test," said a BNFL spokesman. "The industry has done extensive research on the cylinders and there is no conclusive evidence that the current mode of shipping natural hex will not meet the new regulations." Although there had been thousands of shipments of hex, there had never been an incident involving a release of hex nor any danger to the public, he said. "BNFL will continue to transport to a high standard in line with the regulations for the safe transport of radioactive material." BNFL has been under attack from the Nuclear Installation Inspectorate over its safety regime and from its major customers for falsifying quality control data on plutonium fuel. This week the government postponed part-privatisation of the company and demanded management improvements.

title: Australia's latest export, Frances O'Connor
bodyText: One of the first things that ambitous Australian drama students would do well to learn is how to perfect a flawless English accent. Cate Blanchett, Hilary Griffiths and Toni Collette have all done it. Now Frances O'Connor has also made it big over here. The Australian actress is about to pull off a double whammy in the costume drama stakes. She stars in the big screen revisionist adaptation of Jane Austen's Mansfield Park , while on television she plays the passionate title character in Gustave Flaubert's Madame Bovary for BBC2. Both adaptations take a sexy, modern approach to their texts. Mansfield Park is a brave triumph at pains to expose the unsavoury mores of Victorian society. The rebellious Fanny Price will be unfamiliar to Austen fans used to the novel's compliant heroine. Writer-director Patricia Rozema incorporated into the script dialogue and observations from Austen's letters to create a woman who suffers neither fools nor unworthy suitors. Lesbianism, adultery and drug dependency make appearances, occasionally bringing a sensibility more familiar to Brookside Close than the nineteenth century drawing room. O'Connor provides a sharp tongue for the intellectual, self-assertive heroine. Canadian Rozema deliberately chose a non-English actress after 'casting about the entire globe'. 'She's so free, so natural,' the director explains. 'I think it comes from a culture that's actually on the youngish side. It's not an overripe culture. It's almost wide-eyed in a way.' In Madame Bovary O'Connor's passions aren't unrequited. She is a repressed sybarite; a married woman who gets ruinously involved with Greg Wise's Rodolphe. For an actress who grew up watching BBC period adaptations, it's another step forward. She was raised in Perth but born here - in Oxford - where her father, a nuclear physicist, was completing his PhD. She first came to attention playing a lesbian student in the breakout indie film Love and Other Catastrophes . Then, in 1997, came back-to-back roles in Kiss and Kill and Thank God He Met Lizzie , which won her a double nomination at the Australian Film Awards. Sometimes described ambiguously as 'professional' on set, she has apparently cultivated an attitude towards celebrity usually reserved for bigger names: running late for interviews and photo shoots and dodging awkward questions from interviewers. Of her talent there is no doubt though. If Oscar voters had not been been suffering corset fatigue this year O'Connor might have been anticipating the opening of a certain envelope in Los Angeles last week. O'Connor has said: 'I love watching Hollywood movies - I just don't know if I'd be happy doing a Jurassic Park .' But perhaps her agent or bank manager has had a quiet word. In addition to making All About Adam , a romantic comedy, she is currently in Hollywood filming Bedazzled, a remake of the Peter Cook and Dudley Moore comedy, also starring Liz Hurley. If she really wants to be a diva, Ms Hurley should be able to teach her a thing or two. • Mansfield Park opened on Friday. Madame Bovary starts on 10 April on BBC2

title: Online auctioneer QXL is caught in the matrix
bodyText: Shareholders have given us this rating to do something with,' said Jim Rose, chief executive of the online auction company QXL, as he unveiled the purchase of Bidlet, the Swedish online auctioneer. He is certainly making the most of their enthusiasm. Since it sold 28 million shares in its November flotation, QXL has issued about 40 million more - including the 25 million being used to pay for Bidlet - in placings and acquisitions designed to establish it as the leading European online auction house. Yet even after that flurry of paper, only 23 per cent of its shares are available for you and me to buy; the remainder are locked up with founders, original backers and the owners of the various companies that QXL has been snapping up. Rose admits the scarcity could be one of the reasons for the high rating, but believes it is more a reflection of investors' faith that QXL will establish itself as the leading European player. It could already claim that distinction. Bidlet completes a European network that includes all the larger countries, including Germany, France, Italy and Spain. All it now has to prove is that it can make this network a viable business. It has some way to go. At the end of January, it boasted 500,000 registered users, double that at the end of September. Revenues from its auction have also been growing, from £3.6 million in the three months to September to £5.2m in the following quarter. Not all of that is QXL's turnover, however: its take in commissions and profit was £1.5m, about £3 per registered member. Compare that with Boots. Eleven million people hold its Advantage loyalty card, the High Street equivalent of registering online. Sales through its chemist stores in the year to last March were £3.8bn, or £950m a quarter. That works out at £85 per Advantage card holder. Of course, Boots is not enjoying QXL's exponential growth: Boots will be lucky to see sales grow by 5 per cent a year while the value of QXL's auctions are rising by 40 per cent a quarter. Not, however, its share of the auction proceeds: these actually fell 20 per cent in the three months to December as QXL moved away from buying goods for auction to simply hosting third-party sales. Assuming that is a one-off and its revenues increase by 40 per cent annually it should catch Boots soon after 2010. Investors will just have to hope that the cost of getting those sales - a massive £7.7m in the three months to December - does not carry on increasing at the same rate. On the basis of QXL's share price, the Bidlet acquisition looks a good deal all round. The Swedish shareholders get a price of more than 100 times revenues for the nine months from last April. QXL, on the other hand, adds the equivalent of 40 per cent of its revenues (or matrix, as Rose calls it in Internet-speak) for 15 per cent of the shares. Its speed off the starting block means QXL is already well placed in the consumer market. However, is that market worth QXL's £1.3bn value - roughly a quarter of Boots? Only gamblers should bet on it. Power surge fizzles out Demerging National Power into an international business and a domestic generator would, the company promised last November, help 'realise its full potential' and 'focus on its strategic direction and culture'. It is just as well it did not specifically promise that it would create value as well. Since the demerger was announced, the share price has fallen from 445p to 323p. Now it transpires that the dividend, which the company has already warned will be cut from 28.6p to 15p in the year to March, will be halved again. Panglossian NatPower, of course, prefers not to call it a cut. Rather, it is because the two new companies will formulate their dividend policies in line with their respective industries. That means International Power will not pay one - all its resources will be directed into keeping up with rivals like Bechtel.
