Mean while, the UK part, npower, will aim for profits about twice the level of dividends, in line with its peers. Extrapolate that to the likely profits of the two new businesses, allowing for the fact that National Power has still to disclose how debt will be allocated, and the market assumes it can afford to pay about 8p a share at most. So if investors' income is expected to fall, will that be compensated by a rising share price? Unlikely, say analysts. Power generators, even those that give themselves trendy lower-case names, are hardly flavour of the month in the stock market. Electricity prices are weak and the generators are about to be investigated by the Competition Commission. International Power is potentially more interesting. Its record, the disaster in Pakistan notwithstanding, is reasonable, although it will take some years to demonstrate that it can expand profitably without accidents. It could also attract bid attention. It is, however, difficult to see what could push the price above the current level - and when investors realise the threat to the dividend, it could fall still further. Sweet and sour What can Tate & Lyle do to re-establish itself as a credible business? Last November, chairman Sir David Lees said there were 'good grounds for optimism' and gave a more upbeat presentation than had been seen for years. Five months later, he was back with a warning that the US sugar market has 'deteriorated significantly' and profits for the six months to 25 March will be 'somewhat below' the £93m achieved in the same period last year. Lees deserves some sympathy. Sugar is a commodity product and Tate & Lyle has no more chance of controlling its supply and price level than any of its rivals. A bumper harvest coupled with a flood of imports from Mexico have pushed prices lower and Tate can do little other than accept that or get out. That Tate is contemplating the latter shows just how bad the situation has become. Unfortunately for investors, its chances of finding a buyer who would take it off its hands for a good price are slim. Elsewhere, however, Lees has much to prove. The group remains committed to Staley, its sweeteners business, but its lack of power to force through price rises this year suggests that it, too, is fast becoming a commodity business. The group does allude to £90m of disposals, which should cut the heavy borrowings that have always dogged it. Lees still has to demonstrate he can make a decent return on the investment that is left. This year's profits, estimated by analysts at £200m, will be back where they were in 1989. Lees has so far given no clear evidence that he can improve on that.

title: Annan sets out UN targets
bodyText: The secretary general of the United Nations, Kofi Annan, set targets for the organisation for the 21st century yesterday in a wide-ranging speech in which he also called for the reform of the antiquated security council, on which Britain has a permanent seat. His 56-page speech, described by UN officials as the most important he has made, was to prepare the ground for a special UN millennium conference in New York from September 6 to 8 which he hopes will provide a platform for fundamental reforms. He said the composition of the security council, on which Britain, France, the United States, Russia and China have permanent seats and vetoes, was based on the distribution of power and alignments in 1945 and "does not fully represent ei ther the character or the needs of our globalised world". He said: "The council must work effectively, but it must also enjoy unquestioned legitimacy. Those two criteria define the space within which a solution must be found. I urge member states to tackle this challenge without delay." The Foreign Office position is that it is prepared to accept reform but the problem is complex, with questions such as whether the European Union should have a seat and which countries would be chosen to represent Africa and Latin America. It is also to be decided whether the permanent members should retain a veto. The UN millennium meeting could turn out to be the biggest ever meeting of government leaders: between 130 and 150 are expected to attend. One of the main themes of Mr Annan's speech was to achieve a target of lifting 1bn people out of extreme poverty by 2015. Education was a key element in achieving this. He said: "I urge the millennium summit to endorse the objectives of demonstrably narrowing the gender gap in primary and secondary education by 2005 and of ensuring that by 2015 all children complete a full course of primary education." He hoped that by the same date the HIV/Aids epidemic would be halted, and the percentage of people without access to safe water would be cut in half. With the world being transformed by a global economy and instant communications, Mr Annan said, "the central challenge we face today is to ensure that globalisation becomes a positive force for all the world's people, instead of leaving billions of them behind in squalor". Calling extreme poverty "an affront to our common humanity", he said that nearly half the world's 6bn people were now living on less than $2 a day, and about 1.2bn were struggling on less than $1 a day - including 500m in south Asia and 300m in Africa. He announced four new UN initiatives: to provide 10,000 hospitals and clinics in developing countries with access to up-to-date medical information; to have hi-tech volunteers give computer training to groups in developing countries; to provide mobile and satellite telephones for relief workers in disaster areas; and to explore new approaches to the problem of youth unemployment.

title: Claire Duchen
bodyText: The university teacher Claire Duchen, who has died of cancer aged 45, wrote lucidly and sympathetically on modern French feminism, and was central to a global network of feminist scholars, as a writer, translator, research co-ordinator and regional editor for the Women's Studies International Forum. Her book Feminism In France From May 68 To Mitterrand (1986), written as a "participant observer", continues to inspire students because it so successfully links what might seem fancy theory and the everyday lives of ordinary women. French Connections (1987), her brilliant little book of translated texts, showed the full range of French feminist thought. Claire was born in London to South African parents, Myra and Leo Duchen. When she was five, her father became professor of neuro-pathology at the institute of neurology, Queen Square, and the family moved permanently to England. She was educated at North London Collegiate School and read French at Sussex University. Her 1983 doctoral thesis, on contemporary feminist thought, was the first granted by the institute of French studies at New York University. She came to think the thesis subject had chosen her, it was her own way of making the personal political. After teaching briefly at Aston University and Oxford Polytechnic (now Oxford Brookes University), Claire took a post at Bath University, where she wrote another book, Women's Rights, Women's Lives In France 1944-1968 (published in 1994), and helped set up a research centre for women's studies. In 1993, she took a senior post at Sussex University, but her years there were clouded, first by the illness and death of her parents, and then by being diagnosed herself with advanced ovarian cancer in 1996. Claire confronted her illness with honesty and determination. Cancer was, she said, "like being on a wobbly bicycle", and she undertook high-dose chemotherapy in order to stay on the road as long as possible. During the last four years, she continued to work, and co-edited When The War Was Over, a book on women in postwar Europe, which reached proof stage just before her death. Claire never lost her feminism; she thought it should be rooted both in rigorous thinking and human solidarity, but recently wrote an article in which she explained that her interests had gone beyond French culture and she was drawn to people "on the margins", wrestling with conflict, hardship and taboos. Her last article was about women who had their heads shaved after the liberation of France for being wartime collaborators. Claire loved it when students spoofed her strongly-held views, but she also inspired many of them to work in her field, She liked having fun. She travelled widely, wove wool on the loom in the spare room, and through her partner, the musician Ben Mandelson, knew other musicians and performers, to whom she introduced her international circle of friends. You never knew who you might meet in the house in Finsbury Park, north London. She insisted that her funeral should not be too sombre and should be conducted by a woman rabbi. Ben, and her brother and sister, survive her. Sin Reynolds Claire Frances Duchen, academic, born May 15 1954; died March 15 2000

title: China sends out long May Day to boost economy
bodyText: China yesterday began a week-long holiday aimed at getting its sluggish economy going again.The traditional May Day break has been extended to seven days in a desperate attempt to break a three year deflationary spiral that has sent unemployment soaring. More than 10m Chinese are expected to flock to beaches and remote areas such as Tibet, as well as recently-built theme parks and holiday resorts. Beijing is struggling to boost consumer spending. China's economy grew by only 7.1% last year, too slow to create jobs for the millions being laid-off by bankrupt state-owned enterprises, who have staged sporadic and violent protests. The economy is expected to perform only slightly better this year. But the week-long holiday seems to be helping. A Beijing survey found that travelling families expect to spend an average of about 1,558 yuan (£123) over the next seven days, according to the state-run Xinhua news agency. This is a sizeable sum in Chinese terms, amounting to several months' wages, even for relatively affluent urban dwellers. Some are choosing to venture abroad instead, following the loosening of restrictions on the issue of passports. Package tours to southeast Asian and even European destinations reportedly sold out long in advance of this week's holiday, as these are still the only way most people can legally travel overseas. But there are fears that Chinese holidaymakers' lack of experience makes them easy prey for unscrupulous tour agencies and other rip-off artists. "The food was terrible and the hotels unbearable. Even worse were the obligatory stops at endless special tourism sites," said Xiao Quan, who works in south-western China. He joined a£35 tour to the southern island of Hainan, which has beaches that have been dubbed "China's Hawaii", only to find the three day trip degenerate into an exercise in milking as much money as possible from him, as he was dragged from one souvenir shop to another. "Worst of all was the traditional medicine show," he complained. "After an electric current was passed through the audience, women in white shirts rushed to diagnose all kinds of illnesses and dispense healing potions for a hefty fee." Zhang Yuanchao, of the Chinese consumer association, said that being forced to visit souvenir shops, where the guide receives a commission, was the most common source of protests. The association received a record 5,330 complaints about travel agencies last year, up 41% from 1998. Even those who venture overseas do not necessarily fare any better. Beijing lawyer Liu Chi is suing a Chinese travel agency and its Swiss partners for embezzlement and misleading advertising in a Lausanne court after a trip to see the 1998 World Cup in France went badly wrong. He said the agencies never provided the promised match tickets and forced the 22-strong tour party to cross Switzerland by coach rather than plane, despite spending £2,520 a head on the tour package.

title: Racists are so blind
bodyText: Why do governments have immigration controls? It seems a silly question. The answer is so obvious, only fools would not know it. While nearly 5bn people live in the poor countries of the world, only immigration controls stop them pouring into the rich world. As so often with common sense, this turns out to be uncommon nonsense. There have been poor and rich countries since countries began, but only controls on movement (and what goes with them, passports) this century. Why did not all the poor swamp the rich countries then? It is not the poor who move, not those from either the poorest countries or the poorest areas in the countries where immigrants come from. The overwhelming majority stay at home. Even those who think about it are deterred by the costs - especially of moving to the notoriously dangerous, inhospitable and racist conditions of Europe. It is only the foolish vanity of Europeans which makes them think everyone wants to get in. Blaming poverty is a cop-out to hide the real reason for migration. Europe and North America need the workers. Even when unemployment is high, there are areas of great shortages of workers. Anyone who has had the misfortune to work in McDonald's, flipping hamburgers, as a dishwasher in a hotel, or gutting chickens in rural factories, knows the desperate scarcity of workers in jobs where the pay and conditions are rotten. In general, immigrants don't do the jobs the natives want (except if they're students on their uppers), so they don't displace native workers nor drive down wages. In fact, the situation is the opposite. If a hospital cannot recruit enough workers to do the unskilled jobs - porters, cleaners, laundry and canteen workers, security guards - it is impossible for the nurses, doctors, managers to work. So if you cut the number of immigrant workers, you increase native unemployment. Indeed, an increase in the numbers of workers usually expands the economy and so increases the jobs available for native workers. A study of the Los Angeles garment industry showed that the availability of Mexican illegal immigrants made possible the survival of the industry - and so the survival of jobs for Americans as technicians, foremen, managers, salespeople, truckers, shop assistants, not to mention all those others who provided the immigrants with food, accommodation, transport, etc. That is why a million Cubans lifted the economy of Miami out of slump, and a million French colonists returning from Algeria (at independence) lifted the south of France. The extreme rightwing argument - kick the immigrants out and unemployment will be ended - is not only nonsense, it is the opposite of what would happen. Expel the immigrants and native unemployment would soar. In the postwar baby boom, when the children became adults and looked for work, there was no increase in unemployment. Think of the immense increase in the number of women looking for work in the 1960s and 1970s, far more than the number of immigrants: they did not cause unemployment. It is not the size of the labour force that determines unemployment but the profits of business. People only object to immigrants not because they are workers, but because they are foreigners. Furthermore, controls are expensive and ineffective. Hunting illegals inflicts yet further savageries on immigrants, and now on refugees (who are also forbidden to work while waiting a decision on their refugee status, and thus reduced to dependence on government handouts). Indeed, ending controls would go some way to radically reducing unemployment - as happened in the great boom years of the 1950s and 1960s when unemployment was at a low and immigration not limited. So why are there controls? The issue is not about jobs but about sovereignty, the power of the state. That is embedded in the perceptions of the citizens. The population is drilled from an early age in xenophobia, socialised in a culture of hating foreigners and blaming them for whatever goes wrong (and look how it flourishes in international football, or in the beef trade ) . It is also barely concealed racism. Foreigners don't arrive by plane or train, they "invade", "swamp", "flood", "pour in", "take over" - they are soldiers of an enemy onslaught. So increased numbers of immigrants supposedly weaken the foundation of loyalty to the state, muddying it with loyalty to other states. In practice, governments are ambivalent. In a global economy, they are obliged to acknowledge that jobs and incomes depend on increased movement of workers, immigration and emigration - the global labour market. Yet, they fear this undermines what it is to be "British" or "French" or whatever. The compromise is to define many exceptions for selected workers - doctors, nurses, businessmen, employees of multinational corporations or international organisations, seasonal agricultural labour, sometimes hotel workers and domestics, while reserving the full ferocity of persecution to those at the bottom of the pile, the illegal immigrants and refugees. It cannot last forever. The pressures of worker movement are too strong - the system cannot control the world labour market, only drive it into the miseries of illegality. Furthermore, ageing is undermining it. As the numbers of aged increase, the active labour force is set to shrink - and wages, as a result, to soar. The aged need more and more labour intensive services, the caring professions, just when the number of workers is shrinking. Already, the developed countries are beginning to reshape immigration policy in order to recruit the workers they need. The US is currently offering computer programmers and engineers work permits, and Germany is doing the same. But if the misery of the aged is to be tackled, this will have to be vastly expanded to lesser skills. Increased migration is actually an immense opportunity to advance the world. Labour migration generates strong reverse flows of income - workers send money home to their families. The UN has done some calculations on how much immigration controls in the developed countries cost the third world. If they were ended, there could be a massive flow of income from the earnings of migrant workers back to developing countries, far more than current flows of aid or private capital. At last there would be a serious possibility of a real attack on world poverty. And would it lead to the decline of the national state? Let's hope so - their highest 20th-century triumphs were two world wars of unprecedented destruction. Then we can amend the UN Declaration of Human Rights which boldly promises the right to emigrate - but is silent on the right to immigration. The underlying principle, universal in most places up to the last century, needs to be restored - that people have the right to travel where they will, to settle and to work, as they wish. The world belongs to us all. Extracted from the current issue of Red Pepper. Nigel Harris, professor of Urban Economics at University College London, is an adviser to the World Bank. comment@theguardian.com

title: Non à l'exil fiscal, oui à la liberté
bodyText: Laetitia Casta, la nouvelle Marianne, symbole de la République française, dément avoir voulu échapper au fisc français en s'installant à Londres. Cette affaire, qui, ces dernières semaines, a fait les choux gras des journaux britanniques, avait vivement fait réagir la classe politique française. Certains maires avaient même refusé de placer le buste de la Marianne dans leur Hôtel de Ville. Beaucoup de tapage pour rien, puisque le mannequin affirme séjourner effectivement en Grande-Bretagne pour des raisons privées et professionnelles mais non fiscales. Je vis à Londres lorsque j'y travaille, confiait-elle récemment sur Europe Un. Je loue un appartement car je ne veux pas rester à l'hôtel, parce que j'aime cuisiner et parce que j'ai un petit ami. Je n'ai rien fait de mal, je n'ai pas commis de crime et pourtant, j'ai l'impression d'être jugée. Laissez-moi tranquille ! Que pensez-vous de l'affaire Laetitia Casta ? Sandra, 19 ans, vendeuse: Elle se fait la malle à Londres pour ne pas se faire arnaquer par le fisc, et alors ? Il y en a tellement qui le font ! Léa, 19 ans, étudiante: C'est son droit le plus strict. Encore heureux que chacun soit libre de vivre où il veut ! Aurélie, 21 ans, étudiante: Je comprends que les gros salaires se barrent de France pour payer moins d'impôts. Le problème, c'est qu'elle est censée représenter la France. Pour l'instant, c'est plutôt mal parti. Que représente Marianne pour vous ? Sandra: Rien du tout ! Léa: Égalité, fraternité et liberté, mais c'est un symbole tombé en désuétude. Aurélie: La révolution française et la liberté. L'idée que Laetitia Casta, alias Marianne, puisse s'exiler à l'étranger pour des raisons fiscales vous choque-t-elle ? Sandra: C'est son poîgnon. Si j'étais pétée de tunes, moi aussi je partirais. Si le fisc ne nous pompait pas autant de fric, l'exil fiscal n'existerait pas. Léa: Avant son élection, personne ne savait où elle vivait. Ça n'avait aucune importance. Elle était bien foutue, c'est tout ce qui importait aux yeux des maires ! Rien ne prouve qu'elle vive à Londres pour des raisons fiscales. Son métier l'oblige à être par monts et par vaux, comme tout mannequin. Aurélie: Maires et politiciens ont été déstabilisés mais les Français moyens s'en foutent royalement ! Je comprends que les maires patriotes qui ont voté pour elle se sentent trompés, mais de là à dire que c'est une trahison nationale, il y a un pas ! Peut-être aurait-elle d refuser d'incarner Marianne sachant qu'elle irait vivre à l'étranger. Mais si ça ne lui pose pas de problème de conscience, qu'on la laisse tranquille ! Que pensez-vous du choix de Laetitia Casta pour incarner la Marianne ? Sandra: J'aurais aimé quelqu'un de plus intellectuel, une femme de caractère, intelligente et forte. Casta n'a pas l'air très fufute. Elle a été élue parce qu'elle est bien gaulée, mais ça ne suffit pas ! Léa: Elle ne me fait ni chaud ni froid, elle n'est pas représentative des Français. Ce n'est qu'un mannequin. Poser à demi-nu ne donne pas une bonne image de notre pays. Marianne devrait véhiculer certaines valeurs, ce qui n'est pas le cas actuellement. J'aurais vu une femme d'âge plus mûr, méritant, de par ses actes et sa carrière, le titre de Marianne. Catherine Deneuve était très bien. Aurélie: Elle est belle comme un cœur mais ne représente pas la Française moyenne. Elle fait potiche. Il faudrait une femme, pas spécialement connue, ni bien foutue, une mère de famille par exemple, en qui chaque Français puisse s'identifier. Plus généralement, que pensez-vous de la fuite des riches vers les paradis fiscaux ? Sandra: Pourquoi devraient-ils se faire piquer leur poîgnon par l'État ? Ils ont raison ! Quand on voit les scandales financiers qui éclatent, la tune détournée sur le dos des contribuables, ça ne donne pas envie de rester ! Léa: C'est plus intéressant pour eux mais c'est injuste. C'est encore le Français moyen qui trinque, car lui, il reste ! Après tout, on devrait tous être logés à la même enseigne. La solution : baisser les impôts, pour tout le monde. Aurélie: Il faut baisser les impôts, car on est pris à la gorge ! C'est vrai qu'avec le niveau de vie en France, on n'est pas si mal loti, mais les impôts sont vraiment trop lourds. Surtout quand on voit le montant de la cagnotte fiscale ! Glossaire/Glossary se faire arnaquer to get ripped off se barrer (coll.) to split symbole tombé en désuétude obsolete symbol faire ses choux gras d'une situation to make capital out of a situation elle n'a pas l'air très fufute (futée) (very coll.) she doesn't seem to be very clever elle est bien gaulée (bien foutue) ! (coll.) she's a damn good looker encore heureux que it's just as well that nous sommes tous logés à la même enseigne we are all in the same boat se faire la malle to scarper par monts et par vaux up hill and down dale si j'étais pétée de tunes (very coll.) if I were very rich si le fisc ne nous pompait pas autant de fric (very coll.) if the taxes were not so high

title: Big spenders par excellence
bodyText: The British embassy in Paris, a former palace on the exclusive Rue du Faubourg St Honoré near the Champs-Elysées, is one of the splendours of the French capital. So too is its entertainment budget. The embassy spent £293,125, which buys a lot of canapes and wine, in the last financial year. It is the biggest-spending British embassy, its entertainment budget higher than Washington, Tokyo and Moscow, the centres of world political and economic power. Opposition MPs questioned yesterday if the spending was a reflection of France's historical importance rather than a reflection of its position in the present world. Previously unpublished Foreign Office figures obtained by the Guardian reveal that Paris topped the entertainment league table of British embassies in 1997-98 and 1998-99 followed by Tokyo, New York, Brussels and Washington. The top 15 embassies in the entertainment league spent about £2m in the last financial year. The Foreign Office minister, Baroness Scotland, said: "All such expenditure is, of course, to promote UK interests: winning support for our policies and products from businessmen, politicians, the media and other decision-makers." One of the diplomats at the Paris embassy, Tim Livesey, was unapologetic: Paris was expensive."You try entertaining 16,000 in Paris as opposed to doing so in Abu Dhabi." The embassy hosted visits for ministers, provided candle-lit dinners for French politicians and opinion-formers, and held parties and receptions, mainly to help provide a showcase for British products. Mr Livesey said: "If you are in Washington, you are looking at a political kind of market. In New York, you are looking at movers and shakers. In Los Angeles, it is the movie industry." But in France, if you wanted to influence people, "you did it in Paris because it is a very centralised country. When you entertain, you entertain the whole of France". Matthew Taylor, the Liberal Democrat Treasury spokesman, said: "It seems how much they spend depends on how much they spent in the 19th century rather than its importance in the 20th century, never mind the 21st." In the past, the Paris embassy would have concentrated on gathering political intelligence and trying to influence opinion. Mr Livesey said that had changed in favour of the commercial side. Last year, the embassy and the residency had hosted 50 special events, including promotion of such things as British fabrics. British cheese was served on all occasions at the embassy and British wine much of the time. After unfavourable publicity in the mid-1990s about diplomats overseas enjoying a seemingly extravagant lifestyle, the Treasury tightened up on spending. The latest figures appear to show spending is down in Tokyo, New York, Madrid, Rome and Geneva. The Paris embassy's expenditure rose over the same period, though only marginally. More importantly, its spending was down dramatically from 1993-94 when it spent £331,000 in entertainment. Regulars at embassy events have noticed little difference. The Foreign Office itself admitted the reduction was not as dramatic as the figures suggested, saying there were various factors at play, such as the high pound working in favour of the embassies. A dramatic example of currency fluctuations was in Moscow, where spending on entertainment dropped from £126,243 in 1997-98 to £83,455 in 1998-99 due to the decline in the value of the rouble. Brussels also saw a substantial drop, from £240,320 to £184,071. New Delhi, the fourth highest in 1997-98, spending £134,091, dropped out of the top 15 last year. The Foreign Office has 223 overseas posts, and 5,635 staff, of whom 2,295 are based overseas. In addition, there are 7,841 local staff. Paris in the mid-1990s employed 89 staff, including four footmen, a butler and a gardener, as well as 142 local staff. Mr Livesey said this had been cut back to a total of about 160. Paris's entertainment rival is Washington where the ambassador, Sir Christopher Meyer, formerly prime ministerial spokesman for John Major, has made it an important part of the Washington social scene. Michael Douglas and Catherine Zeta-Jones were the star attractions as the ambassador's ball. The embassy claims to have had 14,500 visitors in the past year, increasing the costs while keeping the numbers down. Sir Christopher thought the term entertaining was misleading: "Entertaining is not really the right word. It's also about creating networks of influence so that, when you need to, you have easy access to people whose decisions can affect British interests for better or worse. Our network at the British embassy has paid off repeatedly to Britain's benefit. That's the way Washington works." The Foreign Office complains privately that the image of diplomats having a good time means that they have had to pare down spending and usually it is the embassy buildings that suffer, with insufficient funds for renovation. But Mr Taylor remained unimpressed, especially with the claims that spending on entertainment had been tightened up. "It may have tightened a little but not as much as the stomachs have grown." title: Market merger is good news for small investors
bodyText: What does the merger of the London Stock Exchange and the Deutsche Börse in Frankfurt mean to the private investor? In the short term, the small investor will not notice much difference. In the long term, there could be an opportunity to trade a variety of shares in a number of different countries without many of the barriers that exist at present. The small investor needs simple, low-cost dealing. Dealing costs are still relatively high in the UK and private investors must pay stamp duty on share purchases. For UK investors, trading and holding stocks overseas is even more expensive. Yet private share dealing has boomed within the last year. Much of this enthusiasm for stocks has been fuelled by the growth in the popularity of internet and technology stocks. Many private investors now hold shares in tech stocks whose prices are quoted on the New York-based Nasdaq exchange. The merger between Germany and London is also likely to include a link with the Nasdaq. This would enable the small investor to trade shares across Europe and the United States via one settlement process, and possibly even using one currency. But there are still question marks about how the new exchange will operate. The first challenge is to combine the different technologies used on the German and British markets to create an efficient trading system. Secondly, there has been no decision made yet about whether a UK resident would be able to buy a Nasdaq stock using sterling or euros, or whether investors would still have to make their transactions in dollars and bear the risk of currency fluctuations. There are political issues too. If a London-Frankfurt-Nasdaq link-up is successful, stock markets in countries like France, Holland and Belgium may decide to join too. Negotiations between markets and governments would take time and energy. The good news for private investors is that today's merger may allow them to buy shares in companies outside the UK, but without the prohibitive costs involved at present. It will still be difficult for investors to research foreign companies, however, although the growth of websites providing specialist investment information is making this process easier.

title: The menacing decade
bodyText: The Dark Valley: A Panorama of the 1930s Piers Brendon Jonathan Cape, £25, 880pp Buy it at BOL On New Year's Eve 1899 the German Social Democratic Party organised a glittering ball to celebrate the new century. The party had grown in mighty bounds to become the largest democratic political organisation on earth. The ball attracted all sides, the right wing represented by Edward Bernstein and the revolutionary wing by Rosa Luxemburg. The mood of the evening was triumphalist: the party was about to emerge into the century of great hope. Whatever the means of change, whether through parliament or revolution, it seemed obvious to all the guests that nothing could stop the relentless progress of social democracy and the benefits which it would shower on the whole human race. Less than a decade and a half later, the great hope disintegrated into unimaginable despair. The first world war, supported by the German social democrats and the British Labour Party, consigned millions of the young men of Europe to the charnel house. For a brief moment after the war, some of the optimism of that eve-of-century ball returned, only to be plunged into gloom in the 1930s, "the dark valley". The great hope vanished and in its place came slump, fascism, another war and the Holocaust. The rest of the century was haunted by the howling ghosts of the dark valley: every rise in unemployment, every attack on racial minorities, every slippage on the stock exchange, every censored broadcast sent out tremors of apprehension that what happened in the 30s might be coming again. Globalisation is usually presented as a modern phenomenon. The outstanding contribution of Piers Brendon's history is his presentation of the 30s as a decade in which each apparently independent part of the world depended on the others. The slump and the depression set off by the Wall Street crash in 1929 quickly enveloped Britain, France, Germany, Italy and Japan. Brendon fits the story of what happened in each of these nations into an international pattern. Seasoned democracies such as the US, Britain and France, whose governments had won the war, somehow managed to survive the economic blizzard, but the effect on Germany, Japan and Italy was to replace democracy with a crazed fascist militarism which speedily degenerated into racist terror and imperialist aggression. In Spain, the elected government was toppled after a long and ferocious civil war set off by a fascist rebellion. As they drowned in what seemed an unstoppable right-wing tide, millions of victims and their sympathisers cast hopeful eyes towards what they were led to believe was a new socialist dawn in Russia. In fact the new dawn was by that stage a grotesque hoax, kept alive by merciless and mendacious propaganda. A consistent theme in this book is that official mendacity was common to all countries, whether or not their governments depended on votes. Everywhere those in power laid hold of the means of informing people - and informed them in terms opposite to the truth. In this process there was very little to choose between, for instance, the war propaganda of the Japanese government and British ministers' assumption that the purpose of the BBC and almost all the free press was to state and argue the ministers' case. Brendon tells his story kaleidoscopically, alternating his chapters country by country. Somehow he manages to get through his vast panorama without a moment's dullness; his characters come to life in a flurry of detail about their appearances and habits. He never takes refuge in blandness, the usual retreat of boring historians. And there is no comfort here for the mass of increasingly fashionable historians who surmise that there may, after all, have been something to be said for Hitler or Franco or Mussolini or Hoover or Chamberlain or Ramsay MacDonald. In Brendon's view, MacDonald's socialism was "like Harris tweed in Bavaria"; his "cut the dole" policy in 1931 was based on "penalising the indigent for the failures of the opulent". Lord Plymouth, who headed the British government's non-intervention committee for Spain, was a "patrician nincompoop", Ward Price of the Daily Mail "an odious toady". The young king Edward VIII "sympathised with the poor but associated with the rich"; the French foreign minister Georges Bonnet had "the courage of his own lack of conviction" and Randolph Churchill was "not fit to be allowed out in private". Although Brendon is the keeper of the Churchill archives at Cambridge, he is by no means sycophantic towards Randolph's celebrated father, Winston, who, he reminds us, was tremendously impressed by Italian fascism and by extremist militarism in Japan. But the strength of this book does not depend on such insights into its characters, nor on the flashes of literary criticism, for instance of George Orwell, Ernest Hemingway and Louis MacNeice. Rather, great chunks of this history rest on unfashionable opinions. The "most fundamental cause" of the great crash in the US, for instance, was "financial inequalities". Republican Spain was smothered "by the poisonous tendrils of Stalinism". And the key to understanding Stalin was not his association with the Russian revolution but his obsession with eradicating every vestige of that revolution in order, as Brendon puts it, "to destroy every shred of left-wing opposition". All these ideas shed new light into the gloomiest recesses of the dark valley. There are times when the author's obsession with detail distracts him from the central issues. The biggest question of all about the 30s is surely "could Hitler have been stopped?" Brendon explains how much his chancellorship owed to chance and intrigue. In the November 1932 elections only weeks previously, the Nazis lost 2m votes, and even after Hitler became chancellor, the electorate refused him the endorsement he confidently expected. He got the constitutional majority he needed by the subtle device of imprisoning all the elected communist deputies. But what Brendon almost completely ignores is the response of the communists and social democrats to the rise of fascism. Though their combined vote and their influence in the country was substantially greater than those of the Nazis, both sides - especially the communists - rigidly refused to form a united front against the fascists. The communists, who at one stage were getting 6m votes, renamed the social democrats "social fascists". So great was the sectarian divide in those crucial months before the deluge that the communists preferred even to link up and stage strikes with the fascists rather than campaign in the country and in the factories for a unified force against fascism. "After Hitler, our turn" was the boast of the communist leader Ernst Thalmann. After Hitler, as it happened, communists and social democrats were at last united - in the concentration camps. Brendon's refusal even to ask the question whether and how Hitler could have been stopped is reflected elsewhere in his book - for instance over the growth of fascism in Italy or the failure of the Popular Front government in France to go to the aid of republican Spain. Brendon might reply that his job is to tell history as it was, not as it might have been. But at least one of the virtues of such an authoritative and moving account is to arm us against any repetition of similar horrors in the future.

title: From high earner  to highbrow
bodyText: It's been a long time since we saw Demi Moore. And it seems like longer, because for most of the Nineties she was inescapable, no matter where you hid. G.I. Jane, the last Demi Moore star vehicle, came out three years ago and was instantly notorious. There were think pieces about women in the military, Demi did one-armed push-ups on talk shows, the critics howled and there were was sheer incredulity at the fact that Demi actually shouts 'Suck my dick' in the movie. But that was the Nineties for Moore. Even if you didn't see any of her films, you couldn't avoid her, or - more specifically - her body. Those Vanity Fair covers, the Disclosure poster, the stories of implants and her work-out regime. Then there were the spurious debates: the Indecent Proposal issue about whether any woman was worth $1 million for one night, or the Disclosure question about whether women sexually harassed men. Not to mention the marriage to Bruce, the kids, and the stories about their overblown lifestyle. Even her name, which we all had to learn to say with the unexpected stress. And all the while, her wages went up, until she was the best paid actress in the world. And then it stopped. Maybe the ridicule that followed The Scarlet Letter, Striptease and G.I. Jane did it. Maybe she had just made enough money to take a break. Or maybe it was the final collapse of her marriage. Whatever the reason, after a surprisingly effective appearance in Woody Allen's Deconstructing Harry, she went away. Her return is a curious one, free of the headline-grabbing bombast that we've come to expect. It's easy enough to see what she saw in Passion Of Mind: she gets to play both of the main characters. One is a widow living quietly in rural France, the other is a Manhattan career woman. One of them is dreaming the life of the other: the question is which is dream and which is reality. But the script - by Ron Bass, who wrote Rain Man and My Best Friend's Wedding - has been kicking around since 1985 when it was intended for Michelle Pfeiffer. These days Bass is so powerful that he was able to insist that his script couldn't be tampered with, even though he wasn't on set to help. The director Bass chose was Alain Berliner, whose debut, Ma Vie En Rose, a fantasy about a sexually confused little boy, won a Golden Globe for best foreign film. Moore's two leading men were hardly in the Michael Douglas bracket either: William Fitchner is an excellent character actor who normally plays bad guys, while Stellan Skarsg rd is a star in Scandinavia but not in Hollywood. The budget was $15 million, which would barely cover what Moore normally gets paid. But as anyone who has worked with slumming stars will tell you, it is not the money that causes problems. It's the working conditions. And so it was on Passion Of Mind, where it seemed that Moore's band of nannies and helpers wasn't much smaller than the crew itself. Berliner wasn't happy. He also had trouble with Moore's overly literal approach. 'Demi needs a lot of details,' he said. ' What created some misunderstandings is that the movie is like a dream - there's not always a logic.' Add the fact that the film sat on the shelf for nearly a year before finding a release date, and this is no triumphant comeback. But you can see what Moore was trying to do: scale back and make a film that would not be a target for critics. But of course, it was Moore who had put herself in the firing line in the first place. If you're looking for answers in a childhood, Moore's seems to have them all. She was born Demetria Guynes in 1962 in the appropriately infamous town of Roswell, New Mexico. She says that her family moved 30 times when she was young. And yes, they were poor. The man she grew up thinking was her father turned out not to be: he committed suicide when she was 15. She had two operations to correct a crossed right eye. She left school at 16, got married at 18 to a 30-year-old rock musician. Legend has it that it was their next-door neighbour, Natassja Kinski, who encouraged Demi to act. After some modelling - including some nude work - she got a part in General Hospital, then America's most popular daytime soap. Her new salary also allowed her to acquire a hefty coke habit, which lasted until she got her first big film role in St Elmo's Fire; director Joel Schumacher insisted that she go into rehab before playing a girl with a coke problem in the movie. If you want to know how powerful Moore's combination of will power and luck has been, look at the rest of the cast of St Elmo's Fire . They were the hottest young stars around: Judd Nelson, Emilio Estevez, Rob Lowe, Andrew McCarthy and Ally Sheedy. Schumacher's overblown melodrama about a group of friends trying to come to grips with life after college was going to be their way out of teen movies into the big time. It might just be the definitive Eighties movie, complete with a bludgeoning, anthemic rock theme tune, Reaganite politics, coke and Rob Lowe playing the saxophone while wearing a bandanna. And perhaps because it was so firmly of its time, not to mention such a showcase of wretched overacting, only Moore would grow up to have an A-list career. But at the time, Moore was as Brat Pack as you could get. She shared a histrionic acting technique with Nelson. She went out with Estevez. She starred with Lowe in About Last Night (1986), a crude adaptation of David Mamet's play Sexual Perversity In Chicago, notable mainly for just how long Moore manages to be naked. But she broke with the clique when she split from Estevez and married Moonlighting star Bruce Willis. Then he made Die Hard and surprised everyone by becoming an action star. And then Demi got lucky too. Ghost was one of those hits nobody saw coming. It was only meant to be a medium-sized movie in every way, a quiet bit of slush. But it took off, and made Demi - shorn of her big hair for the new decade - famous when her contemporaries were fading. At which point, her talent for self-promotion took over when she posed, nude and pregnant, for the cover of Vanity Fair. No actor and magazine have ever been as entwined as Demi was with Vanity Fair. 'I'm sure there are a lot of people who think I'm a bitch,' she was willing to confess. As the decade went on, the hits (A Few Good Men, Indecent Proposal) mixed with the flops (The Butcher's Wife, Now And Then) but it wasn't until The Scarlet Letter (1995) that things seemed to be going wrong. Sure, the film was a disaster, a terrible idea from the start, but it was Moore, rather than director Roland Joffe or anyone else, who took the blame. The same happened with Striptease and G.I. Jane. It can't have helped that Moore was now getting paid more than any actress in history, at a time when the films just seemed worse and worse. Striptease was the nadir: far from the public demanding nudity, people felt Moore was imposing her increasingly weird, muscle-bound body on them. Like Madonna's behaviour a few years earlier, Moore's exhibitionism seemed to be less to do with sex than a kind of Nietzschean feminism. As Janet Maslin in the New York Times put it: 'These scenes suggest not unfettered sexuality but gruelling hard work: prodigious efforts by costumer, choreographer, make-up squad, work-out coach, maybe even the Army Corps of Engineers.' But Moore wasn't taking on board what the real criticisms were. 'There's this idea that if you take your clothes off, somehow you must have loose morals,' she said. 'There's still a negative attitude in our society towards women who use a strength that's inherent - their femininity - in any way that might be considered seductive.' Watching the film, seduction seemed the last thing on the agenda. But Striptease could have saved her. Far from being a Showgirls clone, it was based on a novel by the the satirical crime writer Carl Hiassen. With a pre-Boogie Nights Burt Reynolds playing a sleazy politician and Ving Rhames providing deft support, Striptease might have done for Moore what Out Of Sight did for George Clooney. But although director Andrew Bergman deserves some of the blame, it was Moore who seemed to have got the tone wrong. The rest of the cast knew it was a comedy, but Demi seemed to think it was all about one brave woman's struggle against the system... through stripping. Which makes the one thing she has got right recently more mysterious. Check the producer credits on the Austin Powers movies and her name appears. That's not the semi-symbolic role of executive producer: that's the normally hands-on job of producer. Maybe it means she has a sense of humour after all. Or maybe it just means that she still has good instincts, but can't use them for her own movies. Either way, if Moore is going to shake off the baggage of the Nineties as effectively as she did the Eighties Mike Myers would be a better ally than Ron Bass. After all, these days everyone loves Rob Lowe. • Passion of Mind was released in the US last month and is released here later this year The ups and downs of Demi 1983 Beat 1,000 other wannabes to get her screen break on long-running daytime soap General Hospital. 1984 Plays Michael Caine's daughter in dire sex comedy Blame It On Rio. First topless scene. 1985 Stars in defining Brat Pack movie St Elmo's Fire as a coke-snorting yuppie . Follows up with About Last Night. 1987 Marries Bruce Willis. 1990 Ghost is her first blockbuster. Good performance as the winsome, grieving widow in the sleeper hit of the year. 1993 Indecent Proposal. Moore was the $1 million dollar object of desire in this tawdry morality tale. A controversial hit. 1994 Sexually harrasses Michael Douglas in corporate drama Disclosure. Another controversial hit. 1996 Earns record $12.5m fee for playing a stripper in Striptease. Strips on the David Letterman show to promote movie. It still flops. 1997 Shaves head and becomes super-buff to play trainee Navy Seal in G.I .Jane. Universally reviled flop. 1998 Seperates from Bruce Willis. 2000 Returns to screen in low-budget, arthouse movie Passion of Mind.

title: Monday briefing
bodyText: Observer A Texas-based consultancy specialising in "union-busting" is planning to open office in Britain and claims it has already been approached by a number of top 100 British companies who are worried about the new Employment Relations Act. A subsidiary of Interpublic, the New York-based marketing company, has emerged as the favourite to buy a 10% stake in Arsenal football club. Sunday Business Orange , the mobile phone group sold by Vodafone to France Télécom last week, has drawn up ambitious plans to merge with both Holland's KPN Mobile and Finnish rival Sonera , possibly creating a group worth £135bn. Henry Kaufman, the famous Wall Street economist, believes the US is headed for recession due to the delayed reaction of Federal Reserve in raising interest rates and failing to choke off the rise in financial asset prices. Aegon , the Netherlands insurance group which owns Scottish Equitable, has emerged as the front runner to buy Scottish Provident , the life assurer which has decided to review its mutual status. Trade and industry secretary Stephen Byers will launch a rescue package for the hard-hit textiles industry this week, though extra funding may be limited to £5m. Sunday Telegraph British Energy is close to winning a contract to run Canada's largest nuclear power station. The deal, worth up to £700m, makes it one of the British firm's most ambitious forays overseas. The Inland Revenue is examining the tax treatment of staff bonuses paid from an offshore trust at Durlacher, the high-flying internet investment firm. John Duffield, ousted boss of Jupiter Asset Management, is planning to sue the fund management group's parent Commerzbank. His claim could run into millions of pounds. Sunday Times The Budget proposal to scrap double taxation relief for multinationals based in Britain will cost Vodafone AirTouch £500m in a full financial year - wiping out half the group's profits at current levels. A transatlantic "super-alliance" is being considered by BA, the Dutch carrier KLM , and American Airlines, as the US airline continues its attempt to takeover its American rival Northwest Airlines . Mail on Sunday The reshaping of WH Smith by chief executive Richard Handover has claimed two more executive scalps, with the divisional finance and marketing directors at the high street retail arm leaving the group. A new hole in the pension scheme of a subsidiary of Geoffrey Robinson's failed TransTec engineering concern has come to light. Problems with the fund at Earby Light Engineers date back to 1995 - three years before TransTec bought the company. Sunday Express Standard Life policyholders will get much larger payouts than previously thought if the insurer sheds its mutual status. Internal figures suggest even many ordinary policyholders could get more than £10,000 in free shares. IoS NTL and ONdigital, the media groups that are challenging BSkyB for the rights to broadcast live Premiership matches, are understood to have complained to the European competition authorities over the handling of the sale of the rights. Ronald Baron, the largest independent shareholder in Sotheby's, is questioning the move to promote Robert Taubman, the son of former chairman Albert Taubman, to the board of the auction house. Sotheby's is at the centre of a price-fixing investigation by the US justice department. Reports out this week Today Interims Acal, Fountain, Jennings Brothers, Sanctuary Group. title: Britain faces losing out to the single currency
bodyText: The new Ernst & Young report on inward investment underlines the disadvantages of staying out of the euro. While Britain has retained its position as the biggest single recipient of projects in 1999, its market share has slipped from 28% to 24%. Kitty Ussher, chief economist of Britain in Europe, has also shown how the recent strength of the pound in relation to the euro is hitting manufacturing jobs which, in contrast to those in the euro economies, are declining. The emerging costs of Britain's decision not to join the single currency at the outset make it all the more essential that the government sticks firmly to the policy of deciding early in the next parliament whether the economic conditions are right for Britain to go in then. Assuming that the next election is held in early summer 2001, the following months could provide a favourable combination of circumstances - making a decision to join much more likely. The economic and financial committee of the European Council has recently confirmed that Britain already fulfils the Maastricht criteria regarding converging inflation rates and long-term rates, and the ceiling on public deficits and debt. The only question mark is over the exchange rate, which EU finance ministers understandably do not yet consider sufficiently stable. Turning to the chancellor's five economic tests announced in October 1997, the two key issues are convergence - between the British economy and the economies of the euro area - and flexibility. The questions of the impact on investment and jobs are to a considerable extent dependent on the first two tests. As to the impact on the City, it is already, thanks to careful preparation, acting as the de facto financial centre of the eurozone. Only if Britain decides to remain out for the longer term would the City be in danger of losing out. The British economy is already one of the most flexible in Europe. Following the Vodafone-Mannesmann merger, the signs are that the continental economies are already becoming more flexible, with Emu acting as an agent of change. It is also possible that the Lisbon summit, with its emphasis on targets, will encourage further progress. With regard to convergence between the British and continental economies, the commission expects the eurozone economies to grow 3.45% in 2000 and 3.1% in 2001, while the British economy is predicted to expand at much the same rate. Our short-term interest rates are still about double those set by the ECB, but, as growth in the eurozone picks up, short-term rates could converge as well. Exchange rate stability remains a problem with the pound having been at least 20% overvalued against the euro. Although the entry rate for the pound will be determined by negotiation with Britain's partners rather than the markets, it would clearly be helpful if the pound fell to a more competitive level against the euro some time before the entry decision was taken. With the strong recovery of the eurozone economies predicted over the next two years, the markets could begin to turn in favour of the euro. Indeed this may already be happening. But, it may well be that a firm government statement at an appropriate time of its intention to join the single currency early in the next parlia ment, provided the economic circumstances are right, could itself influence the market decisively. Political pressures for Britain to join could also have increased. Greece will already be a member, Denmark could well join after its referendum this autumn and Sweden could also be on the brink of entry. By the end of 2001, Britain could be the only EU member which is not also a member of the euro. The Euro-11 Committee - which France has plans to beef up - could by that time have become the Euro-14 Committee and the real economic powerhouse of Europe. British opinion may at present be wary of the euro. But polls show that the voters' hostility could be converted to support in a referendum if they thought that joining the euro was in both their and their nation's interest. By 2001, it should have become very clear that the euro is here to stay; that there would be significant benefits if Britain joins; and that there will be substantial costs if we stay out. The case for British entry is there to be won. Giles Radice MP, chairman of the European Movement and of the Commons treasury committee, has recently published a European Movement pamphlet - The Case for the Euro

title: Come to the fair
bodyText: Travelling fairs are fun and "immensely valuable in economic, social and historical terms", according to a committee of MPs. In a report that is a ringing endorsement of a centuries-old tradition, the Commons environment and regional affairs committee urged local councils to preserve old sites and provide new ones for funfairs. The MPs said: "It is a foolish local authority which does not do all it can to encourage travelling fairs." There were some less favourable aspects: "Problems complained of include noise and light pollution; the nuisance of drunken youths urinating or vomiting in areas close to the fairground site; rowdyism; and a popular belief that fairs encourage crime." Complaints were few compared with the number of fairs. Liverpool, which hosted 12 last year, received only five letters of complaint and eight phone calls. A survey of chief constables produced no evidence of a rise in crime associated with fairs. The committee praised Strasbourg, in France, which has provided an area for fairs. "It seemed particularly sad that so many English towns and cities have not followed this example, but have tried to banish fairs to out-of-the-way parts of the town in the expectation that they will fail and the town be rid of the problem," said the MPs. "We are strongly in favour of an urban renaissance. No large community ought to be without a central focus for public entertainment ... Villages, towns and cities with such spaces ought to ensure they are designed to make holding funfairs easy, with good access to services such as water and electricity, while those which do not have such areas ought to be striving to get them." The committee was concerned that the sites for showpeople to stay at during winter and stop overnight between fairs were disappearing. It urged the Showman's Guild, which was praised elsewhere for its efforts, to compile reports by region on this problem so the committee could return to the issue before the next general election.

title: Superplonk
bodyText: Bottles with a Spanish accent are noticeably prominent on the shelves of the nation's wine retailers, and the day is not far distant when such liquids - principally those from Spain, Chile and Argentina, with exotica such as Uruguay and Mexico coming up behind - could, as a linguistic group, overtake France as the number-one importer of wine to the UK. A fanciful idea? Not a bit of it. Spain's economic need to export much of its wine on an exchange-rate basis so favourable to Britain will ensure that it will continue to increase its share of the market; Chile's exports could expand to three times their present size; and Argentina has the opportunity to become a significant force with the possibility, one day, of being only spitting distance behind Italy as an exporter to the UK. It is to Tesco that we repair for the first of these wines. And it is, surprisingly, a rioja - £3.79 for a rioja? Yes, I queried that price, too, but it's as genuine as the wine. Vina Azbache Rioja 1998 (15.5 points out of 20) has firmly engaging fruit that undulates gently across the tastebuds and then accelerates with a gush of stylish tannins and a complex, layered finish. A stunning little wine for generous summer drinking. Spend more and you get more. Asda brings us Santa Julia Tempranillo Reserva 1999 (16.5 points, £6.96), from Argentina, and this has a classy texture, impressive depth and well-muscled yet ameliorated tannins - it is (not to put too fine a cliché upon it) delicious. I'll cheerfully, hic, say that again. Where this wine is concerned, it is delicious, delicious, delicious. Safeway has an interesting, very individual malbec from Argentina. Malbec is Bordeaux's makeweight grape, which never gets star-billing, but in Weinert Malbec 1994 you receive an exotic jolt of superbly creamy tannins that give the wine a marvellously distinctive richness and haute-couture cut. It rates 16 points, costs £8.99, and 79 Safeways have it (call 01622 712926 for details). At Thresher and Victoria Wine, there is Corazon Tempranillo 1999 (16.5 points, £4.49), from Argentina. It is a stunning experience: vivacious, sexy, committed and cheap. Corazon Bonarda 1999 (16 points, £3.99) is a superbly spicy food wine that makes up for everything beaujolais has lost: juice, freshness, personality and gushing fruit. Thresher and Victoria Wine also have a lovely Argentinian white wine, Norton Semillon Chardonnay 1999 (16 points, £4.99). It has positive plumpness and richness of purpose, a wonderful, baked-melon vinosity, soft-textured acids and a gorgeous finish. These retailers even have a terrific summer rosé: the non-vintage Castillo de Liria Rosé (15 points, £3.49), from Spain. Cherry-ripe and flirtatious, there are oodles of charm to its fruit, which will happily accompany barbecued food. And for the ice cream afterwards? Victoria Wine has the non-vintage Moscatel de Valencia (16 points, £3.99), easily the best-value dessert wine in the world. It parades rich, honey fruit with an undertone of orange marmalade. Thresher's Wine Rack and Bottoms Up have Marques de Grinon Valdepusa Cabernet Sauvignon 1997 (16.5 points, £9.99), from Spain. It demonstrates stunning development as it courses its sinuous route over the tongue. It is complex, corpulent, pliable and polished, yet stays dry. The same stores also have, in limited supply, Marques de Grinon Valdepusa Syrah 1997 (17 points, £10.99), which makes many an Aussie construct employing the same shiraz grape seem like junior-league liquid. The Marques's syrah is an exuberant experience of seriously multi-faceted fruit that is full of surprises. At Wine Rack branches only, and in short supply, there is Abadia Retuerta Rivola Duero 1996 (16.5 points, £6.99), from Spain. It has elongated fruit, elegant reach, and great balance of elements. Lovely label, too; very charming. And lastly, a magnificent rarity that is only available at Bottoms Up: Alvaro Palacios Finca Dofi Priorat 1996 (18 points, £59.95), which is one of the most nigh-perfect red wines in Spain. A truly exciting, world-class wine, and the unique product of an obsessed individual, it has dramatic tannins that adhere to the teeth with almost as much tenacity as the funereal label sticks to the bottle.

title: Carnival greets French farmer who took on McDonald's
bodyText: Amid scenes resembling an old-fashioned country fair, José Bové, the pipesmoking French activist, sheep farmer and icon of the modern anti-globalisation movement, arrived at his trial yesterday in an open wagon towed by a tractor. On trial for ransacking a half-built McDonald's restaurant in the southern French town of Millau last August, Mr Bové and nine fellow members of his Confédération Paysanne small farmers' union could face five years in prison and fines of up to £50,000. But that did not seem the main concern of the estimated 30,000 protesters crammed into the small town, more than doubling its population. "Even if it rains, we'll carry on partying," one small crowd of demonstrators chanted to the rhythm of a bass drum. Others outside the courthouse carried large banners saying "The World is Not for Sale" - the title of Mr Bové's book, which has become a bestseller in France. He has turned his trial into a huge anti-globalisation party, featuring concerts by French rock bands, a feast to promote traditional French food and more than a dozen public debates led by intellectuals and activists. But justice officials insisted that the happy gathering would not influence the outcome of the two-day trial. "There is no question of taking French justice hostage," said the court president, François Mallet. "What happens in the street does not interest this court." Mr Bové and his friends trashed the McDonald's restaurant as part of a symbolic protest against America's decision to impose crippling import duties on a variety of emblematic French goods including Roquefort cheese, for which Millau is famous, Dijon mustard and foie gras. The US move, itself a retaliation for the European Union's decision to ban imports of hormone-treated beef, sparked anger among France's traditional farmers and provided a focus for growing Gallic misgivings about the power of American multinationals, the inequality of world trade, and the dangers of industrialised food. The moustachioed activist, a veteran of protests in the Pacific against French nuclear testing, has become almost as well known in America as he is in France after leading protests against November's world trade talks in Seattle. The US magazine Business Week recently named him one of the top 50 people helping to shape the new Europe. His popularity is due in part to his capacity to boil complex issues down to pithy protest statements. Asked once why he didn't resolve his differences by having McDonald's put Roquefort on cheeseburgers, he replied: "That would be like selling holy water in a sex shop." In front of the McDonald's restaurant they dismantled almost a year ago, a small group of sheep farmers set up a stand where visitors could buy a slice of pungent Roquefort cheese, a glass of wine and a sticker denouncing globalisation, all for £1. The restaurant itself was closed. Mr Bové, 47, has said he hopes the trial will be the start of a vast campaign coinciding with the start of France's presidency of the EU tomorrow. "Any sentence would be unacceptable," he said yesterday. "At any rate, the verdict will be political. We are fighting for the dismissal of this case."

title: Ian Black on the French presidency's agenda
bodyText: France faces a hard grind and little glory when it assumes the presidency of the EU today at a time of deepening controversy surrounding the character and direction of the entire European project. The main task facing the French is vital but not glamourous: to push through the reforms needed to ensure that creaking decision-making machinery will work when new members from the emerging democracies of eastern Europe join in coming years. Between now and the Nice summit in December, French ministers must persuade, cajole and bargain with their EU partners on a narrow but extremely difficult agenda. The key items contrast starkly with the ambitious vision of a Europe led by a bold Franco-German "pioneer group" sketched out by President Jacques Chirac in Berlin on Tuesday. True, the new treaty that enshrines the latest changes will not match the scope of the Maastricht agreement of 1992, which launched the single currency project. But without success in Nice, paralysis will surely set in. French officials know this is not a moment for grandstanding: there is already embarrassment in Paris at the glaring difference between their Gaullist president - keen to ally with Germany - and his centre-left prime minister, Lionel Jospin. "If the 15 [member states] do not meet this challenge, all the other speculations, proposals, reflections and orientations will not have a leg to stand on," the foreign minister, Hubert Védrine, warned. At stake is the inter-governmental conference (IGC), a rolling negotiation on issues left over from the 1997 Amsterdam treaty because they were just too tricky. Portugal made virtually no progress during its presidency, which ended last night. The basic problem is as simple as the details are complex: institutions created for the EEC in 1957 are close to breakdown. And with 12 countries lining up to join from 2003, the machinery must be modernised. Even though the enlargement timetable is slipping and enthusiasm waning, the need is urgent. Treaties require time-consuming ratification by national parliaments while anxious Poles, Hungarians and Cypriots need reassuring that existing member states are serious. France's insistence that enlargement is as high a priority as the IGC reforms underlines the way in which the two issues are intertwined. The weight of each member's vote needs to be readjusted: Germany has no more votes now than before reunification. Are 80m Germans "equal" only to 57-60m Britons, Frenchmen or Italians? Larger states want to redress a balance which favours smaller ones, fearing that in an expanded union they could be blocked by the tiddlers. Spain wants more votes. Experts say this is a mathematical problem, but it is highly sensitive politically. Most states accept the need to reduce the use of the national veto and increase majority voting to avoid deadlock in an EU with up to 27 members. Romano Prodi, the president of the European Commission, calls the veto a ball and chain. At the recent summit in Portugal, Austria was able to hold up agreements on a withholding tax on savings. Britain insists on retaining the veto for tax, defence, social security, treaty changes and border controls. Minor changes are likely. As regards the 20-strong European commission, it is a supranational body. But having a national bum on a seat is a right that no member state is prepared to forego. Britain, France, Germany, Italy and Spain are ready to give up one of their current two jobs. Future commissioners from Cyprus and Estonia are likely to find themselves in the de facto role of junior or deputy commissioners. There is a plan to let some states integrate faster than others, so-called reinforced cooperation. In the British political debate this is about a "two-speed" Europe, with Britain in the slow lane. London's greatest but largely unspoken fear is that if closer cooperation is made easier, the 11 euro zone countries may move to formalise their role and harmonise taxation and budgetary policies, making a British decision on the single currency even tougher than it is. Laurent Fabius, the French finance minister, who is worried by the weakness of the euro in the global currency markets, is a key figure here. Diplomats predict that the outcome at Nice will be just enough for the French to be able to claim success. "It has to work," says one veteran Brussels negotiator. "Failure is unthinkable. And it's better to have a bad treaty than no treaty at all." Other big issues seem likely to be put off. Divisive plans for an EU charter of fundamental rights may be boiled down to a declaration, with a promise to take it up again in future, probably in the new IGC that Germany is already demanding. The same is true of plans to look at the hot potato that is a future EU constitution. France is certain to mount a big push on defence, where Mr Chirac has forged a powerful alliance with Mr Blair. Progress has already been made on institutional mechanisms, although the US and Turkey are nervous about Nato's interests. Neutrals such as Ireland and Finland are worried about their role in plans to field an EU rapid reaction force with 60,000 soldiers. A November conference on troop numbers will be a key fixture of the French presidency, as will a Balkan summit and initiatives to reinforce links with the Mediterranean. The hot seat presidential points • European Union presidencies last for six months, from January to June or July to December, and rotate between the 15 member states • Portugal was last at the helm. Sweden and Belgium respectively are next in line • France will chair nearly 200 meetings including an informal summit in Biarritz in October and one at Nice in December. President Chirac will also preside over a meeting of Balkan leaders • Lenotre, France's biggest delicatessen chain, has been commissioned to provide gourmet catering at both summits. Nice will include a grand fireworks display over the bay • Presidencies manage the business of the European council. They convene meetings, set the agenda, draft compromises and call votes. They represent the council and member states in dealings with third countries • Large states such as France try to have defined ambitions for their presidencies. Smaller ones make do with being "good presidents" • France has designed its own cubist- inspired logo in the form of a red, white and blue face combining the colours of the national flag with the 12 golden stars of Europe. It says the image represents "a homo Europeus" as well as "dialogue and France's resolutely European outlook"

title: Still stuck in the world of Jim Hacker
bodyText: It will be business as usual today at one French factory in Pessac, near Bordeaux. The national mint will be striking coins, but they will not be francs. The last franc was produced in May. The demise of the currency - which was born the day Jean Le Bon was ransomed from his English captors in 1360 for three million gold crowns and declared "franc des Anglais", or free of the English - has barely been remarked upon in France: not a single headline, not a single outraged column. Here, the euro appears to have been universally accepted, if not embraced. Shops, restaurants and cafes print their prices in francs and euros. Shoppers can pay with a euro chequebook from a euro bank account. But where the French, and for that matter the Germans, have taken a pragmatic approach to their currencies, most in Britain seem horrified at the mere prospect of the Queen's head being removed from their banknotes. Tony Blair may privately accept that Britain will weaken itself politically and economically if it remains outside the single currency. But in public, overwhelming hostility to the euro means the prime minister will say only that he plans a referendum after the next election if a series of economic tests have been met. As France takes up the six-month rotating presidency of the EU today, two very different popular interpretations of the European project will clash once more. On one side of the Channel, mistrust and a visceral rejection of all that Brussels represents; on the other, a population revealed by a new survey this week to be generally enthusiastic about Europe (59%), and overwhelmingly in favour of further and faster integration (70%). Yet even as that poll was being published in Paris, a family butcher in the Essex town of Leigh-on-Sea was setting out to become the latest martyr of the Eurosceptic cause, being jailed for resisting new European regulations on metrication. David Stephens, whose Mandy's Chop Shop is decked out with union flags, is leading an "imperial revolution" of Great Britain. "Napoleon said England is a nation of shopkeepers - well the shopkeepers are revolting," warned the butcher, who continues to sell his meat in pounds and ounces in defiance of EU regulations. His lone campaign highlights a deep distrust of the role Brussels plays in the lives of British people. Regulations that cause barely a ripple in continental Europe lead to endless scare stories as anti-Europeans follow the example of Jim Hacker, who rose to the top job in Yes, Prime Minister after fighting to prevent Brussels renaming the British sausage the emulsified high-fat offal tube. Much of the damage is done by the press. Mr Blair, who says he wants to work at the heart of Europe and who has engaged more positively with his continental partners than any other recent British prime minister, issued a plea on Thursday night for the media to stop seeing the EU as a "conspiracy against Britain". A speech to the Reichstag earlier this week by the French president, Jacques Chirac, in which he floated the idea of a "pioneering group" of EU nations, led to predictable howls of protest. "Blair isolated as Berlin and Paris press for EU fast track," the Daily Mail screamed - although the proposals would, in reality, quite possibly create a club Britain could join. In France, Mr Chirac's speech was carefully analysed and variously declared "important", "a positive contribution to a vital debate", and "a much-needed breadth of vision from a traditional pillar of European construction". Even if it quite seriously embarrassed the more cautious Socialist-led government of Lionel Jospin, the point was that Europe deserved, and needed, big ideas. This is despite reservations about Europe expressed on either side of the political divide in Franc. Wary of too many "big ideas", Mr Jospin's government wants to use the presidency to make progress on some concrete, practical issues designed to appeal to the average citizen. "It's true France used to be Europe's idealist, but in many ways it's now much more pragmatic - it wants practical and positive advantages, things that will make Europe work, things the people will appreciate," said Pascal Boniface, the director of the Institute for International and Strategic Relations, a Paris thinktank. So France will be putting greater welfare protection and social justice high up on the agenda, as well as food safety, doping in sport, a more effective European immigration policy in the wake of the Dover tragedy last week, in which 58 illegal Chinese immigrants died, and stricter controls on shipping in European waters after the sinking of the oil tanker Erika off the Brittany coast. Many of those issues, the French public indicated in this week's survey, are ones they feel European institutions may be able to deal with more effectively than national governments. With its fears fanned by scare stories splashed all over the press, the British public may never even contemplate that possibility. British scare stories about Europe tend to have two themes: our isolation in the face of hostile Europeans, or our triumphs over hostile Europeans. Hence the reporting of the prime minister's management of the recent EU summit in Portugal. It was, the British were told, a triumph for Mr Blair. "Britain has defeated demands by Brussels for a European savings tax," the Mail reported after Gordon Brown hammered out a compromise with his European partners on the withholding tax. In fact, for the French the hard-won compromise was the result not of the British prime minister's negotiating skills, but of Austrian blackmail in an attempt to return to the European fold. As it enters this presidency, the French government knows it will have popular support for almost any fast-track measure it pursues. It is actually more interested in not rocking the boat and in completing the intergovernmental conference on schedule. There is little on that agenda to cause serious friction with Britain. Yet the British press and public opinion, through a combination of what Mr Boniface calls "ignorance and misrepresentation", will want to see the next six months as a battle. They are not the first to think that way. "When was the last time we got our way with France?" Jim Hacker once asked his cabinet secretary, Sir Humphrey Appleby. "Battle of Waterloo, 1815," came the reply.

title: No 10 savages Eurosceptic press
bodyText: Tony Blair gave unambiguously enthusiastic backing to the European Union yesterday while Labour accused newspapers of "distortion and misrepresentation" over Europe.
