The land of plenty • Granted independent status in 1191 with the vocation of defending Christianity against the Saracens, Monaco has been ruled by the Grimaldi family for more than 700 years. • In 1993, the principality was made a UN member. • Only 5,000 residents - out of a population of 30,000 -have Monégasque nationality. The remainder include 12,000 French citizens and 5,000 Italians. • Intense building, much of it on reclaimed land, has changed the economy of the only city, Monte Carlo , over the past 50 years. Most of its wealth now comes from banking and tourism. • The principality's casino, founded in 1856, once the main source of revenue, provides only about 4% of Monaco's annual income. • Legislative power is shared between Prince Rainier, who has more than a dozen other titles, and an 18-member elected national council. The executive is controlled by a French-appointed minister of state. • The security forces are made up of a ceremonial army unit, which is not issued with ammunition, and 400 police, who are nearly all French nationals.

title: Diary
bodyText: •And so, at last, to the new Book of the Month for November, Tony Really Loves Me (Bell Vanity Publishing, £6.99) by my old friend Stuart Bell, the MP for Middlesbrough. What a colourful life Stuart has led. One minute the author of the sub-porn novel Paris, 69, the next an ecclesiastic commissioner, not to mention an alleged bust-up after a family car prang with the neighbours. Anyone looking for a definition of "renaissance man" need seek no further. Our first extract comes from the letter to Mr Tony Blair, marked for opening on election day 1997, that opens this memoir. "Tony. You have not written your name in water," writes Stuart, in a reference to Keats's epitaph to himself, "but in the history books because you built New Labour on the traditions and values of the old; you built the future on to the past in the present. It falls to few men." I'm sorry, I've come over all queasy and will have to lie down. Perhaps we'll return to it tomorrow. •A rousing hats off to Harrow council, which was featured here recently for so generously giving to £15,000-per-annum Harrow school the portion of the public highway leading to its gates. Now, we gather, the council is to launch celebrations for being awarded "beacon status" by the government. A beacon of what is a little unclear - the council has a £3.2m deficit and is cutting all sorts of services - but who can argue with abolishing the children's librarian at Stanmore, when the council finds at least £60,000 to celebrate its beaconhood with a website, a roadshow and other such amusements? •Today's Carry On Up The Cabinet item is, as a novelty, a reverse piece of casting. Our thanks to Rod Humphries of St Ives, who faxed to suggest that, in any remake of the classic Carry On Cleo, the part of Hingist Pod should be offered to John Prescott. Pod, of course, was the British transport masterbrain who invented the square wheel. •The mainstream view that the Church of England is run almost exclusively by people of infirm mind and simpletons takes a knock from a leaflet sent to members of the General Synod invited to forthcoming meetings in Westminster. "Clothes:" begins one passage. "In London in November and February, it is often very cold outside and very warm inside the hall. Bring a removable warm coat, jacket, cardigan." Very kind. •A press release arrives from Hovis concerning National Toast Week, which started on Monday. Hovis asks whether we would like separate press releases for different parts of the country, "detailing specific regional toast trends". You're very kind. If there's still time, we'll have the lot. •The European Space Agency seeks volunteers for a three-month bed-rest experiment in Toulouse, France early next year, the purpose of which, it claims, "is to improve the rehabilitation of patients and evaluate the consequences of long space flights. Candidates should be male, EU citizens and aged from 25 to 45, not overweight and not under medical treatment." "Most of all," it continues, "they must be highly motivated." Well of course. You can hardly hope to laze around in bed for three months without a keen sense of purpose. The ESA is said to be particularly keen on William Hague, but will perhaps consider other applicants. •Still in France, meanwhile, the consoling news is that the weather has been almost as bad as here. Earlier this week Jacques Charant of Cherbourg took the flood warnings so seriously that he bought a rowing boat and placed it by his bedside for a quick getaway through the window in case of disaster. The ploy worked brilliantly. Admittedly, when M Charant rose in the night for a pee he tripped over the boat and broke his leg - but at no point did he come close to drowning. diary@theguardian.com

title: Britain resists demands for ban on French beef
bodyText: Agriculture Minister Nick Brown yesterday ruled out a British ban on French beef imports despite mounting fears that BSE is widespread among France's cattle. A Ministry official said: 'As law-abiding Europeans, we would not break the law by unilaterally imposing a ban. That is something for the European Commission and its scientific advisers to decide.' From tomorrow the majority of schools in Paris will have taken beef off the menu, and the French government is shortly expected to announce a halt to sales of beef-on-the bone following a threefold rise in the number of BSE cases in the past year. On Friday, France's biggest steak-house chain became the latest to withdraw T-bone steaks from the menu in its 220 restaurants. The row over safety standards in the French meat industry deepened last month when it was revealed that eight tonnes of meat from herds infected with BSE had ended up on supermarket shelves. The country's beef sales have gone into freefall, and farmers learnt yesterday that Russia and Hungary, which account for 14 per cent of their exports, had embargoed imports of French beef. This weekend the Tories called on Brussels to ban exports of French beef. Shadow agriculture spokesman James Paice said: 'If the the European Commission won't ban it, I think our Government should. That's exactly what the French have done to us in refusing to accept our beef. Europe lifted the ban and the French unilaterally decided not to.' Britain imports around 5,000 tonnes of French beef each year, although few firms in the supermarket or catering businesses would own up this weekend to using it, as they moved to reassure the public that most beef sold by them was British. A spokeswoman for Marks & Spencer said: 'None of our products sold in Britain are made from French beef, and although we do sell French beef in our Paris store we have imposed strict controls on the meat we use and are sure that it is safe.' However, neither she nor anybody from Tesco, Sainsbury or Whitbread - which owns hundreds of restaurants, including the Pizza Hut, Café Rouge and Bella Pasta chains - could say categorically that French beef was not used in its products. A spokesman from Tesco said that most of the French beef was used in processed food such as burgers and pies. He also suggested that French beef could end up in British school meals. The Tories pointed out that the French still allow farmers to feed their cattle on meat and bone meal which has been banned in the UK for 10 years. There is also a concern that the French still let beef from cows more than 30 months old - the animals most at risk of contracting BSE - enter the human food chain. However, the UK Food Standards Agency defended the French beef industry. A spokesman said: 'Controls throughout Europe should, if correctly implemented and enforced, be removing all the high-risk material from beef before it enters the human food chain. 'The reported incidence of BSE in cattle in the UK is still more than 150 times greater than in France, and there have been no substantiated cases of imported beef over 30 months old reaching consumers in the UK.' In 1999, the number of cases of BSE in British cattle was 472 per million compared with only 2.8 cases per million in France. However, while the number of diseased cattle in Britain continues to decline, the rate of infection in France is increasing. Recently two more cows infected with mad cow disease have been discovered in western France, bringing the number found in France to 86. There were fewer than 30 cases reported throughout the whole of last year. In Britain this year, there have so far been more than 1,000 cows infected with BSE. There has also been anecdotal evidence of French farmers concealing the extent of the disease in their herds by killing and burying sick cows rather than reporting them to the authorities. France's deepening BSE crisis has dealt a blow to a country that prides himself on taking more precautions than any other nation to make the meat safe to eat. Last month a French meat trader and his son spent a week in jail after being accused of knowingly selling a BSE-infected cow to an abattoir. They had tried to hide the sick animal in a batch of healthy ones. Useful links BSE Inquiry Food Standards Agency BSE Review MAFF BSE site Department of Health BSE/CJD site Human BSE Foundation - voluntary support group BSE news and research

title: The CBI has been onside.
bodyText: The timing is perfect. Just 48 hours before Chancellor Gordon Brown delivers what will probably be the last Pre-Budget Report before the general election, he faces business leaders at the Confederation of British Industry conference. If next year's March Budget is to provide Brown with a chance to charm the electorate with tax cuts, this week's report will be about getting UK plc firmly on his side. And with Treasury coffers overflowing with a larger than predicted surplus, the CBI audience in Birmingham will be expecting goodies. Director-General Digby Jones says: 'We believe that government could give some £3 billion back to business.' Despite wobbles over manufacturing, the CBI and the Government have been riding in tandem. Since Jones's appointment last January, the CBI has been banging the drum for increased transport spending. 'We were delighted by the wonderful decision to invest £180bn on transport,' says Jones. 'If we're going to be more productive, we've got to get our goods to market.' The employers' leader warned against a resumption of the fuel protests because they 'won't work', will make a poor impression on investors and 'hurt wealth creation'. The pro-euro campaign by the CBI was handily placed on a backburner soon after Jones's arrival. He says: 'The Government's European policy is going down the right lines'. The current imperative is to iron out the workings of the single market, a view that will be echoed in the Chancellor's speech tomorrow. Jones even invokes the Treasury terminology about not returning the economy to 'boom and bust'. But aside from the mantra of macroeconomic stability, it is the UK's poor productivity that has preoccupied previous pre-Budget statements. Indeed this week's statement, to be entitled 'Building Long-term Prosperity for All', will set out the Government's approach to dealing with this medium-term question. And although the CBI has promised to work with the Government to tackle the productivity gap, their immediate approaches will differ. The employers will launch a major initiative to cut the red tape and regulation stifling the growth of small businesses. The Government, it says, has swamped firms with 16 new pieces of employment legislation since coming to power. 'We don't want any more,' says Jones. He wants Brown to 'walk the walk' after talking about getting government out of the way where its actions harm business. For the Chancellor, the report, aside from petrol and pensioners, will be about reviving entrepreneurship to energise economically de-funct urban areas, uniting the productivity and social justice agendas. But Brown, his pockets overflowing with as much as £15bn in loose change, will be pressed to grant some form of tax cut to businesses. Tax competition has taken off across Europe, with Germany, Italy and France slashing companies' burdens to attract investment. International comparisons of the tax burden, however, show the UK faring reasonably well. Instead, the report will again focus on Brown's favoured business constituency: the revered hi-tech entrepreneurs, particularly those with a social conscience. There will be more microeconomic tinkering with tax credits to provide incentives for research and development, and skills investment. This follows a well-trodden path of micro adjustments to encourage productivity improvements. Following the recent Cohen report on 'social investment', there will be tax breaks to encourage venture capital and start-ups in deprived urban areas. Stamp duty on 'brownfield' developments is expected to be abolished, as recommended by Lord Rogers's Urban Task Force. The CBI's immediate priorities lie elsewhere, but it is not out of tune with this agenda. Jones says his organisation wants 'socially inclusive wealth creation', with more money and more control over planning for regional development agencies. This moves in the same direction: trying to prepare fertile ground for small businesses to grow in new industries. Many of the areas in need of regeneration, however, have suffered because of the decline of large-scale manufacturing. 'Old economy' business leaders will want proof that their understated role in the development of new technology and higher productivity will be fully recognised, particularly after the dampening of dotcom fever. Employee share ownership may be less attractive now that the hi-tech sector has taken a pasting. But the long- awaited reform of approved share options schemes will be launched in the report. The current limit of 15 employees is expected to be lifted, and the total tax-free limit expanded to £2.5m per company. Such schemes will allow all employees to share in the stellar performance of a listed small business. Business groups, however, are lobbying for a bolder move. The Computer and Software Services Association, for example, seeks a blanket simplification of the scheme featuring a basic rule on how long options need to be held to justify tax breaks. Such schemes now need to be approved by the Inland Revenue. Whenever the Chancellor gives to business with one narrowly targeted hand, as in this case, he invariably takes something back through extra red tape. As a rule of thumb, any tax breaks so specifically targeted need to be verified to prevent abuse, so adding to the complexity of the system. But in this case the stringent tax-avoidance rules affected the flexibility of high growth start-ups to offer capital stakes, rather than their dwindling cash funds, to attract and retain staff. Brown may decide this bureaucratic burden is relatively small beer compared to the low taxes paid by companies. Another factor is that the TUC, which lent crucial support to the Government during the fuel crisis, opposes the CBI's anti-red tape campaign, viewing the move as an attempt to restrict employees' rights. The employers also want the Chancellor to review the climate change levy, remove stamp duty on stock exchange transactions and make a major cut in vehicle excise duty on lorries. They are making a general plea for greater consultation on the minutiae of policy - a return to the original rationale for the pre-Budget report as the basis for at least four months' policy discussion. The single biggest cause of business distress, the strong pound, will not be the subject of consultation. Indeed exchange-rate woes and skills shortages are seen, in the Government's unspoken policy, as a spur to UK plc to use innovative technology and good management to get the most out of workers. Two years ago the UK exporters claimed the bearable DM exchange rate was 2.70. It now says that DM3 would not be too bad. That, and the trade surplus with the EU, shows manufacturers are making headway on productivity. But that is a medium and long-term challenge helped by public investment in transport and education. Right now, profit warnings are on the increase, the election is near and it is essential to retain business confidence. The Chancellor may rule out a return to the old short- termism, but perhaps this week will see the emergence of a politically expedient 'new' short-termism. The CBI's wish list • Tax cuts of up to £3bn to ease rising business costs • Slash red tape • Improve consultation on tax changes • Lower cost to business of road transport before next Budget • Reconsider National Insurance on unapproved share options TUC • Pensions, not petrol • Increase in basic state pension • Age-related supplement for over-75s • Regional manufacturing aid fund, similar to the £1.6bn agriculture fund • No justification for across-the-board tax cuts IoD • Lighten tax and regulatory load on business • Abandon plans for Employment tax credit • 1p a litre cut in fuel duties • A cap on the the tax/GDP ratio • Maintain fiscal prudence Computer and Software Services Association • Review National Insurance on unapproved share options • Expand and simplify approved share-option schemes • Reform IR35 to protect solo contractors Assorted fuel lobbies and hauliers • 26p off fuel duty, but would settle for 15p • Reduction in Vehicle Excise Duty • Foreign hauliers to pay special tax for using UK roads National Pensioners' Convention • Substantial increase in the basic state pension to make up for the £30 a week that pensioners have lost since the earnings link was abolished in 1980 • Restoration of the earnings link

title: Analysis: Poor relations and European Union enlargement
bodyText: The European Union has long wanted to create a single "social space" and a declared aim of the social charter incorporated into the Maastricht treaty is that Europe's social dimension be given the same importance as economics and markets. The Lisbon summit agreed earlier this year on new strategic goals for the next decade and among them was greater social cohesion. But how does this social dimension relate to enlargement? Ten of the countries wanting to join the European Union - preparing for enlargement is supposed to be the main point of next week's Nice summit - are in central and eastern Europe. The others are Malta and Cyprus, which are both small and relatively prosperous. Turkey's application is not progressing because of human rights abuses. For the applicants from the east, the EU represents prosperity; membership is assumed to bestow it. Yet it may be the consequences of enlargement for poverty across Europe that are going to be most dramatic. Officially, poverty is defined as getting by on half the average per capita consumption level. The population of the 10 eastern European candidates comes to just over 25% of the EU total but their combined GDP is only one-tenth the EU's. Consumption per head in the EU, expressed in euros, ranges from 9,277 in Greece to 15,325 in Luxembourg. In the candidates the range is from 3,305 in Bulgaria to 7,850 in Slovenia. Average consumption level is just over two-fifths that of the EU. In terms of inequality, there is not a marked difference in inequality within the individual EU states and the candidates. The poorest tenth of the population has between 2.5% and 4.4% of all income in the EU whereas in the candidates the range is slightly wider from 2.2% to 5.1%. At present about 17% of the EU's population is poor, ranging from 9.4% in Denmark to 33.3% in Portugal. Most of the poor are concentrated, not surprisingly, in the larger nations; over two-thirds of Europe's poor live in France, the UK, Spain and Germany. The extent of poverty in the candidate countries is shown in the chart. It is far higher than for existing EU members: it ranges from 30% in Slovenia to 92% in Latvia. Among the candidates in the likely first round of enlargement the average is 69% and among the second-round countries 83%. Full enlargement would almost double the proportion of people living in poverty from 17% to 30% and that poverty would be heavily concentrated - 55% - in the 10 candidate countries. The total number of people living in poverty would more than double. One of the presumed benefits of enlargement for the eastern European nations is that their growth would be accelerated. We simulated the consequences of all the candidates growing by 2% per annum faster than existing EU members for two decades. Such growth would cut poverty but one-third of the population in the first-round countries and half in the second-round countries would still be below the poverty standard after 20 years. These disparities raise a question about how far countries would converge in an enlarged EU. From the perspective of "social space", the extent and distribution of poverty is critical. Will the candidates ever catch up? Getting to the EU average by 2015 would require sustained levels of growth in the front-running states of between 5% and 9% every year - rates which are far in excess of any achieved over a sustained period by any European nation ever. Even if growth were sustained at 2% a year faster in the candidates - a remarkable achievement in itself - poverty would still be twice as prevalent in the first-round applicants and three times as prevalent in the second-round counties as in the EU. The extent and distribution of poverty affects other policies. The EU cohesion and structural funds have the goal of reducing inequality. If these funds were to be redistributed according to the extent of poverty (which is not at present the only criterion) then countries such as Greece, Portugal, Spain and the Irish Republic could lose most of what they now receive. The largest chunk of EU spending is on agriculture. It claims a far larger share of output and employment in the candidates. If the common agricultural policy (CAP) were to be extended to an enlarged EU on the present basis the cost would be enormous. There would be huge losses for farmers in Germany, France and Britain. Yet if means were found of excluding new entrants from CAP this would reduce their chances of catching up and result in yet higher levels of poverty in the candidates. What is clear is that poverty in the candidates will have massive implications for existing EU programmes. One of the effects of joining the EU ought to be free movement of workers. Thus far the extent of migration within the EU has been quite small. It, too, will be affected by the distribution and extent of poverty - there could be very substantial levels of migration east to west, with major political and social consequences. These too have, thus far, been given little attention. Decisions about enlarging the EU will not and should not be decided only on the basis of concerns about poverty. But they are important: how the EU develops will affect the lives of hundreds of million of people and the most vulnerable of them are those who are victims of poverty. David Piachaud is professor of social policy at the London School of Economics d.piachaud@lse.ac.uk

title: Watchdog plans BSE checks in Ireland
bodyText: Food safety watchdogs are stepping up checks on Irish beef and other processed meat from cattle to help reassure consumers that fast increasing floods of imports are at least as safe as home-grown produce. The republic provides a tenth of all beef eaten in Britain, 20 times the amount imported from France, and the BSE epidemic has hit a far higher proportion of cows than it has across the Channel. The board of the food standards agency has asked the government's BSE advisers to assess early next week whether present safeguards are good enough and has reinforced instructions to local authorities to ensure paperwork enabling food to be traced back to overseas suppliers is fully in order. The agency may also send officials to Dublin to ensure that BSE controls in that country are adequate. The turning of attention to Ireland coincides with an European Union-wide tightening of anti-BSE measures in an effort to support a rapidly collapsing market. These include a temporary ban on feeding all meat and bone meal to other livestock, and a ban on any animals over 30 months old going into food unless their carcasses have been tested and show no signs of BSE. A meeting of agriculture ministers on Monday, to which health ministers have also been invited, is expected to endorse the package which brings the rest of the EU more into line with Britain. Commission officials are also likely to press for a speedy implementation of tougher rules on country of origin labelling. The new focus on Ireland is unlikely to embarrass the food safety commissioner, David Byrne, an Irishman who has been urging member states to take far more seriously the dangers of BSE . He attacked Germany and Spain, which have recently reported cases, for being complacent. He has warned that enforcement of rules is essential if human health is to be protected and the beef industry is to survive. Italy, Spain, Greece and the Netherlands have restricted some imports of French beef, but European commission scientists have so far said there are no grounds for such unilateral action. Imports account for a quarter of the 1m tonne British beef market - a proportion that has been rising steadily as the British farming crisis deepens, and Irish beef makes up nearly 108,000 tonnes, or 40% of the 256,000 tonne total. By contrast French exports to Britain are 5,570 tonnes, but there is far greater political hostility because of the continuing French ban on British exports, lifted by the EU in August last year. Many of France's exports here are in the form of corned beef and other processed products, whereas most of Ireland's is as carcass meat, whose origin is often easier to check. Neither country is allowed to export meat from animals over 30 months to Britain, even though, for a few weeks more, they are feeding their own populations such beef. Only 14 countries that are BSE-free are allowed to do so, they include South American countries, much of whose supply is still as corned beef, Australia, New Zealand and the USA. The Conservatives have been pressing hard for an import ban on French beef, but farmers have been more cautious especially because they do not want the export of live sheep to the continent to be jeopardised by tit-for-tat measures. But any doubts about Irish or French produce could only add to the crisis across Europe. The food standards agency is also worried that controls in Britain are not perfect and are worried that some cattle, legally killed by farmers for their own consumption, are being distributed far more widely and escaping anti-BSE controls. Main beef exporters to Britain in year to end August 2000 (tonnes) Ireland 107,865 Brazil 71,662 Netherlands 12, 966 Argentina 9,735 Uruguay 9,124 Australia 6,814 Namibia 6,187 Zimbabwe 6.086 France 5,573 Botswana 4,746 Germany 4,042

title: Searching for reason amid Euro-hysteria
bodyText: It is so much easier to handle personalities than issues, and the stories are so much more accessible, readable, even interesting. Ask yourself, yes even Guardian readers ask yourselves, would you rather read about qualified majority voting or John Prescott being branded a male chauvinist by a woman French environment minister? He had accused Dominique Voynet of torpedoing his attempts to broker an international deal on global warming because she was "too tired" to concentrate. The French minister, not used to the ways and idioms of Two Jags, did not take kindly to such remarks, and told the world so. It is so much easier to indulge in schadenfreude than to wrestle with enlargement. BSE in Germany and France had the press, and not just the Eurosceptic press, very close to a state of gloating. It was as though the serious European issues of the previous week had been erased from media memory. We were back at the usual point on the dial, where Europe was another country that we did not trust. And the Nice summit grows ever closer. The Eurosceptic press was in full cry. "How dare you preach to us about patriotism," wrote Frederick Forsyth in the Mail, ranting at the prime minister. "Memories of 1066 mean we cannot trust our neighbours from hell," the Express billed a piece reflecting on the Prescott Voynet spat. We are looking in this column each week at the way the press reflects the most important issue of our time, the one that destroys prime ministers and unseats governments, the one that fuels xenophobia and nostalgia alike. Europe. And as another crunch summit approaches, we are asking if sensible debate exists in our newspapers, if reason can exist amid the hysteria. So we turn to the Sun. Here is the newspaper so often accused of ignoring or trivialising serious issues giving nearly a page to the "battlegrounds of EU summit". The hardest thing is to explain issues as arcane as constitutional change in the EU to a Sun audience. Yet, admittedly from its known Euro-sceptic position, the Sun took its readers through the major Nice issues. For those who hold dear John Birt's famous "mission to explain" dictum it was The Times which this week served the European debate well. In a three-day, six page preview to the Nice summit, it explained in great and accessible detail the issues. Qualified majority voting and the veto, enlargement and the size of the commission, the voting strengths of individual member states, were all laid out. Issues not personalities, description not propaganda. Was it read? One of the more significant stories of the week was the latest social attitudes survey, which found large class divides in support for Europe. The survey, as reported in the Financial Times, found only 8% of the working class supported the single currency, compared with 28% of the middle class. Another was the entry into the pre-summit argument of the governor of the bank of England, Eddie George, which provided a classic illustration of how the Euro-agenda of different papers can influence the way they report the same event. "Euro will hurt on pay and tax says bank boss" was the Mail's headline over a story that started: "Britain faces a return to the stark 1970s policies of high taxes and pay restraint if we join the Euro, Sir Eddie George warned yesterday." The Financial Times said of the same speech: "He said the use of fiscal policy instead of interest rates would involve recognition by people that their behaviour would have to be driven by the need to control inflation. Employees, for example, would be under pressure to moderate wage demands to avoid punishing tax rises." But after all the scare stories about the European rapid reaction force, the assault on British sovereignty, and the embarrassment of John Prescott's altercation in the Hague, came the public's response. A Mori poll in the Times put the Labour lead over the Tories two points up at 15%. Europe in the spotlight, and Labour's co-ordinated offensive provoking a strong sceptic response has not damaged Labour at all. Will that be the case after next week's summit? Peter Cole is professor of journalism at Sheffield University

title: From trailer park to Central Park
bodyText: When it comes to class distinctions and serious snobbery, we British tend to believe we're top dogs. We have royalty in Buckingham Palace. We have aristocrats in the House of Lords. We have an extremely snooty middle class, a vociferous, boisterous working class and a nearly invisible underclass. Each is separated from its nearest neighbour by layers of well-nuanced resentment, smugness, irrational fear and mutual contempt. And whenever two or more Englishmen gather in foreign parts, they will spend several minutes circling each other warily until they decide whether the other is someone they'd ever be caught dead talking to back home. It's a sickness. Americans would have us believe that they're past all this. They had a revolution, after all, and the Declaration of Independence enshrined social equality as a right shared by all men. But as any slave or woman could have told you in 1776, America had a class system the moment the citizenry briefly tried to make George Washington king. Though they're loath to admit it, Americans have a class system that in its own pseudo-egalitarian way is just as visible and divisive as the British one. We're used to decoding all the social signals that separate, say, Lucinda Lambton from Ricky Tomlinson, but unlike us, Americans don't spend much time attempting to locate precisely and minutely the dodgy social origins and non-U accents of their neighbours and co-workers. It's almost impossible to imagine America breeding an indigenous Mike Leigh to chronicle acute moments of social embarrassment and class awkwardness. Which is not to say these things don't exist. Cultural distance means that we're less adept at analysing those signals in a roomful of Americans, but they're still legible if you know how. Class masquerades as wealth at the top of the scale and is often displaced into race at the bottom end, but in the broad middle there are a zillion finely calibrated distinctions. Look at Woody Allen's Small Time Crooks, in which Brooklyn manicurist Frenchy Winkler (Tracey Ullman) suddenly finds she and her husband Ray (Allen) are rich beyond their wildest dreams and able to hobnob with a better class of person. Frenchy lusts after "the finer things" and hooks up with Hugh Grant's crooked art dealer, who has the very thing that guarantees class and style to many Americans: he's English. Thus he can aid her in her quest for "quality" and good taste, that indefinable je ne sais quoi allegedly imparted by close association with European culture and cuisine, and with British nobs (he finds her "unspeakably vulgar" , of course). So it is that ex-stripper Frenchy (formerly known as "Frenchy, The Topless Wonder" ) and ex-con and hapless thief Ray find themselves ineptly serving snails to the kind of people who, unlike them, actually know how to eat them. "Snails leave a scum trail when they walk," whines Ray. "Not in France," snaps Frenchy. The chance to express themselves through expenditure and acquisition is what brings the couple out in all their tacky glory. Money may well be the root of all evil, it may well buy you happiness, but if you have rotten cheap tastes then the one thing you can guarantee is money will find you out. If money can do one thing, many Americans theorise, it can go some way towards erasing the stain of one's origins. The fear that others might discover you have one foot in the trailer-park is a great motivator. Just as the British know precisely what separates South Kensington from, say, Droitwich, Americans are highly alert to the differences between Beverly Hills and Catfish Junction, Alabama - and will move heaven and earth to erase any stigma based on origin. Just look at the Kennedys, America's "Royal Family" . There wouldn't have been a President Kennedy or any senators had not their father Joseph Kennedy spent his entire life attempting to break away from his Boston Irish roots and ingratiate himself with the landed American aristocrats of Boston and New York. "Tell me," Rose Kennedy is said to have asked an aristocratic schoolfriend of JFK's in 1939, when her family was already rich but beset by the wildest forms of social insecurity, "when are the good people of Boston going to accept us Irish?" In the end they had to accept them, if only because of the sheer force of Kennedy Sr's social ambitions. But it will take generations before the aristocracy Joe wanted his sons to join will let the Kennedys forget that their real roots are in the Irish ghetto and their past is deeply tainted by Joe Kennedy's extensive activities as a bootlegger during Prohibition. Similarly the moguls of young Hollywood. They feared anti-Semitism above all else and skirted it by utterly suppressing their Jewish origins both in their public lives and in the movies they oversaw down to the last detail. Having been kept out of "respectable" business activities by anti-Semitism on the part of the kind of people Joe Kennedy aspired to be like, they got their sweet revenge when the "disreputable" nickelodeons they'd bought up suddenly began to make them the richest men alive. As movies came of age in the 1930s, the moguls constructed a notion of "class" or classiness through the decor, the repartee, the costumes and the social attitude of their productions that came to be seen as uniquely expressive of "The American Dream" . Thus all of America came to dream in the terms laid out by a group of socially awkward insecure Jewish immigrants, which must have been supremely gratifying to them, even as they were kept out of gentile neighbourhoods and country clubs till well into the 60s. And at the top of the heap, of course, is the class that kept those country clubs "restricted", the class that sneered at the Kennedys' nouveau-riche aspirations, and which still largely owns and runs the United States. America's Wasp aristocracy (of which George W Bush is a Texicanised scion) is based around the Groton and Harvard-educated owners of the great banking and investment houses on Wall Street and the ancient law firms that do their bidding. This is the class Edith Wharton wrote about in The Age Of Innocence and The House Of Mirth. They're the people painted by John Singer Sargent: the Roosevelts, the Vanderbilts, the Rockefellers, the Aldriches and so on. They'd like us to believe that they're all descended from the pilgrims who arrived on the Mayflower in 1620, though any fool can tell you that if everyone who claimed to be descended from the Pilgrim Fathers actually was, then the Mayflower must have been a jumbo-supertanker. In fact it was the size of a double-decker bus. Their tailoring is echt-British aristocracy (Savile Row, LL Bean and Brooks Brothers), as were many of their accents until the turn of the century (Scorcese cast English actors as Yankee aristos in The Age Of Innocence) and their ghastly social isolationism. There's a distinct element of the cultural cringe in the way this class associates Englishness with high style and good taste, while apparently remaining blithely unaware that England is no longer a hellhole of Merchant-Ivory literary adaptations, eccentric toffs, smiling butlers and singing chimney sweeps. Above all there is a determination to reassociate themselves with the very Brits the pilgrims sought to get away from. All of which leads one to speculate that when it comes to moronic social minutiae and divisive class differences, we British really are the top dogs. • Small Time Crooks is on general release

title: Right up the creek
bodyText: Last summer was one long learning curve. I thought I knew about travelling in developing countries. I knew that journeys can be tough, but sagely told people that there is always something, whether it is the journey or what lies at the end of it, that redeems it. I had never been to sub-Saharan Africa. My fiancé and I met each other in China, and we imagined that West Africa could be no more stressful than travelling in Asia. We took six weeks off, and set off for an adventure. We returned wiser, thinner, and much less smug. I was highly excited about a four-day journey in Mali, down the Niger in a steamboat to Timbuktu. Katharine Hepburn and Humphrey Bogart in The African Queen sprang to mind. I would lounge languidly on the deck, while Timbuktu shimmered on the horizon. I had vaguely heard that, when you get there, it can disappoint, but put that conveniently to the back of my mind. Tuesday This battered boat would tower over the African Queen. It was built in 1964, but it seems to float. It awaits us on the Niger, at Koulikoro, the port town nearest to Mali's capital, Bamako. Excitement is in the air. This is the first boat of the rainy season: the service only operates when there is enough water in the river - usually between August and January - to make it navigable. The roads round here are often impassable, and the steamboat moves faster than the wooden fishing boats we see throughout the journey. We spend the afternoon negotiating the bureaucracy involved in buying tickets. There are forms to be completed, pieces of paper to carry from one functionary to another, cash to be handed over. We were considering travelling third or fourth class - huddling on the decks with the vast majority of the African travellers - but we lose our nerve and book a first-class cabin. The heat is intense, and we like the idea of privacy, air conditioning, and access to loos and showers that are shared with the few others in first class. Meals are included. As I open the cabin door, and the smell hits me, I know we've made a mistake. There is no romance here. We edge inside. The linoleum beneath our feet is sticky. It has buckled, presumably under weather induced duress, and it rarely touches the floor. In the eight months the ferry has been out of commission, cleaning has not been a priority. James sets off to find someone to take away the dismantled loo that fills the floor. 'The sheets look clean,' I tell myself. The air conditioning is not working, because the engine is off. Outside, the people of Koulikoro have set up stalls selling anything one might need for the journey. The chatter outside goes on past midnight. Departure is scheduled for 6am. Wednesday It's too hot to sleep. Our window doesn't open, because of the air conditioning. I bless the woven fan we bought for 10p in a village last week; but whenever I stop fanning, I am wide awake. Sweat pours off me and there is no air. At 6am I venture onto the deck and breathe deeply. We are gliding down the river in a stately manner. The sky and the water are both silver. There is a slight breeze, and the banks are deeply green. I watch Africa drift by. From time to time we pass clusters of huts which form remote settlements. The rest of the time there is nothing. I could be on the African Queen. I think it's going to be all right. The air conditioning hasn't come on. After breakfast - bread and jam, with a guffaw from the laconic waiter when James enquires about butter - we politely ask the captain why our expensive cabin resembles a stinking furnace. He cheerfully explains that the aircon in first class hasn't worked for years. 'Oui, c'est domage,' he agrees. I dangle my legs through the railings and exchange idle pleasantries with the African family on one side and Christophe and Sandrine on the other. The passing landscape is flat and green. Mali is one of the world's poorest countries, suffering both from desertification and from the after-effects of the Sahel drought of the Sixties and Seventies. It swiftly becomes clear that most of the 400 or so passengers prefer to use the first-class loos, partly, perhaps, because they are European style. Whatever the reason, they have been blocked since before we left the port, and we are usually downwind of them. Meanwhile, the showers, which dispense river water, only occasionally work, and appear to double up as extra toilets. On the rare occasions I get to shower, I keep my sandals on. The people on the upper deck fling the contents of their children's potties overboard with merry abandon. Their deck is set back from ours, so everything they throw lands directly on top of us. This factor, combined with the stench, drives us back into the cabin regularly, until the heat drives us out again. I lie still and wonder how we're going to get through the days. Sometimes I manage to sleep, if I lie on a wet sarong. I fan myself constantly, and read a detective story, set in Florida, in French. Lunch and dinner consist of lumps of meat in some kind of sauce, usually served with stony rice. There is always murky soup for starters. 'Qu'est-ce que c'est?' James asks the waiter, every time. 'C'est de la soupe,' he invariably replies. He varies this on the last day, when he states: 'C'est toujours de la soupe.' Still soup. We don't eat the meat. Sandrine becomes violently ill when she does. I chip my teeth on the gravel in the rice. No one's hungry, anyway. It's too hot. I find myself lying on the bunk, laughing at the absurdity of the situation, and simultaneously crying at its misery. Friday We wake up - or would have, had we slept - in Mopti, a trading town on the Niger. Delighted to escape, we charge down the gangplank. Groups of children trail us, shouting ' Toubab, ça va? '. Toubab is best translated as 'whitey', and we are assured that it's an affectionate term. ' Donne moi un cadeau, ' becomes a familiar refrain. We are glad to take some mild exercise, and to see the mud buildings of Mopti. 'Shall we just leave the boat?' James suggests. 'Not go to Timbuktu?' It is powerfully tempting. We can't stand the idea of another night aboard, let alone the probable two (in the event, it's three). But can we really set off for Timbuktu, then give up halfway? Bogart and Hepburn would have had more mettle. The nominated departure time, 2pm, passes. We are on board. Several familiar people have joined us: an Israeli and Polish contingent we met on a train last week, and Dan, a British student whom James met in China. There's a lot of cargo on the bank which hasn't been loaded. There is no sign that anyone's thinking about loading it. We remain as indulgent as we can, desperately trying not to force our first-world expectations onto a doddery ferry service in Saharan Africa. The captain plucks a time out of the air to make us go away. We will leave at 6pm. Four hours after that, the lights of Mopti recede. We are pressing on, even though there is no guarantee of ever leaving Timbuktu (the roads are notoriously bad, and the planes often overbooked). I have to be in Bamako in 10 days to correct the proofs of my novel, which are arriving by Fedex. I try to suppress doubts about the extent of the publishers' sympathy if I tell them I'm stuck in Timbuktu. It's probably not on Fedex's route. I can feel the weight slipping off me, but would not recommend a diet of stony rice, stale bread and anti-malaria pills. James's cheeks hollow out. We fill the time by making guest lists for our wedding next year, and discussing the improbability of the existence of a world outside this boat. Sunday We should have arrived yesterday, and knowledgeable passengers predict we'll reach Kabara, the port for Timbuktu, tonight. A huge storm sweeps in from the desert and provides some glorious relief. I sleep for most of the night, as the rain cleans the decks and brings cool air into our horrible cabin. The passengers from the top deck shelter down below, where every inch of space is already crammed with people and cargo. We get stuck on a sandbank, and, sometime in the night, the boat is blown across to the opposite bank, where it becomes firmly entrenched. It tips sideways, and we plot our escape route for when it rolls over. This involves an alarming amount of underwater swimming. The bar has run out of all liquid apart from beer. The men get off and start a brave but futile pushing exercise. James joins in, and Dan follows, giving me his camera to record the occasion. The other westerners prefer to video events, impervious to the anger they are inciting. I descend, with most of the other women. A rope is attached to the bows, and while the women pull the rope, the men, waist deep in the river, push the boat. Hours later, we have no result, not least because the vessel has been firmly attached to the riverbed by a sturdy anchor throughout. The project is abandoned. James and I wander into the dunes, where clean animal bones poke out of the sand. We are at the limits of our endurance, and wonder whether we have actually died. A lethargic discussion ensues about whether this is Purgatory or Hell. For entertainment, I take a photograph of James's bare bottom with Dan's camera. We laugh about it, stupidly, for days. Monday Sometime in the afternoon, the rescue vessel rounds a bend in the river. When it pulls up alongside us, there is much climbing between the boats, and sharing of tea by old friends. We wait, giggling with cabin fever, until, hours later, it is deemed appropriate for the rescue to begin. Thick metal cables tie the boats together, and the new one accelerates away. One cable snaps and would have decap itated anyone in its path. The ferry inches away from the sandbank. The rescue boat suddenly charges back, to give us that final push. When it hurtles in our direction, we run with our neighbours into their cabin, which is the nearest. We are halfway in when the crash happens, but no one suffers serious injuries. A little girl bursts into tears. The boats chug to Kabara, and, shakily, we disembark. We team up with Annie and Jacques, a French couple who set an example of tolerance and good humour that we are unable to follow. A young man who calls himself Ali Baba George Washington ('not my given name,' he later confides) takes it upon himself to look after us. Although his prices are more reasonable than those of his rivals, who suggest that £40 is an acceptable rate for the 16km car journey into town, Ali later tries to charge us £180 for an outing to see some hippos, and accidentally admits that he makes more than £175 every week. This is a fortune in Mali where the GDP per head is £550. Although Timbuktu turns out to be a miserable place, where the roads are ankle deep in sand as the Sahara encroaches further every year, it still represents freedom. We visit the old mud mosques, which don't have much going for them except their age. Tourism is a significant industry here, but the town's sole attraction lies in the mythology associated with its name. All traces of Timbuktu's golden age vanished hundreds of years ago; what remains is a quiet, poor town in the middle of nowhere. To our delight we are able to book ourselves on a plane back to Bamako the following day. Our cursory sightseeing leaves us overwhelmed by heat and hassle. Along with Annie and Jacques, we are the first at the airport. We climb aboard a propeller plane whose signs, ominously, are all in Russian. It is piloted by three Eastern European men who nattily team their gold-trimmed pilots' shirts with jeans. We don't like to contemplate what sort of pilots end up assigned to the Timbuktu run on Air Mali. We are, however, ecstatic to be leaving. After that expedition, I feel more entitled to call myself an experienced traveller, because I now know that travelling can be hell. Belatedly, I remember that Bogart and Hepburn's journey wasn't comfortable, either. West African essentials The cheapest way to get to West Africa is by charter flight to The Gambia. Contact the Gambia Experience (02380 730888) for prices from £286. Malian visas are available in Paris. Alternatively, land in Senegal and get a visa in Dakar for a modest £7. Air France, Lufthansa and Sabena fly to Dakar. British citizens do not need a visa to visit Senegal. A grounding in French is essential to get around. West Africa is Francophone: only The Gambia speaks English. Dakar is a nightmare for the uninitiated. People come here from all over the region, whether they are Sierra Leonian refugees or just people from the countryside in need of work. The city teems with confidence tricksters. It's essential to take anti-malarial medication. Chloroquine-resistant malaria is reported all over West Africa. Yellow fever certificates are nominally an entry requirement for most countries (ours were never examined). Typhoid, polio and tetanus shots should be up to date, and a hepatitis A jab is a good idea. Rough Guide and Lonely Planet both publish West Africa guidebooks. We found the Bradt guide to Mali (£13.95) to be better and more detailed than either. • Emily Barr's column 'Woman of the World' starts next week in Rio de Janeiro.

title: New year slowdown starts early
bodyText: The year 2000 was a vintage one for the global economy which grew more than 4%, the fastest pace of expansion for more than a decade. Financial markets ought to have celebrated. Yet all around the world they were in despair. By mid-December only eight out of 40 stock markets covered by the Economist's weekly economic indicators were above their level of a year ago. The reason? Stock markets were looking forward to 2001, not backward. They clearly believe this year will be a stinker. The immediate cause of the anxiety is, of course, the uncertainly surrounding the fate of the US economy, the world's largest and the main engine of global growth in the aftermath of the Asian crisis. After a record breaking boom lasting a decade, growth is now slowing rapidly towards a more sustainable rate amid a rash of profit warnings. Instead of last year's 5%, it will probably be no more than 3% in 2001. While a slowdown of that order could ultimately prolong the length of the upswing by easing some of the stresses and strains which have developed, notably the yawning current account deficit and the negative savings ratio, the nagging question for financial markets now is: will it become a recession? If America catches a cold, the rest of the globe runs the risk of getting double pneumonia. Britain, in particular, would suffer because of the web of trade and financial market connections between the two countries. There is certainly a significant risk of a hard landing in the US, though not for the usual reason. Booms normally end in bust because booms generate excess demand and inflation which then has to be squeezed out of the system by higher official interest rates. Although both inflation and interest rates have risen in the past year, they have done so only modestly, certainly not enough to send the global economy into a tailspin. No, the real cause of the recession - if we get one - will be a combination of over-investment in IT and tough competition which is preventing rising costs being passed on in price rises, with the resulting contraction of profit margins. "It is then stock market crashes, loan losses, bad loans and bankruptcies which do the damage, causing contemporaneously monetary contraction rather than being its lagged result," says Brian Reading of Lombard Street Research in an end of year note published last week. Optimism reigns For what it is worth, most forecasters are confident that a hard landing in the US can be avoided. They have faith in Alan Greenspan, chairman of the US Federal Reserve, cutting interest rates at just the right time and in just the right quantity to keep the ball rolling. He did it in 1998 when the global economy stood on the edge of a precipice and he will do it again in 2001, according to US investment banks Merrill Lynch and Goldman Sachs, which are both backing the soft landing scenario. Credit Suisse First Boston also takes comfort from the fact that slowing US growth will in itself help to support global growth because it is already bringing down oil prices and the dollar, which together with lower interest rates, will nip a nascent credit crunch in the bud. "The tightening of credit conditions is starting to act as a significant drag on growth in the US, but has not yet become systemic and has hardly spread to Europe," says the firm. "The prospect of easier monetary conditions, and the positive effect on non-oil companies' profits from lower oil prices, will not reverse the tighter conditions overnight but will greatly reduce the risk of their becoming systemic, and limit the likely spill over to Europe." Perhaps a more worrying threat to the outlook for the global economy is the rapidly deteriorating situation in Japan, the world's second largest economy, still struggling to get to grips with the bursting of its asset price bubble 10 years ago. Although it managed anaemic growth in 2000, end of year figures showed plunging industrial output, retail sales falling for the 44th straight month and unemployment at an eight-month high, suggesting that a descent back into full-blown recession cannot be ruled out. The Japanese prime minister, Yoshiro Mori, went on a walkabout in a shopping district in Tokyo last Wednesday, to highlight the continued weakness of personal consumption, which accounts for 60% of gross domestic product. He bought a pair of shoes for his forthcoming trip to Africa and a bag of carrots, but it will take much greater largess by consumers to establish a self-sustaining recovery. The global economy has done very nicely without much of a contribution from Japan over the past decade but that is only because the US has compensated by growing so strongly. If Japan goes into reverse at the same time as the US economy is slowing, everyone will feel the pinch. Of the three big industrialised economies, euroland seems to have the best prospects over the next year or so. Cyclically less advanced than the US, it has plenty of slack in the labour market and is slowly but steadily undertaking structural reforms which will make it more globally competitive. Not least of these reforms will be a major round of tax cuts in Germany, France and Italy which this year are expected to boost euroland GDP by 0.5% percentage point. While euroland will undoubtedly suffer from the slowdown in the US, it has none of the imbalances that plague the US economy and should for once outperform its transatlantic rival - assuming the European Central Bank is prepared to cut interest rates if necessary. Rising stars However, the star performer of 2001 could well be the economies of the Middle East and north Africa. This year's slowdown in growth in the advanced economies owes much to last year's surge in the price of oil, which forced up producers' costs and reduced the purchasing power of consumers. But what caused protests on the streets of Europe lined the pockets of the governments of the big oil producing countries, which are now starting to spend again. The Economist Intelligence Unit says this will transform the economic outlook because such spending feeds through to the rest of the non-oil economy and helps to boost corporate profits. As a result, the EIU expects economic growth in the Middle East and north Africa to accelerate to 4.7% in 2001, making it the fastest growing region in the world. The fastest growing country is expected to be New Guinea, a one-off ranking due to rapid expansion of its oil sector. Another region which could surprise on the upside is Latin America, where, having finally shaken off the impact of the Asian financial crisis, Brazil is forecast to strengthen, acting as a locomotive for surrounding countries, although its pulling power will be offset by Mexico, which is likely to slow in line with its next door neighbour, the US. Overall 2001, looks like being a reasonably healthy one for the global economy. Although fears of recession in America could yet become a self-fulfilling prophecy, the odds are still on continued growth. Barring a hard landing in the US, global GDP is likely to expand by more than 3%. After growth of more than 4% last year, it will not feel very fast but it is likely to be more evenly distributed. Whether it will translate into a recovery of equity prices remains unclear. Assuming unchanged profit shares in GDP, steady growth normally means a bull not a bear market. But fierce global competition may well continue to keep the lid on price rises and keep profit margins tight, hence depressing share prices. Producers will not like it but consumers will be happy - as long as they keep their jobs.

title: Monday briefing
bodyText: Sunday Telegraph J Sainsbury is poised to become the latest entrant into the mobile phone market by launching its own service, Sainsbury's One. Bills for the service, which will operate over BT Cellnet 's network, will provide an analysis of how much calls would cost on all four UK networks. Sainsbury's One would then charge the lowest rate. The service will target the 12m holders of the supermarket group's Reward loyalty card. Extreme Group, which produces and distributes TV sports programmes, is to float in the first half of the year valued at around £50m. The company is chaired by Chris Akers, founder of Sports Internet, and is likely to use the proceeds to expand in America. Carcraft, Britain's largest secondhand car dealership, has been put up for sale by its private equity owners with a £200m price tag. Israeli technology firm Brightcom, which designs chips and software for wireless technology Bluetooth, is planning a $400m float in London. Sunday Times Lloyds TSB is preparing to lodge an application with the competition commission to take over Abbey National. It is likely to file its case early in the new year. If it does not succeed in a last ditch attempt to persuade Abbey to agree to a deal, it is prepared to launch a hostile offer. Britain's economy is set for its ninth consecutive year of growing faster than 2%, despite fears of an American-led slowdown. Forecasters believe growth will be lower than the 3% estimated for 2000, but that expansion will continue. The director of a failed timeshare company called Sunweeks International is being investigated by the official receiver amid claims the business made false statements to entice customers to part with up to £5,000 each. The auction to buy Go, British Airways' low cost subsidiary, has gone flat, and the company fears it may now only get £100m for the airline. Executives had believed the operation, run by Barbara Cassani, could fetch up to £300m, but enthusiasm among potential buyers has waned. Sunday Business Nearly 2,000 staff at high street chain C&A will lose their jobs when the company pulls out of Britain in January. This is far more than originally expected. Leisure group Chorion plans to expand its Tiger Tiger nightclub chain across Europe. Union chief Sir Ken Jackson launched a scathing attack on the way the European Commission has dealt with the aid package being considered for the Nissan car manufacturing plant in Sunderland. The City fund management divisions of Deutsche Bank and Legal & General each gained £10bn of new institutional business during 2000, with Phillips & Drew and Schroders the biggest losers. ING Barings is likely to close its New York operations as well as Barings in London, after no buyers were found. Observer Despite hopes that manufacturing would recover in 2000, the year saw the shedding of 100,000 jobs, output falling across most sectors and captains of industry reeling from one disaster to the next. Mail on Sunday Department store partnership John Lewis has conceded that it will have to stay open more than five days a week if it is to meet fierce high street competition. Coal production looks on the way up again after years of decline, with RJB Mining planning to open a deep mine at Thorne near Doncaster, South Yorkshire. Sunday Express Tour operators, department stores and car companies are in line to benefit from a massive consumer spending spree in 2001 as £21bn worth of Tessas mature. IoS Employers who monitor their staff's emails and record their phone calls - practices common in the financial services industry - could be breaking the law, according to a group of senior lawyers. Reports out this week Today Interims, Finals, AGMs, EGMs None scheduled. Tuesday Interims None scheduled. Finals None scheduled. AGMs None scheduled. EGMs London Town, Sports Resource. Wednesday Interims None scheduled. Finals None scheduled. AGMs None scheduled. EGMs Fleming Claverhouse, Isoft. Thursday Interims Abbey. Finals None scheduled. AGMs None scheduled. EGMs None scheduled. Friday Interims None scheduled. Finals None scheduled. AGMs None scheduled. EGMs Densitron Electronics. Indicators this week Today UK Bank Holiday. No data scheduled. Tuesday UK CIPS manufacturing sector survey (December). UK Provisional M0 estimate (December). France PMI index. Italy Government budget balance (December). Wednesday UK CIPS construction sector survey (December). UK CBI property trends survey. Euro-zone Unemployment (November). Spain One-year and 18-months T-bill auctions. Thursday UK M4 lending (November). UK M4 final (November). UK Official reserves (December). UK CIPS service sector survey (December). UK CBI survey of distributive trends (December). Euro-zone M3 money supply. Friday UK Halifax house price survey (December).

title: Hopes high as Sweden takes helm of EU
bodyText: Sweden takes over the EU presidency today, promising to bring a touch of Nordic pragmatism and rigour to some of the toughest challenges facing the union. Goodwill and expectations are unusually high as Goran Persson, the prime minister, settles into the rotating hot seat after disappointment at France's rocky stewardship, which ended last night to near universal relief. Sweden joined the club in 1995, and this is the first time it has run the rotating presidency, always a demanding job for any small country but still a golden six-month opportunity to make an impression on union business. France will be remembered for the Treaty of Nice, an ill-tempered and unambitious affair that just about agreed the long-overdue institutional reforms needed to allow the EU to take in up to 13 new members in the coming years. Enlargement is Mr Persson's biggest priority, not only because success is central to the future of the entire EU, but also because leading candidates such as Poland, Estonia and Latvia are situated in Sweden's own Baltic backyard. And closely linked to this issue is the thorny question of relations with Russia and Ukraine, which will become the union's immediate neighbours when the first newcomers join. "I find it hard to think that anything is more important for the enlargement process than that we have a good relationship between the EU and Russia," Mr Persson said recently. The Swedish social democrat wants to quicken the glacial pace of negotiations and may even set a date for the first accessions, which France repeatedly refused to do. But with concern growing in Germany and elsewhere about the cost of the project, and some of the toughest issues such as agriculture and aid funds still looming, it remains to be seen how much progress can actually be made. Mr Persson has big ideas on the environment and employment. In Brussels, the Swedish environment commissioner, Margot Wallstrom, has established solid "green" credentials and the Gothenburg summit in June will be devoted to sustainable development. Sweden also wants to use its presidency to help salvage a deal on climate change, after the collapse of the Hague conference over huge differences between the EU and US. In March, at the Stockholm summit, leaders will review progress made since ambitious targets for social and employment policy were set in Lisbon last year. Not surprisingly, the country that is the home to the telecoms giant Ericsson will be pushing for better opportunities to exploit new technologies and bring Europe up to enviable US levels of growth and job generation. Transparency is a key concern - Sweden's instinctive openness has often brought clashes with more secretive member states. Its presidency website is already a model of clarity and accessibility. Exuding probity and Scandinavian values, Mr Persson has pledged to work for a union "characterised by cost efficiency and budget restrictiveness" as well as "mainstreaming" gender equality - the theme of a ministerial strategy session later this month. Other challenges include the difficult defence dossier, currently stuck on the crucial question of relations between the EU's nascent rapid reaction force and Nato, and specific difficulties with France and Turkey. As Sweden is a long-standing neutral which sat out the cold war, this is an especially intriguing and delicate mission. Further afield, the crisis in the Middle East will be a test for Stockholm's ability to represent the union on the world stage, and an important time for the foreign policy supremo, Javier Solana, repeatedly upstaged by France. North Korea, emerging from its long years as the Stalinist hermit kingdom, will be a preoccupation. More widely, President George W Bush will be less of an enigma by the time Mr Persson greets him at the EU-US summit in June. "Assuming the presidency for the first time is a proud moment for relative newcomers to the EU scene," commented the Brussels European Voice, widely read by Eurocrats. "It will be up to Sweden to restore faith in the current system and show that it can work in the way that it should." Mr Persson has got that message loud and clear. "The EU is not only talk," he declared. "But it is also hard facts, delivery, a practical approach... about politics that make a difference and change the daily lives of citizens." • The former German chancellor Helmut Schmidt said in an interview published yesterday that the EU had achieved very little over the past decade, and criticised its expansion plans. "With the sole exception of the euro, it has achieved nothing in the last 10 years," Mr Schmidt, 82, told the Tagesspiegel daily. As Social Democrat chancellor from 1974 to 1982, Mr Schmidt, in partnership with French president Valéry Giscard D'Estaing, helped forge the Exchange Rate Mechanism - the forerunner to European monetary union. But in the outspoken interview, Mr Schmidt criticised the EU's latest steps aimed at accepting new members. "The more members join, the harder it will be to change... Expectations are developing too quickly, internal rules too slowly," he warned. Snow and scepticism • Euroscepticism in Sweden is as familiar as crispbread, with a survey last month showing that only 37%of Swedes support membership, let alone deeper integration. Polls show far greater support for enlargement. • Public feeling against joining the single currency has hardened since Denmark rejected the euro in September. A recent poll showed that 62% of Swedes do not want to scrap the krone. • Analysts say one reason for the anti-euro sentiment is confidence that Sweden has emerged leaner and fitter from a recession and does not want the shelter or the straitjacket of the single currency. • The Swedish government, which backs euro membership "in principle", has pledged to hold a referendum on the euro, though it has not fixed a date. • Swedes are sensitive to the EU's reputation for secrecy, and paid close attention to a fraud scandal at the European commission's Stockholm office last year.

title: Leader: Brown's budget needs a European context
bodyText: This year the fate of the British economy could - for the first time under Labour's stewardship - be scuppered by events outside its direct control. Gordon Brown will return from his new year festivities to prepare his pre-electoral budget knowing there is a real prospect that the current downturn in the US could turn into a recession. As Sir Edward George, governor of the Bank of England, warned last week, the US economy is slowing down unexpectedly quickly. He reminded us that "it is still true that if America really sneezes we all catch cold". There are no signs of an economic crash yet, despite the collapse of hi-tech stocks last year, when 60 of the largest internet companies suffered a 90% drop in their share prices since the peak in March. Even Yahoo!, arguably the most solidly based of the web's superstar companies, saw its shares fall by 86% last year on the stock markets. So far all this has not triggered the disaster scenario in which collapsing share prices force US consumers to stop spending and rebuild their battered savings. That is mainly because the Old Economy stocks have not joined the Gadarene rush to the edge. The Dow-Jones industrial index only dropped by 6% last year. But it still looks vulnerable after the 46% rise of the previous two years. US consumers, their confidence buoyed up by skyrocketing share prices, have drawn down their savings to next to nothing to finance a ravenous spending habit. If they now stop spending that could trigger a collapse of imports and an end to an era in which the ballooning US trade deficit has financed expansion in the rest of the world including the UK. A sinking US economy would reverse the strong capital flows from the rest of the world, including Europe, to the US and risk a vicious spiral of economic decline. The rest of the world is a mixed bag. Japan, the second biggest economy in the world, is still locked in a recession that has lasted a decade. Whereas Americans will not save more, the Japanese will not spend more - and since 60% of economic activity is consumption, that spells trouble. Exhortations by the prime minister to spend more have fallen on deaf wallets. It is a timely reminder that behind confident sounding economic equations lie the free choices made by people. If they decide not to spend, as in Japan, then that is that. And if people in the US decide en masse to rein in their spending too sharply, then there is a not a lot that even the mighty US Federal Reserve can do about it. Elsewhere, Russia's economy is expanding at a very encouraging 7% - but it has to be set against a 50% contraction since the fall of communism; China is growing by 8%, having largely shrugged off the Asian downturn, while Singapore, Hong Kong and South Korea are back in the 9% to 10% a year growth league. But none of these countries are big enough to take up the slack if the US and Japan (which together account for over 50% of OECD output) are both in serious recession. At present the US is merely adjusting from unsustainable growth of over 5% a year to a sustainable pace of around 3%. If it can do that and notch up a 10th year of continuous growth without recession, then maybe the US economy should be awarded the Nobel prize for economics. And if it does not, then there will be no shortage of economists rewriting history and claiming that the warning signs were there for everyone to see. In these circumstances there is one thing above all the chancellor should do. Think European. Europe is the third biggest economy in the world and the only one with the potential to take up the slack of a flagging US recovery. Unlike the US, it has a strong trade balance and as a trading zone it is largely self sufficient - well over 90% of its trade is done with other European countries - so the scope for spontaneous combustion is big. It is surprising that Europe has recently had more success in areas of industrial policy (like challenging Boeing with its planned super-jumbo jet) than in coordinating economic policies. There are signs of change. Germany and France are now planning tax cuts. This should be encouraged, as long as Europe's central bank does not spoil the revival by raising interest rates unnecessarily. Britain does not need tax cuts, though we will almost certainly get some in Gordon Brown's budget, if only to defuse the Conservatives' irresponsible populist clamour for even steeper tax cuts. Britain's problem is different from Europe's one of struggling to sustain a moody recovery. It is to prevent an unnecessarily steep downturn in the wake of the US slowdown. There is no need to feed our near-record trade deficit with yet more spending on imports. What people desperately want, and are daily reminded of as they struggle into work, is decent infrastructure. The government, after an unnecessarily severe freeze during its first two years, has stepped up spending considerably. But it is not enough to deal with the backlog. Any money left over from Mr Brown's unspent budget surplus, beyond a ritual cut in taxes, should go to improve our infrastructure in the broadest sense, including railways, roads, hospitals, schools and the arts. Maybe some of the small change should be used to send civil servants to night school to learn how to spend public money efficiently, since years of squeeze have dimmed their memories about how it is done. The Treasury also needs to be reminded of the vital importance of a proper manufacturing sector, at a time when industries like steel, motors and coal (at least until higher energy prices changed the outlook) are reeling from the effects of an overvalued pound. As dot.com companies bite the dust almost daily, the government should need no reminding that the bulk of our exports still come from a manufacturing base that has been wantonly eroded by a Labour administration that ought to have known better. If ministers are looking for ways to cushion the dangers of a downturn, then the rediscovery of the importance of manufacturing would be a sign that they really mean business.

title: Leader: Managing German aims in the EU
bodyText: Sweden takes over the rotating presidency of the European Union this week to undisguised relief all round. The judgment on Paris is uniformly negative. The French presidency is blamed for its conduct of the Nice summit last month, inconsistency over beef, the way Jacques Chirac played silly buggers on Nato and the rapid reaction force and above all French failure to see just how old-fashioned Gaullism now looks, with its pretence that the interests of the club and those of France are the same. Paris's loss looks increasingly like Berlin's gain. Chancellor Gerhard Schröder emerged from Nice with enhanced authority. He won a voting scheme that gives the Germans more clout if they need a veto, but also the right to insist - which he undoubtedly will - that the accession of Poland, Slovenia and the other candidates goes ahead and on largely German terms. Despite Robin Cook's modernising impulse, our Foreign Office still does nothing as human as sigh with relief. It is a moot point, too, whether the Blair government should join the French bashing. In their single-minded pursuit of national interest within the EU and their willingness to take unilateral action when they see fit, the French are doing approximately what the latest Blairite script suggests the UK should do too. The doctrine as set out in the recent book by French foreign minister Hubert Védrine, especially its "Europe of nation states", shares ground with the prime minister's recent speech in Warsaw. The trouble is that the French also have a certain idea of pan-European action - the charter of rights, common policies on unemployment - which London hates. But London and Paris will, one way or another, have to come together if the momentum behind the "German version" grows. Of course there is no single German view of Europe's future development. Foreign minister Joschka Fischer is something of a maverick. His colleagues in the Schröder government may share his faith in federation as Europe's future, but they are going to be a lot more cautious in advancing it as the subject for the 2004 intergovernmental conference. Even so, it is a distinctly different vision from Mr Blair's, let alone the British public's, and managing German aspirations is going to constitute one of the great challenges of EU policy during the next few years, longer if the Schröder government wins power again. There is certainly not much point in looking to Paris for an alternative, at least until the legislative and presidential elections are safely out of the way next year.

title: Martin Woollacott on the nonsense of European decision-making
bodyText: If the Archduke Francis Ferdinand had not married beneath himself, it has been speculated, Europe might well have avoided its 20th-century wars. The archduke would still have been assassinated in Sarajevo in June 1914, but instead of the meagre 15-minute funeral ceremony which Emperor Francis Joseph then ordained, there would instead have been a full gathering of the crowned heads of Europe. They, while consoling the old man, would have backed Austria's demands on Serbia, or in some other way arrived at a solution to the problem.
