Hence, perhaps, no first world war and no second world war, but instead a long period of order and democratic evolution. Coming to our times, Prince Charles might have contemplated marriage with a Hohenzollern princess. If such a different century had happened, given the energy and capacity for growth that Germany had shown before 1914, it would have been a "German century" marked not by conflict and destruction, but by economic success and by a hegemony peacefully achieved. The Germany of today cannot be blamed for occasionally musing that what did not happen in the 20th century might still in some way be possible in the 21st. The other European states, however, spent the same years protecting themselves against German power, and the habits of the past are not easily set aside. Such shades do still loiter behind the arras when France and Germany debate today's issues of reform and change in the European union, or when Britain considers joining the common currency. Of course, Germany's recent pressure for a constitutional definition of powers in the union is not a bid for dominance in Europe. Yet the combination of Germany's economic prowess and Germany's reforming instincts in Europe do give other countries cause for concern. The reason is that Germany, alone among European states, has nothing to lose in advocating a more federal union. Germany has the largest population in Europe, and generates a third of the eurozone's gross product. Its corporations are embarking on a new phase of expansion in this continent and in North America characterised both by much friction and by large ambitions. It is the dominant outside economic power in eastern Europe, where local firms fall to its investors every month. France is economically vigorous as well, and Britain and other countries are not wholly out of the race, but Germany's greater weight is clear. Germany is reorganising its armed forces and will, in the not too distant future, be able to match French and British military capacities. The simple point in this is that the most powerful member of a federation is not often outvoted or outclassed. Its derogation of powers to the supranational level is more nominal than real, since it will frequently get its way at the centre without great difficulty. Indeed, its power may well be enhanced by federation. The German demand for extra votes for Germany at the Nice summit, a demand that was only headed off by other concessions that also give Germany a numerical advantage in European decision-making, dramatised these issues. At the same time as Germany argued for constitutional changes, it tried to ensure that, when those changes came, it would be in a strong position to win most of the arguments. If one was to be completely cynical about the German line on the definition of powers, it might be said that the aim is to reserve to Germany the powers it deems essential to keep under absolutely full control while arranging to dominate the use of the powers that Germany and all the rest of us would pass to European institutions. The truth, no doubt, is more middling: European idealism and the German left's tradition of anchoring German power within a containing European framework play as much a part as any calculation of self-interest. Yet Joschka Fischer and Chancellor Schröder have not been shrewd or considerate in their timing. Schröder's sudden enthusiasm for his foreign minister's views, from which he earlier preserved a certain distance, suggests a lurching quality to Germany's policy-making. France is already deep into the campaign for this year's local and next year's general elections, a campaign in which Lionel Jospin and Jacques Chirac will both try to steer a delicate course on European matters. Britain also faces an election in which Europe will be an issue, to be followed (assuming a Labour victory) by a referendum on the euro. Other members of the union have their special difficulties. Not the moment, perhaps, at which to stir the pot. But this is not how Germans see the problem, which arises instead, as Gunther Nonnenmacher of the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung puts it, from the "fixed fundamental beliefs" of the French political class. These beliefs are that the union provides a structure within which French rivalry with Germany can be peacefully pursued with the aim of ensuring "precedence over Germany or at least equal status". Nonnenmacher adds that France seems equally disturbed when Germany fails to lead as when it tries to do so. Other members of the union might not disagree with that thumbnail sketch of French attitudes. What can be held against both countries is their failure to grasp the alienating effect on others of their continuing pre-occupation with each other. Even if Germany sees itself as weaning France away from a too exclusive relationship, the effort to do so still leaves other states in the irritating position of onlookers, or being forced to choose sides when they do not wish to do so. French and German leaders came out of their informal summit on Wednesday, supposed to ease the strains of Nice, stressing that the Franco-German relationship was as strong as ever and that the "deepening" of Europe was a joint priority. What that means, if anything, we will wait to see, but French and German differences have surely not disappeared this week over a few plates of foie gras and choucroute in Strasbourg. The European story is a strange one. Every day we argue the merits of various schemes of governance that will make Europe more united, and every day we demonstrate a refusal to arrive at common policies in everything from the regulation of telecommunications to relations with Iraq. Javier de Solana, in charge of Europe's common foreign and security policy, complained recently that we either cannot arrive at one or, when we do, that it is so "over-negotiated" as to be useless. It might be deemed sensible to try to achieve an informal unity of policy before trying to impose unity institutionally, and that is the main objection to Germany's present approach. That, and the fact that it is less selfless than it appears. If German leaders were to admit that the federal path is significantly easier for them to take than it is for others, more honest discussions might then be possible. martin.woollacott@theguardian.com

title: Euroland prepares for real money
bodyText: Lise has a sweet smile and wide eyes - and she wants to make it easier for her fellow countrymen and women to adapt to the biggest currency changeover they have ever seen. ''L'euro est plus facile ensemble'' - the euro is easier together - she says. Lise's pretty pre-teen face and encouraging message have been splashed all over newspapers and TV screens in France in the last few days, the first sign of the ambitious advertising campaigns getting under way as euroland prepares for the big day. The timetable is clear - and immutable: euro notes and coins go into circulation in just under 11 months, next January 1, and there is much hard work to be done before then. Everyone knows that their deutschemarks, francs, lira, guilders, drachmas, pesetas and schillings will be disappearing for good. But over 300m people in 12 countries must also get used to the fact that what has been a "virtual'' currency since January 1999, used only for paper financial transactions, is about to become the real thing. And real means the staggering 15bn banknotes and 56bn coins which will have been shipped to banks and retail outlets by December 15 and will be in full use a fortnight later. Yet there is still far too little understanding of this enormous change. ''Surveys show that public knowledge of the euro is improving,'' Pedro Solbes, the European commissioner in charge of economic and monetary affairs, said this week.''But many people still have only a vague idea about the actual changeover timetable. And most consumers have still not begun to acquaint themselves with the new scale of values. To remedy this, far more information will have to be made available.'' In Frankfurt, European central bank officials agree, but are careful to emphasise the distinction between the role of the euro system - the ECB plus the 12 national central banks - and that of national governments. ''National campaigns are addressing more political themes in the sense that they are selling the euro to their population and trying to foster confidence,'' said one. ''The Euro System is focussing on a product - the banknotes and coins. The scope of these two messages is quite different.'' Here is snapshot of how the information is being conveyed to ordinary people 333 days before E-Day. In Germany, euroland's largest member, the euro action committee began its campaign in December with its central message that ''real values will prevail'' scheduled to increase in intensity in a five stage campaign throughout the year. Plans include newspaper adverts, TV spots, a ''euro-tent'' tour, a telephone hotline and internet sites. Ireland, one of the zone's smallest economies, is having an advertising campaign on the theme ''one year to go.'' People are being encouraged to use up hoarded coins before what is being dubbed Changeover Day. In the Netherlands - which has the best euro website of the 12 - information efforts double this year compared to 2001. Campaigns will focus on the dual display of prices, characteristics of the new notes and coins and guidelines on how to act in the period of dual circulation. Special programmes are being prepared for the mentally handicapped and ethnic minorities. Italy has concentrated on a TV campaign using actors struggling with euro calculations in ''normal'' daily situations. In one sketch a man flirts with a pretty shop assistant while his wife waits outside. And the public are being encouraged to learn an easy conversion formula - ''take out the zeros, divide by two, and there you are.'' Soccer games are to be used to explain basic issues. In Belgium an operation to collect hoarded national coins will be conducted from October 15 to November 15. Everything, of course, has to be published in both French and Dutch. Portuguese are using touch screens for euro information and there are campaigns tailored specially for schools and the tourist industry. The Lisbon underground has done its own dedicated work. Some of the 12 euroland countries are more advanced than others. France's slick publicity campaign, run by the Publicis group, puts it at the top of the preparations league: all civil servants are due to be paid in euros from July 3. And since last month all electricity and gas invoices have been detailed in euros only - though the total is still in dual display. Finland is placing special emphasis on small and medium enterprises - identified by Mr Solbes as the key problem everywhere: one in every three believes it is too early to make preparations and has not worked out how the changeover will affect them. Ominously for Finnish consumers, tabloid newspapers have already rounded up their prices from five to six markka -the exchange value of the euro. Right across euroland there is a recognition that a smooth transition is vital for political reasons and thus that the millions of citizens for whom the whole thing is still a distant nightmare must be helped to master the intricacies ahead. ''Conversion to the euro remains an abstract notion in the minds of many,'' admitted the French finance minister, Laurent Fabius, as he launched little Lise's face across the land. ''Our intention is to make each of us feel concerned.'' And in France, as everywhere else, old habits will certainly die hard, as one journalist neatly illustrated, writing: ''The learning campaign will cost France 120 million francs each year - Oops, that's 18.3 million euros a year.'' Email ian.black@theguardian.com Related special reports European integration Economic and monetary union European commission European parliament Other articles More articles by Ian Black Useful links European central bank Belgian eurogroup presidency French euro website (in French) Belgian euro website Dutch euro website (in Dutch) European government Le Monde Le Soir Süddeutsche Zeitung El Pais La Stampa Aftonbladet

title: A people skating on thin ice
bodyText: Just when it seems that the solitary, snow-bound highway might really lead nowhere, the ghost town of Oktyabrskiy peers over the permafrost, its skyline a grim, gap-toothed leer. As we approach, a hammer-and-sickle-encrusted sign by the roadside announces what was once one of the busiest fishing ports in the Russian far east, a model soviet enterprise bustling with life. Stretching away behind, however, Oktyabrskiy's main Lenin Street now looks little more than a sliver of wasteland, lined on both sides by abandoned apartment blocks, their sunken windows staring blankly, their facades in the advanced stages of decay. Dilapidated wooden cottages, stripped to timber skeletons, clutter the moul dering side-streets. Everywhere, tatty scraps of open ground appear to have been seeded with trash and twisted metal. And all along the pier, the rotting shells of trawlers lie entombed in ice. Only occasionally do the last sad remnants of Oktyabrskiy's population stumble into this desolate scene, some of them sullen teenagers who pass without expression, but the majority hunched and elderly, the human detritus of an unprecedented decade of decline: "It's difficult to believe this place was once crammed with people," says Lidia Ivanovna, the town's current governor, and our guide through the ruins. "All we have left are the very young, the very old and the sick - the people who couldn't leave. We're trapped between the tundra on one side and the sea on the other. Oktyabrskiy has been condemned to a gradual death." Over the past 10 years, Russia has been haemorrhaging humanity at a rate unprecedented for a modern, industrialised nation, except during times of famine or war. At the fall of communism, the population stood at 148.3m, a number that has been steadily sinking by between 300,000 a year, in 1993, and almost 800,000 in 1999, the most dramatic 12-month slump to date, a net loss of 0.5 % of Russia's total inhabitants. In an atmosphere of deep pessimism about the country's future, the traditionally high birth rate has plummeted to 1.3 children per woman, the lowest in Russia's history. At the same time, the death rate has soared; average life expectancy for the Russian male is now just 59, some 10-15 years less than in any western country, while women can expect to live to 72, six to eight years less than their European sisters. In yet another symptom of the Russian demographic malaise, this male/female discrepancy is the largest in the world. Other indicators are equally alarming: infant mortality is on the rise, 15.8 per 1,000 births in 1999 as against 6.9 in the US that year; Russia has become one of the world's TB hot spots, and Aids is said to threaten an epidemic of sub-Saharan proportions; meanwhile, migration is depleting the human resources of the far-flung regions. As the scale of the situation was becoming clear in 1998 and '99, the Russian Duma included genocide among the charges in an impeachment drive against the then president Boris Yeltsin, regarded as the architect of the debilitating reforms widely seen as the catalyst for the population collapse. However, if the projections of Russia's state statistics department, GosKomStat, are correct, the mammoth scale of the demographic catastrophe has yet to unfold. By the year 2016, they predict that Russia will lose another 8-10m, making the total loss for the post-soviet period more than the combined victims of Stalin's purges. Even in relatively thriving Moscow, which serves as a magnet for impoverished migrants from all over the former Soviet Union, there is still foreboding evidence of demographic subsidence: a precipitous 27% drop in the numbers of pre- and primary school infants has forced the closure of scores of the city's kindergartens and junior classes. Just a few hundred kilometres south of the capital, there are already dwindling villages, each one home to a meagre handful of pensioners. However, it is at the exposed and vulnerable extremities of the vast Russian territories that the atrophy of the population has been most acute. Perhaps the most startling example is Chukotka, a massive chunk of the far east three times the size of Britain, where the population has withered by a staggering 60%, from 180,000 in 1990 to just 65,000 today, a figure that is expected to slump to just 20,000 within the next five years, making the region's infrastructure unsustainable. Others include the Taimyr region in the far north, which enjoys 10 months of arctic winter and two of "polar night", which has bled more than 20% of its inhabitants and may now be in considerable danger of being evacuated by central government, and Kamchatka, an isolated peninsula, and the region where Oktyabrskiy is located. The final approach into Kamchatka after a nine-hour Aeroflot flight affords a view that might pass for an aerial vision of the edge of the earth. Its capital, Petropavlovsk-Kamchatskiy, is surrounded by a crescent of gently smouldering volcanoes, just a few of the 800 scattered throughout the peninsula, including Asia's largest and most lethal, the Klyuchevskaya Sopka. The ground beneath hides a mesh of grinding fault lines; we had barely stepped from the plane when an earthquake struck, jolting the ground for a few unnerving seconds. Since the fall of communism, almost a quarter of the overall population has seeped away. Unemployment in the grubby, low-lying capital is running at about 30%; 40% of its people live below Russia's already low poverty line, healthcare is nonexistent and fuel shortages have meant that until recently, heating and electricity were routinely shut down for as much as 17 hours a day. However, as we venture outside Petropavlovsk in the company of a Red Cross contingent, the situation worsens exponentially. Mountain-locked microclimates send the temperatures crashing to -30F. For hours, we encounter no other vehicles, just the occasional group of fishermen trudging through the snow. And then, at the end of one of the world's bleakest stretches of roadway, a desolate strip of asphalt separating the heaving lead-coloured waters of the Okhotsk Sea on one side and a huge frozen lake on the other, we finally encounter Oktyabrskiy. Perhaps fittingly, governor Lidia Ivanovna begins not with the physical wreckage of the town, but with a virtual tour of how it once was: "Right here there used to be two rows of houses." She gestures towards sawn-off foundations in a completely deserted district known as Basa II. "That was once a small port, where supplies were delivered before the road was built..." The decay began, she explains, in the early 90s, with the liquidation of the fish-processing plant and cannery; in the new, market-driven environment, harsher even than the merciless natural climate the town had survived for decades, no one could justify the ballooning costs of transporting canned fish across the vastness of Russia. Since then almost 60% of the population of 4,500 has evaporated, and the spirit of the town with them. A heavy-set woman in her 50s who brooks little sentimentality, Lidia Ivanovna finds herself continually fighting back tears: "People once lived well in Oktyabrskiy," she says. "People once lived very well..." By anyone's estimation, however, many of the remaining inhabitants of Oktyabrskiy now endure an almost bestial existence. One filthy apartment we visited, home of an alcoholic and his retarded son, was the most sordid imaginable, strewn with vodka bottles, stinking mattresses and the guts of ancient televisions. Across the stairwell, we find a woman of Koryak origin in a dark and sooty living room with her two youngest children, a mischievous boy and pretty moon-faced girl called Veronika, both suffering from tuberculosis. Ironically, because of spells in the TB hospital in Petropavlovsk receiving proper nutrition, they look infinitely healthier than the three emaciated older boys. Most of these pitiable human beings pray for escape - in any form - from their grimy purgatory. In the first block we visit, pen sioner Maria Riabchenko shows us round her two almost entirely bare, unheated rooms. Aged 72 and virtually blind, she has carefully stacked all of her possessions in one corner, in preparation for a journey she was almost certainly never going to make: "I want to get out of this prison," she hisses. "Many people have already fled but the rest of us would also get out if we could. I have everything packed. I'd have left long ago, except that I don't have any money." With tears streaming, she adds, bitterly: "But I can wait. I'm very patient..." In the next stairwell, we call on Valintin Igulayev, an amiable old man who arrived in Oktyabrskiy in the 50s, brimming with idealism: "A friend of mine said one day: 'Let's go to Kamchatka' and I immediately answered, 'Why not?'. I was up for some romance and adventure." His elderly wife, Lilia, has just been diagnosed with cancer of the colon; but with their combined pensions totalling about $70 (£45) a month, she has no hope of getting adequate treatment. Physically shaking and with tears welling up in her eyes, she explains that her only option is to sit at home and wait for the tumour to consume her: "The doctor says that I must pay 15,000 roubles for the treatment, but I don't even have 40 roubles for fish in the market..." Oktyabrskiy's clean but impossibly spartan hospital, which is forced to rely on the Red Cross for basic commodities such as soap and washing powder, has almost given up trying to help the seriously ill. Instead, certain wards serve as a rest home for the most chronically destitute. In one upstairs room, Alexei, who has lost all coordination because of the devastation wrought by alcohol on his nervous system and looks 25 years older than his actual age of 48, shares a room with Vladimir, whose binge drinking caused a cerebral haemorrhage that deprived him of the use of one arm and his legs. "We don't hope to cure them," Oktyabrskiy's head doctor, Nikolai Kuznetsov, tells me, "but if we let them out now, they wouldn't survive long. The only option is to look after them here until we find a place for them in the old people's home in Petropavovsk or until they die..." Our final stop is Oktyabrskiy's modest, two-storey school, which has seen numbers fall from 680 students 10 years ago to 240 today. This will sink further as every one of the children intends to flee the ailing town immediately on graduation: "Oktyabrskiy is just a long street with the sea on both sides of it," says Oksana, a forthright 16-year-old who intends to move to Petropavlovsk, "It's boring here. There are no sports facilities or anything. I want to study psychology and there are no higher education facilities. And I won't come back - there is no work and nothing to do." Another 16-year-old, Spartak, hopes to move either to England or Greece: "Of course I'll miss Oktyabrskiy. It's my home," he says. "But I have to survive and, for that reason, I have to move. I know that without us, Oktyabrskiy will die, but what can I do? Not a single one of my friends wants to stay..." "Russia has been shunted back a century," says Sergei Kaleshnikov of the statistics committee, GosKomStat. "A contemporary man who is 50 can, according to statistics, live for another four to 10 years It's almost the same as it was 100 years ago." As is obvious from the situation in Oktyabrskiy, the primary causes of the demographic catastrophe are brutally simple: poverty, bad diet, poor living conditions and, in more industrialised parts of Russia, a polluted environment. This has been fatally compounded by an almost total implosion of the healthcare system, even in the more populous cities. The TB hospital in Petropavlovsk-Kamchatskiy relies entirely on a 50-year-old fleurography machine, a hulking piece of apparatus that produces tiny imprints of lungs the size of postage stamps. In St Petersburg and Moscow, very few hospitals have the resources to perform what have become routine operations in the west such as heart bypasses; the leading TB hospital in St Petersburg can only afford to spend about 55p per day on its patients, much less than is required to cure many of them. Contemporary Russia is also an ideal breeding ground for infectious diseases such as TB which, incubated in the labyrinthine prison system, are now raging through the population. "We are now on the verge of an epidemic," says Tatyana Suprun, one of St Petersburg's leading TB doctors. "It's a consequence of the way we live now in Russia, often three generations in a tiny flat. If one person gets sick, everyone does." Aids too is rumbling beneath the surface, due to a potent combination of rising intravenous drug use, the post-soviet sexual revolution and an almost total lack of safe-sex education. However, the Russian populace is not only being corroded by disease, but also by its own self-destructive social reflexes. Alcohol consumption in Russia is the highest in the world, and deaths from alcohol poisoning far outstrips those in any other country: about 50,000 cases in 1999 as opposed to just 350 in the US, mostly as a result of a national predilection for binge drinking and the nature of the alcohol itself: "Since about 1990 there has been no control of alcohol production," says Viktor Petrovich, head of Petropavlosk's addiction clinic. "This has led to the availability of very low-grade spirits. People also started to brew their own, low-quality home brews that led to a lot of poisoning. In some cases, people even drank technical spirits." Finally, there is the issue of violent death: the Russian murder rate per capita, which has doubled since 1990, is the highest in the world, three times more than the US and four times more than France. And in the atmosphere of social gloom, suicides are also rising, up by a third from their 1990 levels. Russia and its long-suffering people have, throughout most of the country's demographic history, had a complicated, almost intimate relationship with the mechanics of death. As Catherine Merridale reported in her recent book on the death culture of Russia, Night of Stone (Granta £25), crude death rates in Russia in the 1870s were far higher than for any European country: 38 deaths per 1,000 members of the population per year, compared to the corresponding rate in France and England of just over 22 per 1,000. If the situation continues to deteriorate today, Russia's accelerating population decline could become the most important challenge to the country's already unstable economic and political status in the world: "If this trend does not change in 15 to 20 years," claims Valentin Pokrovsky, of the Russian Academy of Medical Science, "for each working person, there will be one or two people who cannot work. It will be very difficult for the country." However, despite an insulating fatalism nurtured by Russians over centuries, the ultimate toll is a very human and individual one. The cemetery in Petropavlovsk-Kamchatskiy is probably one of the most beautiful spots in the city, a sweeping, steep, snow-covered hill framed by the icy peaks of volcanoes. On one of the most picturesque slopes, a small army of youthful gravediggers is opening the frozen earth with picks, working simultaneously on three or four graves at once. All around, small picnic tables are piled with food, sustenance for the dead on their journey, while ravens, believed by Russians to be celestial pallbearers, circle hungrily overhead. One funeral (pictured above) is already in progress, the deceased an unemployed man of 44 who died suddenly from a heart attack, leaving behind a wife and young daughter. "It was totally unexpected," said his brother, "He was so young, still relatively healthy. Life killed him." As soon as it ends, another gets underway, this time of a 21-year-old man who had been kicked to death by a group of soldiers: "He was just coming home and they beat him up. Five or six of them just set upon him," says his father. "They beat him up and took his clothes. He was just left there lying naked in the snow; people just walked around him, nobody helped him. They only found him in the morning lying there dead - he didn't have a chance because of the cold." Once the body has been laid in the ground, the entire mountainside, as bleak and depleted a stretch of wilderness as any we have encountered during our journey to Kamchatka's depopulated margins, seems to fill up with the keening of his mother as she is led away.

title: Scambuster!
bodyText: For the past two weeks, Paris has been witnessing the spectacle of a former foreign minister, Roland Dumas,appearing in the dock to answer charges of corruption. There is some £6m in kickbacks and a mistress at the heart of these charges, but what has devastated the French establishment is that Dumas was, at the time of the original accusation in 1997, France's highest legal authority, the president of the Constitutional Council. He finally resigned last year. What law officer could have dared to initiate and obstinately bring to its awful conclusion an investigation targeting the highest legal authority in the land? Well, it wasn't a Frenchman. It was, in fact, a former Norwegian au pair, Gro Eva Farseth, who married into a distinguished French family and then appalled them by transforming herself into the all-powerful Investigating Magistrate, Madame Eva Joly. As such, she has become the scourge of France's untouchables - the business moguls and political leaders. A co-defendant in the Dumas affair is the former president of the oil giant Elf-Aquitaine, one-time head of the state-owned railway network, SNCF. But this case forms only a small part of Eva Joly's spectacular reach. She was also instrumental in uncovering the Crédit Lyonnais banking scandal, described in 1997 as the world's biggest financial disaster. Her investigations into the Elf affair uncovered a political slush fund that went beyond the borders of France to touch Germany's former chancellor, Helmut Kohl. And then there was the affair of the frigates sold to Taiwan... All this was achieved in a post that Eva Joly has held for only seven years. It all started more than 30 years ago, when the 20-year-old Gro Eva Farseth travelled to Paris from her home in Oslo to study French. Already on the train down, there was a hint of the determination and steadiness of purpose to come: instead of preparing herself for Parisian life with Elle magazine or Paris Match, she passed the journey studying French verbs. She very soon found a position as au pair in an urban palace by the Luxembourg gardens, home of the Joly family. This was a family that had for generations contributed soldiers, scientists and academics to the glory of their country. When Gro was courteously permitted by her employers to dine with them, she became privy to the etiquette that distinguishes such a family of true taste from that of - well, plebeian is too base a concept - let's say the nouveau riche. As you stir your coffee, for instance, the ear must not be offended by the sound of the spoon touching the bottom. Nor must you ever cut your lettuce; it must be folded. Mlle Farseth caught on quickly that observance of a dozen or so rules would see you comfortably through the best drawing rooms. But what this condescending family of distinguished lineage did not realise about Growas that, while she might be what they would call brute de coffrage (unpolished), she had powerful values of her own. The most deadly proved to be an inherent lack of respect for hierarchy. There is no aristocracy in Norway. Gro is the daughter of a carpenter, and her family lived at one point in the same apartment block in Oslo as their prime minister. There is one photograph of Madame Eva Joly that delights egalitarians. It is January 23, 1998, and we see a reserved lady escorting a distinguished, grey-haired man into his residence in the 5th arrondissement. It is Joly arriving to conduct personally a search of the home of the president of the Constitutional Council, Roland Dumas. To give the full measure to the difficulties faced by Joly: before her appointment as Investigating Magistrate in 1994, no corporate financial prosecution of any real weight had ever been brought before a French tribunal. There was neither resource nor inclination for such an enterprise. To bring to justice some of the country's most powerful men and corporations, certain conditions must prevail: the perpetrators must be already vulnerable (usually when a belief in immunity makes them careless); tradition must be a shield for the magistrate, rather than a block; luck must provide opportunity; and, above all, you must have the character to want to act. Over many years, all these fell into place for Mme Joly. Seven years ago, Eva Joly (you couldn't go around France calling yourself "Gro") finally made it as a Juge d'Instruction (Investigating Magistrate). Many of the world's legal systems harbour a subversive corner, protected by tradition, that caters for society's need to guarantee a last-ditch stand against tyranny. Such a system operated successfully in Spain against Pinochet; and we saw similar independent legal powers abused in the Lewinsky/Clinton affair. A Juge d'Instruction is one of them: a legal officer with unlimited powers of investigation and rigorously protected independence. Not even the president of the republic can fire a Juge d'Instruction. The establishment's one recourse, when investigations become too embarrassing, is to promote him, or her, out of office, normally a very successful ploy creating only minimal criticism. Mme Joly has refused that kind of promotion. When Roland Dumas, who denies the allegations, was preparing to face the court, I met Eva Joly in Paris. She received me in the new headquarters of the Investigating Magistrates close to metro L'Opéra. There is a certain symbolism in the recent removal of this department from the Palais de Justice, where it formerly occupied a few cubbyholes. For all its intimidating exterior, the Palais de Justice is not secure. Joly's office in the Palais was burgled more than once. The new spacious offices are protected by surveillance cameras, metal detectors and an armed policeman with his copy of Joly's book, Notre Affaire à Tous (Everybody's Business - a reference to the degree fiscal fraud affects us all). "I haven't had the chance to ask her to autograph it yet," the policeman told me. Joly currently enjoys that rarest of honours in France: the hero worship of cops... well, some of them, at any rate. Inherently democratic (and still short of staff), Joly herself came down to the lobby to escort me back to her office. She wore a bright, sky-blue suit, very unmagisterial. You would have guessed an executive of a fashionable corporation. She is 57. It is only recently, she confided, that she has had the confidence to discard the traditional authoritarian black garb of the lawyer. She speaks with careful precision, but without the theatrical gravity one expects from lawyers, nor do you get any sense of a lawyer's perpetual calculation. The character that comes through is almost neighbourly - a confident, candid person, but not lacking in prudence. It's hard to know what the Joly family was thinking of, taking a 20-year-old Scandinavian into a family where there was a 21-year-old son. Aristocratic inattention might explain it. "In opening their doors to me," she agrees, "the Joly family were perhaps playing with fire." Very soon, the scion declared he would marry Gro Farseth. They disinherited him. Values must be maintained. Initially, the young couple went through the usual impecunious struggle with first one child and then another coming in close succession. He studied to be a doctor; she worked as a secretary in a factory. There was an early manifestation of Eva's determination to see justice done. To the astonishment of her employer, the young foreigner decided that the working conditions in the factory were "not worthy". She formed a branch of the CFDT (Christian trade union). "We have nourished a viper in our bosom," wailed her boss, rather elegantly. But the successful crusader never allows a passion for justice to carry her to self-destructive extremes. "I found," she says, "that trades union organisation was too much for me while at the same time having to hold down a job and take care of children in a sixth-storey walk-up". The time for action was not yet ripe. In 1973, her husband moved to a new practice in Essonne, to the south of Paris. By then, Eva had a law degree and quickly found work in a psychiatric hospital in the region. Her role was to deal with the legal problems of the mentally disturbed - for instance, a patient who (against Eva's advice) made a generous gift of all his money to his family and then found himself abandoned as his relatives grabbed the money and emigrated to Canada. The hospital had, she says, a congenial atmosphere of dedicated public service. "I received my political education there." She dealt compassionately with socially dysfunctional creatures, recognised as such and treated accordingly - unlike the socially dysfunctional high-level thieves she was to deal with later, whom tradition protected from either treatment or restraint. But she was still a long way from the Palais de Justice. Being a foreigner, there was little possibility of making her way up the judicial ladder. She would be considered by the establishment as having "an insufficiency of general French culture". To break through that barrier would require a small miracle. Joly does not believe in miracles. But when I put to her the Shakespearean proposition that "There is a tide in the affairs of men which, taken at the flood", she admitted this much: "When you work hard, at one point a kind of resonance, a harmony, is created and there comes the moment when 'the mayonnaise takes'." In 1981, the French government decided to hold a one-off open competition for entry to the magistrature. French culture would not be crucial. Not only was it an opening to the magistrature, it was an accelerated one for those with 10 years' legal experience, which Joly now had. After a couple of months at magistrates' school, the successful applicant would go to work in the courts immediately. Suddenly, Eva Joly found herself assistant to the public prosecutor of Orléans. The first day was not propitious. When she greeted her new boss with a civil "Bonjour, Monsieur", he snapped: "Please, Madame, in the time, which I hope will be short, that we will spend together, I ask you to address me as Monsieur le Procureur. We have not spent time together taking care of pigs." She left, disenchanted, after two years, convinced that the main preoccupations of her superior "were problems of parking his car". She gave up the magistrature and got a position with the CIRI, in the ministry of finance, in Paris. She was already 46, with apparently nothing but a routine future in store. But she entered the ministry at a time when the government was focusing on the industrial reconstruction of France, a noble national enterprise within which many a maggot was to find nourishment. Her job concerned "businesses in difficulties" - ie, bankruptcies - which were then reaching dizzying proportions in France. "I was given genuine responsibility and this gave me wings," she says. After three years of desk duty, she was back in the magistrature. Appointed a Juge d'Instruction concerned with financial crimes, she moved to the Palais de Justice. "It was only seven years ago," she said, "and I was a million miles from imagining what I would discover. I still had confidence in the institutions." She assumed that her resources at the Palais would be as grandiose as that intimidating building. "What I found was a poky little office," she said, "with an old Olivetti typewriter. There was no fax and no Minitel [the French computer linked to the telephone system]. I had one clerk. "No one said that you cannot deal with an affair involving great international banks, concerning tens of billions of francs, with one judge in a chambre de bonne, a ballpoint pen and a copy of the Penal Code. The contrast with the ministry of finance was shocking. I discovered that one of the most serious cases had been on the books for five years. At the CIRI, I had worked with people who could have cleared it up in 10 days." During the course of her investigation into the Elf case, she had to deal with more than 60 lawyers, studying 50 volumes of evidence. "I dumped the typewriter in a cupboard. My daughter [now a doctor] had a basic computer bought from the supermarket; I borrowed it. I installed the Minitel from my own home. I bought a fax with my own money, not for comfort but efficiency - the Penal Code permits the use of fax in place of expensive registered letter," she says. "I sold my old car to an architect and then persuaded him to redesign my office. The other magistrates were conquered, amused." Joly soon discovered the dubious worth of amused admiration: it vanished when she showed her combative side. The only hope for obtaining any real improvement in her working conditions was to go public. She took part in a short TV documentary exposing the farcical lack of resources granted to fiscal investigators. It created a sensation. Then she gave a long interview to an economic journal, La Tribune, which appeared the very day she descended on Crédit Lyonnais with a search warrant. Horror at the Palais! But there was serious panic when the Garde des Sceaux (minister for justice), Elisabeth Guigou, made her displeasure at such publicity tactics known in an unsisterly interview in the newspaper Le Figaro. Joly was summoned before the vice-president of the courts and warned that she was overstepping the line (actually, he said she was walking outside the pedestrian crossing). Jacques Chirac's government learned of the serious problems at the state-owned Crédit Lyonnais in October 1995. Although the losses, attributed to bad management, were estimated at a staggering £12.8bn, no one believed there would be a criminal prosecution. Financial mismanagement was not thought to be a criminal offence. But this was not a belief shared by Mme Joly. The taxpayer had to meet these losses. The bank was headed by Jean-Yves Haberer, a high-flyer in the financial world and at one time fellow student of President Chirac at the elite Ecole National d'Administration (ENA). Haberer's most bizarre and ultimately fatal decision was, in 1991, to lend a dodgy Italian financier, Giancarlo Parretti, money to buy the Hollywood studio, MGM. After a number of farcical manoeuvres, which included giving Dustin Hoffman a six-figure rubber cheque, Crédit Lyonnais had to move in and become a working partner of MGM. It soon had to sell the studio at a huge loss. The bank became known as Débit Lyonnais. Eva Joly will not, on principle, discuss any of her cases, past or current, but it is known that after a year of investigations at Crédit Lyonnais, when her team was getting uncomfortably close to essentials, there was a mysterious fire at the bank. More than 2,500 files relating to the case were destroyed. This is the kind of setback Mme Joly and her colleagues take in their stride. The Crédit Lyonnais case still lumbers on, spawning other investigations that now suggest a presidential cover-up. She continued with her campaign for better facilities and suddenly one evening the president of the courts paid a visit to her chambre de bonne. "He had probably never been in that part of the building," she says. Fellow magistrates had expressed approval for her stand, but none of them turned up for the confrontation with the president. "Magistrates prefer more velvety tête-à-têtes." By this time, Joly had a valuable new colleague, another Juge d'Instruction, Laurence Vichnievsky. If the men chose diplomatic absence, the two women were not intimidated in their confrontation with the president. From that day everything changed. "Suddenly, there was nothing more urgent than to create a financial centre." That was only three years ago. Joly and Vichnievsky found themselves with a new HQ. They also established contact with a like-minded magistrate in a crucial financial area - Switzerland. Changes were already taking place in France that help explain Eva Joly's success. Following the economic crisis of the 80s and the increase in bankruptcy frauds, a new law on bankruptcy was passed. The role of the courts was reinforced. There was a new breed of lawyers and there was also a new generation of better-informed police; the economic crisis had attracted police with more diplomas than the earlier ones. The commissaire who handled the Elf case was a product of St Cyr (an elite academy for the military and gendarmerie), and only 29 years old. But one vital factor was for her to have, at a crucial moment, a colleague of similar resolution and courage in Laurence Vichnievsky. The main facts of the Elf case are now in the open. There are many strands. The former French foreign minister is not only accused of accepting kickbacks indirectly from the bagman of the affair, a former director of Elf-Aquitaine now said to be in hiding in the Philippines, but is also accused of having inexplicably (at the time) reversed a government embargo on arms sales to Taiwan and encouraged the sale of frigates to go through (which involved generous comissions for some). Elf was also involved in this affair. And so was Christine Deviers-Joncour, Dumas's mistress and Elf's favourite lobbyist. Elf allegedly gave her a company credit line of £20,000 a month and a £1.7m apartment on the Left Bank for entertainment. Joly and Vichnievsky allege that she passed on expensive "gifts" to Dumas. The 53-year-old Mme Deviers-Joncour has now become a notorious performer on French TV, following the publication of her memoirs called, The Whore Of The Republic. "To deal with an affair such as Elf," Joly says, "common sense suggests the mobilisation of dozens of policemen, the collaboration of public services such as the secret service, of tax officials, and that all the weight of the republic be brought to bear on foreign countries who refuse to aid us. But the state, that is you and I, and all taxpayers, allow ourselves to be robbed without reacting." What is behind this curious laxity? Joly says that the intrinsic problem is that high-class financial crimes "were not really considered a crime". There was also the undermining tradition that those at a certain level of power and influence were above the law. "The great fiscal frauds involved very powerful and respectable people who were convinced - and still are - that they are entitled to be above the law. There is a culture of passe droit. That the law is not equal for everybody. Because you are a minister or a high-ranking personage, you could have immunity. In Norway, there is not this submission to power, to the establishment. Here, there is a notion that this kind of thing does not concern Justice [the courts]. There is also a tradition of 'proximity' regarding government and the law. Some of the attorney generals in France were collaborators of ministers and the president. "But I realised I would have to treat financial scandals like any other crime. Someone robs a petrol station. He is pursued methodically; the culprit is caught, his home searched and he is sentenced to 10 years. But when the head of an organisation steals a hundred million, Justice surrounds itself with precautionary luxuries. There are prolonged preliminary enquiries, interviews, interminable strings of 'experts'." Joly was determined, so far as possible, to speed up the process. "We investigated," she said, "put a tail on people and searched premises, no matter whose." In the past year alone, Joly took part in 40 searches. In five years, she has dealt with 200 cases. What was the initial reaction when she started to put a tail on distinguished citizens as if they were common thieves, and went rummaging through their private property? At first, they blamed it on her "Protestantism" and her nationality. "People tried to get the public to believe that, because I was a Norwegian and a Protestant, I did not understand anything of what was going on in France," she says. "Actually, I have little contact with religion. This line worked for a while because most people did not want to believe that you can't always have confidence in elected chiefs." There were also indignant articles in the press, of a kind familiar to us in Britain, about the cruel treatment of wealthy offenders whose "poor health" becomes a major issue. Dumas succeeded in putting off his court appearance for months on these grounds. Then the attacks took a more sophisticated and deadly turn. "They began to say that the independent powers of the Juge d'Instruction were an attack on liberty, that the judges were dangerous. There was a public debate, which totally falsified the issues. It nearly succeeded." To begin with, public opinion was not always 100% behind her. The public and the media can be indulgent towards popular figures. One example is Bernard Tapie. A rather reckless businessman and one-time minister for urban affairs, he was briefly revered as the owner of European Cup winners, Olympique Marseille. He became bankrupt (he owed Crédit Lyonnais more than £100m) and went to prison for corruption. How supportive was the media in these affairs, I asked. For the first time, Joly becomes extremely guarded. She is only too well aware that taking on the press can be a losing game. She hints at disappointment, not just with the popular press (although in France they are nothing like as muckily "popular" as our crowd), but also with the more responsible papers. She rarely responds to such attacks, but when one newspaper, alluding to prosecutor Kenneth Starr's investigations into the Clinton/Lewinsky affair in the US, carried the headline, "EVA - OUR STARR IN STILETTOS", and Joly felt it implied that she had suspects beaten up, she fought back. The story was based on a mythical remark, supposedly made to a police escort: "Why did you bring him to me so fresh?" Joly invoked her right of reply. Sternly quoting the legal reference - article 13 of the law of July 29, 1881 - she took apart these fictions about "this blonde capable of humiliating [people] and making them tremble". "I felt I must answer. It was the messenger they wanted killed." So, are the media more supportive now? All she would say to this is: "The newspapers still don't adequately cover this issue." The next day, Saturday, I waited for her in a cafe across from the Palais de Justice. When on weekend duty, she is obliged to work again from the Palais. Any crime of a serious nature taking place that weekend in Paris, she and her clerk had to deal with. As she crossed the road, I noted she had reverted to black. With her was a tall, athletic man in running shoes. He was attentive, but with the air of a discreet servant rather than a friend. He showed no sign of wanting to take part in our conversation, but every now and then he spoke into his coat collar. Fifty yards away, another man was putting on one of those performances of taking no interest in us, but he also nosed his collar at intervals. At one point, she made a puzzling remark: "I got my first new car in 1996, but I still have not driven it." I realised then why she never managed to drive her new car. She has permanent, around-the-clock driver-bodyguards. At crucial moments in her investigations, her office in the Palais was burgled, drawers ransacked or locks tampered with. There was clear intimidation in the absence of any attempt to conceal traces of forced entry. There have also been death threats. When she went jogging the next morning at 8am, the tall discreet man jogged alongside, a weapon somehow concealed in his jogging gear. The other drove the car. She trotted around the grounds, bereft of any vanity aids. So I was surprised when she agreed to be photographed panting around in sweaty gear. She admitted later that it was a deliberate decision. This magistrate has another mission - to demonstrate to other women that life is by no means over at 57. This is one of the reasons she dares nowadays to dress colourfully, to declare her femininity even on duty. "I lost a lot of time to get to this point," she admitted. Gradually, there was a transformation in her manner, a definite shift from jurisprudential objectivity to bubbling, subterranean exuberance, hinting at satisfaction with the joys of life. Now that she is - at least for the moment - master of her trade, such satisfaction would be understandable. But a recent long holiday in the heartstopping beauty and purity of the fjords of her native Norway might have contributed to her joie de vivre. What does her family-in-law now think of little Gro Farseth? There appears to have been something of a reconciliation. "They think it is funny that I am a judge, but some of the family still think it is not right that a Norwegian-born person should be judging French people." Eva Joly is under no illusion as to how successful this fight against global financial crime can be. She says only a tiny fraction of the crimes are being dealt with. "The one achievement is that it is now more difficult to suppress cases... But we are still like sheriffs in those westerns, where you chase the bad men to the Rio Grande and they just ride across the river and we can't follow." Global crime, she believes, requires a global force. To create one will be her next crusade • Notre Affaire à Tous, by Eva Joly, is published by Les Arenes, 33 rue Linné, Paris 5005, priced Fr99.

title: Europe awaits crucial findings
bodyText: With a mixture of hope and trepidation, farmers across Europe were last night awaiting the final outcome of tests that will show whether foot and mouth has spread from Britain to the continent. Officials in the German region of North Rhine-Westphalia said on Tuesday that sheep imported from Britain had tested positive for antibodies, suggesting that they had been in contact with animals that had the disease. But the federal agriculture minister, Renate Künast, said yesterday that the risk of an outbreak was low. She said two sets of subsequent tests had found no indication that the sheep were themselves infected. The results of a third and definitive test were expected today. Elsewhere, there were recriminations against Britain. A senior veterinary inspector at Ciney cattle market in Belgium, Paul Pilotte, told the daily Le Soir: "It was the English who pushed for abolishing Europe's foot and mouth vaccination programme and look where we are now. "The English only value their land in order to extract profit from it and agriculture there has become an industry. Those who don't respect normal standards should be nailed to the stake." The Belgian government is slaughtering 2,000 British sheep and goats and has shut cattle markets. In Spain, where more than 2,000 pigs and sheep imported from Britain are to be slaughtered, the daily El Mundo accused the British government of being too lax in its controls on livestock. "Vaccinations against this disease stopped being compulsory in the EU in 1990 but the UK had stopped well before that now Europe is paying for Britain's excess confidence," it said. In North Rhine-Westphalia, two farms that had imported sheep from Britain were yesterday still under quarantine as a huge, two-day disinfection operation began. A spokesman for the regional agriculture ministry said 1,850 sheep on the farms had been slaughtered as a precaution. Other farms in Brandenburg and Lower Saxony that had imported animals from Britain recently were also put under quarantine. Outside Germany, fears over the spread of disease were greatest in France. Some 45% of the sheep and pigs Britain sells to Europe go to France. Le Monde yesterday called the foot and mouth scare "the gravest sanitary alert in Europe for 30 years". The French agriculture minister, Jean Glavany, has ordered the destruction of 20,000 sheep exported to France from Britain since last February. Among those affected by the outbreak are Belgium's 450,000 Muslims who had been planning to sacrifice thousands of sheep during the annual Aid-El-Kebir festival next month.

title: While England becomes ever more divided, Europe's provincial cities forge ahead
bodyText: Economic divisions between London and England's northern cities have widened over the past four years and show little sign of narrowing, despite government plans to pump hundreds of millions of pounds into neighbourhood renewal schemes, according to a study. Presenting a dismal picture of two nations, with the south motoring ahead at the expense of less-favoured conurbations and regions, it portrays English provincial cities as pale shadows of their European counterparts. Outlining "northern sluggishness and southern ebullience" in a new league table of city prosperity, Brian Robson, head of the Centre for Urban Policy Studies at Manchester University and a government adviser, and Iain Deas say that narrowly focused Whitehall regeneration programmes will fail unless they address the wider economy. And with high-value companies ever more concentrated in the south-east, they warn that the prospects for a "reserve army" of workers in northern cities barely rise above the call centre. "What is it that trainees on the myriad of New Deal schemes can look forward to at the end of their training?" the research, commissioned by the Guardian, says. The analysis (the basis for a Guardian North debate in Salford tonight), which could make grim reading for politicians desperate to bring new life to Labour's fragile heartlands, also warns of a "pressing need" to link the new-found prosperity of city centres to surrounding areas of "impoverished households". "Nowhere is this seen more clearly than in the northern conurbations," the report says. "Despite the welcome recent city centre developments in places like Leeds and Manchester, go half a mile away from the new penthouses, restaurants, offices and multi-screen cinemas and you will be in the land of the forgotten - the endless rows of impoverished (private) terraces and half empty council housing where unemployment levels are horrendous, where houses sell - if at all - for less than £10,000, where crime or the fear of crime traps people in their homes where drugs are common currency." Housing provides one of the more graphic illustrations of the north-south city divide. There is little sign of property markets collapsing in Greater London - unlike in parts of the north. Outlining the "worst" 30 districts on a deprivation table, the researchers found that only one district in London and the south-east - Hastings - has houses selling at under £20,000, while 25 are in the north. And while acknowledging that inner London has a substantial number of impoverished neighbourhoods, they argue that the capital's poorer areas still have a buoyancy which contrasts with northern cities. To place the performance of England's provincial cities in a European context, the Guardian asked Professor Robson and his team to draw comparisons with France, a country with a similar population although almost three times as large. Using four indicators of employment and population change, GDP (or wealth) per head and unemployment, the researchers found that, across the channel, jobs had grown more markedly in the towns of the south and the west (from Bordeaux, Toulouse and Nantes to Montpellier and Marseille) than in the centre. But per capita, GDP remained remarkably uniform across the country with an "economically prospering set of provincial cities". England, by contrast, displayed a consistent pattern of higher growth in Greater London and the south-east, followed by the midlands, with the north showing slowest growth. Calculations by Prof Robson's team indicate a "much greater evenness of economic performance across the provincial cities of France than is true of England". Crucially, they note: "The regional problem appears to be an English phenomenon that is no longer shared with French cities." Prof Robson said the French pattern is repeated across most other European countries, making England the exception as a highly centralised country. Underperforming His report says that Newcastle-centred Tyneside, Liverpool-centred Merseyside and Sheffield-centred South Yorkshire have consistently lost employment, while Greater Manchester and Birmingham-centred West Midlands have remained static. Only West Yorkshire, reflecting the relative health of Leeds, had experienced some growth, while still underperforming. The only places outside London showing expansion on the scale of Montpellier, or Toulouse, were new towns such as Milton Keynes and Swindon. Using a new method to chart the fortunes of London and seven English cities and surrounding conurbations, the Robson team has set urban assets - skills, quality of higher education, quality of land and buildings and efficiency of communications - against six performance measures, from the health of firms, to new business registrations, GDP and the number of economically active residents. They can then judge whether cities are performing to their full potential. On almost every level they are not. Greater London stands alone in both the positive levels of its assets and performance, with even the West Midlands and West Yorkshire - conurbations which are supposed to have turned the corner - well below the capital with marginal performance "pluses" along with Greater Manchester. Merseyside, Tyne and Wear and Cleveland, on the other hand, have "minus" values. "Greater London is clearly endowed with substantial land, labour, infrastructure and other assets and these are reflected, unsurprisingly, in terms of correspondingly buoyant economic performance," says the report. "Even for inner London, in spite of the substantial number of impoverished neighbourhoods - the strength of the asset base and resultant buoyancy are in conspicuous contrast to the listlessness of the provincial cities." On the latter, it warns: "The pattern, for the most part, is one of economic underperformance and this potentially imperils government regeneration efforts." So what to do? Given that the comparison with French cities makes a "sobering contrast", the report urges the government to channel more important research funding, currently concentrated largely in the London-Oxford-Cambridge triangle, to northern conurbations to encourage higher-value industries in cities rather than on (southern) greenfield sites. It also suggests that the "illogically" tightly drawn boundaries of cities, such as Manchester, should be widened to reflect the wider conurbation. Finally, the Robson team says there is a "pressing need" to link the new-found prosperity of city centres to the unemployed a mile or so away in areas often characterised by collapsing house prices. "Success in turning around the prospects of the cities is now all about linkages - between neighbourhood, city, region and nation, between departments in local and central government." In short, says the report, it is time to act - before it is too late.

title: Letters: The regions take a lead
bodyText: Although it was nice to see the old warhorse of city regions being given a fresh airing (Leader, March 1), it was a shame that England's historic regions were given such short shrift. Evidence from continental Europe shows that a strong regional tier of government is necessary for balanced economic development. We will not close the north-south divide until more power is devolved from London and Whitehall. This isn't just because regional assemblies are able to "bat" strongly on behalf of their cities and regions, but more because a region is able to support clusters of highly qualified manpower, to be an effective presence on the international stage and to raise the level of decision-making within the private sector. Economic growth is being held back by the chaotic mish-mash of bodies at regional level. Bringing these together and forging an overarching strategy will help create the right conditions for the renewal of regional economies - sensible and streamlined planning guidance, workable transport plans, a highly trained workforce and region-wide policies for the arts, culture, agriculture and tourism. John Adams Campaign for the English Regions london@cfer.org.uk • Successful provincial cities in France and elsewhere in Europe have had for many years the benefits of decentralised, devolved and democratically accountable regional government, as well as strong city and very local government. Such arrangements exist now, in part, in Scotland, Wales and Ireland, whose cities also seem to have performed much better than those in England. What we need is not a divisive debate about boundaries, but a real commitment to democratically accountable regional government across the whole of England. This could provide the real political clout, resources and vision that are really needed to address the problems and potential of all parts of England - urban and rural - and which most of continental Europe has had the benefit of for many years. George Morran Aston University gmorran@aol.com • Though controlled by two different parties, Lib Dem Liverpool and Labour Manchester are developing a new relationship which will enable us to act jointly as the powerhouse for the north-west. There are regular meetings between the two council leaders and increased cooperation through the regional development agency on issues such as housing, transport, employment and education. Two weeks ago representatives of the five northern core cities and seven northern housing associations met to consider housing problems. We agreed to act as the voice of the north in pressing for an understanding of our problems and for the resources to deal with them. Liverpool, Manchester, Sheffield, Leeds and Newcastle are major economic entities in their own right, and increasingly we are adopting the city region structure you suggest. This is the new reality of the north. We already accept the challenge of regional leadership, but need increased recognition of what we are doing and the assistance that we require to do it properly. Cllr Richard Kemp Liverpool city council • Three city regions for Tyneside, Wearside and Teeside? But none of these conurbations has the population base to make regional government practical. This solution also leaves out "rural" Northumberland and County Durham, where many towns still face deprivation following the pit and steelworks closures. The real answer for the north-east and the other English regions is dynamic, coherent, directly elected government for the whole region, not a patchwork of half-baked city regions. Geraldine Ormonde Newcastle upon Tyne

title: Ireland put under plague siege
bodyText: Ireland's vital multi-billion pound farming industry breathed a collective sigh of relief last night after initial tests on a suspected foot and mouth outbreak in Co Louth proved negative. But this weekend the Irish Republic still resembled an enclosed medieval city fighting to keep out the plague, with border crossing points closed, the movement of people and animals in the countryside banned, masses at Catholic churches cancelled and even rural schools threatened with closure. Meanwhile it emerged that there is to be a crackdown on the way Britain's biggest supermarkets use their power to manipulate the food industry. This follows Tony Blair's comment that they had an 'armlock' on farmers. A legally enforceable code of conduct being drawn up by the Department of Trade and Industry and the Office of Fair Trading will demand that supermarkets have a fairer relationship with farmers, who complain they are bullied into providing food at very cheap prices by the large chains. Despite the good news from Co Louth, Ireland is not lowering its guard. It will take another 24 hours for veterinary scientists analysing tissue samples taken from animals at the farm close to the Northern Ireland border fully to verify a negative result. However, the Irish Agriculture Minister Joe Walsh said he was cautiously optimistic that the state would escape an outbreak of the disease. which would threaten an industry worth IR£11 billion. One economic forecaster, Jim Power of Friends First Asset Management, estimated that the crisis could knock 1.5 per cent off the Republic's national growth rate. There was however a further boost for Ireland's struggle to protect vital agriculture export markets when the United States Department of Agriculture lifted its threat to ban Irish imports into North America. France, though, has banned imports of live stock from Ireland . Ireland only consumes 15 per cent of its agricultural produce; the remaining 85 per cent is exported to 100 countries around the world. A spokesman for the Department of Agriculture in Dublin said the results of preliminary tests on a suspected foot and mouth outbreak have proved negative. The animals were on a farm in the exclusion zone, close to another farm in Northern Ireland where there had been a confirmed outbreak of the disease. The RUC is currently investigating allegations that the infected animals were brought to Ireland from England by South Armagh-based smugglers. In a desperate bid to seal the border, the Irish government called for more police officers to patrol on the northern side of the border. Michael Smith, the Republic's Defence Minister, appealed to the RUC to deploy officers on the South Armagh end of the frontier. But the RUC is thin on the ground in the region because of the ever-present threat of attack from dissident republican paramilitaries. Even heavily armed British troops only travel through the area via helicopter avoiding roads that might be booby-trapped or mined. Smith has already dispatched 4,000 Irish troops to the border in a move to shut down one of the most porous frontiers in the world, which is littered with hundreds of unapproved roads and crossing points. Many sectors of Irish economic and social life have simply shut down as the Republic closes the drawbridge in a bid to keep foot and mouth out. For the first time since the anti-Catholic penal laws of the seventeenth century, Catholic masses have been called off across the country. Thirteen churches in the Louth-South Armagh border zone have been asked not to celebrate Sunday mass. In a letter to priests, the leader of Ireland's four million Catholics, Archbishop Sean Brady said that where it was considered necessary to cancel services, parishioners were excused from Sunday mass and should not travel to neighbouring parishes. The Licensed Vintners Association, which represents Ireland's publicans, has told members to provide disinfectant for rural pubs. In Co Longford a district court judge urged local nightclubs to close for a week. Air travel between Ireland and Britain has also effectively shut down with Aer Lingus and British Airways cancelling many flights. Although Irish trainers and horses will not be travelling to this week's Cheltenham festival there was some good news for punters. Irish bookmakers will refund all bets on Cheltenham races. A spokesman said the gesture could cost the industry hundreds of thousands of pounds. Sporting fixtures have been heavily affected with no soccer, Gaelic games, horse racing, hockey and even Ireland's Six Nations rugby clash with Wales called off. St Patrick's Day parades north and south have also been cancelled including a four-day festival in Dublin. Meanwhile Northern Ireland's Agriculture Minister Brid Rogers announced yesterday that further tests on farms in the Province showed there were no further suspected cases of foot and mouth. She said that other than one confirmed case there are no other cases, which would have forced her officials to send samples to be tested. About 1,000 pigs have already been slaughtered as a precautionary measure on a South Armagh farm. Like the Republic, rural life in Northern Ireland has ground to a halt as a result of restrictions on the movements or animals and people. All sporting fixtures including soccer matches in Belfast city, which is unaffected by the disease, were called off yesterday. But the Province's deputy first minister Seamus Mallon criticised the Belfast Giants - the North's new ice hockey side - for going ahead this weekend with home games against Nottingham and Cardiff. Hundreds of Welsh ice hockey fans have come to Belfast even though the rugby international was cancelled to prevent Irish supporters from travelling to Cardiff. 'I say to the Giants it is not in the interests of the people of Northern Ireland,' Mallon said. In Britain supermarkets - including Asda, Safeway, Tesco and Sainsbury's - will face heavy fines if they fail to stick to the new rules. The Government revealed yesterday that seven more cases of foot and mouth had been found - from Cornwall to Dumfries and Galloway - bringing the total number of cases to 51. The chief veterinary officer, Jim Scudamore, said 45,000 cattle had now been slaughtered. Scudamore admitted that there were at least three more highly suspect cases . He could not say how long the crisis would last. title: Around the country - foot and mouth latest
bodyText: • The number of outbreaks rose to 48 yesterday, with new cases confirmed in Cornwall, Scotland, Durham, Cumbria, Lancashire and Oxfordshire. • Foot and mouth panic spread to mainland Europe, with Belgium getting the first suspected case of foot and mouth disease in pigs imported from Britain. Belgium banned all animals exports, outlawed the transport of farm animals around the country and cancelled all horse races. • Preliminary results from the first suspected case in Ireland proved negative but it still announced a complete halt to slaughtering and banned the movement of animals. Border crossings with Northern Ireland were closed, mass at Catholic churches was cancelled and rural schools were threatened with closure. France banned imports of livestock from Ireland. • The Government announced it was bringing forward payments of £156 million from the European Union to compensate farmers for foot and mouth disease. • Panic-buying spread in the UK, as Tesco supermarket reported sales of beef were up by 45 per cent, lamb by 65 per cent, pork by 35 per cent, chicken by 35 per cent and bacon by 41 per cent. Somerfield reported sales of pasta and rice were up by 87 per cent on the previous week. Frozen fish sales were up by 40 per cent. • Approximately 45,000 animals have now been slaughtered as a result of the disease, including 6,300 animals which may have come into contact with infected animals. • The Government announced an investigation into the implications of increased world travel, globalisation of trade and modern farming methods on issues of disease control. • Officials started preparing special licences to allow healthy animals to start being moved from disease-free areas to abattoirs on Tuesday. • Walkers abused a local council official putting up signs asking people to keep off footpaths in Basildon, Essex, because of foot and mouth. Police were called and the walkers were reminded local authorities had the power to prosecute. • Police took away the firearms of a distraught farmer 'heartbroken' by the loss of livestock from foot and mouth disease. Cattle and sheep farmer George Thomas called the emergency services asking for police and TV cameras to attend his farm.
