title: Recession, what recession? It's all in your mind
bodyText: When US television companies have trouble filling advertising slots during the Oscars ceremony, it's clear there's a problem in the economy. When the adverts that do run include a mini talk show where the head of stockbroking firm Charles Schwab seeks to soothe jumpy investors, it's clear the problem is becoming very serious. The investment banks are also getting involved. In full-page ads headlined 'The storm before the calm', Merrill Lynch tells Americans that 'our research analysts expect the market to rally this spring'. If bankers and brokers are trying to reinvigorate investor confidence through applied psychology, they are not alone. Alan Greenspan, the Federal Reserve Chairman, made his first stab at this inexact science when he cut the interest rate in between Fed meetings in January. Collectively, US investors and consumers are in the psychiatrist's chair, as financial 'shrinks' seek answers to a miasma of questions: To what extent do consumers feel 'poorer' having seen their share portfolios halve in value? Are things different in Europe? Have investors become more risk-averse in the wake of the burst of the tech bubble? Have they abandoned belief in the 'new economy'? Are British investors more sensitive to negative business sentiment from the US today than they were during the last US downturn? In financial forecasting, many assumptions are being made about the answers to these essentially emotional questions. Put another way, the divergent forecasts of the potential for recession in the US are explained by a mix of economics and assumptions about consumer and investor behaviour. The pessimism over the US situation is rooted in the feeling that consumers won't spend, won't invest, and will start to save, thereby translating a confidence-led slowdown into a genuine recession. It's down to the 'wealth effect' - the extent to which changes in asset prices affect spending behaviour. The comparative resilience of the UK economy can be put down to its lower sensitivity to share prices. Although the table below shows some adverse effect - and certainly more than in other European countries, the principal determinant of the 'wealth effect' in Britain is house prices, not share prices. Specific sectors, such as the City, will take a hit, but the overall effect of share falls is small compared with the US. 'Over $4 trillion has been wiped off people's wealth in the US,' John Llewellyn, global chief economist at Lehman Brothers, points out. If decreases in spending after a reduction in wealth mirror the spending boom on the way up, consumer spending will fall by 1.6 per cent of US GDP. But there's no simple equation. If, as is plausible, aversion to risk has grown since the Nasdaq slump, the effect could be amplified considerably as US consumers batten down the hatches and throw their money into savings accounts. US households' net worth suffered its first fall in a quarter of a century last year. Such a fall has happened on only three previous occasions in the past 50 years, and all have presaged a substantial increase in savings and the emergence of recession, according to HSBC research. Also giving grounds for pessimism is the effect of falling share prices on the willingness of businesses to take investment risks. Stephen King at HSBC, one of the first City economists to predict a US hard landing, sees a retrenchment in corporate and consumer spending behind an unstoppable drop to recession. 'The fall in the Nasdaq is a proxy cost for the availability of investment funds,' he says. The Nasdaq's soaring value, rather than low interest rates, was the source of an investment and jobs boom in the tech sector especially. So slashing interest rates may prove an ineffective way of bolstering business investment plans. 'Corporates will cut back significantly in the next few months. When layoffs pick up, already weak consumers will have to contend with increasing job insecurity.' Layoffs are on the rise: according to surveys, they have doubled to more than 100,000 a month since December. This is not the case in Europe. Although British markets are down, they haven't fallen as far as those in the US. The UK's 'misery index', which is unemployment plus inflation, stands at a 30-year low. Even if the US tumbles into recession, the UK is unlikely to follow, because most of its trade is with continental Europe - and it was never flying so high in the first place. The eurozone trades mainly with itself. The economic risks to this position come mainly through a global slowdown following escalating problems in both Asia and the US. Specific sectors will suffer. The Centre for Economics and Business Research predicts that 20,000 jobs will go in the City by the end of next year as bear markets restrict the flow of deals at investment banks. But another lurking fear about the UK economy brings us back to the psychiatrist's chair. 'In addition to trade linkages, you should not underestimate psychological connections,' says Llewellyn of Lehman Brothers. In 1998, financial difficulties in Thailand, a small player on the global stage in terms of trade linkages, eventually froze US bond markets for two weeks. The extent of the contagion is impossible to measure, but, for example, our sensitivity to US economic distress may have changed in recent years. A huge growth in media coverage of US business may have altered that relationship. But confidence depends on a lot more than economics. Is it coincidence that there is record consumer confidence in France when US fortunes suffer? There are analysts who still believe the increase in consumer confidence in France since 1998 was triggered by its football team's victory in the World Cup. The relationships between asset prices, the wealth effect, confidence, and spending are constantly evolving and cannot be assumed. As Llewellyn says: 'There are much wilder swings in consumer confidence now. History may give you a false sense of confidence.' We will need a lot more therapy before we can stop the recession nightmare.

title: Bankers who must save Europe
bodyText: George W Bush makes Ronald Reagan look a bit of a leftie. Bush's refusal to sign the Kyoto protocol on controlling atmospheric pollution has really shaken the rest of the world. But he has been working on his right-wing reputation for months. Not only are the President's proposed tax cuts blatantly aimed at alleviating the lot of the rich; the New York Times has been reporting that, for good measure, Bush has some proposals for vicious cuts in welfare and childcare provision for the poor. We now know that Bush's definition of compassionate conservatism is compassion for conservatives. It has been suggested to me that some of the Bush tax cut proposals are now seen as compensation for all those ambitious people whose new economy and dotcom stocks did not do so well. Whether they are Bush proposals or Richard Cheney proposals is, of course, an open question. It seems that, at least before his latest heart attack, vice-president Cheney was chairing all the key Cabinet meetings and the president was usually distinguished by his absence. The way the US seems to be going makes the European Union's emphasis on modernising its social model, rather than abandoning it, all the more sensible. The rapid erosion of the US economic 'miracle' also puts the debate about 'Anglo-Saxon capitalism versus the rest' into perspective (and it should never be forgotten that Saxony happens to be in Germany). Having thought they had abolished the business cycle, the more arrogant and dewy-eyed commentators on the US economy are seeing it return with a vengeance. It was to provide a safety net for the victims of business cycles that the European social model was developed in the first place. What the markets are gradually beginning to grasp is that the US economic miracle involved building up so many unsustainable imbalances - falling savings, a vast balance-of-payments deficit, a supposedly ever-rising stock market - that there had to be an adjustment. Whether this involves a long period of slow growth or an outright recession is anybody's guess. So much depends on confidence and people's reactions that at times like this economic forecasts are of limited value. Everybody is guessing. But what history seems to show is that the bigger the boom, the bigger the bust. While the US boom went on, its economy was the 'importer of last resort' and the locomotive for the rest of the world economy. Having recovered only up to a point from the Asian crisis of 1997, south-east Asian economies, heavily dependent on the US market, now face another very uncomfortable period. But week by week it has also become apparent that the European and UK economies are far from immune from the US slowdown. It is remarkable, for instance, that despite all the downward revisions to European economic forecasts and some very gloomy surveys of future plans, the European Central Bank has kept interest rates at the level ruling since the autumn. Last week the ECB's governing council was split, and interest rates remained unchanged, despite evidence that even some of the hawks thought inflation was no longer the main problem. The markets are now saying that a cut will probably come at the ECB meeting on 11 April. It was Harry S Truman (well before Harold Wilson) who said a week is a long time in politics. It remains to be seen how long a fortnight is in terms of ECB internal politics. The weakness of the ECB is all too apparent. It does not even vote on such a crucial matter; it tries to achieve a consensus in circumstances where some member countries are more concerned about the buoyancy of their economies (at the moment, that is) than about the recession that could be staring every European country in the face if the ECB does not act to keep growth going. The stagnation in the Japanese economy is a salutary reminder of how difficult it is to emerge from deflation, or falling prices. It would be unfortunate if the ECB overplayed its disinflationary hand at a time when the world economy is so finely balanced, and it is refreshing that two of the most formidable members of the ECB - Jean-Claude Trichet, governor of the Bank of France, and ECB chief economist Otmar Issing - seem to be aware of the danger. But so far it has to be said that the ECB's behaviour in the recent crisis has provided plenty of ammunition for its enemies, of whom I should like to think I am not one. In a plea of mitigation, it is said some ECB members were worried that, with US interest rates still higher than those in Europe, a cut now might weaken the euro further. But the euro fell anyway after last week's 'no change' announcement because the markets are increasingly concerned about the growth prospects for the euro zone. In the medium term it is difficult to see how the dollar can retain anything like its present levels given the size of the US trade deficit. But the US adjustment problem comes after years of above- average growth; growth in the eurozone has been so modest for so long that the good news is there are precious few imbalances to worry about. Whereas the US has to adjust, which means slow growth, possible recession, and a lower dollar, the euro zone has scope for reasonably robust growth; all it needs is a little encouragement from economic policy. The ECB has a huge weight of responsibility resting on its shoulders.

title: Blair should be more enterprising
bodyText: Tony Blair's opinion poll lead over William Hague is so commanding it probably makes no difference when the election is held. But delaying it to June does involve economic risks and upsets the carefully calibrated timing designed to maximise the feelgood factor for early May. Last month's Budget was supposed to be the final piece of the jigsaw, but memories of the chancellor's package have already faded as the media's agenda has turned to foot and mouth. Unemployment will go back up, if only temporarily, this spring, as a result of the loss of jobs in the tourist industry, and economic growth will slow. The one crumb of economic comfort for a government with one wary eye on the febrile global stock markets is that the Bank of England may cut interest rates this week. It should. Despite the desperate attempts by the opposition to call this Blair's spring of discontent, Labour's biggest problem will be getting its supporters out to vote on election day. It is not that voters believe the government has screwed up, rather that having Labour in power with a 179-seat majority has made precious little difference. Britain now is remarkably similar to Britain on May 1, 1997. Perhaps the single most depressing statistic is that spending on the nation's infrastructure has been lower in each of Labour's four years than in the final 12 months of John Major's administration and only 25% of what it was in the dying months of Jim Callaghan's luckless government. To be spending less than 0.5% of the economy's annual output on restoring the tattered remnants of the public realm is scandalous. Gordon Brown blames spending departments for this, but the chancellor cannot shuffle off responsibility so easily. He holds the purse strings. He imposed the cuts in Labour's first two years. The buck stops with him. Labour's response to the charge that it has been a do-nothing government is two-fold. First, it points to some solid achievements - the minimum wage, extra money for low-income earners through a host of tax credits, help to some pensioners, extra billions for health and education - as evidence of work in progress. There are good things delivered by Blair that would not have been delivered by Hague. Any Labour supporters toying with the idea that there is no difference between left and right in the modern world should have been speedily disabused last week when George Bush decided that America's right to go on polluting the planet took precedence over the Kyoto agreement to cut CO-2 emissions. In retrospect, voting for Ralph Nader in Florida was not such a good idea after all. The second strand of the argument is that Labour's first term was all about showing competence and that things will get a lot better in a second term, honest. Only then will we see a full flowering of the government's progressive vision. Margaret Thatcher's radical phase was her second term between 1983 and 1987, which saw her take on and defeat the miners, sell off chunks of state-owned industry, and abolish the GLC and the metropolitan councils. Before that she had enough on her plate bringing down inflation from 20%, coping with mass unemployment and fighting the Falklands war, without heeding those in her government and in the free-market think-tanks peddling the ideas that would lay the foundations for the second term revolution. With Labour a demoralised rump of a party in 1983, she had the ideas and the opportunity to get radical. It is possible that Blair has merely been biding his time, storing up all his radical ideas for a second term. Did you see a pink, four-legged creature with wings and trotters float past your window? The reality is that politics now is more like the 1950s than the 1980s. In the 50s, the Conservatives were in power but did not do very much. They took over from Labour in 1951 at a time when the economic climate turned benign, when inflationary pressure was minimal and world demand strong. They accepted the status quo inherited from the Attlee government - that large chunks of the economy should be in state hands, that full employment should be the centrepiece of economic policy, that capital should shoulder some of the responsibility for building up the welfare state - and stayed in charge for 13 years, making only incremental changes. In some respects, the Conservatives did some of the things that Labour had never quite got round to doing; the mass expansion of council housing, for example, took place only after 1951. There were regular periods of breast-beating when it became clear that Britain, despite the favourable economic background, was performing far less well than Germany or France, but for the most part it was a time when the Conservative party thought that the point of politics was for it to stay in power rather than to change things. Like the Conservatives, this Labour government took over when the economic climate was set fair. For the first time in decades it is possible to run the economy at about 3% growth without stoking up inflation. Full employment, if not yet a reality, is a definite possibility. Of the four big macroeconomic indicators, only the balance of payments, the Achilles' heel of the British economy, gives cause for concern. Moreover, Labour accepts the traditional consensus. It believes low inflation is the prime objective of economic policy, that the balance of power in the workplace is just about right, that the industries privatised by the Conservatives should remain privatised, with a couple of new ones thrown in for good measure, it idolises business (the bigger the better). It seems to have no trouble, either, with 50s-style social authoritarianism. This is not a government that, even were it to be in power for 50 years, looks interested in rocking the boat. It just wants to change course by a couple of degrees. Such an approach may be justified. It may be that much of what happened in the final quarter of the 20th century was necessary, right and irreversible. It may be that capitalism has now reached a point of near perfection so tinkering with the machine is all that is necessary. Labour's pollsters would argue that anything more radical would risk alienating the contented masses of Middle England, driving them back into the arms of the Conservatives. But there is a different way of looking at things. Despite the good macro-economic numbers, Labour is so cocooned in its micro-management of the country that it fails to see the bigger picture. The global economy spends its time veering from one crisis to another, with the bursting of the hi-tech bubble only the latest in a series of disturbances. As those people in southern England who have spent the past 12 months on flood alert could testify, something weird is happening to the world's weather. When people die in train crashes because the private sector has skimped on safety, there are legitimate questions to ask about ownership. But there's the rub. Where Thatcher had both the opportunity and the ideas, Labour just has the opportunity. Yet the challenges are real and urgent. If the democratic left is not willing to provide a lead, history suggests there will be plenty of others with crackpot panaceas who will. George Bush for one. larry.elliott@theguardian.com

title: Animals for slaughter about to top 1m
bodyText: The foot and mouth epidemic continues unabated with new outbreaks in 13 counties - some where the Ministry of Agriculture had hoped the disease was isolated and on the wane. The total number of animals to be slaughtered was expected to pass 1m today. Evidence that the government is losing the battle came in the numbers of animals condemned but still waiting to be slaughtered. The toll had risen to 345,000 yesterday morning - an increase of 20,000 a day since Thursday. Cumbria was again the worst blackspot on Sunday with 23 new cases and another 11 notified by lunchtime yesterday, making a total for the county of more than 380, out of a national total of 933, defeating all the combined attempts of the army and the Ministry of Agriculture to keep pace. There now seems no immediate prospect of implementing the policy of slaughtering all animals on farms within 24 hours of the disease being discovered, as the prime minister promised last week when he personally took control of the crisis. Just as disturbing for the government were the new cases in the north-east. There were four in Northumberland on Sunday, two in Durham and two in Tyne and Wear. The latter were the first in the county since March 22, ending hopes that the disease was being confined. At the other end of the country there were another seven cases in Devon and one in Cornwall, the first for four weeks. Cheshire, Staffordshire, Herefordshire, Gloucestershire, Monmouthshire, Powys and Dumfries and Galloway all had new outbreaks. The Ministry of Agriculture kept hidden over the weekend a record number of 64 new cases on Friday. There were another 20 cases on Saturday and 54 yesterday. Elsewhere, there were no new cases in France or Ireland, but Holland seemed to be struggling to contain the disease with the number of outbreaks reaching 12. Estimates of the cost in Holland have risen to £50m as the Dutch slaughter all animals within two miles of outbreaks. They have also reportedly begun ring vaccination. In Cumbria the government is still undecided on vaccination. Nick Brown, the agriculture minister, was in the county yesterday apparently trying to convince dairy farmers that vaccination should be implemented. But they were worried their milk might be unsaleable. Mr Brown said: "I have not come here to make any announcement. The issue is still under review. The arguments are not all one way. There are a range of strategies under consideration." The European Commission has advised Britain also to consider vaccinating zoo animals if there is a risk of rare animals catching the disease. The government yesterday gave the army the go ahead to bury 25,000 cattle at the disused Great Orton airfield. More than 40 trucks laden with about 2,500 carcasses had been held up at the airfield because of Department of Health fears that burying cattle could spread mad cow disease. But Mr Brown said that as long as the animals were born after August 1996 there was no danger of spreading BSE. "We are now clearing the backlog," said Lt Col Andrew Taverner, the army's spokesman. "But we will still be burning and rendering cattle as well as burying animals under five years old. "We are anxious not to over fill the Great Orton site and so use up all our sheep space. But we now feel we have the matter in hand." By yesterday 3,657 farmers had asked for 1,354,000 animals to be slaughtered because of restrictions on animal movements. Nearly 1m of these were sheep and 312,000 were pigs. The government is prepared to compensate for 90% of the value of the animals under the so-called welfare slaughter scheme. A spokeswoman for the intervention board in Newcastle said 31,811 animals had been slaughtered and 19 abattoirs were dedicated to the scheme. As soon as they were all operating they would have a capacity to kill 280,000 animals a week. The carcasses would be taken to six landfill sites and one rendering plant for disposal. Applications to the welfare scheme were still being received. The chaos in Cumbria mounted yesterday when a pyre for burning cattle at a farm was scrapped after complaints that it was too close to houses in Carlisle. It was built last week to burn 120 cattle at Cummersdale Grange Farm, plus another 250 from a farm near Rose Castle. The decision came after the intervention of Eric Martle, Labour MP for Carlisle. The animals will now be taken to a rendering plant instead and the pyre dismantled. title: ECB dashes hope of rate cut
bodyText: A defiant European Central Bank yesterday ended hopes that a weekend of arm twisting by US and Japanese policymakers had softened its determination to keep interest rates on hold. Jean-Claude Trichet, governor of the Bank of France and a member of the ECB's governing council, insisted that the bank had to remain on guard against the dangers of inflation. "There is a slowing down of external demand triggered by the slowing down of the US economy but continued vigilance is appropriate due in particular to the evolution of wages and salaries," he said. Inflation in the eurozone is currently running at 2.6% - well above the ECB's 2% ceiling - and the bank is worried that this will trigger high pay demands. Its uncompromising stance was reinforced yesterday by a sharp rise in the money supply in the eurozone - data which the bank sees as a good guide to the future path of inflation. M3, the broad measure of money supply, accelerated to 5% in March from 4.7% the previous month. Analysts blamed investors fleeing tumbling equity markets for the haven of bank accounts for the jump. The money supply numbers had been taken into account when the ECB met last week but Bundesbank president Ernst Welteke noted: "This underlines that we in the ECB were on the right track when we decided not to reduce interest rates too early. The current eurozone inflation data and the M3 numbers confirm the ECB's 'on-hold' policy [on interest rates]." Responding to calls from the IMF for lower euroland rates Welteke commented: "This is one voice that we hear but it is good that we are independent and we have decided on our own assessments." In London the FTSE 100 share index was up 15.5 at 5996.9, while the Frankfurt and Paris stock markets were each up by more than 1%.

title: Confusion reigns as police brace for mayhem
bodyText: The largest force of police ever assembled in London will today confront 10,000 people. Or perhaps 2,000. There may be 1,000 people intent on damaging property, or there may be 500. Or 200. Or 5,000. The police intelligence is at a loss to predict clearly what will happen, just as the protesters themselves do not have a big picture. Modern protest is no longer about people making great speeches and leading orderly marches behind banners; increasingly it is a form of free expression, with disinformation rife by both "sides". With more than 30 separate protests across London against everything from capitalism to the banning of pigeon feeding in Trafalgar Square, the police have a near-impossible task. They must justify why they will be adopting draconian no-tolerance policies, while at the same time they must not be accused of curtailing civil liberties. The line is fine, but to cross it with images of over the top policing would be disastrous for New Labour. The protesters were worried their message of solidarity with others around the world, anger at world debt, or disappointment with global governance might not get through either. No matter that 9,000 people are peaceful, if 1,000 run riot in Oxford Street or in the City the media will focus on the violence. Any appeal the majority would make to the public about the justness of their cause would be lost. Last night there were conflicting messages coming from both sides, with some protesters sensing more people would turn out on the principle that no one should be deterred by a massive police presence, and the police and Ken Livingstone, mayor of London, suggesting the authorities had succeeded in keeping many people away. An opinion poll yesterday indicated people agreed strongly with one of the protesters' main complaints - that corporations influence life more than government does. An NOP poll for C4's Powerhouse programme had 76% agreeing "big international companies usually care only about profits, and not the interests of the people in the countries where they operate". Only 19% disagreed. Of 1,000 people asked whether they agreed or disagreed that "big international companies have more influence on daily life in Britain than our own government", 67% agreed while 25% disagreed. On the other hand, only 25% disagreed with the suggestion that international companies had helped to increase prosperity in Britain by building factories and creating jobs. The protests in London are expected to be mirrored across the world, with many countries preparing for the traditional workers' day with massive street demonstrations against globalisation, unemployment and corruption. In Berlin the government has banned anarchist and other marches in an attempt to prevent violent clashes. But a court decision allowing a march by the neo-nazi National Democratic party threatens to heighten tension. In France, where global free trade is widely blamed for cultural and economic problems, thousands of people are expected to demonstrate against recent job losses at international firms, including Marks & Spencer. In Australia a predicted 10,000 activists will blockade stock exchanges and other financial institutions in the big cities. Spanish trade unions have organised 61 demonstrations to denounce precarious working conditions and labour market reforms by the conservative government, which they say favour companies which want to make redundancies cheaper. Protests over unemployment, discrimination and poverty are also expected in Greece, Poland, Guatemala and Taiwan, where some 20,000 people are expected to take to the streets. The Japanese, for whom May 1 is a normal working day, brought forward their protest to last weekend when 450,000 people turned out to demand wage rises. Peter Mandelson, MP for Hartlepool, and Arthur Scargill, the National Union of Mineworkers leader who is challenging him in the next election, clashed yesterday over the protests . Mr Scargill backed the anti-capitalists but Mr Mandelson responded that he was an "an extremist prepared to give more support to a bunch of violent anarchists than a democratically elected Labour government".

title: Germany wants EC to be a government
bodyText: German leaders of both left and right made it clear yesterday that they were backing an all-out push for the political integration of Europe by the end of the decade. The text of a document drawn up under the guidance of the chancellor, Gerhard Schröder, showed that he was aiming for the key decisions on the continent's future to be taken within the next 10 years. It also showed that Mr Schröder and his fellow Social Democrats see it as essential to harmonise taxes in the European Union: a policy which is anathema to both the main parties in Britain and to governments in some other EU states. The document, which was leaked at the weekend, was unveiled at a press conference in Berlin by the general secretary of Mr Schröder's Social Democratic party, Franz Münterfering. "The debate must begin urgently, even if everyone does not agree on everything," he said. He and other senior Social Democrats will get their first opportunity to lobby for their ideas at a meeting of the pan-European Party of European Socialists in Berlin on Monday. Mr Münterfering presented the scheme as an attempt to democratise the workings of the EU, and the German opposition seized on this aspect to welcome it. "We have a democratic deficit in the EU and that must change," the leader of the Christian Democrat Union (CDU), Angela Merkel, said . Mr Schröder expressed delight at the reaction of the opposition conservatives, describing it as "great". At the heart of his plan is a transformation of the European commission, the union's unelected executive. "We want the EU commission from now [on] to be strengthened to become a government - a real European government beside the national governments," the SPD's chief spokesman, Michael Donnermeyer, said. Many of the details of the plan, which is to be put before the SPD's conference in November, have yet to be defined. In essence, senior SPD sources said yesterday, the chancellor would like a set-up similar to that in Germany with the considerable powers of the states serving as a model for the future powers of nation states. But it remains unclear, for example, how the proposed European government would be chosen, and whether its leader would be elected by one or both of the chambers in a new, two-tier legislature. While the document contains several nods in the direction of preserving national identities, it is emphatic that the SPD wants a pan-continental structure to emerge from talks on the future of Europe due to begin in 2004. "There is no alternative to further integration and Europeanisation", the paper declares. Its key passage is set out in the language of cast-iron inevitability: "In 10 years we shall live in a Europe with a constitution. In 10 years, we shall live in a Europe with a [single] currency". The document argues that "an internal market and a common currency demand more energetic harmonisation of fiscal policy, especially with regard to taxes on business, the taxation of capital gains, the taxation of investment in energy and the organisation of value added and corporation tax". If that is enough to provoke apoplexy among some British MPs, then there are plenty of other passages able to incite an angry reaction elsewhere in the community. A proposal that EU spending be controlled by parliament will be seen in France as a bid to slash spending under the Common Agricultural Policy. Likewise, the document proposes devolving structural funding to national governments - a move that would land Spain, Portugal and Greece with the bill for modernising their economies. The chancellor's blueprint represents the latest, and by far the most decisive, attempt by Germany to set a new European agenda. Mr Schröder, his aides and ministers are convinced that an enlarged European Union will prove unmanageable unless it is rapidly integrated politically as well as economically.

title: Did Things Get Better?
bodyText: A Guardian/ICM poll taken in February 2001 showed that Labour in power had one remarkable achievement under its belt. It had educated the public to think it was as fiscally continent as the Tories. In February 1997 49% of those polled believed that once Labour came to power it would spend more than the Tories. Four years later that figure was 24%. Indeed, more people believed the Tories would spend more if they achieved office. This turnaround stemmed from Labour's key promise in the run-up to the 1997 election: to stick to Tory spending plans for the first two years. Perhaps it was "existentially" necessary to sunder Labour from its past. But from it grew an enervating sense of disappointment in Labour ranks and among the public at large. Where, people asked, were the better schools and surgeries they thought Labour would build - though Labour's formal promises in 1997 promised nothing of the kind. Besides, only a third of the public actually cast their ballots for Tony Blair in 1997, which might imply that most people had willed either the status quo or minimal change. None the less the buzz had been that Labour would be better for public services and, though that has eventually been proved to be true, the differences after four years are not as dramatic as expected. They couldn't be when, in aggregate, the UK state as a proportion of GDP declined. In John Major's last year the ratio of government spending to GDP was 41.2. It will still be less than that (40.6%) when Gordon Brown's extensive spending plans are realised by March 2004. The economy, of course, is growing so the physical resources available for public services will expand. Still, Labour's Third Way has not been a bonanza for public services. Blairism offered no fundamental recalibration of the importance of government in our society or economy. The first Blair government has been busy. Its large majority tramped through the lobbies passing a raft of laws. A book could be written enumerating the policy initiatives and plans, made and accomplished. (We wrote it) An audit of the Blair term might note, first, that there are no signs of improvement on what Labour ministers themselves identified as the battleground of modern governance: productivity. On it depends the capacity of the economy to break through the limits that have kept annual growth below 3%. Labour no longer believes the state should own significant economic assets nor plan in any traditional sense. Its main job is making markets more competitive, pulling down barriers to trade and improving the quality of the factors of production. But government can aid science and research, in so doing improving capital, and above all it can educate and train. Labour would say that it has. The Blair cabinet can notch up improved educational attainments in the primary sector and continuing improvements at exams for 16- and 18-year-olds. The expansion of higher education has continued, unimpeded by Labour's introduction of university tuition charges and the abolition of student maintenance grants. A start has been made on reshaping vocational education. But British productivity rates remain below those in both the United States and the allegedly bloated welfare states of France and Germany. Fearful of saying anything remotely critical about business or the British system of free enterprise, Labour ministers were never heard to speculate about one possible reason - deformations in the business culture and corporate sector. Instead the chancellor, Gordon Brown, led a chorus extolling enterprise, blaming culture for being insufficiently attuned to economic opportunity, but never addressing the structure of economic power as a possible cause of under-achievement. But if Labour did not affect such fundamentals as productivity, it did manage to accomplish some traditional goals of socialism, including making Britain a fairer place. Changes were small in scale but noteworthy. The combined impact of Labour's changes in tax and social security increased the income of the bottom 10th of households by some 8%, over £8 a week, and it cut the income of the richest 10th by 0.5%, or £4.74 a week. Economic growth (2.6% on average since 1997) may have pulled the income distribution at large into an even more unequal shape. Labour's measures hindered rather than helped that outcome. A ledger of achievements is hard to compile because Labour policies often contradicted each other. For example, in fulfilment of a 1997 manifesto promise, Gordon Brown cut VAT on domestic fuel to 5%: in other words, he encouraged the consumption of fossil fuels. His levy on climate change, due to be introduced in April 2001, is meant to do the opposite. It was bitterly resisted by industrialists, though it will be critical in cutting emissions of greenhouse gases in line with promises made at the Kyoto summit. Devolution of power to the new parliament in Edinburgh and, to a lesser extent, the Cardiff assembly was not accompanied by reform at Westminster. The creation of a London mayor and assembly was not matched by a coherent reappraisal of Whitehall's relationship with elective local government in England. A "liberal" approach to matters of sexual orientation led Labour to equalise the age of consent and seek the abolition of Section 28 (accomplished by the Scottish parliament but not at Westminster, after the conservative majority in the House of Lords mobilised). A "progressive" approach to justice for young people was accompanied by a punitive policy for adult offenders leading to a 6,000-person increase in the prison population from 1997 onwards, even though no greater number of criminals was being caught. A muscular determination to uphold human rights in the Balkans did not prevent Labour pursuing commercial opportunities in China, despite the regime's record. The reconfiguration of the UK's defence after a widely praised review still left room for procurement decisions that had more to do with trade and constituency interest than military posture. The social account is especially full, in principle as much as in practice. The various New Deals promised that nobody was going to be left out of the labour market. The Sure Start programme was introduced for poor young children; the incomes of poor families with children were greatly augmented; and money was found for pensioners, not across the board but concentrated on the poorest. Schools, especially primaries, have been given new focus and teachers are returning to the public's esteem. After April 2001, teachers' salaries will reflect that rising esteem. At work Labour established the minimum wage and for the first time gave workers a formal right to belong to a trade union. Of course all judgements must be interim. The great spending increase announced in July 2000 only comes on stream in April 2001. Tony Blair liked to point out how little Margaret Thatcher accomplished in her first term - she had barely thought of privatisation. But if Thatcherism was invented on the run, Labour has shown a dismaying unwillingness to rethink the dogmas it acquired in opposition: what else explains Gordon Brown's insistence on privatisation schemes for air traffic control and the London Underground? The watchword of a second Labour terms seems to be "delivery". But Blairite ministers did not show much interest in the nuts and bolts of public administration, especially the role of elected local authorities in providing "national" services. Expectations have been built up, despite the comparatively modest increment in public spending to meet them. This raises a wider question about government. The Blair government did little or nothing to counter the trend towards a lack of confidence in public officials. Early scandals increased the public's cynicism. The shady dealings of Ecclestone, Robinson, Mandelson et al, soured the atmosphere further. It quickly felt like business as usual. Indifference and suspicion spread through bus queues, wine bars and pubs and were picked up with growing alarm by Labour in its own focus groups. John Dunn, the Cambridge political theorist, speculated that Western politics in this post-socialist, post cold war age was going to be "irritable, reactive and myopic: endlessly saturated with ressentiment." Is there a better way of describing public feeling during the September 2000 fuel protests? The proportion of people expressing attachment to a political party continued to fall. Voters may feel that representative institutions themselves are weakening and little that the Blair government has done, devolution included, seems likely to arrest the trend. Perhaps the government's economic success is a contributory factor. We are losing confidence in political leaders because we no longer think we need them, in the economic realm above all. The view may rest on a mistaken short term idea that macro economic problems have been "solved". Formerly the left wanted the state to intervene and steer the economic ship. The right resented government, but neither side denied its capacity or importance. Now in the age of the Third Way, that has been replaced by indifference. • David Walker is editor of the Guardian's analysis page and joint author of Did Things Get Better? - An Audit of Labour's Successes and Failures (Penguin £6.99).

title: Where le fairplay still rules
bodyText: When Motorola ignored ministerial pleas and announced the closure of its Bathgate plant last month the government expressed disappointment and politely asked for its regional development aid back. In France when Marks & Spencer and food group Danone announced closures and mass lay-offs, workers took to the streets and the government reached for the statute book. The reaction from the government and workers speaks volumes about France's very French approach to capitalism. True, the news from M&S and Danone was followed by a warning from Dim, the underwear maker, that "major restructuring" is on the way; Moulinex, the household appliance manufacturer, has decided to scrap 1,500 jobs in France; Alcatel, the electronics firm, is to sell one of its French mobile phone plants; and 2,700 staff at two domestic airlines have been told their jobs will go by June. The global economic about-turns and shifting consumer behaviour patterns that explain this rash of large-scale redundancies matter little in France. What counts is the corporate response - and except in cases of dire, bankruptcy-looming necessity, selling off, shutting down, cutting back or pulling out are just not acceptable courses of action. That is why Marks & Spencer's decision was described as "brutal", "unacceptable", "inhumane" and "scandalous", not just by furious French trade unions but by an equally furious Socialist prime minister, Lionel Jospin, in what looked at first suspiciously like a bout of Brit-bashing. In a front-page rant, Le Monde Diplomatique asked whether one could expect any better of a country that had for more than 20 years been "a laboratory for ultra-liberal market economics" where "perverted minds" had favoured profit over sensible state regulation. Overwhelming revulsion A fierce Gallic rejection of what are known here as "Anglo-Saxon business practices" in part explains the superstar status accorded to figures like José Bové, the pipe-smoking peasant activist who won fame by trashing a McDonald's restaurant. Now that native French companies are up to the the same sort of thing, it is clear there is more to it. Danone's plan, inspired not by present financial woes but by a desire to shore up future profits, was described as "completely unjustifiable" by 85% of respondents in a recent survey. So why such overwhelming revulsion at a straightforward, if tough, business decision? One answer is that France, which despite the influence of an omnipresent state has always been reluctant to place too much power in any one person's hands, never had its equivalent of the Thatcher revolution with its diet of deregulation and privatisation. In France the great mass of public opinion believes companies have a real social responsibility. "Stock-market layoffs", designed to please shareholders, are unacceptable, and employees, as a trade unionist said, "cannot be treated like pawns to be sacrificed on a chess board". What the French do want and expect from their employers is a virtue they have, paradoxically, imported from the English: le fairplay. Most also believe it is up to the government to ensure businesses do play fair. According to Stéphane Rozès of the CSA polling organisation, in a period of economic growth "the very idea of redundancies is insupportable. People are not stupid - when times were bad, they were content to ask the government to limit the damage. Now they demand regulation." Regulation is what they got. Seizing on the perfect opportunity to make up lost ground after a poor showing in last month's local elections, Mr Jospin's government announced a package of measures to deter companies from mass firings. These included doubling the minimum redundancy pay, forcing companies to offer staff six months of retraining to help them to find other work, and fining those that fail to help develop replacement activities on sites they shut down. Employees are also to be given more say in restructuring plans, and allowed to hire management consultants to come up with alternatives. It was a revival of the kind of orthodox French statist intervention that seems a million miles from Tony Blair's response to Motorola's Bathgate closure. In traditionally dirigiste France it plays well, and for some it did not go far enough. Mr Jospin's Communist coalition partners demanded that all layoffs by profit-making companies be made illegal. France's highly regulated labour market and the hefty social costs of employing workers may be decried by the 200,000 or so French people who have moved to London in recent years to find work, and by the hundreds of young French entrepreneurs who have set up shop in Britain or America where job laws are more flexible, meaning it is easier and cheaper to hire and fire. They do not, at the moment, appear to be doing a great deal of harm to France's economy, which is faring just as well as Britain's and, according to the International Monetary Fund, will grow at 2.6% this year and next - leaving a slowing US economy in its slipstream. Last year the French economy generated 500,000 new jobs. This year, accord ing to Stéphane Déo at investment bank UBS Warburg in Paris the total is likely to be 300,000. Then there is the 35-hour week. The Jospin administration's job-creating brainchild was greeted with hoots of derision when it was announced in 1997. It is now in place for companies employing more than 20 people, and to the surprise of many observers has won over most of its opponents. Underlying reality Surveys have shown 80% of the 5m French men and women benefiting from three or four weeks' extra holiday a year felt the shift to 35 hours had been "positive or very positive" for them personally. Of company directors, 60% believe the law has helped to increase productivity through renewed dialogue with the workforce, reforms to outdated and inflexible working practices, and increased morale. Proof that the Anglo-Saxon camp is wrong? Not quite. The underlying reality is that France has introduced a number of significant economic reforms. Large sections of the economy have been privatised. Little of the banking industry remains in the state sector. Much of the defence industry has been privatised and merged with German and Spanish partners. National symbols such as France Télécom and Air France have been sold to investors. Jospin's Socialist government has been no less willing than some of its rightwing predecessors to roll back the frontiers of the state. Despite France's reputation for strikes, the economy has benefited from wage restraint for much of the 1990s. Many of France's leading companies, like the vast new media conglomerate Vivendi which owns Universal studios, are lean, mean business machines very much on the Anglo-Saxon model. For all the changes France has not embraced the the symbol of the liberal market; shareholder value. The country clings to the notion that employers owe rather more to their employees than the equivalent of a P45 in exchange for a few pence on the share price.

title: Easy appeal of going below the line
bodyText: Michael Wolff, the less famous of the eponymous designers behind Wolff Olins, yesterday admitted to feeling a little sad at the agency's sale to US company Omnicom. As a part of the empire it will sit alongside another 100 or so design, sales promotion, junk mail and PR agencies in a delightfully named division, Diversified Agency Services Group. Mr Wolff had no shares left in Wolff Olins. He quit the agency in 1983 and left Wally Olins to go on and become the celebrity designer. But it was a shame, he noted, that the world is getting so "blobby". The sale of Wolff Olins marks the end of an era. During the design-led 1980s, British design and marketing agencies led the world but have, in large part, failed to capitalise on it. Virtually all of the agencies which made their names during the decade are now owned by the likes of Omnicom and True North from the US or Publicis from France. As far as the big guns are concerned the appeal of the so-called below the line networks is easy to understand. Junk mail or database marketing or even designing a packet of frozen peas ain't glamorous but they are growing faster than TV advertising and the margins are higher. There are also clear synergies to be had. Globalisation means that clients are increasingly looking for marketing services agencies with footprints in every market and the ability to offer a full range of services from design to advertising to PR. Accepting the shilling for the likes of Wolff Olins or Finsbury, the financial PR which sold to WPP last week, must be very tempting. At least with WPP and the unlikely heroic figure of Sir Martin Sorrell, Britain still has a role to play on the global stage. Euro scepticism The European Central Bank insists on keeping the minutes of its meetings secret for 30 years. Presumably that is because they are market sensitive: they might affect the value of the euro. (Although one wonders whether the number of people who will dash to read them in the 2030s quite justifies the space they must take up in the bank's archives.) Yet this coyness disappears the moment the ECB's members are not safely ensconced in Euro Tower. One after an other - but especially the loquacious Mr Duisenberg - they line up to refine and redefine the bank's policy. Every bewildering nuance of every statement - and there are many - sends the euro bouncing around. Yesterday's denials that there will be intervention were enough to send it towards six-month lows against both the pound and the dollar, and a five-month low against the yen. Apparently, we discovered, the strength of the euro does not particularly interest Mr Duisenberg. "It only becomes important if it would no longer support our inflation target in a serious way," he said. "And that's not the case." Surely the link between exchange rates and inflation in an open economy cannot have passed Mr Duisenberg by? In which case he is presumably just saying whatever he thinks will hold the markets firm. But the problem with that approach is that it eats away at the bank's credibility. And credibility matters in markets: currencies are held up as much by confidence as fundamentals, as the east Asian crises of the late 1990s showed. The fact is, there are only two sensible strategies for a man whose words move the markets. Best of all, say nothing. But if you must say something, learn Greenspan and make sure that it is completely incomprehensible anyway. Burning up Who wants to burn another £50m? No need to phone a friend on this one, Chris. It's Eidos, the firm that keeps topping the charts with games such as the one based on your TV show, but never seems to hang on to the profits for very long. So why does it need the money? Well the last £50m went on an internet retailer and then they had to clear the warehouse of some dusty old stock, and, er, one or two of the previous management had a bit of a Concorde habit and a liking for the high life. Will the new money be spent more wisely? Well let's hope so Chris. The new man, Michael McGarvey is making all the right noises and cutting the right costs. And he knows there is a huge credibility mountain to climb. But Eidos is still short of a finance director, while on the gaming market side so many factors (such as the release dates of new consoles) remain outside of its control. Any other misgivings? Well yes, one big one Chris. It's the chairman, Ian Livingstone. Unlike the rest of the board, he will not be pumping any new money into rights issue and instead will simply sell enough nil paid rights to take up the balance. Given the millions Mr Livingstone has taken out of the business over the years, he should cough up now when the company needs it.

title: ECB chief tips euro over the edge
bodyText: Europe's embattled single currency fell to six-months lows yesterday after comments from senior figures at the European Central Bank suggested policymakers have shelved any ideas of intervening to support it. The wave of selling began after Wim Duisenberg, president of the ECB, dismissed worries that the euro's decline was pushing up eurozone inflation, contradicting the line taken by his chief economist last week. ECB council member Klaus Liebscher also appeared to play down the threat of intervention, saying conditions were "completely different" from last autumn when the bank last turned its financial firepower on to the markets. "The ECB has committed its usual faux pas and, rather than just shoot itself in the foot, it has blown its leg off from below the knee," said David Brown, chief European economist at Bear Stearns. "It had been waging a reasonably successful war of words, using the psychology of threatened intervention to keep the market on tenterhooks and the euro reasonably well supported. But the latest unguarded words have seen all this crumble away." Dealers dumped the currency, sending the euro nearly a cent lower to 84.50 at one point, its lowest level since November 27 last year. It recovered slightly to close in London at 84.67. Strong British economic data helped push the euro down against the pound, to a six-month low of 59.60p. Asked to comment on the recent decline in the euro, Mr Duisenberg told a conference: "It [the exchange rate] only becomes important if it would no longer support our inflation target in a serious way - and that's not the case." The markets interpreted his remarks as a clear signal that it was safe to sell the euro without being caught short by official intervention. "The ECB is playing a dangerous game and giving information which, as far as the forex market is concerned, is like a red rag to a bull," said Jeremy Hawkins, chief economic adviser at Bank of America. The dollar has gained 5% against the single currency since the latest bout of euro weakness began in late April. The greenback's strength is piling further pressure on US companies already struggling with the American downturn. Sun Microsystems this week said a 10% fall in its earnings was due to weakness in European markets. The number of Americans staying on the jobless roll has risen to a seven-and-a-half year high, according to figures released yesterday and, a report today is expected to reveal US manufacturers cutting output and jobs at a faster rate. Some 2.85m people continued to collect unemployment benefits last week, according to the labour department, the largest total since November 1993 and an increase from 2.76m in the previous week. Despite ongoing weakness in the US economy, the dollar has remained strong, suggesting investors prefer its long-term prospects to those of the eurozone. Mr Duisenberg said he expected European growth to be robust this year and next. "We think that economic growth this year and next will be at the highest potential output rate that we estimate. I cannot make any predictions as to what our monetary policy stance would be were these circumstances to change." However, analysts think the bank may be forced to downgrade its forecasts in the wake of faltering growth in the eurozone's two biggest economies, France and Germany.

title: Style:  Pregnancy and the high street
bodyText: During the early months of pregnancy, I felt my old flirty, dappy, argumentative self slip-sliding away, destined, I thought gloomily, to be replaced by a sober, responsible maternal version. Bad enough: then I went shopping. Why are most maternity clothes so horrible? At the very moment you are anxiously watching your old identity disappear, you also have to suffer the indignity of being dressed as a knocked-up Maggie Thatcher. Maternity departments are mostly filled with polyester or nylon sacks in sludgy beige or brushed navy; the bras are made of gleaming synthetics, the shirts make you look like Miriam Stoppard and the trousers have outsize panels at the front that remind you of a kangaroo's pouch. "The problem is that the market for maternity wear is relatively small," explains Barbara Pound, whose shop Bumpsadaisy has been providing for mums-to-be for 20 years. "Only a tiny percentage of women are pregnant at any point, and that's from age 16 to 40-odd, so maternity ranges have got to cover a huge breadth of women. When you realise that we are looking at businesswear, swimwear, eveningwear, underwear and casualwear, you begin to understand the problem." Although the high street revolution has made stylish clothes available to far more people over the past 15 years, designers have been slow to tap into the pregnancy zone. On a quest to solve the problem, my breakthrough came when I spotted a pregnant manager in Gap. Heidi Schnecke was about to go off on maternity leave and looked great in jeans and a grey T-shirt over a long-sleeved white top. She gave me two tips: Gap cotton lounge pants - "the most comfortable trousers you can imagine" - and Next maternity jeans. I bought the lounge pants, in black, on the spot. But it's not just about trousers. Many women expand all over during pregnancy: your bum, thighs and upper arms may thicken. Buying tops in bigger sizes means they won't fit across your shoulders, which will make you look even larger. If you have any money to spare, head for somewhere like Great Expectations, which imports designer maternity wear from France and Italy that actually flatters your lovely, curvy figure. Otherwise get low-price T-shirts and trousers from Next, Gap and Dorothy Perkins, and team with well-cut tops from Bumpsadaisy and Formes. Accept that there is a smaller range of clothes available to you, so you will need to go into shops you don't normally visit, force yourself to do mail order and try on lots. And don't forget: you will wear normal clothes again one day, honest. Your identity will return. Just give it 20 years. Where to go Dorothy Perkins Does the best value T-shirts, at £8, in red, navy, grey and white, as well as more decorative efforts. Also does quite nice cheap jeans. Branches nationwide, tel 0800 731 8285. Next Directory Has very good maternity boot-cut and straight-legged jeans for £24.99, plus two-packs of T-shirts for £22.99 and stretchy black trousers for £24.99. Tel 08702 435435. Gap Does wonderful drawstring stretchy cotton lounge pants in a variety of colours for £20. Or try the karate pants in the men's department for £24.50 - they're drawstring, 100% cotton and very comfortable. Branches nationwide, tel 0800 427 789. Bumpsadaisy Look for Dutch maternity label Vida Vita's beautiful light linen shifts, trousers and shirts in saffron, sage green, orange, tomato and white. The shops also carry a wide range of other maternity labels, many seemingly aimed at the more mature mother-to-be. Branches in Camberley, Edgbaston, Glasgow, Lancaster, Leeds, London, Marlow and Worcester, tel 020-8789 0329 for details. Great Expectations Has upmarket, vaguely designery clothes. Best buy: Neuf Lunes jeans with stretchy denim front - £74, but very flattering. They also carry lovely Italian twinsets in light materials, cut to give you a bit of shape. At Night Owls, 78 Fulham Rd, London SW3, 020-7584 2451. Formes French-style boutiques. If you like to dress smart, head here for good fabrics, a modern range of colours and well-cut shapes. If, like me, you're a bit more slobby, go for light trousers and long-sleeved tops. Branches in Belfast, Cheltenham, Edinburgh, Glasgow, Guildford, Leeds, London, Manchester and Nottingham, tel 020-8689 1133 for details. Harrods Stocks the Belly Basics Pregnancy Survival Kit, a box containing leggings, slim skirt, long-sleeved maternity dress and maternity T-shirt, in black only, for £130. Also stocks Vida Vita and Attesa. At Knightsbridge, London SW1, tel 020-7730 1234. Pleats Please by Issey Miyake Miyake's long-established pleated style is supposed to look fantastic on pregnant women, but I think they are the same women who look good in it when not pregnant. I tried a couple of sleeveless tops and a skinny skirt, which seemed cruelly to highlight my curves and bumps. Works brilliantly for some, though. Tel 020-7495 2306. Maharishi pants Supposedly the pregnant stars' favourite - well, Nicole Appleton's, anyway - these are helpful because of their adjustable waistline. Sadly, though the baggy legs are very comfy, the pocket-flaps stand out like ears on my hips, not emphasis I need at the moment. Tel 020-7749 2290.

title: Vaulting ambition did for the Dome
bodyText: One of the most disappointing aspects of the Government's first term is its failure to learn anything about management. At one level, it misses its targets, or at best hits some at the expense of others equally important. At another, some of its 'modernisation' actually becomes the reverse, setting back causes it holds dear. The Government assumes that what matters is making the numbers. If that were the case, with its enviable unemployment and growth rates, tax and inward investment levels, the UK would be the toast of Europe. But it is not the numbers but the failings that are blindingly obvious to foreigners: shameful inequalities between rich and poor, unaffordable prices and an infrastructure of ramshackle schools, hospitals and railways that increases rather than irons out disparities. Not so much Third Way as Third World, as the German magazine Stern tartly put it. Take productivity, where the Chancellor has launched yet another initiative to bridge the gap between the UK and its closest rivals. Productivity is complex. But because the Treasury insists on seeing it only in quantifiable economic terms, the Government will pass up a fine opportunity to make a difference by focusing on the 'soft' issues. The opportunity exists because, although the causalities are far from clear, a mountain of research now demonstrates beyond ambiguity that there is a link between performance and good management of people. Other things being equal, the more people work in teams, know and help set objectives, get feedback and are communicated with, the better an organisation performs. How and why these linkages function needs further work. But you might think that people management would rank among the priorities on the productivity agenda - a unique opportunity to treat both the mental and physical malaises of the 'English patient'. Think again. Unfortunately the Government's enthusiasm for an evidence base for medicine doesn't stretch to its own management. Instead of mandating a progressive, decentralised, people-centred management approach for the knowledge age, its management methods for schools, universities and the NHS represent a decisive step backwards. According to the Economist, the public sector now has 600 separate targets to juggle, a number probably unmatched since pre-glasnost Soviet Union. But targets don't work. Instruments of mass production, they are centralised and disempowering, turning managers into numbers-chasers and teachers, doctors and professors into petty bureaucrats. They quickly become a form of tyranny. The crassest illustration of target abuse is the Government's very own Dome. To make the financial equation work required 12 million visitors paying £20 each. Each of these targets in itself was arbitrary and unrealistic; and the second made the first not just unrealistic, but impossible. Both in their turn were conditioned by the equally demented top-level goal of making the structure profitable in 12 months. These fantastical targets were used as sticks to beat the management with. Management-by-targets addresses the issue from the wrong end. The Dome was primarily a systems failure. As a shocked PY Gerbeau put it: 'Never before have I met so much unprofessionalism, stupidity and arrogance.' Another example of unjoined-up management are the joint public-private sector partnerships, which are supposed to revitalise public services. Leave aside for the moment why on Earth anyone would want to hand over schools or hospitals to a private sector that has given us such a notable example of service as the railways. The UK private sector is hardly an ideal to worship. But in management terms the solution proposed - combining organisations with different goals, aptitudes, success factors and mindsets - is, if not actually impossible, about the most demanding challenge it is possible to concoct. Unfortunately, management is not a core UK competency. Pace Sir Steve Robson, wouldn't it be simpler to accept that we are not likely to be able to deliver public services of the same quality as those of France and Germany at half the cost? At least not until we have invested in the people and sensible management practices to get the best from what already exists. For joined-up management, look across the Channel. When France announced the 35-hour week, the chorus of amused derision from Anglo-Saxon economists was deafening. From an orthodox viewpoint, it made no sense. But look at what happens when people are included in the equation. Hours were annualised, and managers had an incentive to plan and organise better. After the initial grumble, productivity has gone up. And after a lag, employment will go up, too. People like the changes. What's more, with more time and the same money, they eat out and travel more. Reliable, cheap transport and a well-developed hotel industry give them every incentive to do so. Out-of-season tourism is booming. The 35-hour week has launched a benevolent, not vicious, circle. Contrast this with the poverty - and panic-stricken pleas of UK tourism for more cash to 'correct the image' of this country abroad after the foot and mouth epidemic. Wrong target. The problem isn't only burning animals - it's 25 million visitors going home and telling their friends about chaotic transport, impossible prices and surly, low-paid service. It's not the image that's wrong, it's the reality. It's management, stupid. simon.caulkin@observer.co.uk

title: Sven silences snipers
bodyText: All things considered, Sven-Göran Eriksson is unlikely to take as long as the previous England manager to win his first four matches. Walter Winterbottom was the first England manager after the Second World War and only gave it up in 1962. Eriksson, unlike Winterbottom, fully expects the job - or more accurately the media scrutiny - to kill him in the end; and even if results go extraordinarily well the era of international coaches picking up long-service awards is long past. How did Aimé Jacquet celebrate winning the last World Cup? By standing down and letting someone else have a go. Success in international football is all to do with the system and the players it produces, not the motivational ability of the man in charge, which is why the French are rather proud of being able to rotate their coaches without discernible effect. It will be a long time before England, still stuck in the culture of heaping praise or blame on the heads of individual managers in the manner of the most short-sighted of club owners, can do the same. The embarrassing fuss over Eriksson's arrival was a sure indication that we still see the manager as the ultimate captain of the team - hence the imputation that employing a foreigner was tantamount to cheating - instead of a dispassionate assessor of our national assets. Eriksson is far too polite to have mentioned it, but he must have been puzzled at the surprised reaction when he began attending so many games. Yet what else would the first foreign England coach do but watch as many teams and players as he possibly could? From the amount of comment it provoked one might have assumed the nation would have preferred Eriksson to only be seen at Old Trafford, Anfield and Highbury, or simply to have stuck to the numbers in Kevin Keegan's contacts book. The point of looking beyond these shores for the first time and hiring a technical coach of proven ability in more than one country was not to gain the sort of tactical genius who could turn round a 2-0 deficit against Brazil with a few well-chosen words at half-time. If Eriksson can do that, all well and good, but don't be surprised if he cannot. What Eriksson is capable of, and the reason he was appointed, is ensuring that the standard of English football seen every week in the Premiership and upper echelons of the Nationwide League is not misrepresented on the international stage. Terry Venables and Glenn Hoddle, in fairness, presented English football in a reasonable light at Euro 96 and France 98 respectively, but Keegan was unable to do so at Euro 2000. Apart from the dearth of left-sided players in his squads, Keegan let it be known well before the German defeat which brought about his departure that English players were incapable of passing the ball. Most Premiership spectators know this not to be true, and thanks to four wins in four games under Eriksson, particularly the scintillating first half hour against Mexico last week, they now know it not to be true even when the same players pull on England shirts. David Beckham, Paul Scholes, Steven Gerrard and Steve McManaman are among the best passers in Europe. All that remains for the England players is to continue in the same vein when the fixture carries World Cup significance. It was noticeable that England were not quite so cavalier or commanding against Finland and Albania as they were in the friendly against Mexico. Fair enough, the players are still adapting to Eriksson and the real thing is always going to be tougher than exhibition victories against semi-interested Latin opposition. Greece will not be a doddle in Athens on Wednesday, yet for all the hype engendered by preparing with a week of temperature acclimatisation in La Manga, England must be looking to do what Germany did and take all three points from a team which has spent most of its time at the bottom of Group Nine. Such an outcome would secure a comfortable summer for Eriksson, who must be hoping to carry his unbeaten record into the rematch with Germany on 1 September. The coach knows his first important defeat will change the nature of his relationship with the press, and by extension the country at large, but Eriksson is here to play the long game. These are still early days, but Eriksson has made up a lot of that ground. You don't hear anyone moaning that England are too old any more. You don't hear anyone saying they are boring or predictable, or that such-a-body plays better for his club than his country. Most significant of all, you don't hear much criticism of Eriksson, either from the Venables lobby or the Little Englanders. Perhaps they are keeping their powder dry for a bad result sometime around the play-offs, but if England keep up their improvement there might not be much to criticise. Already the nit-picking tendency have gone off on a tangent, fretting unnecessarily about whether Beckham is an ideal captain. On the basis that if that is all his critics can come up with he cannot be doing too much wrong, Eriksson must be highly satisfied with his first five months.

title: New Zealand 37-12 France
bodyText: New Zealand avenged their dramatic 1999 World Cup semi-final defeat to France with a convincing victory in the one-off Test in Wellington. Recalled veteran Jeff Wilson, Reuben Thorne, Jonah Lomu and sub Doug Howlett scored the tries while Tony Brown added 17 points from the boot for the All Blacks. France's David Skrela had kicked two penalties in the first 11 minutes to put France in front before the home side rallied. Brown reduced the deficit moments later before he superbly off-loaded in the tackle for Wilson to cross over on 21 minutes and regain his New Zealand Test try-scoring record. Nine minutes later and the All Blacks extended the lead with Justin Marshall setting up Thorne for his maiden Test try, again converted by Brown. The sides exchanged two penalties each in the first 10 minutes after the break as the game tightened up and in contrast to New Zealand's World Cup defeat, where France came back from the dead, the All Blacks dominated the closing stages. Lomu raced down the left wing in his usual battering-ram style to score in the 71st minute and five minutes later Howlett crossed for his eighth try in seven Tests less than 30 seconds after coming off the bench to rubber-stamp the victory. New Zealand : L MacDonald (D Howlett 76); J Wilson, T Umaga, P Alatini, J Lomu; T Brown, J Marshall (B Kelleher 72); C Hoeft, A Oliver (capt), G Somerville (C Hayman 49), N Maxwell, T Flavell, R Thorne, T Randell (M Holah 63), R Cribb. France : P Elhorga; N Jeanjean, Y Jauzion, S Glas, C Dominici; D Skrela (G Merceron 70), F Galthie (capt); O Milloud (J Crenca 49), O Azam (R Ibanez 49), C Califano (P de Villiers 64), L Nallet (S Chabal 44), O Brouzet, J Bouilhou (D Auradou 37), O Magne, P Tabacco. Referee: A Lewis (Ireland).

title: We're more connected than we think
bodyText: In a fascinating article in the current London Review of Books Lord Gilmour argues that, with the exception of some of its work in the social field, New Labour was such a disappointment in 1997-2001 that a Conservative party under Kenneth Clarke 'would either have won the recent election against a very vulnerable Labour government or come close to doing so'. Gilmour says that, with the exception of the minimum wage, the Government 'much as it preferred to tax by stealth, preferred to do good by stealth and did not trumpet its deeds' (such as the increases in child benefit and other measures which took more than a million children out of poverty). Apart from that, Labour failed to notice that 'if anything, public opinion moved to the left during the Thatcher era' and with its obsessions with privatisation, and its failure to improve public services 'New Labour was but old Thatcherism writ large'. Gilmour argues plausibly that New Labour's victory on 7 June reflected not public gratitude, but 'New Conservatism's failure to offer even a remotely credible alternative'. Why? Because 'by then the Conservative party had been painstakingly and skilfully positioned to repel any voters who had been disappointed or disillusioned by the poor performance of the Government'. Gilmour's article could hardly be more timely. At a time when the re-elected Blair government appears dead set on spreading privatisation to those areas Magraret Thatcher was too cautious to reach, the Conservatives have a last chance to reintroduce two-party-politics into this country by electing as their leader someone who is a member of the human race. Clarke, before his superb chancellorship, may have been responsible for some Thatcherite departmental policies. But his personal manifesto for the Conservative leadership stakes shows that he knows when enough is enough. It was, after all, none other than Clarke who said he would never have stuck to his own planned freeze on public spending in 1997-1999. Unlike certain elements in New Labour, traditional Tories such as Gilmour have always thought twice about applying the profit motive to the public sector; they have also tended to ask whether the end justifies the means test. But as Gilmour noted in 1997 (Whatever Happened to the Tories?, Fourth Estate) Churchill's 'one nation' policies had been abandoned, and the Conservatives faced 'their Wilderness Years'. Clarke is not as wild a Europhile as his enemies paint him. He is neither federalist in a British sense (which is the opposite of Federalist in the Continental sense) nor blind to the problems attached to joining the euro at current exchange rates. When announcing his leadership bid he made the reasonable point that 'a balanced tone on Europe will free us to address in depth the domestic, economic and social agenda which we neglected throughout the last Parliament'. Clarke's 1993-1997 chancel lorship was one of recovery and growth. The Treasury's chief economic adviser at the time, Sir Alan Budd, said recently at an Institute of Economics Affairs Conference that 'the outcome over the past four years has been remarkably close to the projections set out in the Budget of November 1996, the last Conservative Budget'. Labour objected to the element of colour introduced into macro economic policy by the monthly meetings of Chancellor and Governor known as 'the Ken and Eddie show' (for which title your correspondent is happy to cave in to a paternity suit). But outside members of the new Monetary Policy Committee, such as Willem Buiter and DeAnne Julius, brought colour of their own. And Sushil Wadhwani has kept up the outsiders' tradition of making the MPC interesting. Even Bank deputy governor Mervyn King, who would like monetary policy to be boring, has spiced things up recently with his warnings of the potential - one might even say prospective - instability in the British economy. In the bad old days of Old Labour and Thatcher, we were told that low inflation would solve all our economic problems. It certainly helps those on fixed incomes. But no less an authority than the Bank for International Settlements has recently noted: 'It may be the case that sustained low inflation, particularly if accompanied by strong central bank credibility and robust economic growth, generates the very optimism that helps fuel credit booms and unsustainable increases in asset prices.'
