As I pointed out earlier this year, the 'bust' which is following the low-inflation US boom is reminiscent of the classic Schumpeterian business cycle of pre-war years, in which companies overinvest like mad, and the subsequent contraction is severely disruptive. The Group of Seven leading industrial countries are closely linked by trade and by financial markets. The BIS annual report points out, for instance, that foreign trade (the average of exports and imports in goods and services) as a percentage of gross domestic product rose from 7.9 per cent to 14.8 per cent between the Eighties and 2000 for the US, from 11.9 per cent to 19.0 per cent for the Eurozone, and from 22.9 per cent to 35.9 per cent for the UK. We are very interconnected, and those who thought the slowdown would not have marked repercussions elsewhere were whistling in the dark. The US is in a 'growth' recession; as a result, almost every new indicator and forecast from Germany and France shows downward revisions to their growth prospects. The BIS raises the question of whether monetary policy should take into account not just inflation outcomes and forecasts, but the possibility (or probability) of 'busts' induced by excessive financial and business optimism associated with low inflation (low inflation in goods and services that is, not in asset prices). And the Bank of England's estimable Financial Stability Review sounds some warning notes about possible banking problems associated with imprudent lending during our recent consumer boom, not to say on what the fall out from Wall Street may do to London. Which brings us to the interesting question of how long the economy of this sceptred isle can withstand the US and Continental slowdown. After some hesitation, the MPC embarked in February on a series of interest rate cuts to offset the impact of the global slowdown. More recently the Bank has begun to warn that, having deliberately stoked up domestic demand, it has concerns on two fronts. One is that a fall in sterling associated (or not) with anticipation of entry to the euro might threaten the inflation target and force the MPC to raise interest rates. A second is that the Government's flagship for its second term - HMS Public Services - involves such a sharp increase in spending that the growth of private domestic demand needs to slow appreciably. It is possible, of course, that the pound will remain high, now that the euro issue has been kicked into touch yet again, so that that particular inflation scare is also pushed into the future. It is also possible that the surge in public spending is especially well timed, in classic Keynesian fashion, to offset the depressing effect of goodness knows what the rest of the world economy has in store. Richard Jeffrey, economist at Charterhouse Securities, puts it in an interesting way: 'At issue is whether problems in overseas economies will have sufficient impact on the UK [to slow demand]'. Can it be that the UK will somehow escape the worst of the world 'boom and bust'? It is always possible. But it doesn't seem likely. Also, Budd says: 'Within the next few years I believe the fiscal constraints will start to bite... not, as the economically illiterate argue, because of a cyclical downturn, but because of a fall in the structural level of tax receipts. The chances are also that the MPC, that great institution, will encounter harder times.' Budd adds: 'I do not have stronger evidence for this view other than my profound belief that we were not put on this earth to enjoy ourselves.' For myself I take a less puritanical view than my old friend. But I wonder whether the Government's economic luck can last.

title: Russia blocks new sanctions on Iraqi arms
bodyText: A British-American plan to revamp sanctions against Iraq suffered a big setback at the UN security council last night when Russia resisted the so-called "smart" sanctions meant to replace the oil-for-food programme. Instead members agreed to extend the oil-for-food system for several months. "Smart" sanctions would relax civilian trade with Iraq but step up pressure on the regime by tightening controls on military items and smuggling. The US and Britain had been hoping to win approval in the council before midnight tonight, when the current oil-for-food programme expires. But although intense diplomatic efforts secured support from France and China, the plan was opposed by Russia, which threatened to veto it. A Foreign Office spokesman in London said: "Clearly not everyone is ready to reach agreement. We have therefore decided not to press for a vote but we will continue to work for an agreement because we are serious about the needs of the Iraqi people." The revamped sanctions were intended to remove much of the controversy about the current system, particularly about the hardship caused to ordinary Iraqis. But the plan ran into a number of problems. One was getting security council members to agree on a list of "controlled" items with possible military uses which would need special approval before being allowed into Iraq. Another was that implementation of the new sanctions regime would have placed a special responsibility on Iraq's neighbours - particularly Jordan, Syria, Turkey and Iran - to control imports. Iraq has threatened to cut all economic ties with them if they cooperate with such a system. Jordan, which is especially dependent on Iraq, stood to lose its entire oil supply and one-third of its export trade if Baghdad carried out the threat. During the last few days, security council members have been discussing ways of compensating Iraq's neighbours for any losses - including, possibly, paying them with unspent Iraqi funds in the UN's oil-for-food account. Western efforts to revamp policy towards Iraq have also been dogged by Arab complaints of double standards in the way attempts have been made to enforce UN resolutions against Iraq but not against Israel. A similar British-American proposal in the security council at the beginning of June stalled mainly on the question of listing "controlled" goods, but on that occasion the oil-for-food programme was extended for only a month to allow time for more talking. Four of the five permanent members of the security council - Britain, the US, France and China - have now agreed on the list. Britain is still hoping to get Russia on board during the extension of the present sanctions regime. Discussions about the length of the extension were continuing last night. British diplomatic sources suggested one month might be too short but said they would resist a six-month extension because it would remove the pressure to achieve agreement. A long extension might also pose a dilemma for Iraq, which suspended oil exports on June 4 in protest at the new plan. Some observers were suggesting last night that the longer the extension, the less the likelihood that "smart" sanctions would ever come. Russia has begun to question the whole concept of the new sanctions proposal, not just its technicalities. Collapse of the plan would be a blow for the reputation of the US secretary of state, Colin Powell, who has made "re-energising" sanctions a key plank of his foreign policy.

title: Comment: Our answer to G7 protesters
bodyText: When G7 finance ministers assemble in Italy this weekend, humanitarian goals are at the heart of the agenda. Momentous decisions have to be made to tackle global ill health, illiteracy and poverty. Already, the IMF, the World Bank, the UN and OECD - and individual governments - have signed up to challenging 2015 targets: that poverty across the globe is cut by half; that every one of the 120m children in the world currently denied primary education receive it; and that infant mortality is cut to one third of its present level. "Poverty in all its forms," they state, "is the greatest challenge to the international community." It is our task to show the demonstrators against globalisation that the way to attack poverty is not to retreat from global cooperation - to the free-for-all or protectionism of the 1930s - but to strengthen it. To achieve this we must assemble a new global partnership. Some of the building blocks are already there. Twenty-three countries are already benefiting from debt relief. We already provide 100% relief on UK debt to countries committed to spending the proceeds on poverty reduction and we are pressing other countries to follow our lead. We will work to prevent new debt burdens through international agreement not to give export credits for excessive military spending or prestige projects. We also need to do far more to build the virtuous circle of debt relief, poverty reduction and sustainable development. So in Italy this weekend, our focus will be on health and education. Only 10% of all biomedical research is devoted to 90% of the global disease burden affecting the world's poor - Britain is therefore creating new tax incentives to accelerate research on diseases such as Aids, TB and malaria. We will propose that all countries join us. The Department for International Development (DFID) is playing a key role in setting up an international health fund for tackling these diseases. Such a fund, supported at the UN last week, would assure the long-term availability of critical commodities, drive down prices and act as a catalyst for research by providing a credible market for current and future treatments. If implemented in the right way, this will ensure help goes to those who need it most. DFID will contribute $200m to the successful fund. The US and France have announced their contributions too. Europe must show a lead. Next week, the UK will propose a significant contribution direct from the community budget. With further contributions from all our global partners public and private sector we can initially expect at least $1bn. Such a proposal must be matched by a commitment from pharmaceutical companies to create new drugs and vaccines in ways that truly help the poor and sick. We call on them to build on recent announcements and urgently establish a clear and comprehensive, global, two-tier pricing system and provide to the global health fund and to other legitimate purchasers, deep discounts for the full range of drugs, treatments and vaccines for HIV/Aids, malaria, TB and other killer diseases. In education in the past decade, primary enrolments have increased at twice the rate of the 1980s. But still 120m children do not attend primary school, two thirds of them are girls. All of us must go further to meet the Dakar commitment that no country committed to universal primary education will be thwarted by lack of resources. Canada has indicated it will make education its priority as G7 president in the coming year. No children should be denied basic education because they cannot afford to pay for it. The UK government has created a separate Department for International Development, increased the aid budget by 45% in real terms since 1997, and will make further substantial increases over this parliament. Elsewhere, the IMF and World Bank should support countries in putting poverty reduction strategies, including targets to reduce child poverty and an assessment of the social impact of their policies, at the centre of economic and financial policy; the EU and the UN family should support developing countries in making health, education and poverty reduction a priority; developed countries should increase and untie their aid and open their markets; developing countries should create community-driven poverty reduction strategies. We must speed our efforts so that in our generation we can reach our goal, the goal of decent minded people everywhere in the world, that poverty be banished and justice prevail. • Gordon Brown is chancellor of the exchequer, Clare Short is international development secretary

title: The making of Czech war drama Dark Blue World
bodyText: Two young wartime pilots use their flying skills to take on the enemy. While they face the daily threat of death in the skies, on land they fall for the same beautiful, dark-haired English woman. Which brings them to confront the question: which is stronger, love or friendship? But this is not Pearl Harbor. It is Tmavomodry Svet, or Dark Blue World, a Czech film that is breaking box-office records in the Czech Republic, taking the best part of £600,000 since it opened just over a month ago. Not bad for a film with a total budget of £4m - or, as Dark Blue World's producers point out, just 50% more than the budget of the Pearl Harbor premiere party. This latest offering by Jan Sverak (the Oscar-winning director of Kolya, a heart-warming tale of bonding between a cellist and a young boy) tells the story of Czech fighter pilots who fought in Britain's Royal Air Force during the war. Starring Charles Dance, Tara Fitzgerald and Czech heartthrob Ondrej Vetchy, the low- budget movie, although highly sentimental, impresses with its use of wartime Spitfires and retouched out-takes from the 1969 British classic, The Battle of Britain. More than 2,000 Czechs fled the Nazi occupation of Czechoslovakia after the Germans breached the Munich agreement in 1939, and headed to Britain to fight under British colours in the Battle of Britain. A formal agreement was signed in October 1940, following the fall of France, under which Czech airmen were enlisted or commissioned into the RAF Voluntary Reserve while officially remaining members of the embryonic Czechoslovak air force. By the end of the war, the three Czech fighter squadrons had completed 28,355 operational flights. The film is as much a tribute to them as to the many thousands more who came to fight in the army. But Dark Blue World tackles more than the average war movie: the battle scenes are a series of flashbacks seen through the eyes of Flight Lt Franta Slama, as he recuperates from pneumonia in a communist prison in the early 1950s. The heroes' welcome received by those pilots who returned was short-lived. Three years after the end of the war, the communists took power in Czechoslovakia, and branded the men traitors - "contaminated by capitalism, democracy and freedom" and as such saw them as a threat to the state. Many were unceremoniously thrown out of the services, imprisoned, tortured, executed, or at best confined to menial jobs. Most were not rehabilitated until 1991; even then, some were still viewed with suspicion. "The film throws up an interesting dilemma for Czech audiences," says Sverak. "Should they feel proud or ashamed? These are our heroes, our knights in shining armour, and we treated them so badly." Some of the film's more dubious characters are given the task of confronting the audience with the film's disturbing message: "Nazis, communists? The blows are all the same," says the former SS doctor who treats Slama and his fellow pilots in the prison. In a small attempt to make amends, a Dark Blue World fund has been set up. It has raised almost £10,000 since the premiere. Made with German, Czech and British backing, Dark Blue World has been warmly received by the veteran fliers and bomber pilots for whom proper recognition has been more than 50 years in coming. Former Spitfire pilot Frantisek ("call me Frank") Veitl, aged 90, was close to tears as he emerged from a special screening in Prague last month. "I felt like I was back in the cockpit," he grinned. Dark Blue World centres on the friendship between pilot Franta Slama and young trainee Karel Vojtisek. Frustrated that they have been ordered not to fight the Nazis, they flee to England where they join other Czech pilots at an RAF base. After three months of preparation - including using bicycles equipped with wings and learning important phrases such as "Tally-ho" and "Hitler's only got one ball" - they are finally scrambled. One night Karel's plane takes a direct hit and he manages to bail out, finding refuge in a remote cottage, home to Susan (Tara Fitzgerald), whose Navy husband is missing in action. Karel falls in love and brings Franta to meet Susan. The two are drawn together. Karel and Franta's relationship flounders and, for months, they don't speak. One day, while escorting a fleet of bombers over the channel, Franta's plane malfunctions and crashes into the sea. Karel attempts to save him but his plane plunges into the waves and he is gone forever. Franta survives, but is laden with guilt. He returns to Czechoslovakia to find that his girlfriend has married. Even his dog has forgotten him. Then, three years later, he is imprisoned by the communists. It could hardly be a more miserable homecoming. Czechoslovakia's politicians had prepared for war for 10 years. But when it came to the crunch, they decided not to fight. The decision deeply scarred the succeeding generations and, as a result, it is still considered shameful to wear a uniform in the Czech Republic today. "These men went to Britain to fight because they couldn't stomach allowing their country to go to the dogs," says 36-year-old Sverak. He sees his latest creation as a chance to "celebrate the country's last heroes". These include people like Ivo Tonder. When Germany annexed Czechoslovakia in 1939, Tonder made his way via Hungary and Turkey to Beirut, where he joined the French Foreign Legion. Later, via France and Spain, he arrived in England, joining the second Czech squadron, number 312, based at Duxford, which was equipped with Hurricanes and later Spitfires. He took part in the last days of the Battle of Britain before being shot down in a dogfight over the channel. His capture by a German float plane led to his imprisonment in Silesia. There he became the main tunneller in what was later immortalised as the Great Escape. He was soon caught and sent to Colditz, which was liberated before the death sentence against him could be carried out. On his return to London after the war he married Jirina, who had served in the Women's Auxiliary Air Force. They moved back to Prague, only to escape separately in 1948 as the communists seized power. Their children were smuggled out by CIA agents a year later. The family settled in Henley-on-Thames where Tonder relished recounting his wartime adventures until his death at the age of 82, six years ago. He was always disappointed that, on returning to the RAF after the war, he was assigned a much lower rank than the flight-lieutenant position he had previously held. But a harsher fate befell the war brides. Around 1,000 British women who fell in love with Czechs serving in the army orair force in the 1940s moved to an uncertain life in Czechoslovakia in 1945. Many stayed and suffered decades of communist repression - forced into poverty and often persecution in what, prewar, had been one of Europe's most prosperous capitals. Some did return home immediately, and there was a second wave of departees during the relaxation of the Prague Spring in 1968. Nowadays, due to departures and old age, there are only around a dozen war brides left in the Czech Republic, including Lucy Dolezalova and Yvonne Sebestakova (see below). Most of them are now widows. Lucy Dolezalova, née Westcott, aged 79, who returned to Prague with her Czech husband Honza and young son Milan after the war, recalls the high-standing reputation the Czech men had in Britain. "The Czechs and Poles were frightfully popular. The Czechs had the good manners and gentlemanly behaviour, but it was thought that the Poles went too far too fast - and mothers were frightened of them. Very different to English men." During Lucy's days as a switchboard operator, she came across Honza Dolezal, an officer with the Czech anti-tank brigade, and arranged a blind date - via the telephone - at a village hall dance. "Later I took him home to show my parents, on June 22 1941, the day war was declared between Russia and Germany," Lucy recalls. "They said to me, 'As you make your bed, so you may lie in it.' " Lucy and Honza's son was born in a Worcestershire village in 1943. "The locals were convinced he'd be black, and were quite surprised when they looked in the pram," she says. She had to renounce her British passport on marrying a Czech, and as an "alien" was obliged to report to the local police station, much to the amusement of the police. H onza's war was far less glamorous than that depicted in Dark Blue World. Towards the end, he was dispatched by the British to help the Red Army push the Nazis back on the eastern front in the Slovak mountains, which left him a physical wreck. A Slovak farmer betrayed him to the Nazis and he was sent to Mauthausen concentration camp in Austria. "I didn't recognise him when he returned," Lucy says. "He was covered in ulcers, a terrible state." The communist takeover did not particularly shock them, with both women, who were Labour voters, believing in its principles. But the regime stripped both men of their military status, ending any career plans. They were seen as "tainted" by their experiences in capitalist England, although Honza had joined the Red Army to fight the Nazi scourge. He pursued an itinerant existence, prepared to leave jobs before he was thrown out as his lack of allegiance to the Communist Party was plain. The insecure and stressful lifestyle eventually broke up their marriage. Both Lucy and Yvonne felt the brunt of the aftermath of the Prague Spring of 1968. Lucy's eldest son, who was in Sweden on a student exchange, used the opportunity to escape and never returned home, later emigrating to Australia. Both women had the choice of losing their jobs or taking a pay cut for denouncing the "friendly help of the Soviet Union" after Soviet tanks had crushed the more enlightened communist rule under Alexander Dubcek. Lucy, who lives in a tiny two-roomed flat in the south of Prague, says she has few regrets. "We have our children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren, and we've had interesting lives. I'm far more at home here now than I would be in old Albion." They are luckier than some. In the final days of the war, Joy Kadeckova married a Czech pilot and pledged to move to his homeland - with or without her husband. When he was killed, she carried out her promise and has spent 46 years living in Prague, surviving the hard times by working as a translator. "I made the decision to come here and I don't regret it at all," she says adamantly. Jean Spacek met her husband Tony, a lieutenant with the Czechoslovak infantry brigade, in Wiltshire, where she was a section officer in the WAAF. They left England at the end of the war with their son aged 15 months set for a new life in Prague. But, following their attempts to flee during the communist coup, Tony was arrested and thrown in prison. "We agreed to divorce in the hope that by severing his connections with me, an Englishwoman, Tony would stand a better chance of getting out," says Jean. She escaped to England, while Tony spent six years in jail followed by 11 years as a forced labourer. They saw each other just a handful of times after the 1989 revolution, having a reunion at London's Waldorf hotel two years ago. A reunion every Christmas, put on by the British Embassy in Prague, is a high point in the social calendar of most of the dozen or so war brides who remain abroad. "They put on the full works," says Yvonne, "including a soundtrack of air-raid sirens, Winston Churchill, guns exploding, crackers and Christmas pud, and we sing our favourite wartime songs like Bye Bye Blackbird, and Roll Out the Barrel." None of the war brides complains about the low pensions (between £90 and £110 a month) most of them are living on, which with inflation barely cover the rent and must be supplemented with transla tion work. There are occasional small grants from the private British charity, the Air-Bridge Association, and the RAF benevolent fund, but no sign that the UK government has any plans to offer them British pensions. "You have to remember that the welfare state only came in in 1948, long after the brides left for Prague," says Colonel Wynne Davis, military attaché at the British Embassy in Prague. But Jim Auton, an RAF veteran who is chairman of the charity, insists the cause is worth fighting for. "Their husbands were after all the ones who formed 25 squadrons in Britain - which made such a difference and quite possibly even saved us." But Yvonne says the war brides are flattered that anyone still thinks of them at all: "I can't understand how they manage to persuade people to donate money to strange old women who made the eccentric decision to move abroad for love all those decades ago."

title: Pass the Old Git
bodyText: Just pretend for a moment that you're Jilly Goolden. Or, if that threatens your masculinity, Oz Clarke. We're going to have a quick wine quiz. I'm going to give you the name, vintage, price and critical verdict on three wines and you're going to tell me which country they're from. Got that? Let's go! Wine number 1. The Fat Cat Chardonnay, 1998 (£4.99 from the Co-Op). Critic's verdict: "And very fat it is - indeed, it's positively obese with folds of rich, fleshy, sunburned fruit... Exceedingly juicy." Wine number 2. The Old Git Grenache Syrah 1999 (£3.99 from Booths supermarket). Critic's verdict: "Great fun. I'm bound to love the name and the dry, Midi fruit." Wine number 3. Kiwi Cuvée Sauvignon Blanc 2000 (£3.99 from Oddbins). Critic's verdict: "Lovely price to a startlingly complete sauvignon of tangy flavoursomeness and stylish finish." The answers are: 1. New Zealand. 2. France. 3. France. Not quite as knowledgeable as we thought we were, eh Jilly? What's that, Oz? Stumped you, too? What's surprising about these answers is that they show that France, home of the all-but-inscrutable appellation d'origine contrllée system and unspeakable snobbery about viticulture, is now coming up with names for its wines just as daft and simple as its New World competitors - at least for the daft and simple British. But the French needed to do something drastic to halt the disastrous decline of their wine exports to Britain. In the past 10 years, France's share of UK wine imports has fallen from 50% to 25%, much of it to New World competition. Around the world, French wine exports fell by 79m bottles in 1999 and by 93m last year. However, while the world market for wine is in decline, consumption in Britain has risen by 60% in the past decade. Now the British drink an average of 20 litres of wine each year. The French had to decide what to do to get the British to lay off the Jacob's Creek, the Wombat's Revenge and Steve Waugh's Moist Crotch, and drink Gallic wines once more. One tactic the Bordeaux Wine Bureau used was to employ a British ad agency. The agency cunningly linked Bordeaux consumption with sex. The resulting ads depicted a woman in lacy lingerie and a man in rather less. A wine glass was superimposed over the couple, bearing the words "lingering", "deep", "lip-staining", "velvet" and "kisses". "Drink Bordeaux," said the sub-text, "and you"ll pull." But some French winemakers have also been fighting back against their New World competitors on the British market by ditching pretentious labels. They now realise it's easier to remember the name Old Git than a Chteau Baron de Frou-Frou 1999 appellation de Frou-Frou contrlée. In this, the French are belatedly copying the Australians, those masters of simple and sometimes vulgar-sounding labels. It was the Australians, for instance, who pioneered the idea of branding wines by grape variety - shiraz, merlot, cabernet sauvignon - rather than place of origin. For many French winemakers, this was New World barbarism. Merely saying the year, grape and country the wine was made in was a philistine way of labelling wine. What was - and is - important to the buyers of wine should also be the terroir (the bit of land on which the grapes that went into the wine were grown) and the name of the château and winemaker (to know what production techniques were employed). But Britons prefer to buy alcoholic drinks with silly names devoid of intractable nomenclature. Consider real ale. Only if its authenticity is underwritten by such names as The Brewer's Toenails or The Sweet Savour of the Humber Estuary, will a real ale be worth drinking. Antipodean winemakers - or at least the clever people who marketed their stuff - realised two things that the French are only now coming to grips with. First, that Britons don't care for - or understand - pretentious French wine labels, but are reassured by nice, Anglo-Saxon sounding names such as Clare Valley or Cook's Landing, and by a straightforward statement of what kind of grapes were in the bottle. Second, that British people wanted to buy reliably tasty wine. This is where the Australians in particular have the French over a barrel (sorry). Australia's wine industry is dominated by three companies - BRL Hardy, Southcorp and Foster's. This allows the Australians - unlike traditional French producers - to offer consistent, branded products inimical to château-produced wines (Piat d'Or notwithstanding). But the French are learning. Not just with names such as Old Git, but with Kiwi Cuvée. The latter has caused a great rumpus in New Zealand, since this wine is made in the Loire Valley but sounds as though it were made outside Auckland. What's astounding about Kiwi Cuvée, though, is that British distributors Western Wines commissioned it as a branded product to sell exclusively on the UK market. They asked Kiwi winemaker Rhyan Woodman to create a New Zealand-style aromatic, concentrated sauvignon blanc for wine company Lachéteau at Doué-La-Fontaine, a few miles from Saumur. For two years now the results of this project, blandly subtitled a Vin de Pays du Jardin de la France, have been sold in Britain. True, imitation may be the sincerest form of flattery, but French pride must have taken a battering thanks to Kiwi Cuvée. After all, it shows that what it takes to flog plonk to those barbarians across the Channel is British PR, Kiwi expertise, and wine that tastes as though it wasn't made anywhere near France.

title: Worrying time for Woolies
bodyText: After a gestation period of nearly a year, the retail conglomerate Kingfisher has finally spelled out its detailed proposals for the demerger of its Woolworths operation. From August 28, and after 20 years as part of a larger group, Woolworths will once again be an independent company. Kingfisher investors will be given one Woolworths share for every Kingfisher share they hold. They are expected to hit the market at about 35p - and will then, likely as not, fall rapidly. On the surface, Woolworths has a lot going for it. It is the UK's biggest retailer of CDs and videos. It is a market leader in sweets and toys and, through its Ladybird brand, the third biggest children's clothes retailer. It has a foothold in online retailing and videos through its smaller sister operations Streets Online, MVC and Entertainment UK. But Kingfisher boss Sir Geoff Mulcahy has loaded the new company with £200m of debt and is selling the freehold property that Woolworths occupies to raise cash for Kingfisher. Some of the Woolworths stores have been locked into onerous long leases. This morning, to send the new Woolworths on its way, Kingfisher issued its second profits warning in less than a month, stating that sales in Woolworths last month were "marginally" down year-on-year. Last month, Kingfisher warned that Woolworths' profits this year would be hit by a programme to reduce stock levels. Sir Geoff warned not to read too much into the warning, blaming - as usual - the recent hot weather and the absence of new blockbuster video and CD releases. Woolworths is a confusing business for City analysts, most of whom would not be seen dead in one its outlets. They regard it as a ragbag, unfocused business selling goods where price competition is tough and margins are constantly under pressure. Never mind its dominance in so many markets and the fact that it generates cash at a rate of knots. The combination of those factors does not look good for Woolworths. What of Kingfisher, now reduced to a pan-European DIY and electricals retailer? It is not retaining a stake to benefit from any rise in share price from the new Woolworths. But it is benefiting from a £1.1bn boost as the result of unloading Woolworths - as well as Superdrug, which has been sold separately to Dutch group Kruidvat - and the huge property portfolios attached to both. It needs to expand to be a really big hitter and should now have the muscle to buy the half of French DIY giant Castorama that it does not already own. If a full-scale recession arrives, its business will undoubtedly be hit. A downturn in the housing market would have an impact on DIY, while one of the first purchases to go when consumers tighten their belts is big ticket electricals. At the same time, with what rivals might have regarded as the irritating baggage of Woolworths, Superdrug etc, the rump of Kingfisher is a wholly more appetising target for a takeover. There is always speculation that the likes of US group Home Depot would want to snap up B&Q and Castorama, while Dixons, which has twice locked horns in takeover battles with Kingfisher in the last 15 years, would dearly love the group's Darty electricals business in France. It would certainly be quite a swansong for Sir Stanley Kalms, Dixons' septugenarian chairman, who is retiring next year. Related articles 01.08.2001: Kingfisher spins off Woolworths 30.07.2001: Corbett in new bonus row 29.07.2001: 50% rise for Corbettt Comment and analysis 29.07.2001, leader: Sorry, fat cats, no more cream Useful links Kingfisher Woolworths

title: The Kurdish dream: emigration
bodyText: On the face of it the Sheikhallah bazaar is just the shabby little side street in Irbil where you go to change money. But the whole of "liberated" Kurdistan knows that another, more serious business is being conducted behind those counters piled high with debased Iraqi banknotes. Emigration is the Kurdish national obsession, and it is here that the would-be emigrants begin the long, clandestine, perilous yet highly organised odyssey in containers and ramshackle hulks to the Europe of promise and plenty. It is here that they acquire a false passport. For none of Kurdistan's 3.6m inhabitants has a legitimate one. As Iraqis they are entitled to one, but they dare not go to Baghdad to get it. Saddam Hussein is the reason, above all, why they want to leave. "We have had 60 or 70 years of war, or the laws of war," said Azar Barwari, a Kurdistan Democratic party (KDP) official. "Saddam was the worst, the summit of chauvinism and brutality. It is enough that he is still there, however weakened. We fear another Anfal [the chemical attacks in which an estimated 180,000 civilians died], genocide, liquidation. It is a fear inside every Kurd." There are economic and social reasons too for wanting to migrate. Unemployment is high and, for most, salaries very low. The recent coming of satellite television has greatly enhanced the allure of exile. "I know that if you really look carefully, it shows a negative as well as a positive side of Europe," said Samar Fawzi, a law student at Sulaymaniyah University, who tried and failed to get to Britain, "but lots see it as a kind of paradise." And some do make good and return - on holiday, to buy a house, or to marry a local girl - with real money in their pockets. But the most impressive evidence that the political imperative outweighs the economic one is that this is a society where, unusually, the rich emigrate as ardently as the poor. Huge cost The decision to go often involves an enormous investment, psychologically as well as economically. Families may sell off most of their possessions to finance it: it costs twice as much to get to Europe as it does to build a modest house. Partly because of the steadily growing web of contacts with the west, "liberated" Kurdistan has become a main source of Kurdish emigration; more - probably 30,000-plus last year - leave here than from Baghdad-controlled territory, where conditions are infinitely worse, or from Iranian Kurdistan. The Kurdish regional government (KRG) tries to discourage emigration, through youth training centres, debates in parliament, and group marriages, and television programmes highlighting the miseries of European asylum centres, the difficulties of adjustment, and the exploitation of and prejudice against immigrants. "But you surely know that not just Kurds, half the Middle East would emigrate if it could. We can't physically stop them," the deputy prime minister Sami Abdul Rahman said. So the KRG tolerates the semi-clandestine Sheikhallah passport bazaar, where aspirant emigrants publicly loiter, gleaning the latest news, gossip and hot tips from the underground travel circuits. Like Europeans planning their summer holidays or stock market investments, they know the exact price and risks of every route and destination. The passports are smuggled from Baghdad with the help of corrupt officials. The current price is $1 000 (£700) for a virgin one, $400 for a used one that has been "cleaned". The passport serves one purpose only: legal entry into Turkey. After that it is thrown away. Turkey offers the Kurds 40 visas a day, half of them through Massoud Barzani's KDP, which sells them for $90, half through the Turcoman Front, a Turkish created party ostensibly representing "liberated" Kurdistan's 10,000 Turcomans, which sells them for $600. The cheap visa means a long wait, the expensive one is "express". With a virgin passport the fugitive stands a much better chance at the frontier than with a used one. But the supply does not match demand. The alternative is to be smuggled over the frontier, often through Iran. That is cheaper - $350 - and quicker but, with mines and the Turkish army, it is perilous. "My brother went that way," Mahdi Abdul Rahman said. "He only lost his kidney, but nine of his companions were killed when a helicopter, thinking they were PKK guerrillas, attacked them near Van." The next stop on the standard route is Istanbul, where contact is made with a representative of the international smuggling network, almost always a Kurd, who arranges the sea or land crossing to Greece. The current cost is $2,000 to $2,200. At this point it is not only those who are captured, drowned, or asphyxiated in containers who fail to make it. Some, like Ary Ahmad, an unemployed technician in Sulaymaniyah, just grow too dispirited to continue. "After five days hiding in a seaside building site at Bodrum and waiting for a boat that never came, I called it off," he said. "I had a valid passport and Turkish visa, after all." His companions eventually fetched up on a Greek island near the Turkish coast, and from there took the regular tourist ferry to the mainland. On arrival in Athens they phoned their families back home and the $2,000 already deposited with a third party was handed over to the network. From Athens the fugitive can take a plane to western Europe. But that is the deluxe route, only possible with a stolen European passport, whose bearer requires no entry visa; and at $6,000, only a tiny handful can afford it. The other, normal, route is across the Adriatic by Albanian fishing boat, or secreted inside a long vehicle on a ferry. Current cost: between $1,000 and $1,200. The rest, by train, bus or car from Italy to France, Britain or Germany, is relatively cheap - $400 - and easy. Rejection The great drawback now, however, is that asylum itself has become much more difficult to acquire as Europe clamps down on the traffic. There are 9,000 unaccepted Kurdish asylum seekers in the Netherlands alone. "I hear that in Britain there is now a rejection rate of 90%," the humanitarian affairs minister, Shafiq Qazzaz, said. "There is more and more talk of 'voluntary repatriation'. And the point of view is developing that our safe haven is, after all, safe." But the upshot of a recent debate in the Swedish parliament was that, in truth, it is not safe. There lies the nub. "In my view more and more Kurds will try to get to Europe, if only because there are so many already there," said Fakhir Barzani, of the ministry of humanitarian affairs. "The only solution lies here in Kurdistan, and it requires western and UN involvement." Obliging Baghdad to respect resolution 986 and spend oil-for-food money on development and income support is the lesser part of it, because solid long-term investment will only come when there is political security. And that, Kurds say, can only come with the completion of what the world left incomplete when, under resolution 688 of April 1991, it created the safe haven and the US and Britain added their no-fly zones. The world may have largely forgotten about 688, a call for the Kurds' "human and political rights" to be respected. But to them it is holy writ, a vital legitimisation of their cause which should be built on until they achieve a secure, legal and internationally recognised status, either the independence they dream of or the federation within a post-Saddam Iraq they realistically aim for. Without that, they fear, the worst will happen again. "And believe me," Fakhir Barzani said, "next time you could have millions clamouring at the gates of Europe."

title: International news in brief
bodyText: Chirac cash for travel bill soars Jacques Chirac's woes multiplied yesterday with the news that investigators have further increased their estimate of what he paid in cash for luxury trips for himself, his family and friends in the early 1990s. The newspaper Le Monde said police accountants had told the magistrates investigating alleged kickback schemes on building contracts let by Paris town hall that the president - who was mayor of Paris at the time - had paid out £310,000 in cash for the private trips rather than the original estimate of £240,000. The latest total apparently contradicts Mr Chirac's statements. During a television interview on July 14, he insisted that he had "nothing to hide" and added that even the earlier figure was "so far off the mark...as to be stupefying". He also said in his Bastille Day interview that many of the trips were professional and had not involved his family or friends. But the accountants have found that at least seven were journeys by his wife, Bernadette, on which she did not seem to be accompanied by her husband. The news followed claims by Le Canard Enchané earlier this week that in 1978 Mr Chirac abused his position as an official of a charity for the handicapped to gain control of land near the family chteau in Corrèze, south-west France. Worried by plans to build a holiday camp and an electricity sub-station on the site, Mr Chirac allegedly persuaded the Fondation Claude-Pompidou to buy the 4.8 hectares (12 acres) of land and preserve the peace and quiet of his chateau at Bity. The foundation planned to use the site for a holiday home for the aged, but it has not been built. Jon Henley, Paris Boy 'killed father after chores row' A teenager charged with killing his father and his father's girlfriend and hiding their bodies in a barn partied with friends afterwards, according to prosecution documents lodged with a court in Wisconsin. Joshua Lepley, 14, committed the murders after his father woke him to do household chores, the prosecutors say. He allegedly told the police that he had been arguing with his father for several days before the killing on Saturday in Shell Lake, northern Wisconsin, 270 miles north-west of Milwaukee. He is accused of shooting Wilber Lepley, 38, and Amber Williams, 23, with a hunting rifle, then tying a rope around their ankles and dragging the bodies into a barn. He stayed home for the next three days, the prosecutors say. "He had friends come over, talked to them, partied and drank beer with them and lied to them about the whereabouts of his father and Ms Williams," the prosecution claims. The police were brought in after the couple missed work for three days. Colleagues said the teenager called the company and said that his father and Williams were "very sick with the flu and intended to see a doctor". AP, Shell Lake Dozens hurt in national strike riots A national one-day strike against a VAT increase paralysed much of Guatemala on Wednesday and underlined the weakness of President Alfonso Portillo's struggling government. The accompanying protests turned violent in several areas, leading to at least 83 arrests and leaving dozens injured. Riot police fired rounds of teargas into groups of stone-throwing protesters in the central square of the capital, Guatemala City, crowds set light to several buildings associated with the government party, the FRG, in the central highlands' city of Totonicapan, and farmers blocked the main road leading to the southern border with El Salvador. The day of protest against a 2% VAT rise approved earlier this week by the government was called by the main employers organisation, but was joined by unions, student bodies, and other groups in a highly unusual show of cross-class unity. The organisers claimed that more than 90% of businesses were closed by the stoppage. The VAT rise is designed to boost the country's paltry tax revenues, which amounted to less than 10% of GDP last year, giving Guatemala the worst tax revenue record in Latin America. The mass protest was encouraged by widespread disillusion with the Portillo government, which is regarded by many as both ineffectual and corrupt. One of the most popular banners at the protests proclaimed "No more taxes, no more corruption." Jo Tuckman, Mexico City Jailed US 'spy' may go free in days An American exchange student who was jailed for a year in southern Russia on a drugs offence after he was accused of spying may be freed within days. A parole board in southern Russia advised yesterday that the Fulbright scholar John Tobin should be released halfway through his jail sentence. It is thought that he could be freed today or Monday, but he may have to spend a few days in Russia finalising the paperwork before returning home. Tobin's arrest on drugs possession charges attracted international attention after the Russian security police claimed that he was training to be a spy and had worked as an interrogator in the US. But no spying charges were pressed against him. Tobin's father, also called John, remains cautious, however. "Anything can happen, and of course we are guarded at this point," he said at his home in Ridgefield, Connecticut. "It needs to be signed off by a judge and by the prosecutor's office...When that happens we can breathe a sigh of relief." But he added: "I feel pretty darn good. We've been expecting it." Tobin was jailed for three years for possessing a small amount of marijuana, but the sentence was reduced to a year, making him eligible for parole after a six-month period, which expired yesterday. He has denied the charges and an appeal is pending. In an email he claimed that the security police had offered to drop the charges if he acted as an informant. Reuters, Moscow Greek most wanted man escapes The Greek police intensified a big manhunt yesterday for its most wanted fugitive, who he slipped away during a botched ambush which forced the resignation of the national police chief. The search for the convict Constantinos Passaris, 26, was concentrated on working-class districts of western Athens and the port, Piraeus, the police said. Roadblocks were set up and hospitals and clinics combed. Passaris was apparently hit by police gunfire before bolting from a central Athens flat surrounded by about 100 armed police, including members of an elite unit. The prime minister, Costas Simitis, postponed his annual holiday to call an emergency meeting of the defence and foreign policy council today. The council is expected to choose a new police chief to replace Lieutenant-General Yannis Georgakopoulos. One of Passaris' alleged accomplices was detained yesterday with a cache of weapons, including hand grenades and an automatic rifle. Passaris, jailed for a series of violent crimes, has been on the run since February. He escaped after allegedly shooting dead two policemen escorting him to hospital. The botched raid was a further embarrassment for the Greek police, who are already suffering from a poor image after a series of notable failures to catch suspects and escaped felons. The fiasco also added to the worry that the Greek authorities may be incapable of providing adequate security during the 2004 Olympics. Just before his resignation, Gen Georgakopoulos defended the police action, describing it as a "well-planned operation that unfortunately failed in its execution". Gen Georgakopoulos replaced Thannasis Vassilopoulos, who quit in 1998 after two died died and 10 were injured during the botched police handling of a hostage crisis. AP, Athens

title: ECB ignores case for cut
bodyText: The European Central Bank yesterday shrugged off evidence of a worse than expected slowdown in the global economy and kept interest rates in the 12-nation zone unchanged at 4.5%. Although Bank of England fears about the darkening outlook for the world economy prompted a surprise cut in British interest rates yesterday, the ECB declined the opportunity to join global efforts to boost flagging growth. Its decision came despite data which showed economic confidence in Europe continuing to collapse and a further fall in US manufacturing orders as American industry struggles to climb out of recession. The ECB has cut interest rates once this year , compared with six cuts by the US Federal Reserve and four by the Bank of England's monetary policy committee. "Compared with the activist policies of the MPC and the US Fed, where the real economy is more of a priority, the ECB is starkly isolated," said Nigel Anderson at RBS Financial Markets. Survey evidence from the European commission yesterday showed industrial and consumer confidence in Europe falling last month as more and more companies such as Siemens, ABB and Alcatel announced hefty job cuts. In Germany, retailers are forecasting stagnant sales this year while the German chamber of commerce expects the economy to grow by between 1% and 2%. In France, the latest evidence showed consumer confidence at its lowest level for two years. "Economic fundamentals and developments, and certainly those for the last couple of weeks, tell us it is high time for the ECB to move," said Adolf Rosenstock, an economist at Nomura. However, analysts believe the bank will have to use the four-week break before the next meeting of its interest rate-setting governing council to prepare financial markets for a cut - and the reasoning behind such a move - or the ECB will risk damaging its credibility as it did with its only previous reduction in borrowing costs this year, when it cut rates to 4.5% in May. The Bank of England's decision to cut rates by 0.25 percentage points to 5% took financial markets by surprise and pushed sterling down by half a cent to $1.43 against the dollar. The euro ended the day little changed at $0.8830. Analysts had expected worries about inflation, including a buoyant housing sector and an increased appetite for credit, to persuade the committee to keep rates on hold. Although the general view is that yesterday's reduction in UK borrowing costs is likely to be the last in the current cycle and rates could start nudging up again next year, some analysts believe that the weakness in the global economy - cited by the MPC as the reason for yesterday's move - could force further downward action. "We see continued weakness in the global environment and a further impact on the UK. The weakness we have seen on the industrial side so far will spread into other areas of the domestic economy," said Jonathon Loynes at Capital Economics. Darren Winder at UBS Warburg said he was surprised the MPC had decided to cut rates. "The risk is that the domestic economy is perhaps stronger than the MPC realises and that the international economy improves sooner than anticipated." But he noted that the MPC would be able to move swiftly to raise rates if the upturn came through more rapidly than expected.

title: Keeper of the Queen's piece
bodyText: Sir Christopher Howes, the former surveyor who revolutionised one of Britain's dowdiest institutions, last night left his London office overlooking St James's Park for the last time, in the same quiet way as he arrived 12 years ago. A 10-gun salute from nearby Horseguards Parade could have been appropriate for the 59-year-old, whose work has swollen the public purse by turning the Queen into the country's top property developer. Sir Christopher has stepped down as chief executive of the Crown Estate, the country's biggest landowner, whose empire stretches far from Regent Street and takes in half the nation's foreshore. These assets are hereditary possessions of the monarch, although since 1760 any surplus made is handed over to the Treasury. Sir Christopher's swansong was to report annual profits of £148m - up from £133m the year before - which most private sector companies would be proud of given that they do not include any gains from asset sales. The value of Crown Estate holdings, which include Windsor Great Park and a gold mine in south Wales, reached £4bn, up 10% over the previous 12 months. Former ICI boss Sir Denys Henderson, the tough industrialist who is Crown Estate chairman, points out that the 15.7% return on investment property is better than the industry benchmark of 11.5%. He describes his outgoing chief executive as "energetic and determined" and adds that the Crown Estate has had to cope with falling agricultural land values and government limits on rent rises from affordable housing - something most private rivals are immune from. Sir Christopher might have earned a lot more wheeling and dealing in the private sector but he still received a decent £43,000 performance bonus last year. This on top of a beefy £195,000 annual salary and the perk of working at 16 Carlton House Terrace, with its finely restored Inigo Jones interiors. Valuable assets Entering that imposing building you might expect to find a rather gruff and pompous man, keenly aware of his status as a keeper of the Queen's most valuable assets, especially as Sir Christopher has dozens of letters after his name as might befit a loyal servant of the crown. He turns out to be a slightly shy if straight-talking man with a gentle manner and a sharp wit, which is no doubt useful for mixing dainty royal protocol with tough property negotiation. Underneath the soft façade is the strong will that has enabled the East Anglian-born Sir Christopher to bring much-needed change to what was seen as a staid and dull organisation criticised in the past by the National Audit Office, a watchdog for public bodies. Over the last decade he has pushed aside a more traditional civil service ethos at the Crown Estate for one that is overtly commercial. This involved the introduction of modern accounting methods and other standard private sector disciplines, such as setting targets for individual business units. Sir Christopher is an admirer of private sector methodology but critical of corporate excess. In the past he has griped about his own salary, but he is happy to take a sideswipe at rivals who spend the summer at Ascot, Goodwood or Aintree. He says he was a follower of the Third Way, mixing public with private disciplines, before it was trumpeted as a political doctrine by Tony Blair and former US president Bill Clinton. "I had seen it used in France and could never understand in the 1970s why the public and private sectors were not working together on urban regeneration," he says. He is proud that a strong public service commitment to fairness and social responsibility has been retained at the Crown Estate. He restructured the Queen's enterprises and survived the 1990s property recession with virtually no redundancies among his 500 staff. Admirers point out that he presided over a doubling of capital values during his 12-year tenure. He has not just been counting the pennies, but also spending them. In 2000 the Crown Estate invested £207m, double that of any previous year, and has been greedily buying back leases to wipe out problems of property vacancies. The organisation also has a £500m investment plan to refurbish Regent Street, which is the jewel in the crown of its 600 commercial and 2,400 residential properties. The Crown Estate is not just involved in upgrading historical buildings. It is also at the cutting edge of developing new industries such as renewable energy. This is the agency that grants leases to companies wishing to develop offshore wind farms. It is up to the Crown Estate to decide on the commercial viability of proposed plans. The agency also looks after 120,000 hectares of agricultural land, and so has been hit by the foot and mouth outbreak as farmers struggle to pay their rents. 'Little old Norfolk boy' They can expect sympathetic treatment from Sir Christopher, who is steeped in the traditions of the countryside after being raised in Norfolk. His father, who ran a family firm of surveyors and land agents, and whose ancestors acted for John Falstaff, was mayor of Norwich. After Gresham's School, Holt, and then a degree in estate management at the University of London, Sir Christopher spent 12 years working for his family in Norwich. He became honorary surveyor for the city's cathedral, married there and lived nearby. He was a member of Norwich city council and a magistrate. But he was always attracted by the bright lights of the capital and in 1979 took a job in Whitehall as head of the land policy unit at the Department of the Environment. He went on to become director of land and property there before joining the Crown Estate in 1989. His days in Whitehall allowed him to paint on a global canvas as well. He was chairman of the world congress on land policy in London and served as UK representative on committees of the OECD in Paris. His royal duties have given him the gongs that come with such jobs. He was made a Companion of the Order of the Bath in 1993 and four years later became a knight commander of the Victorian Order. But he likes to present himself as a "little old Norfolk boy" who wears a liberal heart on his sleeve. Given his strong views on the importance of maintaining the environment, his love of painting and architecture plus his mild manners it is not surprising he has found himself working for a kindred spirit in Prince Charles. "I am there [helping to oversee the Duchy of Cornwall] because of my professional experience, but I do think the Prince of Wales is an extraordinarily gifted person and I share with him an impatience for change," says Sir Christopher. Personal friends are more likely to be found in the world of the arts and media, and near his Suffolk home. It is not surprising to learn he is matey with humourist Richard Stilgoe, who also has East Anglian connections. Has Sir Christopher had any disappointments? He has so far failed to win support for extensive traffic calming measures for Regent Street. He champions pedestrianisation but faces opposition from local businesses and politicians. He rather regrets not having dabbled more in politics. This donnish polymath says his job did not allow him to lift his head to try a few other things. He does manage to get out a bit. He is a visiting lecturer at University College, London, and sits on the councils of both the Duchy of Cornwall for Prince Charles and the Duchy of Lancaster for the Queen. Sir Christopher is also keenly involved in the music festival at Aldeburgh, Suffolk, where he lives at weekends. So after more than a decade on the grindstone is he looking forward to retiring back to his rural home? He is not so sure. "As long as I feel energetic I want to work. Although I do not want to be a chief executive again, I would go mad sitting in the garden. I once tried to take a week off there, but after two days I was looking for things to do. I like the social and responsibility side of work, although when I exercise power it is usually an abysmal failure," he says in typical self-deprecating style. One soon gets the picture that Sir Christopher is retiring at 60 because that is what is stipulated in his contract. "It's a bit of a shame really, because it is only now I am beginning to cope with the job. It takes 12 years to understand it, it's so diverse and complicated," he laughs. 'At a bus stop' To avoid conflicts of interest he has avoided putting himself about on the job market, although he will hang on to a number of positions he already holds outside the Crown Estate. He says there are many options but he is cagey about going into detail. "I am at a bus stop and there are various vehicles coming but I am not sure which one I want to get on - and anyway I am a great believer in pedestrianisation." Sir Christopher's Crown Estate successor, former deputy Roger Bright, is no doubt committed but is unlikely to take the task quite so seriously as his predecessor did in his early days. Sir Christopher moved a bed into the office so he could work most of the night as well as the day. "I suffer from chronic conscientiousness," he says, clearly fretting that he may not be able to find a new outlet for his restless energy. The CV Born January 30, 1942 in Norfolk Education Gresham's School, Holt, Norfolk; University of London (BSc Economics); MPhil, University of Reading Career 1967/77, partner in firm of chartered surveyors in Norwich; 1978/81, deputy director, land economy, Department of the Environment; 1981/89, director, land economy, and land and property, DoE; 1989-2001, chief executive and commissioner of the Crown Estate Other interests Visiting professor in the Department of Architecture and Planning at University College, London ; 1985/86, deputy chairman and UK representative of the committee on urban issues, OECD, Paris; member of the Prince of Wales's council and the Duchy of Lancaster council; non-executive director, Norwich and Peterborough Building Society Family Married Clare Cunliffe in 1967. Two sons, one daughter Hobbies Music, painting, sailing and architecture

title: France? Simply a better place to live
bodyText: Those duplicitous and incomprehensible French! Their farmers, fishermen and lorry drivers hold not just France but the entire EU to ransom. Their politicians are corrupt. They have a 35-hour week and high taxes. Their public sector spends extravagantly. They champion fashionable left-wing causes, capping it last week with their ex-Trotskyite Prime Minister backing an impractical tax on foreign-exchange speculators. All proof that Britain should never have gone near the EU and why the euro must be resisted to the last. Under Lionel Jospin, France gets short shrift from the British media, reflecting deep-seated suspicions about matters French and, especially, French socialism. New Labour finds the Jospin government ideologically irresponsible. Its preoccupation with outmoded ideas like the social contract and the interests of workers consistently prevent the EU's 'modernisation' along the lines advocated by American conservatives. New Labour's hostility is part of a wider cultural divide. In a small village in the Gard valley, I learned how the villagers were coming to terms with the new English couple. Strident in their denunciations of the EU, local tradesmen and French public services, it was a matter of some wonder why they were in France at all. But what really fascinated the locals were the stories of her imported hostess trolley, complete with hot plates in a climate where midday temperatures climb to 38C, where pre-cooked chicken famously languished for hours before being served. The trolley had become a symbol of an unbridgeable cultural and culinary chasm. Last week, the editorial and City comment pages of our leading broadsheets were afflicted by hostess-trolley malaise over Lionel Jospin's willingness to throw France's weight behind the Tobin tax. Since economist James Tobin proposed a tiny tax on foreign-exchange transactions 30 years ago turnover in the foreign-exchange markets has increased 20 times while world GDP has grown at a tenth of the rate. Successive economies have been hit by irresistible speculation, and British manufacturing is currently on the rack as the foreign-exchange markets overvalue the pound. As more than 80 per cent of these transactions are reversed in a week, taxing foreign-exchange turnover would be an effective, if small, deterrent to some of the instability it generates. The difficulty is implementation, because every major financial centre would have to agree to make the tax policeable. But if one G7 country expressed interest, there is a chance of the necessary coalition being built. But this was not how Jospin's move was interpreted. The FT insisted he must have ulterior motives - placating the French anti-globalisation movement - to explain his otherwise silly idea, while the Guardian City pages included a bankers' homily in praise of liquidity as the lubrication of markets, missing the point that there can be too much liquidity which leaches into speculation, and which is the rationale for the tax. But behind those reflex responses stands an English cultural distrust which is all embracing. New Labour strategists will have groaned. Now they have another stupid idea coming from Europe which they will have to head off. That the Tobin tax or some variant might be an interesting proposition, that Jospin might actually believe in it and that there was now a coalition to be built was the ultra minority view. Britain's reflex opposition is rather depressing. The French are perfidious, remember, and have no grasp of the laws of economics or the practicalities of contemporary capitalism. Proof-positive of this is their 35-hour week. Only the economically illiterate could even begin to justify this massive, job-destroying infringement of labour market flexibility. Yet the paradox is that in the 20 months it has been going, France has generated more than 500,000 jobs, as an Industrial Society study explains.(France versus the Anglo-Saxon economists, www.indsoc.co.uk) The reason is that the legislation is not for a 35-hour week, but for a 1,600-hour year (250 hours less than we work) and that within this framework French employers and employees can organise enormously flexible work patterns and clever use of capital. It's part of a tradition of smart regulation that has led French output per man hour to grow explosively over the last 15 years, so that it is now 9 per cent higher than in the US. On the other hand, 20 years of labour market deregulation in Britain has hardly closed our productivity gap while leaving large sections of the community desperately poor. And what that has bought is a public infrastructure to die for. The Paris Metro system and the high-speed train network speak for themselves, but so do the spare capacity in their hospital system and the extraordinary investment in schools in low income areas. Equally, the concern to maintain the social contract means that French citizens can expect to retire with a decent pension, instead of increasingly having to rely on the lottery of the capital markets, as we do. Maybe the French state has shortcomings in delivering its objectives, while parts of its political and official class are mired in corruption. But the state can be reformed and politicians and officials made accountable. Meanwhile, we watch our financial and business class being paid stratospheric sums for superintending what remains a low-wage, low-productivity, low-investment economy, with negligible chances of their becoming more accountable. In short, the dreary laws of Gradgrind economics that we accept as axiomatic in Britain are disproved daily by our old rival. And the British are beginning to vote with their feet. We've bought half-a-million homes in France, not only because of the sunshine and the wine, but because it's a better place to live. Fourteen years of the socialist Mitterand and now four years of the socialist Jospin have left their mark - and from which we could learn. Down with the culture of the hostess trolley and euro-scepticism. Vive smart regulation. Vive the social contract. Vive high public spending. Vive la France.

title: Time running out for Tempus
bodyText: One of the most bruising takeover battles in the history of the advertising industry is reaching its climax. The clash has all the ingredients of City drama on a grand scale: media bosses with big egos, Anglo-French antagonism and bucket-loads of cash. At the centre of the joust is Sir Martin Sorrell, chief executive of WPP, the world's second-largest advertising agency after IPG of the US. 'People have called me all sorts of things: ruthless, ambitious, obstinate, a serial pessimist - but you'll have to ask others about that. What I can tell you is that I am a businessman, and my first duty has always been to add shareholder value. That's what this battle is all about,' he says. Sorrell, a former finance director with Saatchi & Saatchi, is holidaying on the Cte D'Azur, where he owns a second home. Shortly before he set off for 'his favourite country in Europe', WPP, a British company with a market value of £7 billion, launched a counter-bid for London-based rival Tempus, headed by Chris Ingram. Earlier, Tempus had agreed to a £425 million offer from Havas of France, which is led by the urbane Alain de Pouzilhac. Sorrell is a cunning operator and gives the impression of someone who couldn't give a damn whether he wins the fight or not. 'I'm really not worried. Tempus would be a good buy for us, but it is not absolutely necessary. We won't pay over the odds. At the moment, I'm enjoying the French sunshine, playing cricket and eating good food. Do I sound like I am fretting?' In the next few days, WPP will send details of its offer to Tempus's shareholders. Once Sorrell's proposals are known in full, De Pouzilhac is expected to strike back with an even higher cash offer than WPP's package of 551p a share, which values the company at £440m. The Square Mile is awash with rumours about what the French will come up with: some say they would overstretch themselves if they offered more than 575p a share. But, De Pouzilhac could call Sorrell's bluff and offer 600p. However, with the advertising market in a tailspin, this in no time to overpay - so shareholders on both sides of the Channel are nervously awaiting the next move. Sorrell looks to be sitting pretty whichever way the battle goes. WPP has built up a 22 per cent stake in Tempus over the past five years. That means Sorrell, whose 1 per cent stake in WPP is worth nearly £100m, could afford to pay much more for Tempus than Havas, which must bid for the entire company. If Sorrell decides to walk away, WPP will make a massive return on its shares in Tempus, which were acquired for an average price of 250p. Tempus's stock price closed at 554p on Friday. But if Sorrell decides that he really wants Tempus, he will need to sweet-talk Ingram. The two men have never got on, partly because Ingram has always viewed WPP's stake - acquired in 1996 - as an affront. Sorrell, however, claims that reports of animosity between the two men have been overplayed. 'We met a couple of weeks ago in the Howard Hotel in London and we got on perfectly well,' he says. Advertising is a people business, so a hostile bid would be pointless: Tempus's senior executives could simply walk out of the door, taking most of the agency's clients with them. But Sorrell has a problem in that 39 of Tempus's top managers, including Ingram, have agreed to accept the French offer. Nonetheless, he believes that cash talks louder than words, and he is almost certainly right. After all, Tempus is a public company and the shareholders will expect Ingram to accept the highest bid. If WPP offers enough, the company is assuming that Havas will simply cave in. When and if that happens, Sorrell believes that most of Tempus's managers will fall into line. But Sorrell does not mind admitting that he feels misled by Ingram, who has always contended that Tempus would retain its independence. 'As you can imagine, it was a surprise when he agreed to an offer from Havas; at one point, when we thought he might be a target, we offered to be a white knight. But that never went anywhere; we did not think there was a deal in the offing.' Tempus is a media planning and buying agency, which means it comes up with ways for clients to maximise their public exposure, and then purchases airtime or advertising space on their behalf. Ingram's company would fit nicely into similar operations owned by either Havas or WPP. Needless to say, Sorrell believes the fit with WPP would be better - 'It would be more complementary in that we are strong in the US and they have a good position in Europe. There is more overlap with Havas.' Consolidation has been driven by the desire of advertisers to concentrate their buying with fewer agencies. The bigger the agency, the heftier its purchasing power, which means the advertisers can get a tailored, global service for less money. Another factor propelling consolidation is the fact that the market is entering a downturn; mergers are a defensive move in that they increase client rolls at a time when revenues are falling. Sorrell, who is 56, is the only child of a prosperous London family. He went to Cambridge, took an MBA at Harvard and then moved on to join sports promoter Mark McCormack. But it was when he worked with the Saatchis in the 1970s that he first came to prominence. Under Sorrell, WPP has attracted heavyweight clients such as Ford, Unilever, American Express and IBM. Over the years, he has gained a reputation for being combative, but most find him charming and agreeable. Few would deny that he has been tremendously successful - over the past two years, WPP has been one of the best performing stocks in the FTSE 100 index. Above all, Sorrell is a survivor. In the early Nineties, WPP nearly went bust after being left with a mountain of debt after acquiring US agencies J Walter Thompson and Ogilvy & Mather on the eve of recession. The banks agreed to bail the company out, but it was a close-run thing. In the mid-Nineties, Sorrell oversaw an astonishing recovery. Today, WPP's balance sheet is far stronger than a decade ago. Its interest payments are relatively low and a large number of staff are on temporary contracts, which means it will be easy to axe staff and reduce costs. The payroll has already fallen from 53,000 to 50,000 to take account of softening demand. Sorrell said earlier this month that he expected advertising conditions to get worse before they get better, even though WPP posted better than expected half-time profits. But he is not running around like a headless chicken, worrying about the possibility of a prolonged global slump. He describes as 'biblical' the prediction of Warren Buffet, the legendary US investor, that it could take seven years to work through the excesses of the Nineties. 'He seems to be saying that seven fat years will be followed by seven lean ones. That might be so, but there again, it may not be as simple as that.' Is it ever?

title: Game of high stakes and low cunning
bodyText: It seems a pity to drag Sir Martin Sorrell back to reality as he enjoys the pleasures of life in la France profonde, but he really should take a look at the latest comments from Zenith, the big media buyer owned by two of his main rivals, Cordiant and Publicis. Zenith believes a recovery in ad spend is unlikely before 2003 because of the possibility of a slide in consumer confidence. The implication is that share values in media companies are still overvalued. Sorrell should think on this as he reaches for another glass of rosé , and reassess how far he can take his game of poker with Havas, his French rival in the contested bid for media buyer Tempus. He already has a 550p cash per share bid on the table, topping the Havas cash and paper bid. He has forced the French to contemplate lifting their bid if they are to stand any chance of getting Tempus. He must hope that they will do so. For Sorrell it would neatly kill two birds - he would have forced a rival to overpay, while netting a nice profit on his own 22 per cent stake in Tempus - worth perhaps £130 million. It would also leave him with a topped-up war-chest to pursue the other available media-buying group, Aegis. This is the real prize. Tempus advisers are right when they point out that the company is one of only two big independent media buyers in the world. What they don't like to emphasise is how far behind the leader Tempus is. At a take-out price of £1.2 billion, Aegis is nearly three times the size of its nearest rival. There is also the question of staff loyalty in this ultimate people business. On this level, Aegis would also be a far cleaner deal for Sorrell. Tempus chairman Chris Ingram may now deny that he ever said that he'd rather lick the floor of an abattoir than work for Sorrell but a hostile victory for WPP would doubtless still involve the departure of some key Tempus executives, and leave some of the rest sullen and demoralised. In contrast, as Richard Wachman reports on page one, Aegis executives would welcome a deal with Sorrell. The WPP bid document, which should be out in the next fortnight or so, will no doubt be keen to stress how much it wants Tempus, and will leave room for a counter-counterbid, to top Havas again. But Sorrell knows that Aegis is the better deal, and must hope that his brinkmanship pays off. The danger is that Havas does not increase its bid, and leaves the field clear for WPP. It would not be good for Sorrell to be the resented owner of a company trailing in second place in a recession-hit market. He should contemplate that by his pool on the Cte d'Azur.

title: Smashing stuff
bodyText: The marketing industry is not having a very good time of it at the moment. Budgets are being slashed, agencies are cutting staff and pundits are speaking in gloomy tones of the worst advertising recession in 20 years. But new research into attitudes to advertising could provide a glimmer of hope. Far from being an excuse to stick the kettle on or nip to the loo, the TV commercial break is, it turns out, surprisingly popular. A new survey conducted by the market analysts BMRB shows more than one in five viewers over the age of 15 actually enjoys the ads on TV more than the programmes. Nearly a quarter said they found TV ads interesting and it gave them something to talk about, while 22% enjoyed the ads so much they watched them even when watching a video. Interestingly, those least likely to enjoy the commercial break were in the AB social grades. Similarly, those who found the commercial break the most entertaining part of the evening's viewing tended to be younger, in the lower social grades and to live in the Midlands and the North-West. "One reason for this may be that the young, and those in lower social grades, are heavier users of commercial broadcast media than their older, wealthier counterparts," says James Powell of BMRB. Predictably, heavy commercial radio listeners were 22% more likely than the average to find ITV advertising more entertaining than the programmes. And heavy ITV viewers were 50% more likely than average to find TV advertising interesting. The bad news is that over the years viewers' tolerance of advertising has declined slightly - although this could have as much to do with the fact that we are now having to watch so much more of it as with the quality of the ads.
