In 1992, when BMRB last conducted the research, nearly a third of those surveyed said they enjoyed TV ads as much as the programmes, and the proportion of people who expect advertising to be entertaining has also slipped. The increasing amount of commercial airspace available to advertisers is a double- edged sword. Thanks to the explosion of digital channels and the increased amount of advertising allowed on ITV, advertisers can now peddle their wares in ever more places. And lower prices mean smaller companies that were once unable to consider TV as an advertising medium can now afford it. But you can have too much of a good thing, as British viewers are beginning to discover. As TV audiences fragment, advertising is having to target smaller groups of people - and that often means lower production budgets and lower-quality commercials. But despite the waning appeal of ads, we are still way beyond our European counterparts when it comes to enjoying the commercial breaks. Research conducted last year by Lowe Lintas, the advertising agency whose clients include Stella Artois, Reebok and Tesco, showed the British are far bigger ad-lovers than the French or the Germans. The results indicated that just half of all UK viewers switch channels during the break - compared with more than 55% in France and 68% in Germany. And Britain's love of advertising has long been recognised by the tabloid newspapers. Where else would a national newspaper devote its front page to an image from a commercial as the Mirror did recently with the latest Heineken campaign, in which viewers are blackmailed into buying Heineken with the threat of Paul Daniels and co singing Carpenters hits? When Channel 4 broadcast its 100 Greatest TV Ads last year, it was inundated with calls from viewers keen to register their votes for old favourites such as the Smash Martians, R White's secret lemonade drinker and the long-running "Happiness is..." campaign for Hamlet cigars. But our love of advertising is not just driven by nostalgia. Recent ads - those that ran in the 1990s - actually accounted for six of the top 10 best-loved TV ads in Channel 4's poll, with the top spot going to Guinness's "Surfer", an ad made in 1999. The fact that the programme, basically a three-hour showing of old ads, pulled in four million viewers on a bank holiday Monday night on Channel 4 is testimony to our love of advertising. All of which has to be good news for the advertising industry. It might seem obvious, but consumer surveys show we are far more likely to remember ads that we like than those we do not - something advertisers should bear in mind.

title: Bridgend 24 - 30 Harlequins
bodyText: The Bridgend assistant coach Richard Webster, dismissed Harlequins last week as a rich, posh club, an observation given testimony by the visiting supporters who took over a section of the stand rather than assume the position for away fans on the popular bank. It was rich coming from Webster considering Bridgend were Welsh rugby's biggest spenders this summer. Harlequins are regarded in Wales as a soft touch, but Saturday night's win maintained an unbeaten record there in European games. It was an open contest with both sides regularly losing the ball. Bridgend had more play but struggled against an obdurate defence: the first of their two second half tries followed a lucky bounce and the other, in the game's final minute, came after the replacement flanker Pat Sanderson was sin-binned for preventing release of the ball at a ruck. Quins were leading 30-17 when Sanderson departed but they were hanging on at the end after Dafydd James had forced his way over the line. "We should have shut them out at the end," said the Quins' director of rugby John Kingston. "Things like that come with habit, and while we are getting better, we are still nothing to write home about." While Bridgend applied pressure, Harlequins were more inventive, prompted by outside-half Paul Burke, and their third try 17 minutes from the end was a gem with Matt Moore coming into midfield from the wing to free Luger. The result was hard on Bridgend but no more than Quins deserved. "We must be the most attractive losers in Wales," said the Bridgend coach Dennis John. It was Bridgend's fourth home defeat in five matches this season, poor reward for the outlay which brought six internationals to the Brewery Field during the summer. "We defended well and that was why we won," said Kingston. "We are winning despite not getting anywhere near our potential." With players like Will Greenwood to return and with the No 8 Tony Diprose starting to savour defensive duties, Quins are more spice than posh. Bridgend: Durston; Jones, Thomas, Funnell (Devereux 68min), James; Rees, Harries (capt; Hewlett 68); Loader, Williams, Noon, Clapham (Stewart 61), Stephens, Molitika (Budgett 61), Ringer, Bryan. Scorers: Tries: Durston, James. Conversion: Rees. Penalties: Rees 4. Harlequins: Mapletoft; Moore, Greenstock, Bell, Luger; Burke, Bemand; Starr (Leonard 57), Wood, Olver, Morgan (capt), White-Cooper, Winter, Tamarua (Sanderson 72), Diprose. Scorers: Tries: Burke, Tamarua, Luger. Conversions: Burke 3. Penalties: Burke 3. Sin-bin: Sanderson 74. Referee: J Dume (France). Attendance : 4,000.

title: Brown rejects tax rise to protect public projects
bodyText: Gordon Brown will today reject Labour backbench and trade union calls to raise taxes to protect the government's commitment to higher public services spending against the threat of global recession, following the attacks in the US. The chancellor yesterday did not rule out extra government borrowing if required to keep additional funds flowing to hospitals, schools, transport needs and crime prevention - a suggestion made more explicitly yesterday by Tony Blair on BBC1's Breakfast with Frost programme. Mr Blair said the "fundamentals of the British economy" were strong, and the government could loosen monetary policy if necessary. Mr Brown matched a determination not to be blown off course by the deflationary impact of the US attacks with a list of steps to prevent that happening and to curb international terrorism. He faces a double agenda, meeting the challenge of global terrorism while protecting domestic achievements. But he said he was confident that his "caution and prudence" policy had given Britain more latitude than some threatened economies. Because of its long battle against Irish terrorism Britain had already done much of what the UN now wanted states to do to implement a "no safe havens for terrorism" policy. Mr Brown is also to reveal that £61.5m worth of Taliban funds in UK banks has been frozen, compared with £2.79m similarly frozen by France.

title: David Walker: Guns or butter?
bodyText: Gordon Brown told the cabinet the other day that paying for war would soon squeeze other (social) spending. For him the crisis is opportune. With colleagues clamouring for money, it suits him to say we cannot have both guns and butter. Privately, though, Brownites are cheery. Despite economic downturn, the Treasury is still rolling in money. It will not need to borrow this financial year - Goldman Sachs estimates the surplus at £200m. As for spending, the "contingency reserve" for 2001-02, worth £2.25bn, has been raided mainly for foot and mouth. But Whitehall is awash with money underspent in earlier years. Some of this funny money was included in plans for 2001-02. The chart shows the estimates: billions are available for recycling this financial year and, paradoxically, planning to spend more may increase this year's undershoot. Armchair generals in action since September 11 have tended to overestimate the impact of new UK military commitments. The huge exercise under way in Oman was planned and costed a while ago. Special forces are already in the budget and their new deployment is unlikely to cost a great deal. Labour's 1998 defence review, the basis on which defence spending estimates are prepared, envisaged fighting one and a half hot wars at any one time, excluding Northern Ireland. Previous conflicts were not especially dear: the Gulf war consumed only an extra 1% of total public spending. Additional police and security costs at home are, thus far, marginal. Besides, Mr Brown is extremely anxious (he wants to damage David Blunkett as a rival in the leadership stakes) to compare Home Office claims with its own admission of police service inefficiencies. In budgetary terms, any UK contribution to an operation in Afghanistan would cost very little extra and would, as things stands, not disturb Mr Brown's basic financial plan unless it became a fixed commitment lasting years. In his plan, the government slips into the red next year and, for the first time for a while, there will actually be a public-sector "borrowing requirement". But the markets have been primed and for the state to spend more than its income when private business is in the doldrums makes perfect sense, even in this post-Keynesian age. Gordon Brown's "golden rule" says long-term borrowing is justified only for investment - what else, you might ask, is spending on long-term national security? When the economic cycle turns up (in 2003-04 or thereabouts), Brownian motion will restore the public accounts to surplus. But the Brown plan is based on spending assumptions that are already out of date. Last month Whitehall departments began marshalling their claims for more. This is the comprehensive spending review, which next summer will produce a new map of spending to run from April 2003 to March 2006. In fact, some new money was supposed to kick in as soon as April 2002. This is the first big spending exercise of the second term and thus a defining moment for Labour. The Institute for Fiscal Studies has done some simple arithmetic for 2003-06. To keep the current rate of increase in spending on schools and hospitals going, Labour would have either to borrow or raise taxes to the tune of about £5bn extra a year. But even before, say, Estelle Morris worries about 2004, there is the present to think about. Teachers' pay has to rise if the slice of the best and brightest going into the schools is to stop declining - and as soon as possible. Top universities have to be more generously funded if their international standing is not going to be lost. The academic appointments that will lose or secure it are being made now. And so on, in transport, childcare (Sure Start runs out soon), pensions and rural affairs. Then there is defence. Even an I-speak-your-weight loyalist such as Geoff Hoon will soon be briefing about the need to stop the decline in defence's share of national income. The dividend from the end of the cold war has come and gone. When the Berlin Wall fell defence was 5% of GDP; now it is below 2.5%. Whatever else George Bush's war on terrorism turns out to be, it is a long-run enterprise. Even if eventually it is about domestic counter-terrorism rather than armed intervention overseas, the next decade is likely to see strong upwards pressure on defence budgets everywhere. Despite cuts, the UK spends disproportionately on defence, compared with other European Union countries aside from France. In a fairer world, UK defence spending might be pooled within the EU with countries such as Germany and Italy taking a larger share. But both those are facing growing budget deficits - already - and neither Gerhard Schröder nor Silvio Berlusconi shows signs of telling their respective electorates the hard fiscal truth about the costs of welfare and war. There has been a lot of talk since September 11 about the return of "big government", even in the United States, as people have turned, reflexively, to the state for succour, security and insurance. But for the proportion of GDP spent on government to grow electorates will have to be willing to pay more tax. Labour, Gordon Brown in particular, has shown a remarkable unwillingness to "think on its feet" while in office and to revise the nostrums with which it came to power. Such as its antagonism to tax. You can have guns and butter (and social justice) but only if higher rate payers of income tax assent.

title: Hewitt calls summit to help factories
bodyText: The government yesterday called for a "manufacturing summit" next month to help Britain's battered industry as four separate surveys showed firms in the UK, the US, the Eurozone and Japan all suffering from a squeeze on jobs and output. Patricia Hewitt, the trade and industry secretary, bowed to mounting pressure from companies and trade unions to hold the crisis talks after hearing that the aftershocks from the terrorist attacks on the US had intensified the pressure on firms already under threat from the global slowdown and the strength of the pound. Speaking at the Labour Party conference where the chancellor, Gordon Brown, said there would be new innovation tax breaks and investment allowances for industry, Ms Hewitt said: "There is real pain in our manufacturing industry - workers with 10, 20 and 30 years of skill and experience are gutted to find themselves thrown out of work." The summit will bring ministers together with business and union leaders - and chairmen of regional development agencies which are to be entrusted with countering the downturn, which has already pushed manufacturing into recession. Data from the chartered institute for purchasing and supply showed that the order books for British factories are shrinking at their fastest rate for more than 2 years, and that the manufacturing sector contracted for the 7th month. The CIPS recorded a lack of demand from overseas, but said there were few reports linking sagging demand last month with the terrorist attacks. In the US, the monthly national association of purchasing managers' survey indicated that American manufacturing might have been bottoming out last month after 14 months of decline. Analysts warned that any hope of imminent recovery had been snuffed out by the events of September 11. With consumer confidence dropping sharply, economists expect demand for manufacturing goods to fall. The Reuters eurozone purchasing managers' index showed manufacturing in the Eurozone contracting for the 6th month, and at the fastest pace since the survey was introduced four years ago. There were record low findings for Germany, France, Spain, Italy, Ireland and Austria - demonstrating evidence that the slowdown is persistent and widespread in Europe. Japan's main measure of business performance - the quarterly Tankan survey - reported a dramatic deterioration in confidence since the attacks on the US. Optimism among the leading firms in Japan is falling at the second fastest rate on record.

title: Mend the gap
bodyText: The government needs to increase industrial aid dramatically to expand business and create jobs in Britain's less favoured regions, if the gap between the prosperous south and the rest of the country is to be narrowed, a group of senior economists and geographers will argue today. In a critical analysis of government policy towards the regions, the academics claim that 1.4m jobs need to be created to put the old industrial heartlands of Wales, Scotland and northern England on a par with the south east. The group of 10 behind a new report - Labour's New Regional Policy: An Assessment* - argue that claimant jobless figures below a million are badly distorted by a huge hidden level of unemployment. It accuses ministers of placing too much faith in the ability of so-called high-technology "clusters" to generate the jobs and growth needed to create a level economic playing field. It challenges the government to introduce an active regional policy, greatly expanding the current regional selective assistance budget of about £400m annually. In its heyday 20 years ago, the report calculates that, at today's prices, more than £2bn a year in regional aid went to the poorer regions. Anticipating that new Labour will dismiss its findings as a call for a return to old, discredited policies, the report argues that the strategy of the 1960s and 1970s - under which billions of pounds was poured into new industries - was successful in creating hundreds of thousands of jobs in areas of greatest need. "The government should build on the lessons of the past not discard hard-won experience." It warns that Britain is near the bottom of the European league for regional aid spending - devoting half as much as France to industrial support and only one sixth as much as German. A greater level of support is allowed under EU rules. The report adds: "Far from being the tool that the economy needs for the new century, Labour's new regional policy seems designed to meet the challenges of the 1980s and the 1990s. Mass unemployment everywhere is no longer the problem; widespread joblessness across northern industrial Britain still is." While welcoming noises about the need to help under-performing regions the report complains that the government's aspirations are not matched by its policies. *Regional Studies Association. PO Box 2058, Seaford, BN25 4QU. Email rsa@mailbox.ulcc.ac.uk

title: Takeover talk sends dealers scurrying
bodyText: Having spent most of the session bored at their desks, London's dealers suddenly found themselves with a couple of hot stories to play with late yesterday. In unusually brisk turnover of 27m shares, Christian Salvesen, the logistics group, finished the session 9.5p higher at 86.5p. Explanations for the sharp rise differed. One school of thought said a bid was on the way with 10% shareholder, AB Custos, named as a possible predator. Analysts said this was a reasonable assumption, given that Salvesen rejected a 200p a share offer from AB in the summer of last year. Others said the move was related to the clearance of a large stock overhang. Apparently one institution decided to liquidate its holding and, thanks to diligent work by its brokers, found a home for the stock. The buyers, it seems, believe interim results from Salvesen - due on December 4 - will not disappoint. Indeed, a few weeks ago, Salvesen boss Edward Roderick appeared at a transport conference organised by investment bank UBS and, according to those present, his tone was upbeat. The day's other tale revolved around Celltech, Britain's biggest biotech company, and talk that it was close to merging with US peer Biogen. True or not, dealers who were trying to buy Celltech stock for clients found the task all but impossible as the sellers pulled their offers. Sector specialists say a combination of the two companies would be a sound strategic move. Celltech closed 57p higher at 984p. Meanwhile, events in the wider market were being driven by more concrete factors. After the strongest showing on Wall Street in three weeks, leading shares got off to a good start. But volume was light, with few traders willing to open positions ahead of employment figures from the US. Following Thursday's grim news from the American manufacturing sector, many were bracing themselves for further bad news. In the event, that caution proved unwarranted. Despite the unemployment rate rising to its highest level in nearly five years in October, markets on both side of the Atlantic remained calm. Dealers said investors were taking the view that the figures guaranteed a 50 basis-point reduction in interest rates at next week's Federal Reserve meeting. All of which left the FTSE 100 index 58.3 points higher at 5,129.5, extending its winning streak to three sessions. Over the week, however, the index lost 59 points. British Airways gained 5.75p to 146.75p as the company moved to increase its the cash on its balance sheet by selling its remaining stake in France Télécom for £23m. However, dealers remain concerned it could be kicked out the FTSE 100 on November 15 when the demerger of BT's mobile phone arm leaves the index with one too many companies. Other candidates for the chop are United Business Media, up 13p to 457p, and fund manager Schroders, 40p stronger at 726p. Tesco, 1p better at 246.5p, also ended higher despite a bearish research report from ABN Amro. Repeating its "sell" recommendation, the Dutch house said recent market share data suggested a slowdown in sales at Tesco since late August. Among the mid caps, Selfridges, the upmarket department store, declined 8.5p to 268.5p as UBS Warburg took the red pen to its profits forecasts, citing this week's poor retail sales figures. Cutting its estimate for the current year by 8% to £41m, UBS said the slowdown in consumer spending was likely to have a disproportionate effect on Selfridges because of its reliance on central London - 91% of group profits. Rival Debenhams was also unsettled by Warburg's call, losing 13.5p to 354p, while discount retailer Matalan, down 14.5p at 331p, was also in the doldrums despite chairman John Hargreaves revealing he had purchased 1m shares at 348.35p on Thursday. His stake now stands at 26.9%. Marks & Spencer managed to buck the weak trend among the retailers, gaining 3.5p to 298p after the company completed the sale of eight stores in Spain. The stock was also boosted by talk of strong current trading. Although the firm will not provide an update on trading with next week's interim numbers, dealers hear that the company had its busiest day of the year two weeks ago. This followed a promotion in which M&S offered 10% off all goods for customers using its charge card. Hamleys, the Regent Street toy shop, was also in demand, rising 15p to 117.5p as retail investors bet the release of the Harry Potter film on November 16 would stimulate sales in the run-up to Christmas. Analysts were less impressed with that line of argument - not least because of Hamleys' reliance on overseas tourists, who have been avoiding central London since September 11. Cruise company P&O Princess extended its recent strong run, gaining 14p to 252p, after Deutsche Bank placed the stock on its mid-cap focus list. The German broker reckons concerns over falling passenger numbers are fully factored into the company's share price. In the small cap world, there was a flurry of interest in ML Laboratories, unchanged at 35p. Once again speculation that the company is preparing to break itself up in attempt to boost its flagging share price was behind the activity. In January ML, which most analysts regard as an unwieldy biotech conglomerate, hired investment bank NM Rothschild to carry out a strategic review with the aim of determining how it "might best optimise shareholder value". According to the gossips, the company is close to announcing the results of that review and is likely to recommend the sale of various divisions. The gossips reckon the sales will make a mockery of ML's current valuation of £54m.

title: Wenger is stuck at the crossroads
bodyText: A strange fixture intrudes on the calendar tomorrow night: Arsenal v Manchester United, just a day after both play important Premiership matches. Do not, consequently, expect this to be one of the games of the season, which is more likely to occur in three Sundays time when the two meet in the League, again at Highbury. For this irritant to both clubs, crammed into a crammed schedule, is the third round of the Worthington Cup and as such will be largely a reserve-team contest. What it could well show is which club have the greater depth of resources for when injuries and suspensions bite later in the season as they pursue honours in both Premiership and Champions League. Recent evidence suggests it will be United. For it occurred, watching Arsenal lose to Schalke 04 in familiar fashion in midweek - concede early goal away, man sent off - just what a collection of dud purchases Arsène Wenger has made over the past few years. They came, they saw, they confused. Remember David Grondin? No, neither do I. There are plenty of others who have barely scratched the consciousness: Christopher Wreh, Alberto Mendez, Kaba Diawara, Stefan Malz, Tomas Danilevicius, Igor Stepanovs, for example. A few more are currently threatening to join the list, among them Oleg Luzhny and Edu. In all, Wenger has spent £110 million on transfers during his five years as manager, recouping £80 million. It is not a serious deficit for a big, buying club and one that will easily have been made up with the revenue he has brought in from the Double of 1998 and the subsequent Champions League campaigns. In brownie points he will, too, forever be in credit after making Patrick Vieira his first signing for £3.5 million, about a tenth of his current worth. Then there was Nicolas Anelka, bought for £500,000 and sold for £23 million two years later. Those deals do mask some seriously questionable business, however, which has left the reserves stuffed with players on whom Wenger has gambled several million here and there but who seem unlikely to make any consistent impact, such as Junichi Inamoto. It has led, I understand, to concern among Highbury youth coaches about where the players they develop will progress once they reach a certain age. And not just the English. There is the outstanding German youth player Moritz Volz. It all leaves Wenger, and Arsenal, at a crossroads. All seems to be well - hovering around the top of the Premiership, into the second phase of Champions League with mouthwatering contests against Juventus, Deportivo La Coruna and Bayer Leverkusen to come - but still he does not sign a new contract to take him beyond the end of this season. On Friday, he said that next week's international break would give him the chance to conclude talks. 'It will be sorted out,' he insisted. Does that mean it will be signed? Sorted out could mean signed or rejected. 'Exactly,' he responded with a smile. Various reasons have been advanced for his reluctance to sign, from the club's stadium wrangles to concern about the youth policy or even their ambition, to how much money he will granted for transfers or how his salary can be paid in a tax-efficient way. It is none of these, he said. 'It is just small things linked with my contract, details that I cannot give out,' he added with an inscrutability he must have acquired from his period in Japan. Reports linking him with the Manchester United job show, he said, that 'at least the journalists have not lost imagination in England', but he would not be human if the prospect did not intrigue him, or he did not recognise that he had considerable bargaining power. It could just be, too, that he is re-evaluating where he is with Arsenal - where the team are heading - before deciding. Wenger has recently been bogged down in the question of whether his four French players should travel with the national team to Australia this week, though it should have been simple enough. He has been fortunate that France have played so many home, non-competitive games, and now comes one away. Such is international football, the smooth and the rough. It has apparently depressed him, however, and he has been deemed non-patriotic in his homeland. Arsenal's away form in Europe - eight defeats in nine matches - shows the defects of a team between eras. There is little doubt that Wenger signed Luzhny as Lee Dixon's replacement but such has been his poor form that the Cameroon midfielder Lauren has been remodelled. As well as Dixon, David Seaman, Tony Adams and Martin Keown are also injured - not to mention close to retirement - and the replacements, Richard Wright excepted, look none too convincing. Ashley Cole, needing solidity alongside, has consequently suffered from that difficult-second-season syndrome. Luzhny's dismissal in Gelsenkirchen - the thirty-eighth red card under Wenger - also showed how indiscipline has contributed to their downfall at key moments. In that, Wenger has had a block. Too often he has put the away form down to a referee's decision instead of acknowledging that it is self-inflicted. No one can doubt that Wenger has brought a flair and vitality to Highbury and none would want a return to the days of clean sheets and goals poached on the break. There is a growing feeling, however, that Wenger has himself failed to recreate the balance he had when Emmanuel Petit was alongside Vieira in midfield as defensive shield, that the balance has tilted too far the other way with forward players unwilling to defend from the front. His hope is that Giovanni Van Bronckhorst can become a central pillar, though often he has been played wide. What, though, if and when Vieira decides to move on? During forthcoming matches, Juventus officials will doubtless be drooling again. The time was ripe for Wenger and his progressive methods in England. He did not need the five years it took Sir Alex Ferguson to change the culture of Manchester United. Wenger knew of players he could get for reasonable sums who would change fortunes very quickly. Now, so it has appeared in the past 18 months, he has trouble buying the best. One thing he will surely want to know in talks with the Arsenal vice-chairman David Dein about that contract is whether he is going to be able to spend sums more like £20 million than several. He needs fewer gambles who end up in the reserves and more certainties. Comparatively meaningless tomorrow night's match may be, but it might tell Wenger much as he steps out of the daily hurly-burly into the calm of an international break. He remains an engaging man - and a shrewd one. He knows that managers can have a natural span with a club, that to stay might compound rather than solve problems. And he will be wondering whether he has enough in reserve.

title: Banks poised to reduce rates again
bodyText: The world's central bankers will try their best to boost confidence this week against a background of unremitting gloom. First up will be the US Federal Reserve led by Alan Greenspan, who has emerged as America's most authoritative economic voice in an administration short of figures of real economic clout. As the Fed meets tomorrow, Mr Greenspan is cast in the role of the cavalry riding to the rescue as the world's largest economy finally sinks into recession after 10 years of uninterrupted growth, the longest boom in US history. Mr Greenspan has done his damnedest to keep the American economy afloat, by cutting rates since the beginning of the year in a pre-emptive campaign to avoid a hard landing. Already beginning to slow at the beginning of 2001, the US economy shrank by an annual rate of 0.4% in the third quarter and is set to contract further in the present quarter. That would put the US into recession - defined as two consecutive quarters of no growth. The Fed is widely expected to cut rates by a half-point to 2%, the lowest since 1961, amid expectations that rates will come down to 1% next year. But there are limits to what the cavalry can do and despite the rate cuts, there is little confidence that Mr Greenspan's efforts will put American back on the path to growth any time soon. Most economists expect the US unemployment rate, which sank to 3.9% 13 months ago, to hit 6% next year, its highest level for the first time since 1994. On Thursday, the Bank of England and the European Central Bank are also expected to follow the US lead. Easier to read than the ECB, the Bank of England is widely expected to lop off another quarter-point to bring rates down to 4.25%, the lowest in 30 years. Through a combination of good luck and sound economic management, Britain is best-placed to weather the economic turbulence ahead. For this year, the world's fourth-largest economy is expected to meet its target of between 2.2% and 2.5% and while growth will taper off next year, the UK is expected to be the fastest-growing member of the G7 group of industrialised countries. But as the chancellor, Gordon Brown, noted yesterday, Britain cannot stay immune from ripple effects from the US and Europe. Figures released today from the Office of National Statistics underlined his point. It reported that Britain's manufacturing sector recorded its biggest monthly production drop in almost a decade in September as it sank deeper into recession. The slump in manufacturing coincided with a weak report on activity in the services sector, which far outweighs manufacturing in terms of UK economic activity. The report from the Chartered Institute of Purchasing and Supply showed that key services business index activity fell to 46.3% in October from 48.1% in the previous month. October's figure was the worst since the survey began in July 1996, so the warning signs for the UK economy are there. The Bank of England has little choice but keep on cutting rates. So we come to the ECB, the most problematic of the central banks. The ECB has passed up several opportunities to cut rates in recent months, much to the despair of eurozone politicians who had hoped that Wim Duisenberg, the ECB president, would take as proactive a stand as his US counterpart in the face of the global slowdown. But the ECB remains obsessed by inflation, even though recession is the paramount threat facing the continental European economies. Growth is at a standstill in Germany, although as Avinash Persaud, an analyst with State Street, the US financial services company, notes, it would be incorrect to equate Germany with the eurozone. "France is unlikely to be in recession," says Mr Persaud, who, like many in the City, expects the ECB to cut rates by 0.25% on Thursday. ECB board member Eugenio Domingo Solans today hinted at a rate cut, when he said the ECB expected the outlook for inflation would improve as the eurozone economy slowed and interest rates would respond accordingly. "I think the bank is prudently taking into account, basically as a priority, inflation, but of course, without ignoring that, there is an economic slowdown here that translates into better inflation expectations and that also has to have - of course, is having - its response in interest rates," Mr Solans said in Madrid. As far as the ECB goes, which has a done a poor job of signalling interest rate moves, that seems to indicate that this time it will not disappoint policy makers as it has done so in recent months. With the world economy gripped by uncertainty, the ECB would do well pitch in with the Fed and the Bank of England to boost confidence.

title: Health debate: reactions to the Wanless report
bodyText: It was Gordon Brown's big gamble. Labour was elected to improve the NHS, and after five years even the party chairman admits that in many ways the NHS has gone backwards. Everyone agrees we need to spend more money on health, that it will be the public who will pay for it in some way, and that the rich should pay and the poor shouldn't. But whether it is paid for by tax or by other forms such as social insurance is the subject of a national debate that Gordon Brown said we must have. We do need this debate, yet Brown also declared the answer: more tax is the only way to pay for the NHS. He waved an independent report from Derek Wanless, former chief executive of the NatWest, as proof that he was right. Then the backlash started. The Wanless report was far from independent - his advisory committee was made up solely of mandarins from the Department of Health and Treasury. Wanless then insisted he hadn't ruled out other forms of raising money from the NHS, such as French and German style social insurance. The health secretary Alan Milburn went against Brown by insisting we must consider a hypothecated, or ring-fenced, NHS tax. Chris Powell, chair of the immensely influential Blairite thinktank the Institute for Public Policy Research and brother of Tony Blair's chief of staff, suggested that it might be good to look at other ways than straight tax. Peter Mandelson, still close to Blair, said we should look at the European ways of doing things. Brown's spin simply showed how politicians are not fit to be in charge of the health system: they are far too keen to play politics with it, with endless ridiculous targets, annual root and branch reorganisations, all glossed over by PR gimmicks such as asking Prince Charles to advise on hospital design, and Loyd Grossman to decide on hospital food. While the politicians spin, patients lie bleeding to death untreated on trolleys in hospital corridors as a result of the politicians' policies. Politicians have had total power over the health service for fifty years and have turned it into a third world system. With a track record like that, they should be more humble. Brown tried to close down the debate before it started, but the debate, which is a life and death issue for tens of thousands of people, is far bigger than just one man, or even this government. That is why Gordon lost his big gamble. He showed he had closed his mind to other options. What is surreal about this debate is that those who propose we look at some of the things they do socialist France or socialist Germany or socialist Sweden or middle of the road Netherlands is seen as being unacceptably right-wing in the UK. It just shows how little we know about our European neighbours in this country, and what a mental straightjacket we are in about the NHS. Gordon lost his big gamble. Now let real debate commence. -Anthony Browne, Health Editor, The Observer The Wanless report: What the papers said The Guardian: Wanless place to hide "Behind the words of Iain Duncan Smith and Michael Howard is the illusion that there is a magical way of finding extra money for health without requiring a single extra penny of tax. What was urgently needed from Labour yesterday was an unequivocal statement of support for their ten year plan and the tax-based system on which it was constructed. That was what the chancellor emphatically delivered yesterday. With the interim report from the independent review of the long term funding needs of the NHS, Mr Brown came armed with devastating details of the defects of social insurance: excessive administrative overheads, insufficient incentives for lost control and, in places like France, large premiums for both employers and employees... There is a devastating statistic on page 37 of the Wanless report, which explains why the NHS cannot be turned round overnight : the cumulative under-investment in the NHS compared with the EU average between 1972 and 1998 comes to a staggering £276bn. Mr Brown did not say where the extra cash will come from, but he tiptoed right up to the answer: higher taxation.' The Independent: A welcome rise in funding, but the prescription for the NHS is too conservative 'Mr Wanless found that a tax funded NHS "remains the fairest and most efficient system" for this country. The Labour benches cheered at this harking back to the glories of Nye Bevan, 1948 and all that. But those with more open minds will want to consider in more depth the evidence against alternative funding methods, such as compulsory private insurance to top up public spending, which seems to deliver a first world service in return for higher spending on much of the Continent. We are entitled also to be sceptical about a review which promises consultation with doctors, nurses and NHS staff but makes no consultation with users of the service, the patients, who should have been the first port of call .' The Daily Telegraph: Personal financial sacrifices:"It was when he came to the NHS that Mr Brown really let his colours show. Armed with the entirely predictable conclusion of the interim report by Derek Wanless, formerly chief executive of NatWest, that the NHS should continue as a publicly funded monolith, the Chancellor called for a national consensus that this is the only way forward. Fat chance, given the mess the NHS is in, and Mr Wanless's terms of reference were such that he could hardly have come to any other conclusion. Even he, however, did not hazard a guess yesterday as to what sort of sums might be required to return the service to decent shape... Battle will be joined in earnest when the Chancellor produces his next comprehensive spending review in the summer. But on the Government's side,at least, the lines are already drawn. Tax and spend is back, and it is middle Britain, which New Labour used to court, that will have to pay for the ambitions Mr Brown outlined yesterday - or make 'personal financial sacrifices', but as Peter Mandelson, who is never far from the Prime Minister's thinking, delicately put it in the Commons afterwards. But as the shadow chancellor, Michael Howard, reminded Mr Brown, we have heard these promises of better performance in return for higher spending innumerable times before, and Labour still has to deliver. Without meaningful reform of not just the NHS, but many other public services, it is hard to see how it ever will. Yesterday saw Mr Brown at his most confident, but nothing he said ever began to address this central problem.' The Mirror: Tax us, Tony . Then we can REALLY cure our health service. "Put income tax up. There, that wasn't too painful was it? Why should this be such a taboo subject for a Labour government?... Our NHS is on its knees. Nye Bevan's great dream lies in tatters. We can either turn our heads away or do something about it. Despite one of the best economic cycles for 40 years, health care in Britain has got worse. What on earth will happen if we plunge into a full-blown recession? Gordon Brown didn't commit himself yesterday. But we know what he meant. He wants us to tell the Government to screw its pledge on income tax. To tear up that ludicrous sop to the blue rinse floating voters in Ascot. He's right. The Mirror today calls on Tony Blair to break a pledge. Put up income tax and give us back our health service." Daily Mail: Back in the old mould "Here, lengthy trolley waits are routine, the scandal of mixed wards continues, waiting list are a disgrace and, tragically, the outcome for almost every serious disease is worse than in other EU nations. Yet Mr Brown still insists that state funding is the fairest and most efficient system, though Billions more will have to be spent if Britain is to come anywhere near the European average. But can it possibly be done, without swingeing increases in direct taxation? Even if the public are squeezed until the pips squeak, can the deep-seated problems of the NHS be solved simply by throwing money at them?" The Times: Brown's battleground: A political strategy behind an economic statement. "Derek Wanless, the man charged with estimating the demands facing the health service, must have been flattered to find himself quoted so often by the Chancellor and placed on the sort of pedestal once occupied by Sir William Beveridge. It is not really surprising that Mr Wanless found in favour of the present method of funding the health service. The cost and upheaval of abandoning the basic model of the NHS, as much as ideological bias, have made it improbable that ministers who need to deliver results swiftly would divert their energies into abstract structural questions - no matter what their conceivable merit. Nor is it shocking that Mr Wanless thinks that much more money will have to be found for the NHS if it is to have any chance of matching public expectations". The Sun: Bad medicine "The Government has acknowledged the right problem about the health service. But it has come up with the wrong solution. Yes more money is needed to give patients the fast and effective treatment they deserve. But not by raising the tax burden. Tax and spend is the Old Labour way that Tony Blair promised was long dead. The burden of tax has already gone up significantly over the past four years. If Gordon Brown puts it up anymore, The Sun won't wear it ... The NHS needs massive reform. It needs to bring in imaginative schemes to make use of private money, not sneer at it".

title: Europe chafes at its straitjacket
bodyText: If Gordon Brown's public finance plans could be seen as a diet, then last week's Pre-Budget Report saw an abrupt increase in his target weight. Public borrowing is back, far more quickly and to a far greater extent than expected. Brown would argue that the weight he intends to put on is the muscle of future investment rather than the fat of profligate short-term spending. The Chancellor's dietary plans also build in flexibility for times when he's feeling a bit under the weather. But Brown's weight-watching buddy at the European Union, Romano Prodi, doesn't have this flexibility. Instead, there is a bean-counting neurosis called the European Commission's Stability and Growth Pact that is in danger of slipping into fiscal anorexia. Indeed, detractors would argue that the SGP has brought neither growth nor stability and is more of an inflexible straitjacket than a pact. 'It's possibly the most inappropriately named economic policy since Mao's "Great Leap Forward",' says one academic. Throw in the fiscal acrobatics that saw billions of euros of future spending parked off member countries' balance sheets - the slimming equivalent of weighing yourself with one foot resting on the bathroom carpet - and the reasoning behind the City's nickname of 'instability pact' becomes clear. Europe's public finance framework has come under closer scrutiny than ever in the face of a slowdown that left Eurozone quarterly growth at 0.1 per cent between June and September. Specifically, there had been murmurings about reforming the pact to allow heavier spending or a tax-cutting boost to Europe's economies. Hans Eichel, the German Finance Minister, suggested a target for spending rather than deficits. Set against a background of a 2.7 per cent German deficit, approaching the 3 per cent limit, the comments were inevitably seen as the opening salvo in a move to water down the pact. This is an irony, given that it was Germany that originally lobbied for the strict rules to prevent profligate Italian spending destabilising the Eurozone. France and Portugal, too, are surfing close to the absolute limit on budget deficits. There is rising unemployment and upcoming elections in both France and Germany, making spending increases and tax cuts more likely. A leaked draft of Eichel's annual budget submission to the Commission showing that a balanced budget - the key medium term target of the pact - would be delayed from 2004 to at least 2005. On the face of it, the pact is not doing its job, although its record looks better in the longer sweep of European history. Whatever, the improvement agenda is still here. 'The Commission is discussing the evolution of the pact. There are certain things cast into law, but other matters can evolve by agreement,' says Ray Barrell of the National Institute of Economic and Social Research. There are two broad directions for reform - improving economic flexibility along British lines, and increasing centralisation of fiscal planning. Both are controversial, but not mutually exclusive. In academic circles and in some quarters of the European Commission, the view is that targeting cyclically adjusted deficits, like that in the UK, makes far more sense. This means that fiscal policy does not exacerbate the ups and downs of the economic cycle. In fact, there is already some flexibility in the interpretation of the rules, simply because they have not yet been applied. 'Article 104 - the excessive deficit procedure - has not had bite yet,' says Barrell. Also, there is room for manoeuvre within the pact. 'Even when breaching the 3 per cent threshold, a country has a lot of opportunities to react,' notes CSFB. Automatic stabilisers, such as increased spending on benefits, can be allowed to play out in the event of a severe economic downturn. However, this process of accounting for downturns is more cumbersome and uncertain than an automated cyclical adjustment to deficit targets, as in the UK. Similarly, when growth was strong, there was less of an incentive to temper spending and pay down debt. Cyclical adjustment is one way to make the straitjacket more comfortable, and could be incorporated more formally within the existing framework. First steps were taken at the last meeting of Europe's finance ministers, when they agreed a standard method of making cyclical adjustments. Also, allowing investment expenditure to offset borrowing could make the pact more flexible without damaging it. There is some provision for this in the treaty, but this is ignored by the European Central Bank. Instead, the ECB has praised centralising 'improvements' already made to the framework - such as advance information-sharing on tax policy. 'An agreed overall stance of fiscal policy may gradually emerge, influencing the conduct of national budgetary policies... domestic fiscal policy could come to be defined with reference to aggregate effects for the euro areas,' notes the ECB. Prodi recently called for the establishment of a central European fiscal institution. It is clear that the ECB would prefer to have to react to one fiscal policy rather than 12 - this explains its strict interpretation of the pact. 'Finance Ministers should say that fiscal policy has absolutely nothing to do with [ECB President Wim] Duisenberg,' says Barrell. The basis for Emu was that common monetary policy would provide the stability, and sovereign tax and spending policies would enable adjustment to country-specific financial shocks. Clearly, some degree of co-ordination is essential, particularly given long-term demographic pressures. Any trend towards fiscal centralisation goes to the very heart of the future of European integration. Too much could be counterproductive. If, for example, the Commission was seen to be dictating tax rates it could provoke popular revolt.

title: Larry Elliott: Analysis
bodyText: Ah, the nostalgia of it all. Patricia Hewitt is calling together employers and unions in Birmingham this Wednesday for a summit on manufacturing. Higher taxation and state control of the railways are not the only blasts from the past that are back in fashion. Now we have the return of Neddy, the tripartite body that got everybody round the table from the early 1960s to the late 1980s when it was abolished by Margaret Thatcher as the symbol of post-war corporatism. We are told that Neddy redux will not be the usual manufacturing moanathon but a forward-looking event that will help restore UK manufacturing to its former glory. Well, maybe. Industry's problems are severe, but sustained improvement requires not just an improvement in trading conditions but also a recognition that too many firms are simply not good enough. Encouragingly, there are signs that this message has finally sunk in. Chancellor Gordon Brown thinks too many titans of industry are first-class whingers running third-class companies. He has provided business with economic stability, low inflation, 4% base rates, the lowest main rate of corporation tax in the G7, tax breaks for research and development - and still manufacturers want more. They are unlikely to get it. The Treasury's view of manufacturing was contained in a succinct little box in the pre-budget report, which reads rather like an irascible teacher who has lost patience with the excuses trotted out by the class dunce. It's been a difficult year, says headmaster Brown, but really, it's about time you pulled your socks up and got down to some hard work. He wants to hear less of the "please, sir, the strong pound ate my exports" excuse. All industrialised countries have seen a decline in the share of manufac turing in the economy, he says, and the trend in Britain since 1970 has been similar to that elsewhere. "In the UK, it is estimated that the declining share of manufactures in domestic demand has accounted for perhaps as much as three quarters of manufacturing's relative decline over the past 30 years", the Treasury says. "Most of the relative decline cannot therefore be explained by trade competitiveness." The view from Number 11 is that some sectors of the economy are rising to the challenge and producing excellent work under testing conditions and some are not. "Between 1995 and 2000, output of electrical and optical equipment grew by 45%. The chemicals related industries have also grown steadily in recent years. "The relatively strong performance of these highly export-sensitive sectors again suggests that differing fortunes between manufacturing and services are not solely attributable to exposure of the manufacturing sector to international competition and the exchange rate. Despite the weakness of the euro in recent years, the UK's trade in goods deficit with EU countries has not widened." Getting in first The Treasury says the decline in the information and communications technology sector - computers, mobile phones, chips - has been responsible for over 50% of the downturn in UK manufacturing this year, but other sectors, such as the chemical-related industries, have been doing fine. Next year, the chancellor hopes to see the whole class do better. "UK manufacturing is expected to recover sharply as growth in the major economies picks up from the middle of 2002 and demand for ICT related goods strengthens." In part, the Treasury's argument is a classic case of getting your retaliation in first. Brown's own forecasts suggest that the recovery it is heralding will be less than spectacular. After contracting by 1.75% this year, manufacturing output will fall by between 0.5% and 0.75% in 2002 before growing by around 2.5% in 2003 and 2% in 2004. If that represents a "sharp recovery", the government has low expectations. Moreover, since the middle of the 1990s it has only been the strength of the hi-tech bit of manufacturing that has kept overall output rising. As such, a lower pound is a necessary but not sufficient condition for the renaissance of manufacturing. It would be helpful, to put it mildly, were the dollar to weaken on the foreign exchanges and drag sterling down with it, since that will provide a respite for UK firms outside the hi-tech sector which are being affected by the collapse in world export markets and the fall in profitability. But, with the Japanese trying to talk down the yen and the weakness of Germany likely to prevent the euro from rising, it would be foolish of British firms to bank on this happening. Nor does a rapid rebound in world trade look all that likely. Where Brown is right is that industry needs to do more itself. Here, he ought to be encouraged by today's report from the Engineering Employers Federation* which frankly admits that UK manufacturing can learn some lessons from the other side of the Atlantic. While acknowledging that some of the productivity gulf with the US is caused by structural factors, such as a bigger domestic market, the EEF says that the comparison of US-owned and UK-owned firms operating in Britain points to other, deep-rooted factors. The best firms are committed to lean techniques that are focused on delivering what the customers want when they want it - and this entails limiting over-production, reducing defects, cutting unnecessary stocks, rooting out systems that don't work, eliminating excessive transportation, preventing long periods of inactivity and improving workplace organisation. All that may sound elementary, and it is. It is not just Americans who use lean production. The idea originated in Japan and is widely used in France, where the manufacturing sector boasts an excellent productivity record. The EEF says that many UK firms have improved their performance after latching on to lean production. "Yet there is a significant proportion of firms that has not undertaken any lean manufacturing and is therefore missing out on the benefits. The take-up of lean manufacturing in the UK is very much polarised, with a third of firms pursuing it across the whole organisation while just over 40% are not undertaking any lean manufacturing. "Key barriers to a better take-up of lean manufacturing in the UK are attitudes to change within firms, lack of understanding of lean, shortage of the right lean skills (at management/supervisor/workforce levels) and cultural issues." In fact, US-owned firms in Britain are showing UK-owned firms the way on lean manufacturing. They are much more likely to be using lean tools across the whole of their business and to be using them with greater intensity. As a result, and faced with the same competitive pressures as British-owned and run companies, the US firms tend to be more productive and more profitable. In the last two years, 11% of UK-owned companies showed an increase of profitability of more than 15%; the comparable figure for US-owned firms was 26%. None of this is in any way surprising. Nor does it invalidate the argument that manufacturing would be doing better were the exchange rate to be lower, because that is self-evidently the case. What the report does make clear is that whatever the level of the exchange rate, and whatever the current dire state of the US economy, American manufacturers do capitalism much better than we do. · Catching Up With Uncle Sam (The Engineering Employers Federation)

title: Andrew Smith's answers to your questions
bodyText: Q: The social security committee for work and pensions recommended in their recent report on the integrated child credit that the government organises a working party involving policy makers, academics and other interested parties to assist it to devise publicly acceptable measures of income to avoid poverty, a recommendation also previously made in relation to the social fund. The government response was that it takes an active interest in such questions but that there are a variety of views as to how this question should be approached. Do you agree that there is a need for such research to establish what would constitute a minimum income standard, in order to measure how successful the new tax credits are in alleviating poverty among children? How will the Treasury make decisions as to the adequacy of the new tax credits, given the absence of this type of detail in the consultation document regards the new tax credits? What sort of monitoring and evaluation of the effectiveness in tackling child poverty will there be following the introduction of the new tax credits? AS: This government is determined to build on the progress we have made in tackling child poverty by supporting all families with children in recognition of the costs and responsibilities that come with parenthood. Already there are now 1.2m fewer children in poverty since 1997 than there would otherwise have been, due to the policies we have already put in place. The new child tax credit, which comes into effect in 2003, paid direct to the main carer, will bring together all income related child payments into a single payable tax credit administered by the Inland Revenue. We have been consulting recently on our proposals and in fact have received more than 170 representations. I can assure you that these and the specific points you raise will be considered carefully as we set the rates and thresholds for this new credit in next year's Budget. Q: What happened to the tax credits for small breweries to help British real ale producers that the Treasury was "letting it be known" would be in the pre-Budget report ? AS: We are committed to supporting the hundreds of small breweries throughout Britain, and we are currently considering the scope for reduced rates of duty on their beer. We have been discussing this with the industry itself and decisions will be taken in the Budget. Q: If you do increase tax, will it be via upping the thresholds for national insurance contributions - surely a prima facie case of regressive taxation? AS: The government's current spending plans are fully costed and fully affordable. Derek Wanless has not yet provided detailed figures for the resources required for the NHS, but any implications for the financing of the NHS are a matter for next year's Budget and spending review. Q: Didn't Michael Howard have a point when he said of the report "ask a Labour question, get a Labour answer"? Why were the terms of the report so constrained? AS: There does not seem to have been much in the statement about the role of the private sector in the delivery of public services, indeed the whole basis of the Wanless report seems to be that the NHS will remain predominately under state control. What do you say to those who say that the only way to fundamentally reform the health service is to bring in new capital and outsource because that is the only way to ensure that services are delivered in the interests of the patients rather than the trade unions who hold so much power across the public sector? The report into the long-term trends of the health service by Derek Wanless is the first time in the history of the NHS that such a long-term assessment of the resources required to fund the health service has been conducted. The terms of reference were wide ranging in terms of the facts affecting the NHS over the next 20 years. Mr Wanless concluded that the NHS is sustainable; that the principle of an NHS publicly funded through taxation, available on the basis of clinical need and not ability to pay, remains both the fairest and most efficient system for this country; and that the NHS has suffered from a history of underinvestment. Comparing Britain to other European countries, such as France and Germany, his figures show that these countries have, over decades, committed significantly more resources as a share of national income to healthcare. The government has already increased spending on the health service by a third in real terms over five years. In addition the NHS plan sets out a blueprint for modernisation and reform over the next 10 years. But we recognised that the country in 20 years' time will be a very different place than now. The Wanless review is looking at an assessment of the resources required to deliver a high-quality service in 20 years' time, taking into account the technological, demographic and medical trends that could affect costs over this period. He makes it clear in his interim report published that he has not tried to estimate at this stage the levels of resources required - but he does conclude, after extensive consultation both domestically and internationally, that there is no evidence that any alternative financing methods to the UK's current system, would deliver a given quality of health care at a lower cost to the economy - indeed other systems seem likely to prove more costly. The chancellor, Gordon Brown, has made clear that the choice is now clear - whether we deliver a properly funded health system through the tax system, free at the point of delivery; or as others are advocating, a health system where people have to pay directly for their treatment - with all the unfairness that that can bring. Q: How much pressure has the parliamentary Labour party put on the Treasury to encourage a more open and honest approach to tax policy. Have these issues been raised with you and the chancellor in the regular briefings which you have with backbenchers? AS: The government has always been honest in its approach to taxation. We kept every promise we made on tax in the last parliament, and will do so again in this parliament. Labour's general election manifesto stated that "tax policy will be governed by the health of the public finances, the requirement for public investment, and the needs of families, business and the environment". The chancellor said there should now be a debate about the future funding of the NHS. Q: Do you think the damage caused by the 75p a week increase in pensions has now finally been laid to rest after the announcement of at least 2.5% annual increases? AS: It is certainly the case that the government, as it always has, is committed to helping pensioners to share in the country's rising prosperity. Alongside above inflation increases in this year's Budget, the pre-Budget report announced the details of a new pension credit to reward pensioners for saving during their working lives; a new guaranteed minimum increase to the basic state pension - guaranteeing £100 a year for single pensioners and £160 a year for couples in 2003-04 and in future years by 2.5% or the level of the September inflation rate, whichever is higher; and a guaranteed £200 winter fuel payment for the rest of this parliament. By April 2002, after our measures, on average pensioner households will be £840 a year better off. Q: Call me old fashioned, but I find tax credits impossible to understand. When are you going to introduce a public information campaign which will tell us once and for all what they are all about? AS: We ran very successful information campaigns for the existing working families tax credit and the children's tax credit and I am sure that would look to do the same for our new tax credits. Q: Did you enjoy seeing Michael Howard back at the despatch box, he often got under the skin of Tony Blair when they both did home affairs, do you think he's come close to laying any blows on the chancellor yet. And what about your opposite number, John Bercow, he's crackers isn't he? Have you exchanged any friendly words with him since he got the job? AS: I think the interesting thing about Michael Howard and John Bercow is how little they seem to have learnt from the past. At the last election, the people of Britain voted for investment in schools and hospitals first, rather than the massive spending cuts offered by the Tories. Yet we saw again Mr Howard on behalf of the Tories failing to welcome the additional £1bn for the NHS, and refusing to work with the government to build a consensus on the long-term funding needs of the NHS. Both Mr Howard and Mr Bercow remain committed to cutting public spending to 35% of GDP, which would mean massive cuts to Britain's schools and hospitals.

title: Rate cuts spark home loans boom
bodyText: Mortgage lending hit a three-month high in November as homebuyers took advantage of the cheapest borrowing rates since the early 1960s, new figures revealed yesterday. Lending totalled £10.8bn in the month, according to the British Bankers Association, a 2% increase on October's level. The housing market appears to have ended the year on a strong note, defying predictions that rising unemployment and the global downturn would drag it down. "Loan approvals continue to hold up, suggesting that the mortgage market may be more resilient than in past years," said Ian Mullen, the chief executive of the BBA. Borrowing on credit cards and personal loans was also robust, rising by more than £1bn, compared with a £708m increase in October. Consumers appear to have shrugged off the torrent of bad economic news throughout the autumn, cheered by falling borrowing costs. In November, the Bank of England cut base rates to their lowest level since 1963. "Households are both taking advantage of current cheap loan deals and probably con tinuing to withdraw equity from housing," said George Buckley, UK economist at Deutsche Bank. Confirming that confidence is bearing up, a separate survey showed that UK consumers are more optimistic about the economic outlook than either German or French consumers. Nearly two-thirds of people living in Britain are optimistic that the economy will improve during the next 12 months, compared to 57% of people in France and 58% in Germany, according to research by the Halifax. Spokesman Mark Hemingway said: "Even though the UK will not be part of the eurozone, it is the British who are the most optimistic about general economic improvements during 2002." British consumers were more likely to say they would be increasing borrowing in 2002, with 51% expecting to rack up more debts, compared to 30% of Germans. British households are also more likely than their continental counterparts to expect interest rates to rise again from their current lows, however, with 45% expecting increases, compared with 35% in France. In Germany, 41% think they will stay the same.

title: Letters: Exchanges open on euro
bodyText: Yesterday the euro became a reality for over 300m people across Europe. Anti-Europeans said it would never happen; but in 1999 it became a reality on the financial markets. Then they said it would fail; but over the past three years the new currency has secured jobs, growth and prosperity. The introduction of euro notes and coins changes the debate. The status quo is no longer an option. Over half of our trade is with Europe and up to 3.5m jobs depend on this trade. Euro-zone prices are 16% lower than in Britain. Every day that passes Britons pay what can only amount to a sterling exclusion tax, whether it be in higher prices, the cost of changing money, or the cost of exporting goods to our nearest neighbours. Locked out, we are losing out. Brian Ashby, London ashbybrian@hotmail.com · Things may be different where Fred Catherwood lives in Cambridge (Letters, December 31), but in Northampton a long-established engineering firm has upped sticks and taken production to the Far East. This would not have been alleviated by joining the euro, since the important economic problems are now intercontinental, requiring a common currency, a balance between cheap labour areas and concentrations of capital and, ideally, a common language and legal system. A revived, non-hierarchical sterling area would be better adapted to modern conditions than the little European fortress mentality. DBC Reed, Northampton · Larry Elliot argues that "Europe's language (and to a lesser extent cultural) barriers" are obstacles to the euro competing with the dollar (The euro delusion, December 29). But the US is not the cultural melting-pot he imagines. There is a lot of difference between Alabama and Manhattan. They also have different local rates of inflation and prices. We already have a "one size fits all" currency - the pound. We are used to regional differences in, for example, property prices. Changing the denomination wouldn't make the slightest bit of difference. Since the gold standard was abolished, all that is required of a currency is that it is universally recognised and supply of it restricted. We are using intrinsically worthless pieces of paper to facilitate bartering. "I promise to pay the bearer" is anything but a promise. Anthony Gunnersen, London ajgunnersen@supanet.com · How does the ordinary man, or woman, get to influence the European electorate? By writing letters to El Pais? La Reppublica? Figaro? All those Scandinavian papers with odd scripts? The Americans can speak to each other coast to coast; the Europeans cannot: it is this which makes the project hopelessly democratically flawed, whatever the economic benefits might be. Economic gain is not to be weighed against political independence. Edward Nightingale, London · All surveys show that a clear majority of the British people reject the euro and fear that Tony Blair will connive with Brussels to force the euro on us. Today, at 2pm at a rally outside Parliament, there will be a symbolic burning of a 500 euro note to show Tony Blair the strength of feeling there is against adopting the euro. Duncan Roberts, duncan2@onetel.net.uk · I ordered euros from my post office yesterday to be asked my destination. "France," I said."That means you will need French euros," came the reply. Martin Culverwell, Frome, Somerset

title: Britons join move to oust bank president
bodyText: Pro-Europeans in Britain are backing discreet French pressure to dislodge the president of the European Central Bank, Wim Duisenberg. They fear that if Mr Duisenberg stays in post this year, the euro will not recover lost ground, making it even more difficult for the chancellor, Gordon Brown, to declare in favour of British entry to the euro. The British pro-Europeans, including ministers and senior figures in the Britain in Europe campaign, have been dismayed that Mr Duisenberg is planning to stay on. He has been widely criticised as a poor communicator to the markets and some pro-Europeans believe the Dutch banker's inept handling of events has conspired to drive the euro down. One pro-European British minister said yesterday: "Put it this way, Alan Greenspan he ain't." Jacques Chirac, the French president, is convinced that Mr Duisenberg agreed to stand down in the middle of this year, halfway through his eight-year term, to make way for Jean Clause Trichet, governor of the Bank of France. The deal was agreed in 1998, according to Mr Chirac, as the price for allowing Mr Duisenberg to take the post. However, Mr Duisenberg has over the past three days shown a renewed determination to defy the French and stay on. He told Dutch TV he would still be in his job this time next year - the first time he has said he will remain in the post throughout 2002. French diplomatic circles were increasingly confident that Mr Trichet would take over midway through this year. They are also sure he will be cleared soon of any misde meanours in his role in the collapse of Credit Lyonnais, then a state-owned bank. It might not be possible for Mr Trichet to take the post if he is still subject to a judicial inquiry. A hearing on the legality of the judicial inquiry will be held on January 30. Mr Duisenberg has strongly defended his record, arguing that structural problems in the EU, leading to persistent high unemployment, has been responsible for the euro's fall on the markets over the past year. The British government will publish a white paper on the need for EU structural reforms in time for the EU heads of government summit on the issue in Barcelona in the spring. Nevertheless, British pro-Europeans, led by the Centre for European Reform, the leading pro-Europe thinktank, are also preparing to mount renewed pressure to reform the ECB, including an increase in its transparency and a wider mandate. Mr Duisenberg has set himself against widening the ECB mandate to include objectives of economic growth and to fight unemployment. EU treaties give the ECB the sole objective of preserving price stability. Mr Duisenberg has argued that the best way to achieve the subsidiary objective of full employment is through "predictable price stability", as this, in the long term, attracts investment.

title: What can you buy for 1 euro?
bodyText: John Hooper in Berlin The cosy cash dispensing annexe of the Berliner Volksbank was the most crowded spot in the neighbourhood. "Schön," murmured a young man as he held up to his friends the 50 euro note he had just removed from the slot. Getting hold of the new currency was no problem in Berlin. With the sort of efficiency we have come to expect of the Germans, most of their cash dispensers were smoothly disgorging euros minutes after the changeover. Someone had even stuck a new notice next to this one, coolly informing me that, unless I was a customer of the Berliner Volksbank, I would be charged 1% - with a minimum of 3.50 euros. It doesn't take a Wim Duisenberg to tell you that that's 7% on a 50 euros withdrawal. Even before getting my hands on the new currency, I'd been spending it. The Cafe-Konditorei below our flat was open, and even had a brand-new menu with all the prices set out in euros. Now, according to what we have been told, the conversions will be exact to avoid inflation. The new price in euros will be the old price in marks multiplied by 0.5112919. So was the menu full of oddly priced items at 3.87 euros and the like? You must be joking. My coffee was 1.50 euros and my mineral water 1.80 euros. Yet there was nothing at all at 1 euro. "You can settle up in Deutschmarks. No problem," said the waiter hopefully. No, I said, I absolutely had to pay in euros. "Oh god," said the waiter, and reached for a calculator. "Some people want to pay in euros and some in Deutschmarks," explained the owner. "So we're having to operate two tills." I could see only one. "Where are you keeping the euros?" I asked. "Here," he said, and gestured to a box containing an assortment of paper cake holders with coins in them. It didn't seem to be quite what the European central bank had in mind. Still, I had a glittering gold and silver 1 euro piece in my change and was ready to meet the challenge set for me by the Guardian. I had thought that perhaps that most typical of Berlin dishes, the curry wurst, would cost 1 euro But all the stands were shut. In Berlin, they really go for it on New Year's Eve. Everyone gets tanked up and starts letting off fireworks with a disregard for life and limb that would do credit to a Somali warlord. All that could be seen on the streets yesterday were rocket sticks lying amid the snow and a few visibly hungover Berliners. Quite soon, the combined effects of the mineral water, the coffee and my own hangover were having a foreseeable effect. I needed, as we say here on the continent, to spend a euro. I found a rather smart postmodern structure marked "City Toilette". It used to offer the customary facilities at a price of 50 pfennige. Incredibly, it had already been converted. As other Berliners were dancing and drinking and maiming each other in the streets, a detachment of unsung heroes had been going around converting the city's public conveniences to ensure that their fellow citizens could relieve themselves with euros from January 1. That was the first shock. The second was that the City Toilette was now charging 50 cents - almost double. A sign said that I could now spend up to 40 minutes inside, should I so wish. I may not have managed to spend my euro, but at least I had discovered the conversion rate: 1 euro equals two pee. I didn't have 50 cents, so I put in the euro secure in the knowledge that a people so efficient they can have their cash dispensers and even their public conveniences up and running in a new currency within hours of the changeover would have made provision for small change. Nothing happened. I pushed the button underneath the slot. Nothing happened. The City Toilette had swallowed the euro, yet it remained obstinately shut. I was wrong. The true exchange rate is: 1 euro equals no pee. Jon Henley in Paris A croissant, infuriatingly, cost 99 cents. For 1.06 euros I could have had five fine Corsican clementines. I might have bought six prawns for 1.02euros, an apple for 93 cents, a historic E-day copy of Libération for 1.20euros, a small box of sticking plasters for 1.15 euros, or 100g of best Charolais minced beef for 1.19 euros. But spending precisely 1 euro on the rue des Martyrs in central Paris yesterday was really not very easy. This is one of those bygone village-type shopping streets that the French can do so well: half a dozen greengrocers, a bunch of butchers and boulangeries, cheesemongers, a charcuterie, a couple of chemists, a fishmonger, a clutch of cafes, a veritable panoply of patisseries. "I am trying this morning," I said politely as I entered each one, "to spend exactly 1 euro Can you help?" It was not as if they didn't try. Khaled at Les Halles des Martyrs spent at least five minutes piling grapes, then onions, then figs, then one large and one very small pear on to his scales in a vain attempt to ring up 1 euro. This was particularly considerate since he, along with just about every other shopkeeper on the crowded street, was having to juggle two tills for a steady stream of largely unsteady new year's morn customers, all proffering boring old francs but demanding exciting new euros in change. Matthieu at the ironmonger's couldn't even find a box of tin tacks that would deliver. "Maybe 10 3mm brass crosshead wood screws will do the trick," he suggested hopefully. "Ah, no, that's 1.20 euros. Nine will cost 1.08 euros and eight will cost 96 cents. Any good?" I was fast giving up all hope of finding my mythical 1 euro product when I pushed open the door of M et Mme Gérard Houlbert's boulangerie right up at the top of the street.
