Best baguette in Paris 1995, said a sign behind the counter. Le Martyr d'Or, pain tradition meilleure qualité , 1 euro said another. So there it was. What you could buy in the French capital yesterday for 1 euro was an undeniably handsome if admittedly rather small loaf of crusty white bread, baked according to a traditional recipe dating back to 1856. Well worth the price, said Mme Houlbert. You won't find a finer piece of bread anywhere in Paris. Worn out by my search, I collapsed on the terrace of a cafe across the street and ordered an espresso. "That will be six francs 55," said the white-aproned waiter two minutes later as he placed it in front of me. "Or très précisément one euro, if you prefer. It's the only drink price I know by heart." Giles Tremlett in Madrid Sorting out a hangover is never easy. Sorting one out on New Year's Day, when most of Madrid's shops are closed, is even more difficult. Doing it in a new currency is, well, confusing. I struggled down to the 24-hour pharmacy on Calle Goya. Four white-coated assistants were doing brisk business. Everyone wanted to spend the new currency. People were buying paper handkerchiefs, packets of cough sweets and cotton buds. All they really wanted was to hear the assistant say: "Pesetas or euros?" "No, I don't think I've got anything for a hangover that costs just 1 euro," one of the assistants told me. She tapped efficiently into a machine showing all her stock prices in both currencies. The smallest packet of Alka-Seltzer was 3.46 euros. "Try these," she said, handing over a box of Spanish-made aspirin tablets that cost 1.32 euros. I pulled out my "euro-kit" of coins I got from a bank in France shortly before Christmas. "They're French," I croaked. I shouldn't have said that. She flipped the 2 euro coin over. Instead of a picture of King Juan Carlos and the word "España" there was the design of a tree and the words "Liberté, Egalité, Fraternité". She looked confused. "French? Sorry, we can't take those." I was not up to arguing, but one of her colleagues corrected her. "Of course, it's all the same thing, isn't it? I'm sorry," she said as she handed back the change in euro cents. When I stopped for a coffee, my cafe con leche cost 99 cents; a pastry was 67 cents. The waitress was telling another customer that it would take a while before we all got used to this. At the 24-hour supermarket I found that for just under 1 euro I could buy 10 camomile tea bags, a Radical Pineapple Emotion drink or a packet of 12 mini palmera pastries. Eventually I plumped for the best hangover solution: a 33cl tin of Mahou beer at 96 cents. And they had change. Andrew Osborn in Brussels Spending a euro in Brussels, the self-styled capital of Europe yesterday, was no easy matter. At one o'clock, a grinning European commission spokesman had told the city's press corps that everything was going rather swimmingly and that the Great European public were emptying the continent's cashpoints with enormous enthusiasm. That, at least, was true, for the nearest cashpoint to the EU's rather drab headquarters would only dispense crisp new 50 euro notes and there was not a 10 euro or 20 euro note to be had anywhere. A euro, I discovered soon afterwards, however, would buy me precious little anyway. In the Tiramasu snack bar-cum-grocery store a shiny new euro would only buy me a small tin of Cesar dog food or Whiskas cat food or a can of Coke or Belgian beer. Not having a cat or a dog, I opted for the beer and proffered my fresh new note to the lady behind the counter, who immediately began to shake her head while eyeing up the note with genuine curiosity. "I haven't got any notes, only coins, so your change will have to be in coins. The bank didn't have any notes when I went last Friday and I am feeling too tired after last night to go to the cashpoint today," she explained with a smile. "Today's a bit funny, you know, but tomorrow will be more normal. To be honest it would be easier if you paid in Belgian francs." Not wanting to be weighed down with an absurd number of coins, I agreed and paid in francs. My second attempt at a euro transaction, at a night shop in the centre of Brussels, was equally underwhelming, although the range of 1 euro items was more encouraging. A Bic lighter or a Bic pen came in at exactly 1 euro as did 100g of sweets, a small can of Heineken beer or a 100g bar of dark Belgian chocolate. Plumping for the chocolate, I again held out my note, only to have it rejected once more. It was, I was told, simply too big, and the shopkeeper explained that he did not want to part with the small amount of euro change he had, so I again paid in Belgian francs. Beginning to wonder whether anyone in Brussels would take my euros, I went into the aptly named Euro newsagent just off the Grand Place. The only item priced at 1 euro was Belgium's answer to Exchange & Mart. I have a car already, but decided to buy it anyway. The other customers were paying in francs, but when it came to me the shopkeeper sidled along the counter to a separate shiny new euro till. He tried at first to get me to pay in francs. "Do you not have anything smaller?" he asked. But I stuck to my guns and, wincing, he got my change. I must say I was slightly disappointed. Philip Willan in Rome My market research began at a cash dispenser near Piazza Navona on new year's morning. Progress was slightly delayed as a German tourist posed for a photograph in front of the machine, proudly brandishing her selection of euro notes. My test of the euro's purchasing power got under way at the newsstand in Campo dei Fiori - newsagents appear to have prepared well for the switch to the euro and offer change in whichever currency their customers choose to pay with. A copy of the previous day's L'Unita (there is no edition on the bank holiday) cost 88 cents. I paid with a 10 euro note and the woman talked me through her calculations as she gave me change. A nearby flower stand didn't have anything available for 1 euro. The flower seller had no change in euros for my 2 euro coin and was not keen to perform the mental acrobatics necessary to work out what the change would be in lire. She gave me a single lily for my 2 euros and no change. I moved on to have a cappuccino at the Bar Tre Scalini in Piazza Navona. The woman at the cash desk had no euro change and didn't have a calculator to work out what my change should be in lire. "I'm sorry, it's the first day, so you have to understand," she said apologetically. The Bar Di Rienzo in front of the Pantheon sold me a cappuccino at the bar for 1.03 euros - it would have been four times as much if I had sat at a table outside. One euro does not go far. Three euros was the minimum price for a portion of hot chestnuts on sale further down the square; it was also the price of the smallest of the brightly coloured cotton butterflies being sold by a Tunisian street trader. We bought one that cost 5 euros, receiving 10,000 lire in change for a 10 euro note. The trader appeared pleased at making his debut with the new currency. Helena Smith in Athens It will come as a relief to most Guardian readers that in Greece, at least, a euro can reach parts that are often necessary on New Year's Day. Desperate for my first hair of the dog, I trawled the cafes, tavernas and bars of central Athens, to discover that, in the bars, a quarter of a litre of retsina (one big glass) was readily available with my new shiny coin. At 88 cents, there was even enough change left over to buy two individual chewing gums at a kiosk that sold cans of beers at 59 cents each and small bottles of water at 44 cents a go. Ironically, only ouzo, still the nation's favourite drink, was unobtainable because 1 euro will only buy a third of an individual serving of the stuff and no waiter felt generous enough to oblige. In the shadow of the Acropolis, not far from the spot where Athens, as the first capital to make the monetary changeover, announced the euro's birth, it was also possible to buy a bottle of Coca-Cola (75 cents) and a small carton of milk (73 cents). While a cup of grainy Greek coffee and a cappuccino exceeded the value of the coin, I did discover that it allowed me to buy precisely 123g of freshly ground coffee beans from my local sweet cake store. With the munchies calling, it was comforting to find out that, at 59 cents each, individual Mars, Twix and Bounty bars, as well as chocolate-covered croissants, could also be enjoyed with my euro.

title: Press review: the euro
bodyText: The general mood of the European press is cautious optimism about the future of the euro. Most enthusiastic is Le Soir of Brussels, home of the EU. The changeover is "a clear success," it reports. "There is no better of way of saying it. The introduction of euro coins and notes occurred without serious problems, in Belgium as in the four corners of the eurozone countries." Germany's largest tabloid, Bild, reflects on the unexpected europhoria that swept the country. "What a day! ... everyone wanted to have the new euro, to grasp it, smell it, to crease it and fold it." The paper celebrates the decision by many shops to seduce customers into spending the new currency by lowering their prices. "The Euro intensifies the competition and with that the pressure to keep prices down, because the customer is better able to compare the prices across Europe," Bild writes, under the headline, 'Finally - everything in Euroooooo!' In an editorial entitled "The D-mark is dead! Long live the Euro!" the Berliner Morgenpost says that it is hard to dislike the new currency. "Whoever holds the new currency in their hand for the first time in the next few days and tries to be impartial, will recognise that the Euro is no lighter, no uglier, no harder to deal with, it is - quite simply - different." "But," it continues, "it is too early to talk about equanimity or broad approval. Both the supporters and sceptics will continue to split the nation for some time to come. There are several reasons for that which are mainly psychological, but also in part economic." The paper says the lack of enthusiasm for the euro can be put down to the fact that the German people had little say in the decision-making process. "Right from the start the people were considered too immature to contribute to a decision: so the Euro in Germany is like so many other things: ordered from the top." For its part the Frankfurter Rundschau says that the euro has created a new identity for the citizens of Europe, but that this brings with it increased responsibility. "The path back into the time before the euro is blocked, because if this huge currency project were to fail, it would trigger a European disaster, comparable to some of those of the 20th century. If Europeans want peace at home and to safeguard their interests abroad, then they're damned to make sure the euro succeeds." Taking an even deeper look into the annals of history, Der Tagesspiegel says that by embracing the new currency, over 300 million Europeans are realising Winston Churchill's vision for a united and peaceful Europe, as expressed in his famous Zurich Speech of 1946. "Now it has become a grappable reality," the paper writes. In Spain, the country taking over the six-month presidency of the EU, all papers agree that people have greeted the new currency with excitement and fascination. El Pais notes a mood of "euphoria", but also runs an open letter from a reader, saying simply: "Goodbye, my darling peseta, I shall never forget you. Rest in peace. xxx" El Pais takes a stern view of the challenges facing Spain as it takes over the presidency. It notes that the physical introduction of the currency coincides with a difficult period of economic alignment, and warns that the obstacles to convergence become greater with the enlargement of the union. It welcomes easier price comparisons across borders, and hopes that the new money will give a psychological boost to European integration, but cautions again that real political action is necessary if monetary union is to become economic union. La Vanguardia concentrates on the dangers facing the citizen, warning that unfamiliarity with the money may make counterfeits easy to pass off, and provide an opportunity for unwarranted price hikes. Charmingly, it remarks on a late surge in counterfeiting of the old national currency. "It is," says the paper, "one more way to say goodbye to the peseta." The Catalan daily Avui says that, with the introduction of a supranational currency, increasing alignment in EU foreign policy, and advancing devolution in Spain, the Madrid government is becoming wary of presiding over "a state emptied of content". It rails against the central administration's perceived anti-regional stance on European matters. But El Mundo says shortages of cash across the twelve countries show the euro is a victim of its own success, and that Europeans are clearly keen on the currency. It quotes a German man saying: "It's as if we all started to speak the same language." In France, Le Monde is gushing in its enthusiasm for the new currency. "For the first time in the history of our continent, states, nations, people have decided freely to share one of the essential attributes of sovereignty," it writes. "In this case, no defeat, no currency crushing another, but a victory for all and a collective act. Thanks to the determination of committed Europeans, thanks to the people, the dream of one single currency for all of Europe is being realised." Liberation adds: "It is encouraging that e-day was marked more by curiosity and astonishment than by grumbling and churlishness, those two French characteristics." The Irish Independent says the transition to the euro went so smoothly that one could easily forget the wider implications of its introduction. These range, it says, "from the benign effects for trade to the impact on Britain, including the political impact, to the existing and forthcoming arguments about fiscal policy to the price transparency which will surely prompt Irish consumers to demand cheaper cars ... But there is an even bigger implication. The EU institutions are too remote. The notes and coins handed across counters are real. Their presence does not establish political integration. But it unites the eurozone in the most practical and palpable way possible." In Greece, the country least prepared for the introduction of the euro, there are stern words from Kathimerini. It says "lagging" countries must make an extraordinary effort if there is to be genuine economic convergence and political integration in Europe. And if that is to happen, it argues, there needs to be a change in Greeks' mentality toward Europe and politics in general. "The idea of giving only minimal effort, the near-parasitical tactics aiming to exploit EU funds for self-serving objectives, the refusal to dismantle a huge and extremely anomalous structure of privileges deriving from political favours, and the avoidance of adapting entrenched rights to changing circumstances are not only incompatible with the challenges of the new era, but even threaten to reverse it," the paper says. "The assumptions of the outgoing period will be of no use in euroland. It is only by accepting this reality that Greece will manage to win a place in the new community of European nations."

title: Cornwall's surf capital to get cut-price air link
bodyText: Newquay, promoted as the surf and sex capital of Cornwall's tourism industry, will be linked to London Stansted by daily flights when the budget airline Ryanair begins a new service in April. The company hopes it will make a profit on its Boeing 737-800, which has 189 seats, by highlighting its advantages over congested summer roads and the "appalling rail service" traditionally used by those heading west to the seaside. It is expecting to carry 90,000 holidaymakers a year and plans to make Cornwall a viable weekend destination for those living in London and the south-east. Priced at £39 each way -£78 return - the tickets will undercut British Airways' three flights a day from Gatwick to Newquay in its 22-seater planes. BA's cheapest ticket is £115 return. For Cornwall, the service will provide a significant economic boost to one of the poorest counties in Britain. Unemployment in some towns is as high as 14% due to the collapse of traditional tin mining and the decline in fishing catches. The government funded south west regional development agency, which paid for a feasibility study to lure the airline to Cornwall, welcomed the announcement. John Lobb, the leader of Cornwall county council, said yesterday: "The steady growth of this airport is an example of partnership between the public and private sector ... following the unveiling of the vastly improved terminal and hangar facilities at Newquay." Around 3.5m tourists visit Cornwall each year. The success of surfing around Newquay has drawn in more of the youth market, offering an alternative to the traditional image of beach and rockpool holidays for families with young children. The success of the Eden biosphere project and more upmarket hotels have also attracted visitors outside the high summer season, providing more varied facilities for those sodden days when high winds and low cloud blow in from the Atlantic. "By offering airfares at these prices we are creating a new demand and market for these seats," said a spokesman for Ryanair in Dublin yesterday. "This new service will be a boost to the West Country which has suffered for far too long with an over-congested road network [and] appalling rail service." The flights from Stansted are scheduled to take an hour but Ryanair has been criticised by consumer bodies for delays. The London-based air transport users council said yesterday it had received "a lot of complaints about Ryanair". Most of them, said a spokesman, were to do with delays. "The airline has taken a strident position with us and decided not to talk to us." Ryanair yesterday apologised for any delays but insisted that, based on its London to Dublin routes, it was "the most punctual airline in Europe". The spokesman added: "We fly to secondary airports and we suffer very few delays." The deal to bring Ryanair to Newquay, involving the county council and the airport operator, Sutton Harbour Holdings, was completed in less than three weeks. Ryanair's move is the latest phase in the low cost airline's rapid expansion. The company yesterday also announced new services from Stansted to Rome, Milan, Eindhoven in the Netherlands, Friedrichshafen in southern Germany, Graz and Klagenfurt in Austria, and Montpellier in France. Earlier this month Michael O'Leary, the managing director, revealed that Ryanair, Europe's largest low cost carrier, would purchase 100 Boeing 737-800s by the year 2010 and keep an option on a further 50 planes.The deal is said to be worth as much as £6bn. Since September 11, large national carriers have experi enced a catastrophic drop in passenger numbers and profits. The low cost airlines flying in and out of smaller airports where they pay lower landing fees have, by contrast, boomed.

title: Yesterday in parliament
bodyText: Enron The "funding of democracy" should be reviewed in light of allegations linking the government with the collapsed US energy company Enron, the Liberal Democrat parliamentary affairs spokesman, Paul Tyle, demanded. He said ministers should tackle the public impression that it was possible to "buy access and influence" to parliament. The Commons leader, Robin Cook, said: "I regard with some mystification why it is thought in any way unusual for a minister for energy to meet what at the time was then a major energy company, particularly since the outcome of those discussions was a green paper which actually curtailed - did not expand - the opportunity for Enron within the British market." Transport Commons watchdogs must be given the right to question special advisers on government policy said Labour's Gwyneth Dunwoody, chairman of the transport select committee which was snubbed by Lord Birt. She said there was an "increasing problem" hampering their ability to scrutinise the Government. Mr Cook said if Lord Birt was forced to give evidence the government could find it hard to attract similar unpaid advisers. Post Office Post Office regulator Graham Corbett came under fire from Labour backbenchers over comments on increased competition within the postal service. Left-winger Dennis Skinner said: "I think this fellow Corbett or whatever his name is has gone too far already. He's going to become an embarrassment to this government just like [Chris] Woodhead before. Let's get him out before he causes more damage." Mr Cook acknowledged that the more cherry picking there was of large commercial contracts, the more challenging it would become for the Post Office to maintain its universal service. He said: "Obviously that is a matter that must weigh with us when we come to consider any outcome of the consultation process." Elderly Tories called on the government to fully reimburse councils for the cost of concessionary fares for the elderly. Geoffrey Clifton-Brown, for Conservatives, said some councils failed to get any money reimbursed for the cost of providing the mandatory schemes. In debate on the travel concessions (eligibility) bill, Mr Clifton-Brown said the full cost was estimated to be £54m a year. Junior transport minister Sally Keeble said attempts to change the way the costs were reimbursed would prevent new measures being in place for the next financial year as planned. It could also undermine the established systems for distributing money to councils. Zimbabwe The government has done "too little too late" to protest about the "terrible situation" in Zimbabwe, Tory Julian Lewis said. Mr Cook said: "The present situation in Zimbabwe is alarming and deplorable and the attacks on the opposition are outrageous." The government had worked hard through the EU and the Commonwealth to ensure external observers were at coming elections in a bid to "curb the worst of the intimidation". Gibraltar The government disclosed plans to issue a joint declaration with Spain on the future of Gibraltar. The Europe minister, Peter Hain, announced the two countries were planning to issue a "declaration of principles" once agreement had been reached in the discussions over the rock. The news was attacked by Richard Spring, for Tories, as proof of a "done deal" with shared sovereignty of the territory. Military Ministers were urged to ensure Germany "honours its obligations" to buying its full share of a fleet of new Airbus military transport aircraft. Tory Michael Jack expressed concerns after German defence minister Rudolf Scharping said this week that Berlin cannot make a watertight legal commitment to provide the £5.1bn for 73 German aircraft. Mr Cook said: "They are a sovereign government and they will reach their own decisions, but we are of course making very strong representations to that government. We'll continue to do so and we'll consider any statement they may make." Agricultural shows Animals look set to be allowed to return to agricultural shows after the government said it was to make a decision about lifting a movement ban "in the next few days". The rural affairs minister Alun Michael, responding to appeals from Tory Hugo Swire to allow animals at the Devon county show, said: "We hope within a few days to be able to respond ... in respect to the agricultural shows about which you asked." Farming Farmers should make greater use of the internet and video links to sell their animals, Labour's Paul Flynn told MPs. He said that, although cattle markets were now re-opening, sales methods adopted during the food-and-mouth outbreak should not be abandoned. r Cook said: "If one of the consequences has been to provoke more local slaughter and local sales then personally I would regard that as welcome and would be a helpful step to making sure that we would be able to contain any future outbreak." Hospital scandal Mr Cook praised staff at the hospital where the body of a premature baby was accidentally thrown into a laundry bin and put through a boil wash. Mr Cook said the incident was an "appalling experience" for the family of tiny James Fernandez, but praised staff from Queen Mary's Hospital in Sidcup for their quick response to the mishap. Fridges Environment minister Michael Meacher denied government "incompetence" was to blame for the mountain of fridges being put into store. He said: "We were badly let down by the European commission." Thousands of fridges are being put into store while recycling plants are built to cope with removal of environment-damaging insulation foam. Mr Meacher said officials made repeated requests from 1999 to find out if new disposal rules applied to the foam. He said: "We did not get a formal reply until June 2001." NHS The government's latest health care shake-up was criticised as a "recipe for disaster". Lord Clement-Jones, for Liberal Democrats, said such was the "hostility" to the NHS reform and health care professions bill that his party was urging ministers to delay and bring in a well thought out alternative. Junior health minister Lord Hunt said for patient choice to thrive it needed an environment in which there was greater diversity of services, with freedom to innovate and respond to patient needs. The bill was given an unopposed second reading. Call for inquiry Former Tory leader William Hague demanded a public inquiry into the "uniquely chilling" case of disgraced gynaecologist Richard Neale. A "low key" investigation into how Neale was allowed to practice in the UK for nearly 15 years after being struck off in Canada was inadequate. Opening his first adjournment debate since rejoining the backbenches, he said it was a "tragic, deeply disturbing and utterly scandalous tale". Junior health minister Yvette Cooper acknowledged that the Neale case was "harrowing and distressing". She said: "The government is determined to ensure that procedures are in place to minimise the risk to patients to the extent that that is possible." The issue of whether a public inquiry should be held was now the subject of a judicial review. Red tape Farmers struggling to recover from one of the industry's most disastrous years are to be hit with more unnecessary red tape, the Tories claimed. The shadow environment secretary, Peter Ainsworth, called on the government to relax environmental protection rules due to come into force this week. Junior rural affairs minister Elliot Morley said the department wanted to be as "flexible and as realistic" as possible in implementing the rules. They would be reviewed after 18 months. TB tests for cattle Mr Morley announced movement restrictions on certain beef herds with overdue tuberculosis tests. Around 1,250 herds mainly in the south and west of England are expected to be affected. The government is also introducing additional checks on herds that have been reformed after foot-and-mouth disease. Station closure The government is to question the possible closure of Ashford International station on the weekend of the France v England rugby match in Paris. Transport minister John Spellar said the timing of the proposed shutdown was "particularly unfortunate" and promised to talk to the rail firm concerned. Hedges The government is to issue a new guidance leaflet on neighbours' rights to object to high hedges. But local government spokesman Lord Filkin could give no indication when legislation would be introduced to allow councils to intervene in disputes over trees and hedges. Golden jubilee Employers were urged to do their "patriotic" duty in allowing staff paid leave to celebrate the Queen's golden jubilee. Mr Cook said he hoped staff would be allowed to enjoy the extra bank holiday on June 4. MoD thefts Dental gold, night vision goggles, mobile phone top-up cards and military ration packs are among the more bizarre items stolen from the Ministry of Defence since 1997. Losses include three Land Rovers, a Vauxhall Astra and a motorbike. Total value of items on a list disclosed by armed forces minister Adam Ingram was more than £300,000. Summer tours Summer tours of parliament have been a great success and should be made a permanent attraction, the Commons decided today. But MPs warned that British taxpayers have been subsidising overseas visitors to the Palace of Westminster and that admission prices should be increased. MPs backed a report by the Commons administration select committee after hearing that more than 86,000 tourists had visited the palace during the trial opening last year. Foot and mouth About 6.5m farm animals were slaughtered during the foot-and-mouth outbreak, junior environment minister Lord Whitty reported. Tactic review The lord chancellor, Lord Irving, has ordered a review of the use of "intimidatory tactics" in defamation cases, such as gagging writs and gagging letters. In parliament today Health minister Jacqui Smith opens a Commons debate on implementing the Learning disability white paper. A short backbench debate on tackling long term unemployment in Glasgow is opened by Labour's Ann McKechin. The Lords is not sitting.

title: Schröder 'confident of economic recovery'
bodyText: The German chancellor, Gerhard Schröder, today is expected to strike an upbeat note in a speech at the World Economic Forum in New York. Mr Schröder will express confidence in a recovery in the US and European economies, according to an advance text distributed in Berlin. "Many indicators today point to a recovery in the United States economy," the text said. "At the same time there are welcome signs that during the course of the year there will also be better economic developments in the eurozone and German economies." Germany this week suffered the humiliation of receiving a warning from the European commission on the size of its budget deficit, as it creeps towards the 3% GDP level proscribed by the EU's stability and growth pact. In the speech, Mr Schröder again blamed Germany's economic woes partly on the slowdown in the US, where German firms have a heavy presence. He said turnover of German firms in America was now almost six times higher than German exports to the US. The WEF opened yesterday with an accounting of the world's economy following the September 11 attacks. Economists predicted that the US economy would bounce back by year's end, with Europe expected to follow closely behind. But they said there was no end in sight for Japan's 10-year-old slump. The nearly 3,000 participants - business leaders, politicians and celebrities - kicked off the five-day meeting as hordes of police on Manhattan's streets braced for unrest that never materialised. Normally held in Davos, a small Swiss resort, this year's event was moved in a gesture of sympathy for New York after the September 11 attacks. On the sidelines of the WEF meeting, the US secretary of state, Colin Powell, is expected to consult leaders from Europe, the Middle East and Asia on the Bush administration's new tough line for confronting terrorist groups and "evil axis" countries attempting to build weapons of mass destruction. Mr Powell was planning a meeting today with the Philippine president, Gloria Macapagal Arroyo, as the US steps up its military presence there in its campaign against terrorism. He was also scheduled to hold discussions with the foreign ministers of France, Israel and South Korea and the Nato foreign secretary, Lord Robertson.

title: Recession watch February 1
bodyText: United Pan-Europe Communications, Europe's largest cable company, has moved to wipe out 4.5bn pounds worth of debt in a financial restructuring, raising hopes that NTL can pull off a similar feat. The move, which will see UPC swapping 4.5bn pounds of debt and 900m pounds worth of preferential shares for new shares, will strengthen the grip of John Malone's Liberty Media on the company. Liberty is now the largest bondholder in UPC after buying a majority stake in UnitedGlobalCom - which owns 53% of UPC - a deal that was finally completed this week, As UPC's largest single debt holder, United has already agreed in principal to the restructure, which will see existing shareholders left with just 10% of the company. Mr Malone has been stealthily building up significant stakes in cable operations throughout Europe and Liberty is also understood to be in talks with NTL and have an eye on a takeover bid for troubled German media giant Kirch Gruppe. Triggered by failing to pay a 69m pound interest repayment due today, the announcement came despite the fact UPC has 518m pounds left in the bank. But this money wouldn't have lasted long, with banks unwilling to lend it more and losses currently running at more than 120m pounds a quarter. NTL's chief executive, Barclay Knapp, hopes to push through a similar restructure within the next few months. He was in defiant mood this week as he announced that three banks had been appointed to lead the restructure. However, he still faces a tough battle with shareholders including France Telecom on one side and hard-bitten US bondholders, which have spent the past six weeks amassing bonds at a third of face value, on the other. While confessing to being "heartbroken" about some of the measures that had to be taken to cut costs, he said NTL would meet all its interest repayments and had "sufficient liquidity to approach the recapitalisation process in a considered manner". Like NTL, UPC racked up an enormous debt rapidly buying up cable networks throughout Europe in the belief that the combination of digital television, telephony and fast internet access would prove a hit with consumers. Yet both companies have been hamstrung by high prices, technical issues and customer service concerns, pushing back the launch of new services and the time it would take to make a profit. "This is yet another example of how investors paid heavily for falling for the technology hype," said Gert Jan Geel, asset manager at Eureffect. "We were all seduced by a clever business plan that never became reality." Overoptimistic estimates about how quickly customers would take up new technology have also hit the mobile networks hard. The UK's two biggest operators, Orange and Vodafone, both released figures this week showing that as the market reached saturation, fewer people were buying mobile phones. Both also reported a further decline in the amount spent by each subscriber, fuelled by the huge growth in low spending, pre-pay customers. Orange, which now has 12.4m "active" customers, and Vodafone, which has 11.1m, also said they would concentrate on higher value contract customers and continue to push new models offering high speed internet and data functions. US media giants AOL Time Warner and Walt Disney were also in sanguine mood as they promised to work hard to regain the confidence of investors after difficult years. AOL announced increased losses of 1.8bn dollars for the fourth quarter of 2001 as investors criticised it for failing to capitalise on the synergies offered by the internet and content sides. For Disney, the news wasn't as bad as feared by analysts, but fuelled by the slump in tourism, the advertising downturn and disappointing ratings for its ABC network, it saw income slashed in half to 210m pounds. Recession roulette: winners and losers 01.02.2002: Radio steams ahead as TV falters 31.01.2002: Carlton proposed 'reciprocity' policy 31.01.2002: Cracks show in advertising pickup 31.01.2002: Kirch faces crisis over Axel Springer demand 31.01.2002: Knapp 'heartbroken' over NTL woes 31.01.2002: NTL acts to allay debt fears 31.01.2002: NTL's creditors and shareholders 31.01.2002: Watchdogs warn Malone 30.01.2002: AOL Time Warner's losses deepen 30.01.2002: Ad industry demands competition guarantee 30.01.2002: BSkyB head casts ITV as a failure 30.01.2002: Cordiant shares tumble on Bungey speculation 30.01.2002: EU asked to ease Berlusconi's grip on Italian media 30.01.2002: Subscriber fears turn BSkyB into turn-off 30.01.2002: US giants buy up NTL bonds 30.01.2002: Vodafone and Orange users turn miserly 29.01.2002: BBC website cuts 40 jobs 29.01.2002: Ball lashes out at ITV Digital 29.01.2002: Egg breaks into Zebank 29.01.2002: ITV Sport 'wanted 80m pounds for Sky carriage 29.01.2002: Orange dials up 12.4m subscribers 29.01.2002: Vodafone results 28.01.2002: Advertising recovery 'four years away' 28.01.2002: Gay website puts up 'for sale' sign 28.01.2002: NTL recruits advisers to lead debt refinancing 28.01.2002: NTL timeline 28.01.2002: Where did it all go wrong? 28.01.2002: Sportsworld takes stock market savaging 27.01.2002: Mystery sackings cloud Cordiant

title: France preview
bodyText: If Kilted Kiwis are now a Six Nations fixture, this week has seen much discussion of Boks in berets. The South African-born prop Pieter de Villiers is already a regular among Les Bleus but eyebrows across France will be raised when another player from the Cape, the No8 Steven Hall, stands alongside him this afternoon as the Marseillaise is sung at the Stade de France. With the former New Zealand A centre Tony Marsh as well, France will field three étrangers (foreigners) in their starting line-up. Hall's debut means he is one of only seven imports to have worn the blue jersey since the war, including the Moroccan-born former captain Abdel Benazzi, so the French might be forgiven for currently feeling their home-grown players are being spurned. Both the French Federation president Bernard Lapasset and the national coach Bernard Laporte have been stressing that Hall is there solely on merit. "He's a penetrative No8, who gets about a lot and gives us another option in the line-out," said Laporte. The No8 is actually Rhodesian-born and spoke not a word of French when he arrived in 1997 at Béziers. "When I went to watch the Springboks play France in the autumn I was full on for France," says Hall, who has been listening to the Marseillaise on his CD player to ensure there is no mistake this afternoon. Only eight players survive from the French side which started against Scotland in the opening game last year. The 19-year-old prodigy Frédéric Michalak steps in at scrum-half for the injured captain Fabien Galthié, while Gerald Merceron returns at fly-half. The Toulouse hooker Yannick Bru makes his debut and Olivier Magne takes over the captaincy. Italy's coach Brad Johnstone fields one of his strongest line-ups, including the veteran full-back Paolo Vaccari, who makes his Six Nations debut after two years out with injury. Like Laporte, Johnstone has an import making his debut at No8; the New Zealand-born Matthew Phillips is one of six non-Italians in today's squad. Of the half-dozen the fly-half linchpin Diego Dominguez, or rather the Argentine's boot, is what is worrying Laporte, who has warned his team, young, old, native and import, to be on the alert in keeping their discipline. Presumably the order has been given in French and English.

title: Crime the leading issue for French voters
bodyText: The horrifying death of a young father, killed by a gang of schoolboys because he asked them to stop them bullying his son, has refocused the nation's attention on perhaps the biggest issue of next month's French presidential elections - crime and violence. Two youths aged 17 and 19 are currently under arrest in the town of Evreux and police are interviewing up to 40 others suspected of involvement in the killing of Guy-Patrice Bègue, who died on Sunday from extensive head wounds caused by repeated battering with bricks and bottles. Bègue, 38, a painter and decorator described by his brother as "smiling, happy, an honest worker and a fine father", had gone to Evreux station after school on the Friday before to meet his son Jimmy, who had been accosted on the way home the day before. That afternoon, on the train, a gang of youths had tried to steal the 16-year-old's pocket money and travel card, and hit him repeatedly when he resisted. One of them apparently threatened: "Tell anyone about this and you're dead." Jimmy did tell his parents, however, and Guy-Patrice and his brother-in-law Christophe drove the 30km (19 miles) into town to collect Jimmy from the Evreux station carpark. When they got there, 30 to 40 youths were waiting for them. "They had bottles, bricks, planks of wood. Patrice started to talk to them, to ask them what was going on, but it was impossible," said Christophe. "It all happened so fast. They started hitting us ... I looked at Patrice, he took a brick in the head and fell to the ground. They kicked him in the head until he stopped moving." The killing was brutal, inexcusable and tragic. Quite rightly, it has shocked the nation and dominated front pages for the past two days - yet another outrage in a country that, to judge by the media's obsessive coverage of anything to do with crime, violence and juvenile delinquency, must fast be becoming one of the most dangerous on earth. Except, of course, it isn't. The crime rate did surge by a record 8% last year, exceeding 4 million offences for the first time in the country's history. Violent crimes, particularly armed robberies, increased by 9.8%, while the number of rapes rose by 13.2% and there was a sharp rise in offences carried out by children under 13. But none of that is in the least exceptional compared, say, to British and German statistics. What has happened is that pollsters have identified crime as the issue that most worries the French population - or, to be more precise, the slice of the French population aged over 50 that is most likely actually to get out and vote. Hence, without wanting in the least to belittle the horror of the crime committed last Friday in Evreux, the immense importance of "insecurity" in the presidential election campaign. Jacques Chirac, for example, has not hesitated to blame rising crime rates on the leftwing government headed by his chief rival in the race for the Elysée palace, the Socialist prime minister, Lionel Jospin. "There are two different cultures in France, one I would call lax and permissive, which has traditionally been that of the left, and a more realistic culture respecting rights, especially the right to live a normal life, which is the one I prefer," Mr Chirac said today, adding that the leftwing approach "has not been sufficient to fight crime". The outgoing president said overall crime fell by 11.5% between 1995 and 1997 - the two years when he governed with a rightwing majority in the national assembly - but had risen by 16% since Jospin's leftwing coalition took power in 1997, and proposed boosting the number of judges to try young criminals quickly, and special reform schools for teenage delinquents. Mr Jospin, who like many French leftists has long played down the law and order issue as a rightwing scare tactic, has, in response, been trying to toughen his stand on crime as petty theft and muggings mount inexorably even in the chic neighbourhoods of the capital. He has, however, admitted to a touch of "naivety" on the issue, saying in a television interview last week that he had mistakenly expected that crime would automatically drop as the government succeeded in reducing unemployment. But the link wasn't as evident as that, he conceded. Sociologists contend that one of the biggest dangers of such horrific human tragedies as Evreux, with their accompanying massive publicity, is that they will rush politicians into overhasty and ill-considered declarations that may prove counterproductive in tackling a problem that runs far deeper and is by no means confined to France. Never more so, of course, than during the heat of an election campaign.

title: Jospin unveils plan to woo wavering voters
bodyText: Lionel Jospin, who is favourite to become France's second Socialist president in May, outlined a catch-all platform yesterday designed to counter challenges to his election hopes from the extreme left and moderate right. While his main rightwing rival, President Jacques Chirac, went to Mont Saint-Michel to talk about the environment, his prime minister Mr Jospin announced 10 priorities at a Paris press conference. They included the creation of 900,000 more jobs, better retirement pensions, more decentralisation, fairer taxation, an end to homelessness within five years and joint European action to combat global warning. On foreign policy, he gave priority to the creation of a federated Europe of nation states and the cancellation of third world debt. "I want an active, sure, fair, modern and strong France," he said, introducing a 40-page booklet called Je m'engage in which there was only one reference to Socialism. With only five weeks to go before first round voting on April 21, followed by a run-off on May 5, Mr Jospin, 64, took little inspiration from the centre-left programmes in Britain and Germany except to use a literal, but unacknowledged, translation of Tony Blair's promise to be "tough on crime and the causes of crime". Before the conference at L'Atelier, his campaign headquarters in central Paris, Mr Jospin had warned members of his multiparty coalition, including Communists and Greens, not to expect a Socialist programme. But he had given little indication of what amounted yesterday to a personal selection of reforms. The prime minister, a former Trotskyist, Socialist party leader and education minister, particularly acknowledged his experience as a polytechnic economics professor by committing a future government to establishing the right to job training throughout adult life. His 10 commitments, in a booklet with an eight million print run, were evenly distributed to maintain the support of leftwing voters while trying to attract the wavering electorate of President Chirac who outlined his platform at the weekend in a booklet to be sent to 12 million households. Mr Chirac who succeeded a Socialist president, François Mitterrand in 1995 after beating Mr Jospin, hoped to swamp the Socialist message, but his main polling themes - lower taxes, the fight against crime and a 3% growth rate - were predictably matched by his prime minister. With a 51% to 49% lead in opinion polls published yesterday, Mr Jospin's real worry was whether he could hold together a fragile leftwing vote, essential for his hopes in the second round. Although he took up Communist and Green campaigns by promising accommodation for all homeless people before 2007 and more jobs, his policies seemed unlikely to reverse a drift away from the traditional left to Trotskyist movements which he quit 25 years ago. As much as 15% of the first round vote could go to movements like Lutte Ouvrière - workers' struggle - and its extreme leftwing rivals which have concentrated on attacking the prime minister and said they would not support him in the run-off. Apart from claiming that Mr Chirac's platform was "unrealistic", the prime minister avoided personal attacks yesterday after surveys showed he had lost four points in first round estimations for describing the 69-year-old president as "old, worn out and tired". To rub home unexpected popular reaction to an attack on the head-of-state, the leader of the president's Gaullist RPR, Michèle Alliot-Marie, dismissed Mr Jospin's programme, with its estimated cost of €38bn (£23.5bn), as "old, worn out and tired".

title: Jospin and Chirac circle the middle
bodyText: Now we know, at least, what they are promising. But French voters hoping to base their choice on the programmes of the two main candidates in next month's presidential elections will be hard put to see the difference. The conservative president, Jacques Chirac, and his Socialist prime minister, Lionel Jospin, have both now presented their electoral platforms, the former in a 24-page brochure entitled My Commitment to France and distributed to 12 million people; the latter in 8 million copies of a 40-page pamphlet called I Commit Myself. For two men from opposite ends of the political spectrum, their undertakings are remarkably similar. Both pledge to create a superministry to combat France's rising crime rate; both promise to improve environmental protection, limit the negative effects of globalisation, boost competitiveness, lower taxes and further reduce unemployment. Both also promise - although in such vague terms as to be all but incomprehensible - to do something about probably the most pressing problem facing France over the next decade or so: the need to reform the country's pension system, creaking under an ever-increasing number of retirees dependent on a shrinking number of tax-paying workers. There are a few distinguishing points, of course. Broadly speaking, Mr Jospin seems to be looking to please workers with promises of extra training, a back-to-work deal for the over-50s and tax cuts for the lowest paid, while Mr Chirac is seeking to please their bosses with cuts in income taxes and social charges as well as extra help to create 1 million new firms and a relaxation of the 35-hour working week. Neither candidate is much helped by the fact that where their platforms do differ, the promises appear utterly unrealistic: Mr Chirac's critics reckon he'll need at least 50bn euros to fund his pledge to cut taxes by one third over the next five years, while Mr Jospin's opponents wonder where the money will come from to meet the Socialist prime minister's promise to create 900,000 new jobs. So if they are not identical, the two candidates' programmes are improbable - hardly a situation likely to inspire a patently apathetic electorate. But there are several fundamental reasons why neither Mr Jospin nor Mr Chirac is able to offer the nation anything else. Both are well aware that France's voters are evenly divided. Opinion polls put the two candidates at 50-50, or at best 49-51, in the second round. So neither one can afford to make a mistake: caution has to be the watchword; ideological extravagance will be punished. The fact that both candidates are obliged to fish for their winning votes in the middle ground means Mr Jospin mentions the word "socialist" only once in his booklet, and has even gone so far as to say his platform is not a socialist one. Mr Chirac, on the other hand, makes a point of vaunting such traditionally leftwing values as "solidarity" and concern for the environment. But while striving to appeal to the essential centre, both candidates also know they cannot move so far towards it as to offend the partners they will need to win the second and final run-off round, scheduled for May 5. Mr Jospin cannot afford to upset the Greens or Communists, whose votes he will need to become president once their own candidates have been eliminated in the April 21 first round; while Mr Chirac must not bang on so much about leftwing concerns that he offends the various and independent factions of the right. It all provides, in short, the perfect breeding ground for two lukewarm, indistinguishable, vague and uninspiring electoral programmes - and two lukewarm, indistinguishable, vague and uninspiring campaigns. Add to that the fact that many French voters are by now heartily sick of both Mr Jospin and Mr Chirac, the pair of them having been hanging around the political scene for several decades, and it will be a victory for democracy if anyone turns out to vote on April 21 at all.

title: No racism here please, we're British
bodyText: A wake-up call? Of course. It was the great journalistic cliché of the week, trooping abjectly behind a Parisian dust cart. Fingers were pointed at 'the European Left' (the Independent). Our 'EU elite' and Tony Blair (Trevor Kavanagh in the Sun) or 'the BBC and Guardianista political classes' (Richard Littlejohn); 'bland Euro-correct' politicians and dangerous 'Republicans' (Libby Purves in the Times); the lingering spirit of 'Vichy and collaboration' (Daniel Johnson in the Telegraph); and heedless apostles of electoral reform (Hugo Young in the Guardian). The Los Angeles Times informed Angelinos that France's ordeal stemmed from its non-serious response to the 'war against terror'. Le Pen's success had kicked everybody out of bed, sure enough. But at the most basic level, Fleet Street was still tucked up tight, snoring gently and dreaming of Sven and Ulrika. British newspapers - like French newspapers - didn't get France's first presidential round right. The broadsheets duly noted the opinion polls and reflected them in their final predictions. Le Pen's campaign was either a 'slow burner' (the Telegraph) or a 'spectacular comeback' (the Guardian). But there was 'little chance' of Le Pen closing the gap (in the Times) or disrupting a 'likely' Chirac/Jospin run-off (Financial Times). After the fact, no one was in a position to say 'we told you so' (although Adrian Hamilton in the Indy came closest). Such failure, however, arrived without concomitant humility. Instead, hindsight ruled as the pillars of wisdom pounded inexorably on to the obvious question of could it happen here? Perhaps, said the Sun, because people's 'fears about their country being threatened by crime, illegal immigration, welfare cheats and the heavy, interfering hand of Brussels cannot be ignored'. Maybe, said a Mirror much impressed (after an undercover foray amid the dregs of the British National Party) by the need to 'improve family life, help children at school, guide them towards jobs and generally show why they should lead good, honest, productive lives'. Probably not, said the Guardian, ' because for nearly 40 years we have had political institutions like the Commission for Racial Equality'. Quite possibly, said Libby Purves: and 'no government should feel smug, because it could happen anywhere'. But what was - what is - this mysterious 'it'? The rise of the Far Right? The Telegraph abruptly denounced a (BBC/Guardian ) plot to link racists like Le Pen's 'with mainstream euro-sceptic parties in Norway, Denmark, Portugal and Switzerland'. The Mail took out its familiar broadsword, sheathed after the Queen Mum's funeral, to belabour the Beeb's ' bien pensant elite', who predictably blamed 'a brutish, bigoted Right'. It was, apparently, 'simply a falsehood to pretend that people are prejudiced if they are concerned about losing their national identity in an amorphous Europe with illegal immigrants sweeping across so many borders'. Cue 'apathy, cynicism and distrust' in Britain. Cue a loss of 'democracy, tolerance and social cohesion'. Cue Littlejohn in the Sun: ' We are constantly being harangued about racism. Politicians and much of the media are obsessed with racism to the point of insanity. The race industry is the fastest-growing business in Britain. We are all expected to worship at the twin altars of anti-racism and gay rights, our new state religions.' Cue Simon Heffer and a Mail lecture on 'the right kind of nationalism' for St George's day. The Times, meanwhile, discerned two distinct stages in Le Pen's rise and rise. First, a middle-class fear about what David Blunkett might haplessly call 'swamping' - even though the fearful lived securely in Peter Mayle country - and then a direct, visceral resentment amongst the lower working class. The unaffected but anxious; and the murkily embroiled. And here, I think, is where you can begin drawing (personal) lines. I don't believe in censorship. I believe that the right to a 'free and responsible press' includes the right for it to be irresponsible from time to time. I've bought the Daily Mail every morning since David English gave it its swagger back, and used to read Flashman, by Le Pen soundalike George MacDonald Fraser, who recently told Mail readers that Britain had been 'besieged by hordes of alien scroungers, bums, criminals, layabouts and riff-raff'. But - and there is a but - nothing in the French mainstream press, either before or after the Le Pen vote, equals the vituperation unleashed here as some true-blue 'British' reflect on what the Mail calls an 'odious fantasist'. The language is habitually emotional. So is the attitude to facts. (Even the Mail reminds us on a distant page that large-scale legal immigration to both Britain and France ended in the early 1970s and that immigrant families make up less than 7 per cent of France's population 'roughly the same proportion as ethnic minorities in the UK'). Could 'it' happen here? Perhaps, if readers are told often and luridly enough about the arrogance and corruption of their leaders. Perhaps, if supposed alienation and apathy are stakes battered repeatedly into the heart of the body politic. A vigorous debate about Europe? Fine. Overdue. But doesn't the Guardian's Timothy Garton Ash have a point when he argues that the almost religious fervour of the antis in the press burgeoned just at the moment their old religion - love of Mrs T - vanished, a decade ago? Replacement therapy. Nobody, charting the rise of the Far Right, pointed out that the same Sunday that saw Le Pen's triumph also saw an increasingly xenophobic Hungarian government dumped by its electorate. Nobody stopped to wonder why Switzerland and Norway - non-EU members - were part of this supposedly rising right, or why gallant little euro-shunning Denmark had the anti-immigrationists as part of its governing coalition. Only Quentin Peel in the FT pointed out that the really far-right DVU, which won 13 per cent of the vote in Saxony-Anhalt four years ago, had failed to field a single candidate this time round - or that Umberto Bossi's Northern League in Italy had seen its support skid down to 5 per cent. Whatever 'it' is, it needs better treatment than it got last week. Whatever it is, it is serious enough to engage serious minds bearing seriously gathered fact - not glib, reheated theses with a poisonous edge. Whatever it is, we need to wake up to it.

title: Our answer to Le Pen
bodyText: "Le Pen's views on immigration are the same as Norman Tebbit's," I read in a remarkable interview with Le Pen in last week's Spectator, "while his views on urban blight, social collapse and the decline of traditional values can be found every week in the columns of the Daily Mail or the Spectator." Hmmm. As the French march on the streets against Le Pen, while polls suggest he could gather a third of the vote in Sunday's presidential run-off, we are bound to ask: could it happen here? Of course, much is peculiarly French: the fact that both main candidates, Jacques Chirac and Lionel Jospin, had been in power at the same time, so a protest vote was likely to go against both (think President Blair cohabiting with Prime Minister Duncan Smith); the habit of using the first round of the presidential election to register a protest vote; the fragmentation of the party system. Yet other elements are reproduced across much of Europe: the disillusionment with politics, reflected in soaring rates of abstention ("Better a crook than a fascist" is the French left's resounding second-round endorsement of Chirac); the feeling of working class or unemployed voters that the whole project of "Europe" threatens them; fears about unemployment, crime and immigration. This cocktail has also pumped right-nationalist populist parties up towards the 20% mark in Austria, Belgium and Holland. Each ingredient of this nasty cocktail needs a separate chemical analysis. The most alarming, though, is the attitude to immigration, and to the resulting ethnic minorities. Le Monde recently published a coloured map of Le Pen's vote beside one of the number of "non-EU foreigners" living in each administrative department of France. The correlation is startlingly clear. The one is a vote against the other. If there is such a thing as a typical Le Pen voter, he is a white, male, unemployed worker in the east of France who thinks some Moroccan has stolen his wallet and his job. Now our ethnic minorities will inevitably grow. And they should grow. I say that because I like human diversity. But even if you would rather spend your whole life among white, beer-drinking monoglots, you must want the minorities to grow out of pure economic self-interest. On present trends, the working-age population of the existing EU will be in decline by 2010. Someone has to keep the economy going and pay your pension. Meanwhile, poor people from north Africa and the near east will continue to risk their lives under trucks and trains in the hope of a better life. They are often disappointed. I shall never forget a recent encounter with a young, angry-eyed Moroccan called Yacine, in a poor quarter of Madrid. He had entered Spain under a truck. He had no papers to get a proper job. And so he moonlights and steals. "I live," he told me, "like a wolf." To be quite clear: immigration is not the cause of Europe's Le Pen-itis. But one cause of Le Pen-itis is most certainly the way Europe has handled immigration. When we say Europe in this field we still, in practice, largely mean individual European countries, and of course national approaches differ. Britain, as usual, is more different than most - starting with the fact that our main ethnic minorities don't come from Europe's immediate periphery. Nothing is more urgent in the shadow of Le Pen than to work out which approach to immigration is best, and how it can be improved. This is not just about what we think our governments or the EU should do. It's about what we ourselves should do in our everyday dealings with those who live next door or just up the road. Here are a few thoughts, which certainly don't add up to a policy. First, Europe can't take in everyone who wants to come, any more than the United States can. There is a case for quotas. There is a case for keeping people out. But once they are here to stay, there is no justification at all for letting them go on living like wolves. Or even just as reluctantly tolerated "foreigners". Germany, for example, has until recently had the absurd practice of officially treating Turks who have lived and worked in Germany for 20 years as "foreigners". So those who are here to stay should be full and equal citizens. But citizenship should not be a cheap formality. On the web you can find instructions for passing the US citizenship test. We need something like that too. Citizenship has minimum requirements and obligations. We urgently need a debate about what our minimum is. People cannot actually live in an academic playground version of multiculturalism, based on pure cultural relativism. I am sure the essential minimum includes an absolute commitment to respect the law of the land, embedded as that now is in a European and international framework of human rights. I suspect it will involve some minimal cultural norms, such as a working knowledge of English. But I don't think it should involve a great deal more. Certainly it will come nowhere near the famous Norman Tebbit test of "Which team do you cheer for?" Beyond this minimum, we have to be realistic about multiculturalism. It doesn't mean one long cross-cultural festival, with everyone joining in the other's ritual dance. Even the famous "multi-culti" Sarajevo was never really like that. When Sarajevo worked before the second world war, it was because Orthodox, Catholic, Muslim and Jewish communities lived side by side, mainly in different quarters, with a lot of everyday cooperation and a certain amount of mild interest in the other's culture. When it worked in the latter years of Tito's Yugoslavia, it was the result of far-reaching secular integration, intermarriage and a general mixing-up in which the original cultures were either forgotten or heavily diluted. (During the siege of Sarajevo, I asked one writer to summarise his city's lost multicultural way of life; he answered: "Sex, drugs and rock'n'roll.") I think I know one small European country today that comes close to combining both the Sarajevo models. It has a lot of peaceful coexistence based on separate communities, with mild cross-cultural curiosity. (I like your food, you like my music.) And it has a lot of secularised integration, based on the fading of older cultures. This country is not called Britain or England. It is certainly not the England of Bradford or Oldham, which have more in common with Marseilles than they would ever dream of acknowledging. No, this country is called London. I don't want to idealise it - and I can already foresee an e-bucketful of protesting emails - but I reckon London is the closest anywhere in Europe comes to a civilised way of living with the ethnic diversity that is Europe's future. Of course we should never be complacent, but London alone is one big reason why it's less likely to happen here. Analyse what works in London, and we might have Europe's best answer to Le Pen. timothy.garton.ash@theguardian.com

title: Anthony Giddens: The third way can beat the far right
bodyText: The political geography of Europe is shifting. Less than three years ago, left-of-centre governments were in power in 11 out of 15 EU states. Depending on what happens in elections in France in June and Germany in September, that number could be reduced to five or six. Moreover, we have seen not just the return of the moderate right, but the resurgence of the far right, symbolised by the support gained by Jean-Marie Le Pen in France's presidential contest. What are we to make of these changes? Are we seeing an ideological transition like that of the late 1970s, when free market conservatism gained such an ascendancy? My answer would be no. To understand why the right has come back to power, we have to grasp why left-of-centre parties got into the majority from the mid-90s. It was not because the electorate was moving to the left. Surveys showed no such move. Social democratic parties did well for a cluster of reasons. They revised their political outlook to appeal to a wider constituency, abandoning some of the ideological baggage that had kept them out of power. Labour became New Labour, the German social democrats spoke of the "new middle", and so forth. But there were other, more contingent, reasons too. In Britain and Germany, conservatives had been in power for nearly 20 years - people were tired of them and wanted new faces. In Italy and France, divisions on the right helped give the left victory. In part, the return of the right today is a mirror image of the previous successes of the left. In Spain, centre-right leader Jose-Maria Aznar is in power mainly because voters became disillusioned after a long period of rule by the socialists, whose popularity was dented by corruption scandals. The Italian left was unable to contain its differences and fragmented, whereas the right under Berlusconi put up a show of unity. In Denmark, the social democrats fell from power partly because of losing a referendum on entry to the euro. In the US, Bush won - by the skin of his teeth - only because Ralph Nader took away votes from Al Gore. The fall of centre-left governments is also the result of policy failure, and it is here that the most crucial questions have to be asked. Many critics argue that the left-of-centre governments are in retreat because social democrats have moved too far towards the centre. The third way, it is said, is doomed. The way back for the left is to return to policies the modernisers rejected - high taxation, greater state intervention in industry and more emphasis on redistribution. This view does not stand up to scrutiny. Voters today are mostly non-ideological. Well over 50% in the EU countries (and the US) define themselves as neither on the left nor the right. Parties that have stuck to a traditional leftist agenda get only a small minority of votes - usually below 10%, and that is declining. The policy mistakes of centre-left governments have been the other way around - an inability to modernise enough. Among the emphases of third-way thinking are two prime elements: reform of labour markets and welfare systems, to place an emphasis on job creation; and the need to address issues traditionally dominated by the right, such as crime and immigration. Social democrats in several key EU countries have resisted or been politically unable to make these adaptations - and have ceded support to the right. The core difficulty of France, Germany and Italy is a lack of jobs. Although unemployment did fall for a while, necessary reforms to labour markets were not made by centre-left governments in any of these countries. The proportion of the labour force in work in the UK is currently about 76%. In France and Germany, it is in the mid-60s, while in Italy it is only in the 50s. In France, unemployment is particularly high among young people - a substantial proportion of whom endorsed Le Pen. Tony Blair's celebrated intention to be tough on crime and tough on the causes of crime was a major element of New Labour's rise to prominence. It recognises that people's anxieties about crime are real and must be responded to. It focuses on concerns that were previously an open field for the right. Social democrats elsewhere need to do likewise if they are to sustain or recover wide public support. Much the same applies to the issue of immigration, perhaps the most testing of all for left-of-centre parties. It is no use merely proclaiming that EU countries need immigrants (although they do). Policies have to be developed which are "tough on immigration, but tough on the causes of hostility to immigrants". The renewed polarisation of politics on the left and right is plainly threatening to political stability. However, the cause of the modernising left is by no means lost. It remains the only feasible way forward for European social democrats. Anthony Giddens is director of the LSE and a trustee of Policy Network comment@theguardian.com

title: France forces EU to delay budget rules
bodyText: The euro's credibility will be seriously undermined today when European Union finance ministers bow to pressure from France and postpone the adoption of politically embarrassing economic policy guidelines which demand that Paris balance its budget by 2004. In a new sign that the single currency's management has as much to do with politics as it does economics, France, the eurozone's second-biggest economy after Germany, has successfully lobbied its European partners to ensure that the EU's broad economic policy guidelines are not adopted by finance ministers meeting in Luxembourg today. The deal comes just four months after Germany escaped an embarrassing warning from the European commission over the alarming size of its budget deficit thanks to similar behind the scenes horse trading, and is likely to prompt further questions about the credibility of the rules which govern the euro. The guidelines, drawn up by the commission, demand that French public finances be as "close to balance or surplus" as soon as possible or by 2004 at the latest in order to safeguard the stability of the euro and its member countries as a whole. But such an approach is politically unacceptable for newly elected conservative president Jacques Chirac, who wants to cut taxes and has hinted that a balanced budget may have to wait until 2007, a scenario which has alarmed the commission and the European Central Bank alike. Coming less than a week before the first round of the French parliamentary elections on June 9, adoption of the guidelines by all 15 member states today would be politically awkward for Mr Chirac and his allies who do not want the French electorate to think that their own scope for slashing taxes is limited by Brussels. So instead of formally adopting the guidelines ministers will today merely have "an exchange of views" on the subject and delay adoption until after the French elections, although they will have to adopt them before an EU summit in Seville later this month. France's apparent reluctance to tackle its budget deficit - which stands at about 1.9% of its gross domestic product -has worried economists and EU policymakers.

title: New government in France opens up defence industry
bodyText: President Jacques Chirac's ministers have said they plan to privatise €40bn (£25bn) of state assets to help finance tax-cuts, with the spotlight falling on residual stakes in key defence groups such as Thales, the European Aeronautical Defence and Space Company (EADS) and engine-maker Snecma. Shares in both EADS and Thales rose strongly yesterday on reports that the new government would force the pace of restructuring by engineering a merger between Thales and Snecma and by disposing of the state's 15% stake in EADS. But Denis Ranque, executive chairman of Thales, said that the French defence electronics company - in which private shareholders hold 45% and the state 33% - was determined to remain independent. He insisted he would defend minority shareholders' interests if attempts were made to impose a new core shareholder on the company. He would prefer to see a further float. Separately, Jean-Paul Bechat, chairman of 100% state-owned Snecma, dismissed reports of a tie-up with Thales and part-privatisation. He said these had not been discussed with Francis Mer, finance minister, at recent meetings. Thales, which bought Racal's defence business in the UK two years ago, and is bidding for the multi-billion contract to build the Royal Navy's two new aircraft carriers, is anxious to expand its presence both in the UK and the US, which is suspicious of the French state's influence. Thales wants to avoid the embrace of both EADS and Dassault, the jet-fighter group, which makes the Rafale fighter plane pictured above. Both are potentially interested in taking control of the electronics group, analysts said. "If there is any change in the share capital I will be the one to protect the interests of minority shareholders," Mr Ranque said. "We are happy with the present shareholders. We have no problems if they want to sell."

title: Confusion over France Télécom
bodyText: France Télécom was plunged into confusion yesterday as the French authorities appeared to confirm, then deny, reports that the government was considering renationalising the troubled telecoms company. Shares in the debt-laden operator rose 25% to €11.8 (£7) amid the turmoil, reversing for a day at least the remorseless decline that has wiped almost 80% off its market valuation this year alone. Market excitement was triggered by apparent confirmation of a report that the government - which continues to hold 55% of the company - was prepared to consider renationalising business if sentiment towards its shares did not improve. Unnamed government sources quoted in a Financial Times report said the state had no intention of letting things drift. "We cannot remain indifferent to what is happening to France Télécom shares and bond yields. Buying the minority would be defensible," the source was quoted as saying. A finance ministry spokesman claimed initial confirmation of the report was based on a "misunderstanding". But the official line that "the state does not envisage renationalisation" did not appear an outright denial. France Télécom was privatised in October 1997 at the equivalent of €27.75 a share and has attracted about 1.6m small investors. The shares rose to almost €220 at the height of the telecoms boom but have fallen sharply over the past two years - dragged down by worries about the €61bn debt burden built up as the company went on an expansion spree that included the takeover of mobile phone operator Orange. Analysts believe the company could be forced into a rights issue to shore up the balance sheet and say that buying the minority shareholding would cost just €4.5bn at market levels, compared with the €8.5bn under a share issue.

title: EU puts la belle langue on the shelf
bodyText: The war has been raging for at least half a century but the campaign to preserve the language of Molière in aspic and to keep the increasingly aggressive linguistic invader - English - at bay seems doomed to failure. The latest linguistic skirmish has not for the first time seen the Gallic establishment forced onto the back foot and must be all the more difficult for the guardians of the French language to swallow, because their opponent is not Hollywood or the Anglo-Saxon business world, but the European commission. The commission used to be France's private fiefdom and a staunch ally in the linguistic struggle between French and English. It was modelled on the French civil service and much of its day to day business used to be carried out en français, but to Paris' undisguised irritation, English is now the established lingua franca. To add insult to injury, the bureaucrats in Brussels have just told France to scrap one of its most important pieces of linguistic legislation whose sole purpose is to keep the language of Shakespeare off the supermarket shelves and out of advertising slogans. The law, enacted in 1994, stipulates that all food products and advertising must be printed in French - including labels on food imported into France from other EU countries. But that, insisted Brussels, smacked of market protectionism and the European court of justice agreed. In a ruling which had French academics choking on their croissants it said that as long as food packaging carried a picture of the product inside or was labelled in a language which was "easily understood" there was absolutely no reason to print a single word in French. That was two years ago, but the French have resolutely ignored the hated ruling and the commission has now lost patience and decided to get heavy. France has two months to fall into line and rescind the language ban, or it will be hit with large daily fines. Defenders of French culture and language will have no choice it seems but to sit back and watch in horror as supermarket shelves fill up with "chicken wings" as opposed to "ailes de poulet" and "cornflakes" rather than "petales de mais." Unsurprisingly they are not happy. "This is part of the secret war waged in Europe by those who believe in a single language, which could only be English given its strength," fumed writer Claude Duneton in daily Le Figaro. "This is an attack on cultural diversity. It will be fine for the educated elite and those capable of translation but will condemn the vast majority of Europeans to illiteracy." "It won't just lead to linguistic harmonisation but to cultural harmonisation too. It is easy to imagine that the language of advertising will increasingly be English and that national languages will be less important. This is colonisation pure and simple." "We'll have to speak English to go shopping," agreed daily Le Parisien. To make matters worse for the French, it was a French company which complained about the law in the first place and highlighted its incompatibility with EU law. Supermarket chain Geant decided to act after it was fined £3,500 for selling 432 bottles of Coca-Cola, 47 bottles of cider and 22 bottles of ginger beer with English language labels. In doing so it triggered a process which is about to kill off one of France's most important linguistic laws. But as commission officials are anxious to point out, EU law on the subject also benefits French food producers - they do not have to translate "croissant" into "crescent" or "foie gras" into "fatty liver" when they export their products to Britain. The French may be down in the "bataille des langues" but they are not out - yet. Although they will relent and allow food and drink labelled only in English onto supermarket shelves, the government has come up with a face-saver - shops will by law have to provide a French translation of every such product on the shelf itself.

title: French town bans Le Pen's congress fury at ban on congress
