bodyText: Jean-Marie Le Pen's National Front responded furiously yesterday to a last-minute decision by the Alpine town of Annecy to cancel the far-right party's annual summer congress on security grounds. "We will not allow a blatantly discriminatory policy such as this to go uncontested," a National Front spokesman said. "We will go ahead with our summer congress in Annecy come what may, and reserve the right to launch administrative, civil or even criminal proceedings against the mayor." Annecy became the second French town this year to declare Front activists personae non gratae for the duration of the party's so-called "summer university", a four-day programme of speeches, rallies and social events scheduled for late August. In June the Socialist mayor of the southern town of Pau cancelled the Front's booking "because of what happened on April 21" - the date Mr Le Pen knocked the former Socialist prime minister, Lionel Jospin, out of France's presidential elections in a shock first-round result that plunged the country into political turmoil. The far-right leader was heavily defeated by President Jacques Chirac in the second round two weeks later, and despite winning the support of more than five million French voters during the presidential campaign the Front failed to win a single seat in June's parliamentary polls. The conservative mayor of Annecy, Bernard Bosson, said he had asked the private firm that runs the Imperial Hotel and congress centre in the town to scrap the party's reservation because the complex is in the middle of a council-owned park to which the public could not be denied access. The decision sparked outrage at the National Front's headquarters, where officials have long complained of a secret conspiracy by mainstream political parties and the French media to deprive the party's supporters of their democratic voice. "The theme of our congress is 'the totalitarian democracy', and this is yet another example of it in action," the party spokesman said. "It is an attack on our civil liberties and discriminates viciously against several million French voters. We will not take it lying down." In another blow to France's extreme right, the government yesterday formally banned Unité Radicale, the small neo-fascist group that counts among its members the man who tried to shoot Mr Chirac at the Bastille Day parade. "Unité Radicale propagates a deep hostility to all forms of immigration, its ideology is based on an exaltation of the white race and hatred of the foreign, directed particularly against Jewish and Arab communities," said the hardline interior minister, Nicolas Sarkozy, who used a 1936 law against private militias to ban the group. UR, which has denied it is a paramilitary group and disclaimed responsibility for the failed assassination attempt, said in a statement on its website that the ban would only give it fresh impetus, forcing it to relaunch under a new name and with proper legal status. "Rest assured, the phoenix will warm its wings in the August sun and prepare for its next flight, it added. The group was founded in 1998 in an attempt to weld together half a dozen disparate extreme-right and neo-Nazi clans in France, thought to number some 2-3,000 people. Mr Chirac's would-be killer, Maxime Brunerie, a 25-year-old student with a long history of involvement in skinhead and extreme-right groups, was placed under official investigation on Friday for attempted assassination and moved to a high-security prison wing from a police psychiatric facility.

title: Obituary: Jean Sauvagnargues
bodyText: When Valéry Giscard d'Estaing was elected president of France in 1974, one of his priorities was to strengthen links with West Germany; so, for his foreign minister he turned logically to the country's ambassador to Bonn, the Germanophile Jean Sauvagnargues, who has died aged 87. Although Sauvagnargues held the job for only two years he could claim to have been present at the birth of the annual G7 (now G8) meetings and of the European monetary union. He was a career diplomat and very different from his predecessor, the outspoken Michel Jobert, let alone such imposing Gaullists as Maurice Couve de Murville or Maurice Schumann. He was quoted as remarking that some adored diplomacy in vivid colours, while he was among those others who preferred half-tones. Working for a president as personally involved in foreign policy as Giscard and alongside a prime minister as impatient as Jacques Chirac was not easy. His time as foreign minister was not entirely happy. In the year Sauvagnargues was born, his teacher father was killed in the first world war. Sauvagnargues was a lifelong advocate of closer Franco-German relations and studied German at postgraduate level at the Ecole Normale Supérieure. He was taken prisoner by the Germans in 1940 but escaped, and became a teacher in Paris before joining the foreign service. By 1943 he was at the French embassy in Bucharest, from where he was summoned home by the Vichy regime, but instead joined the Free French in Syria and was a member of De Gaulle's cabinet after the liberation of France. For almost a decade he worked at the commission for German and Austrian affairs, where his sympathy for Germany and its people found room for expression. In 1955 his career suddenly took a new turn; after a period in charge of relations with Morocco and Tunisia, he was appointed ambassador to Ethiopia. On returning to Paris in 1960 he worked briefly on desks dealing with Africa and Madagascar before eight years as ambassador to Tunisia. In 1970 he arrived in Bonn as ambassador and took part in the four-power negotiations over the status of Berlin as well as working on the Franco-German treaty of friendship and cooperation, attracting the favourable attention of Giscard. It was the era of Ostpolitik and Sauvagnargues had to tread delicately, given the reservations over the policy in Paris and other capitals. It was also the era of the Werner report with its call for monetary union within a decade, a goal made unrealistic by events of the early 1970s, among them the collapse of the Bretton Woods system and the oil crisis. He was present at a dinner at the British Embassy in Helsinki in 1975, attended by, among others, the then US President Gerald Ford, Henry Kissinger, Harold Wilson and James Callaghan, at which the principle of regular meetings of the leaders of world's most industrialised nations was agreed. He earned the enmity of some Israelis as the first western foreign minister to shake the hand of Yasser Arafat, whom he described as a moderate leader with the stature of a statesman. When relations between Giscard and Jacques Chirac broke down in 1976 and Raymond Barre was drafted as the new premier, Sauvagnargues was a casualty of the reshuffle. He resumed his diplomatic career and spent the last four years of his working life at the London embassy, during the end of the Callaghan government and the arrival of Margaret Thatcher as prime minister. Given her views on French policy on the EEC (as it then was) and the preference of Sauvagnargues for Germany, a meeting of minds was always improbable. In 1948 he married Lise Marie L'Evesque; they had two sons and two daughters. · Jean Sauvagnargues, diplomat, born April 2 1915; died August 6 2002

title: Destiny, honour, and the fruit of the French connection
bodyText: For 10 years he patiently hunted down and wiped out his father's assassins, one by one. He was jailed as a suspected leader of the infamous French Connection drug ring. He broke out of the high security prison in Marseille in his pyjamas, and spent years on the run. Now, at the age of 63, the legendary mobster called in a recent French parliamentary report "the last Godfather of Corsica" has broken silence for the first time - to complain that he is being harassed by the judiciary on account of an alleged fraud at his wife's fruit and vegetable shop. In an enthralling 11-page interview Jean-Baptiste "Jean-Jé" Colonna paints a picture of the Corsica that few outsiders see: an island where gang warfare and separatist violence go hand-in-hand; honour, duty and vengeance are deadly serious matters; bomb, arson and machine-gun attacks average one a day; and assassinations regularly top 30 a year. Mr Colonna, whom a government rapporteur described in 2000 as "the kingpin, the unavoidable fulcrum of organised crime in Corsica", describes how at the age of 15 he saw his father killed "with two clear holes in his head" by rival gangsters, and how he waited six years, until his majority, before asking an older uncle's blessing on his vendetta. "It took 10 years of my life," he told the magazine Corsica. "Every morning, I awoke wondering how I was going to be able to accomplish the pledge I made over my father's dead body. I am neither ashamed nor proud of what I did; I had to do it to be able to look at myself in the mirror. "I acted alone. I took my responsibilities and my risks. For me, that's conscience, real conscience. All the rest is just a lukewarm accommodation between your courage - or your cowardice - and your personal comfort." Betrayed by an informant, Mr Colonna was arrested in 1975 and imprisoned in Marseille. He denies being involved in the so-called French Connection, or indeed that he ever smuggled drugs, although he admits that a large number of Corsican gangsters, mainly based in Marseille, certainly did. His spectacular night-time escape soon afterwards grabbed French newspaper headlines for days. Nearly 30 years later, he still refuses to say how he did it: "There are certain details that honour commands one not to reveal, even so long afterwards." He fled to Brazil, where for 10 years he farmed, mined tin and ran a brick factory. "I told my wife I was likely to be on the run a long time and that she could start a new life if she wanted. She waited for me. "I came back to France for every holiday. I never missed one, not a single one. Sometimes it took several cars to dodge the police surveillance, but I did it." Safely back in Corsica in 1985, with the statute of limitations applying to his jailbreak, he settled down in the village of Pila Canale, where his wife runs a small supermarket. They declare an annual income of £80,000 and, despite dozens of attempts, the police have never been able to pin anything on him. But this year he was summoned for questioning in relation to a large and allegedly shady deal involving a luxury hotel near the resort of Propriano, and a claim that his wife paid him a salary of £1,000 a month for a non-existent job. "Five years of investigation, and all they can come up with now is a pathetic tale of a fake job, of which I am supposed to be the lucky beneficiary," he complained. "I didn't obey the summons. I knew full well I'd be condemned before I'd even been heard." Mr Colonna insists that it is his silence down the years that has allowed his reputation to grow to such proportions. "In my years of vengeance I made many friends in the underworld," he said. "Some of them are men of great worth, some are less good, some real bastards. But I never have been, and never will be, Godfather of Corsica." The life he has led has been that of "a Corsican of honour; nothing more, nothing less. And in any event, a life has only one path - that which commands you to stay loyal to your own destiny".

title: Extradition mission
bodyText: The future has suddenly become a lot less certain for a small group of middle-aged men and women - including teachers, restaurateurs, company executives and even a successful thriller-writer - who have been living peaceably in France for over 20 years. The unexpected extradition to Italy last weekend of Paolo Persichetti, a member of a far-left terrorist cell whose bombings and assassinations rocked Italy throughout the 1970s and 80s, has left other one-time Red Brigades militants in exile in France wondering whether their adopted homeland is preparing to break a promise it made to them back in 1985. The late president François Mitterrand pledged then that former members of the Italian extreme left would be left to live in peace in France provided they renounced their past, did not go into hiding, stayed out of politics and had not been not directly implicated in blood crimes. Growing disquiet in France about the justice being dispensed at the time by the Italian courts inspired the president's gesture. In a desperate hunt for convictions, anyone even remotely suspected of links with the terrorist groups was liable to be jailed on the strength of tip-offs from informers, who themselves were guaranteed immunity from prosecution and state protection. Between 15 and 20 reformed militants took up Mitterrand's offer, despite strong and repeated Italian protests, and it was respected by successive French administrations of both left and right. In ejecting Mr Persichetti, however, the new centre-right government - elected in June on a tough law-and-order platform - appears to have broken off the deal. The 40-year-old ex-activist, who for the past seven years has taught political sociology at a Paris university, fled to France in 1991 after being condemned to 22 years imprisonment in Italy for complicity in the assassination of an army general in Rome in March 1987. Through his lawyer, he said he had been expecting the extradition, the first of its kind since Mitterrand's promise - "ever since France's political direction changed after the elections this spring". Mr Persichetti's lawyer, Irene Terrel, said she would take his case to the European Court of Human Rights and would appeal against his conviction in Italy, which she said was based on the word of an informer who had since retracted his statement. "My client's extradition was quite simply illegal, a violation of his rights," she said. A French interior ministry spokesman said that in the context of the murder earlier this year of Marco Biagi, an Italian government economics adviser - a killing that sparked widespread fears of a rebirth of the Red Brigades - "France will not refuse judicial cooperation with Italy in the battle against terrorism". Dominique Perben, a rather embarrassed justice minister, explained that the crime for which Mr Persichetti had been convicted was committed in 1987, and that "the famous Mitterrand doctrine dates from 1985 and applies only to crimes committed before that date". The presidential promise had therefore not been broken, he insisted, "although today it is very old". The Italian government, which earlier this year described France as "a safe haven for many criminals who have committed very serious crimes in Italy", has welcomed the extradition. The prime minister, Silvio Berlusconi, declared that he was "hugely satisfied" by the development and encouraged by the news. The justice minister, Roberto Castelli, is even now drawing up a full list of all other exiles Italy would like to see returned. French government sources insist that each case will be examined individually, and hint that those indicted for crimes committed before 1982 will almost certainly be safe, as well as those whose alleged acts are by now covered by the statute of limitations in France. But the cries of betrayal in Paris are loud and long. Michel Tubiana, president of the League of Human Rights, says he is now "extremely concerned about the fate of all the other refugees", while the French Communist party has accused the government of "caving in to pressure from Berlusconi, whose respect for the law is more than doubtful".

title: Centre-right government that holds some state industries sacred
bodyText: The travails of France Télécom underline the quandary facing the centre-right government of Jean-Pierre Raffarin in implementing its privatisation programme. France is a country renowned for nurturing national champions in a range of sectors via state holdings and exercising a Bonapartist control over their daily management and strategic development that remains. But since the 1986 government of then premier Jacques Chirac, the state has embarked on a series of sell-offs designed to raise money to shore up stretched national budgets or finance specific spending programmes - while retaining key levers of control over the businesses. Paradoxically it was the socialist government of Lionel Jospin, trounced in the general election this year, which presided over a record amount of privatisations in its period of office - raising more than €30bn (£19bn) in the five-year legislature, including a partial sell-off of France Télécom. In a further irony, France Télécom may now scupper Raffarin's initially more ambitious programme. With stock markets in continuous turmoil, this had already dwindled to a mere wish-list. The state retains 55% of the telecoms company, whose shares are worth a third of the €27 at their 1997 float. If it subscribed to more than that stake, it would fall foul of EU rules on state aid. It is already in trouble with the EU for running up against the 3% ceiling on budget deficits set by the stability and growth pact, with the deficit already running at more than €37bn. So will Raffarin and finance minister Francis Mer speed up the privatisation process in order to deliver on promises of 5% income tax cuts, increased spending and to tackle the pensions crisis? There are several grounds for believing this option is unavailable, despite the prospect of raising - according that is to Le Monde - €22.3bn from quoted companies alone. The only public candidate for further privatisation so far is Air France, where the state wants to cut its holding to below 20% from 54.4% early next year or "when market conditions permit", raising more than €1bn. Air France has recovered remarkably from the turbulence unleashed by last year's terrorist attacks in the US, posting healthy profits of €153m in the year to March. But the unions are deeply hostile to any sale. Other candidates include Crédit Lyonnais, the once-troubled bank where the state holds 9.5% and which is being courted by rival Crédit Agricole. Or electronics firm Thomson Multimedia, where the government's stake is 21% but the shares have plunged since a placing of capital (under Jospin) in March. Government sources told Le Monde this week that the state had no long-term ambitions to hold on to its stake in such groups but was in no hurry to sell. Or, in the case of car company Renault, where the stake is 25%, partner Nissan - with 36.8% - appears happy with the arrangement. The Raffarin administration is also in no great rush to dispose of its stakes in defence groups Eads and Thales, however much it may want to encourage further consolidation to promote national champions capable of competing with the US. Holdings in this pair are, in any case, frozen at current levels until July next year, even though Denis Ranque, Thales' chief executive, is keen to reduce the state's holding to promote his company's expansion in the US and UK. Other wholly owned businesses such as aero-engine maker Snecma or tank manufacturer Giat are unlikely candidates. Finally, the two huge wholly owned utilities, Electricité de France and Gaz de France, are only long-term prospects despite EU plans to liberalise Europe's energy market. Even centre-right governments consider them a privatisation too far.

title: Télécom hangs up on MobilCom
bodyText: France Télécom yesterday seemed to have soured Franco-German relations as it put 5,000 German jobs on the line in the run-up to the country's election by ending its MobilCom wireless joint venture. MobilCom was last night on the brink of insolvency as debt-laden France Télécom, in which the French government has a 55% stake, stopped funding the business following a late board meeting on Wednesday night which saw the resignation of the chairman and chief executive, Michel Bon. MobilCom, which has had an uneasy relationship with its French backer, accused France Télécom of breaching its contractual obligations. The company is considering legal action, claiming compensation for damages. Analysts fear that Orange, which is majority owned by France Télécom, could be saddled with hundreds of millions of pounds worth of equipment contracts by MobilCom. But Orange finance director Simon Duffy yesterday denied that the company had any liability. "This is a clean break as far as we are concerned," he said. News of the withdrawal of funding for MobilCom was top of Germany's political agenda last night, with Chancellor Gerhard Schröder's govern ment pledging support for the stricken mobile phone operator. France Télécom's decision comes little more than a week before the German general election, and a spokeswoman for Mr Schröder's administration said it was considering offering the business credit guarantees. "As far as we know, MobilCom is a healthy company at its core that one can back with a clear conscience," she said. In MobilCom's home town of Büdelsdorf there was widespread concern about the fall-out from the split with France Télécom. "MobilCom plays such a big role that you can't even think about the town without the company," said mayor Jürgen Hein. German economy minister Werner Müller and senior officials are due to meet MobilCom executives tomorrow to discuss possible government involvement in a rescue. The German government has also made initial representations to the French government, which is considering its role in a potential bailout of France Télécom. The communications group is €70bn (£44bn) in the red - a debt pile more than six times its market capitalisation. The company was expected to announce a €15bn emergency cash call yesterday, but the board could not agree on a refinancing. The France Télécom board is expected to meet again on October 2 to decide on a replacement for Mr Bon, and the French government has made it plain that a refinancing package will not be finalised until a new boss is appointed. As well as a fundraising, analysts believe the company will have to sell assets. Lars Godell, a senior analyst at Forrester Research, believes France Télécom should cut its stake in Orange to just above 50% - a move which would raise an estimated €8bn. The MobilCom situation and departure of Mr Bon overshadowed publication of better than expected figures from Orange yesterday. The firm reported first-half underlying earnings of €2.3bn, up 41%, and raised its full year earnings target by €400m to €4.7bn. Names in the frame There are a number of potential candidates for the France Télécom job. Thierry Breton has emerged as the favourite. The head of consumer electronics group Thomson Multimediais is credited with turning a hopeless cause into a world player. He would have to take a sizeable pay cut to join France Télécom. Orange's chief executive, Jean-François Pontal - a close personal friend of Michel Bon - could also be a candidate, having taken over the reins at the mobile phone company from Hans Snook and concentrated solely on getting the business to generate cash. Nicolas Dufourcq , head of France Télécom's internet service provider business Wanadoo, has been mooted. Speculation in the French business establishment over recent days has named Noel Forgeard , chairman of Airbus, but he is understood to have ruled himself out. Pierre Danon , head of BT's core retail division, has been suggested but is obviously an outsider. The Frenchman, formerly European head of Xerox, has retained a high profile in his home country since taking up his post at BT two years ago. But he has yet to prove he can turn a former monopoly into a growth business.

title: France says 'non' to deficit cut
bodyText: A defiant French government yesterday rejected calls from Brussels to deflate its ballooning deficit, insisting it had more important priorities than balancing its budget. In a further blow to the credibility of Europe's fiscal rule book, the French finance minister, Francis Mer, said Paris had no intention of cutting its deficit by 0.5% next year, as agreed by eurozone finance ministers at their meeting in Luxembourg late on Monday night. "We decided that there are other priorities in France, such as boosting economic growth and raising military spending," he said. Although he promised to start reducing France's deficit in 2004, eurozone member countries which have made great efforts to turn their finances around expressed dismay at the French stance. "It's regrettable," the Dutch finance minister, Hans Hoogervorst, said. "I'm not pleased with the situation we are in." Some of the smaller countries believe the European commission has taken too lax a stance with the eurozone's largest members, France, Italy and Germany, all of which are facing the disagreeable task of cutting budget deficits at a time of slowing growth. Last month the commission proposed giving countries two more years - until 2006 - to balance their budgets. Mr Mer said that France would not breach the upper limit on borrowing of 3% of GDP laid down in Europe's stability and growth pact, but economists said it would be close run after new figures showed a higher than expected deficit so far this year. The French finance ministry said that the budget deficit hit €47.78bn (£30bn) by the end of August, already above the €46bn envisaged for 2002. Calls by Europe's finance ministers for Switzerland to lift the secrecy in its banking system also received a defiant response. For the first time, ministers have discussed imposing sanctions on their Alpine neighbour, which is refusing to hand over information about EU citizens who are suspected of hiding money from the tax authorities. The Swiss finance minister, Kaspar Villiger, who joined the meeting, said Berne had offered no compromise and "wouldn't understand" sanctions over savings tax. Britain's chancellor said the Swiss refusal "potentially allows loopholes to develop in other issues", such as money laundering and financing terrorism. "I believe there is a worldwide movement for exchange of information," Gordon Brown said following the meeting. "I do not believe Switzerland should continue to isolate itself." Britain was considering sanctions to move the issue forward. The internal market commissioner, Frits Bolkestein, who first threatened sanctions last month, sounded more conciliatory and said any EU action might be delayed until 2010. "Switzerland is a friendly country, so one should be very cautious," he said. Mr Bolkestein leads talks with the Swiss. Luxembourg and Austria said they were not ready to trigger a political war with a friendly trading partner. "The way in which some of my colleagues are trying to negotiate with the Swiss is not acceptable. They are treating Switzerland like an Alpine Iraq," Jean-Claude Juncker, Luxembourg's finance minister, said before the talks.

title: Brussels gives EdF a £600m jolt
bodyText: Electricité de France, the state-owned energy group which includes London Electricity and Seeboard, had its predatory instincts curbed in spectacular fashion yesterday when the European commission ordered it to return £600m in illegal state aid. In a move that angered Paris, the commission demanded that EdF, which has been accused of funding an overseas buying spree with French taxpayers' money, return to the French state 900m euros it is said to have received in tax breaks between 1986 and 1997. It also said that Edf should be stripped of its unlimited state guarantee - which means it is exempt from France's bankruptcy and insolvency laws - and demanded that the firm no longer be allowed to borrow money at artificially low interest rates. "The commission does not question the right of the French state to be the owner of EdF but criticises the advantages it receives," said Mario Monti, the EU's competition commissioner. "The commission's preliminary conclusion is that the said measures contain elements of state aid to EdF which, at this stage of the inquiry, appear to be incompatible with EU rules." The advantages the firm enjoys distorted Europe's increasingly open energy market, he added, and could not be justified particularly when they were effectively funding EdF's expansion into overseas markets. EdF has spent billions in the past few years acquiring stakes in privatised utilities in Britain, Italy, Spain and Germany. Its sales generated abroad have tripled since 1998 to 35% last year and EdF aims to raise that to half by 2005. While energy markets in Britain and elsewhere are liberalised, the same is not true in France where EdF retains a virtual monopoly, causing widespread resentment. The EU, in its campaign to become the world's most competitive economy by 2010, wants energy markets fully opened and EdF has long been a thorn in its side. France denied yesterday that it had done anything wrong, setting the scene for a confrontation which could result in a hefty fine. In a joint statement, finance minister Francis Mer and industry minister Nicole Fontaine said they "regretted" Brussels' verdict. "The matters that the commission has examined do not constitute state aid," the two said, adding that they intended to seek clarification from Brussels. France has a month to respond to the commission. There was some comfort for EdF. In a show of even-handedness the commission will take legal proceedings against Spain and Italy, which have passed laws to prevent EdF taking over privatised local utilities. "The commission believes that certain aspects of the Spanish and Italian laws can illegally restrict the free movement of capital," it said "We do not want protectionism," Mr Monti added. "And it must be attacked in the appropriate fashion and at the appropriate time."

title: Soros faces insider dealing charge in France
bodyText: Billionaire financier George Soros will face a French court today on insider dealing charges relating to a failed takeover bid for Société Générale 14 years ago. Mr Soros is one of four men accused of earning some $11m from speculating in shares of the then newly privatised French bank ahead of the public announcement of a takeover bid. Alongside Mr Soros will be banker Jean-Pierre Peyraud; Jean-Charles Naouri, a former French government ministerial adviser and Lebanese businessman Samir Traboulsi. The corporate raider who spearheaded the bid for Soc Gen in 1988, Georges Peberau, was granted an amnesty in 1995 and seven others were cleared of insider dealing two years ago. All four men are reported to deny the charges against them, arguing that they were not privy to any insider information relating to the bid for Soc Gen. Mr Soros sprang to international prominence as the man who made $1bn from speculating that Britain would be forced to withdraw the pound from the exchange rate mechanism in 1992. He used much of the cash to fund a network of foundations to help poor countries. French investigations into the failed bid for Soc Gen began in 1990 following a report into the bid by stock market regulators. Investigators are understood to have sought information from authorities in Britain, Switzerland, the Netherlands and Luxembourg to track allegedly suspicious financial movements. They acknowledge that a number of questions remain unanswered despite the lengthy investigation. The trial is expected to last until November 20, though no date has been given for the court to hand down its verdict.

title: Télécom to cut 20,000 jobs
bodyText: France Télécom yesterday took a step closer to regaining its financial health as it confirmed plans to slash 20,000 jobs over the next three years and continued discussions with the French government about a possible bailout. The indebted communications company intends to cut almost 10% of its workforce, although it will not resort to compulsory redundancies. It is also understood to be in talks with the government about securing support for a fundraising exercise to try to reduce the €70bn (£45bn) of debts it amassed buying Orange as well as licenses to operate next-generation mobile services. Traders are hoping to receive further information about France Télécom's refinancing plans when new chief Thierry Breton, who took over from Michel Bon last month, presents his strategic plan for the business in early December. French union leaders fear that France Télécom will be forced to wield the axe across the communications business, with speculation that as much as 20% of the company's workforce could be culled. Télécom is also believed to be close to securing a deal with the French government, which owns just over half of the company, that will allow the launch of a new round of corporate bonds and possibly a rights issue to raise further funds. News of the job losses helped France Télécom shares climb in Paris as traders applauded management's moves to cut costs. The news came as Orange's former partner in the German mobile phone market, MobilCom, abandoned its ambition to play a major part in the world of the mobile internet. The mobile phone company slashed the value of its 3G licenses and operations by €9.9bn, a move which plunged it into a €3bn loss. The company was saved from falling even further into the red by €7bn it gained from France Télécom, which last week agreed to take on the debts MobilCom ran up work ing with Orange on 3G services in parts of Europe. MobilCom and France Télécom were locked in battle earlier this year over who should pay for the company's failed 3G ambitions. In the end, France Télécom pulled the plug.

title: Agricole poised for Lyonnais swoop
bodyText: Crédit Agricole is poised to launch an agreed bid for Crédit Lyonnais worth euro 20bn in a deal to create a new French banking group that would rival BNP Paribas as the top bank in the eurozone. Sources familiar with the situation told Reuters that the boards of Crédit Lyonnais and Crédit Agricole had met last night to discuss the bid, which is expected to be pegged at around euro 56 per share and contain both a cash and stock component. The deal, which could trigger further banking industry consolidation within France and beyond its borders is expected to be announced this morning. The bid throws down the gauntlet to BNP, which sparked a tussle for control of Lyonnais when it snapped up the French government's 10.9 per cent stake in the bank in a hastily called auction three weeks ago. Initially slow to react, Agricole has gone on the offensive in recent weeks, appointing combative chairman Rene Carron to pursue Lyonnais and pushing up its own stake in the bank to 17.4 per cent with aggressive purchases on the open market. Sources said that Carron had held extensive meetings over the weekend with the bank's powerful regional branches, or "caisses regionales", to secure backing for the bid. According to the sources, Crédit Lyonnais chief executive officer Dominique Ferrero is set to become the operational second-in-command in the combined group under Agricole CEO Jean Laurent. Officials at all three French banks involved in the takeover battle declined comment. As signs emerged last week that Lyonnais was warming to a deal with Agricole, BNP said it would not stand in the way of a friendly deal between the two provided an Agricole offer was "acceptable" to all parties. But BNP could still come back with a higher bid if it deems the Agricole offer too low or sees opposition from Lyonnais shareholders or staff to the deal. Reuters

title: Crédit Agricole coup revives bid fever
bodyText: Crédit Agricole snapped up rival Crédit Lyonnais for €20bn (£16bn) yesterday in a deal that signalled the scramble for dominance in the French banking system. The target of the takeover had shunned the advances of the country's biggest bank, BNP Paribas. Yesterday's deal left banking experts speculating on the next move by BNP Paribas, itself formed through a three-way merger tussle in 1999. One industry source believed that BNP Paribas would refuse to walk away from Crédit Lyonnais, which it started stalking last month after buying the French government's 10.9% stake in the former state-owned bank. Others wondered whether it might be simpler for BNP Paribas to take a €200m profit on its stake in Lyonnais - now almost 17% - and target another rival, Société Générale, especially because Crédit Agricole appears to have the support of 40% of its target's shareholders. SocGen's share price rose 8% yesterday in anticipation of such a move. Whatever the outcome, banking analysts believe that this stage in the consolidation of the French banking system will help clear the way for a long-awaited cross-border deal within Europe. Such a deal, often mooted by British banks Lloyds TSB and Barclays, helped to lift banking shares in London yesterday. From Paris, the two banks said their deal "reflects our shared ambition to create a leading financial group in France that is ideally positioned to address the challenge of integration in the European financial sector". The deal also immediately raised concerns about job cuts at their investment banking operations in the City after the two banks made it clear that "most reductions in head count would take place outside France". One banking expert, who asked not to be named, suggested that such concerns were premature. "There is no certainty about the Crédit Agricole/Crédit Lyonnais deal taking place. BNP has the right to counter-bid - as does anybody - and I expect they will." The big players BNP Paribas is France's biggest bank with a market capitalisation of €36.90bn (£24bn) following BNP's take over of Paribas two years ago. This makes it the fifth largest bank in Europe by market valuation, behind HSBC, Royal Bank of Scotland, UBS and Lloyds TSB. Its dominance on the stock market is not replicated on the French high street where it has a 9% share. The proposed combination of Crédit Agricole with Crédit Lyonnais creates a company with stock market value of €34bn. But the combined group is expected to have a much greater share - about 27% - of French retail banking customers. Société Générale is the next largest market-quoted bank in France with a value of €25bn and a 9% share of customers.

title: SocGen banking chief dies in crash
bodyText: One of France's top bankers has been killed in a car crash, it was announced yesterday. Xavier Debonneuil, the 54-year-old head of Société Générale's corporate and investment banking business and a member of the bank's nine-strong executive committee died in an accident on Thursday in central France, the bank said. His daughter also died as a result of the accident, according to a bank spokeswoman. His wife and grandson were also travelling with Mr Debonneuil when the accident happened in the town of Romorantin, in Loir-et-Cher. Mrs Debonneuil is understood to have received serious injuries. Mr Debonneuil, who was born in 1948, joined Société Générale in 1984 after working for the French national statistics office and the Bank of France. He worked initially in the foreign exchange and money markets departments before being appointed deputy head of the capital markets division in 1995. In 1998 he was appointed deputy head of the corporate and investment arm of the bank and a year later became head of the operation. Mr Debonneuil led a drive to cut costs which saw his area of operations shed about 450 jobs. "He presided over one of the more surprisingly aggressive and you might say innovative restructuring drives among the mid-sized wholesale banks in recent years," an unnamed London analyst told Reuters. "Under him Société Générale's corporate and investment banking arm was not afraid of biting the bullet to create a more profitable structure." No decision about his successor has yet been made.

title: Head of Bank of France appears in court
bodyText: Jean-Claude Trichet, the governor of the Bank of France, and eight others were appearing in court today to face charges relating to a decade-old banking scandal that has clouded Mr Trichet's prospects of becoming the next president of the European Central Bank (ECB). Mr Trichet faces charges of complicity in an alleged cover-up of losses at the state-owned Crédit Lyonnais, when the bank came close to collapse in the early 1990s after a series of disastrous investments. It went on to become the biggest banking failure in French banking history, with accumulated losses of F140bn (£13.8bn). Crédit Lyonnais had be rescued after its whirlwind expansion in property and Hollywood's MGM cinema studios under Jean-Yves Haberer, who was appointed chairman by the socialist government in 1988. Crédit Lyonnais sank into crisis when the investments turned bad. The bank lurched from one rescue package to another at a cost of tens of billions of euros to taxpayers before it was privatised in 1999. Mr Trichet became enmeshed in the saga because he was director general of the Treasury at the time, with responsibilities for monitoring the banking system, including Crédit Lyonnais. Mr Trichet is accused of conspiring to present a misleading picture of Crédit Lyonnais's losses, a charge he vigorously denies. Through his lawyers, Mr Trichet argues that he had neither the precise information nor the power to dictate what the bank reported in its financial results. A parliamentary inquiry found no wrong-doing by Mr Trichet, other civil servants or the three finance ministers in office during the critical period. Moreover, last May, the public prosecutor recommended that the case against Mr Trichet be dismissed. However, the investigating magistrate, Philippe Courroye, overruled the public prosecutor in July, in a decision that stunned France's political establishment and the case proceeded to trial. Mr Haberer and two other former executives, Bernard Thiolon and Francois Gille, face charges of publishing misleading accounts and issuing fictitious dividends. Three auditors are also charged with failing to reveal the problems. Mr Trichet is charged with complicity and a man who worked for him at the Treasury, Jean-Pascal Beaufret - now chief financial officer at the French telecoms equipment maker Alcatel - faces similar charges. One of the key players in the drama, Pierre Bérégovoy, committed suicide from depression in 1993. The former prime minister had rejected suggestions that Mr Haberer's contract not be renewed in 1992 and he would have been a crucial witness. Mr Trichet is supposed to take over as president of the ECB under a deal struck at a European summit in May 1998. Jacques Chirac, the French president, wanted Mr Trichet to lead the ECB from its inception, but had to settle for an agreement whereby Wim Duisenberg, a Dutchman and the current president, would step down half way through his eight-year term in favour of a Frenchman. Mr Duisenberg has said he plans to retire on his 68th birthday in July, but has since said he could stay on longer if needed to ensure a smooth transition. Trial hearings are scheduled to run until mid-February, with a verdict delivered later. Conceivably, if Mr Trichet is cleared in time, he could take over as originally planned.

title: Buffalo Grill sellers cause stampede
bodyText: An avalanche of sell orders cost shares in the scandal-hit French steakhouse chain Buffalo Grill more than half their value yesterday as the stock resumed trading for the first time since December 18. Trading in Buffalo Grill had to be delayed at the start of the session because there were "way too many sell orders", one trader said, and by lunchtime the share was changing hands at 5.50 euros, little more than 40% of its pre-opening value. Four top managers of the company and its purchasing subsidiary, Districoupe, are under formal investigation, one step short of being charged, in an investigation by judge Marie-Odile Bertella-Geffroy into a number of deaths in France from the human form of mad cow disease. At least two of the victims were alleged to be frequent customers at Buffalo Grill, which has 150 restaurants in France and 50 in the rest of Europe. The chain is said to have imported British beef between 1996 and 2000, when the meat was banned in France because of fears it could be tainted with the brain-wasting disease. In an exceptional step yesterday, Ms Bertella-Geffroy wrote to the Paris public prosecutor to ask for the inquiry's initial evidence against Buffalo Grill to be made public in an attempt to prove the continuing necessity of her investigation and halt media speculation. The company's founder and supervisory board chairman, Christian Picard, made the same request - for the opposite reasons - last week, asking the French prime minister and justice minister to order the release of all relevant documents to show that the case against the chain was "inconsistent, dishonest, and completely empty of any telling or serious element". Mr Picard's lawyer, Jean-Pierre Versini-Campinchi, said the public prosecutor's assertion that the documents were covered by French judicial secrecy laws was becoming "more and more untenable". A growing number of "well-directed and carefully organised leaks" to the press made publication of the entire dossier essential, he said. Emeric Ernoult, another Buffalo Grill lawyer, said one such leak - to Le Canard Enchainé - was "just a lot of fuss about nothing". The satirical magazine printed an apparently incriminating email from a quality control manager referring to meat "from the mad cow disease period" which "must be got rid of". Mr Ernoult said the mail referred to stocks of Argentinian and Brazilian beef built up in 2001, when a number of new cases of mad cow disease were coming to light in France.

title: City briefing
bodyText: Finance ministers and central bank governors from the world's seven leading economies arrived in Paris last night for a weekend of talks on the world economy, overshadowed by the threat of war. They arrived as it emerged that the International Monetary Fund scaled back expectations for global growth this year and next, even before any attack on Iraq. According to the report, the Fund expects global growth of 3.3% this year and 4.2% in 2004, down from its original predictions of 3.7% and 4.6%. On the agenda in Paris are terrorist funding and international development, including Gordon Brown's plan to double global aid flows through a new international financing facility. Treasury officials said Mr Brown had been encouraged by his discussions with the new US treasury secretary, John Snow, about raising an extra $50bn a year for international development by borrowing from capital markets. Today's discussion is expected to be dominated by the likely impact of war in Iraq on the global economy and will include discussion of how to prevent skyhigh oil prices from strangling global growth. A lunchtime discussion on trade will put France, the host, in the hotseat with its opposition to reform of the costly common agricultural policy expected to draw fire from others. Lukoil in muddy ethical waters Russia's biggest oil group, Lukoil, said yesterday it had bought a small stake in an affiliate for $398m (£250m) to further consolidate assets, but questions have arisen about the deal's transparency. Analysts said the company, which launched an ambitious restructuring and cost-cutting plan last year to improve its image and catch up with its peers, agreed to buy back a minority stake from unknown investors in Russia's largest such transaction. It had agreed to buy PFPG-Energy, a firm that owns 27% of one of its upstream subsidiaries, Lukoil-Perm, for $398m in cash. Germany caps Tiger orders Airbus parent Eads was hit by another large cut in military orders yesterday, underscoring the tough times which have forced Europe's largest aircraft maker to plan cuts in costs. Eads will announce 2002 results on March 10 as 2003 earnings are threatened by soft demand for military and commercial aircraft and an unprofitable space sector. Germany yesterday capped an order for Tiger military helicopters at 80 in a move that defence minister Peter Struck said would save €700m (£476m). Torex swallows InHealth Torex, which develops software for the health care market, yesterday paid £3.5m in shares for InHealth Solutions, bringing the total it has spent on deals in the past 18 months to over £100m. Customers of InHealth Solutions, which provides laboratory, radiology, oncology, mental health and primary care operations, include 100 NHS hospital trusts and 180 GP surgeries.

title: French government pumps 9bn euros into Télécom
bodyText: The French government yesterday came to the aid of one of the former jewels in its crown as it backed a €15bn (£10.1bn) rights issue by France Télécom, the largest rescue refinancing in European corporate history. The government, which has a 56% stake in France Télécom, said it would pump roughly €9bn into the heavily indebted communications group. A rights issue to raise the remaining €6bn from investors has been fully underwritten by a raft of investment banks which are set to share over €100m in fees. France Télécom has been contemplating a rights issue to help pay down its €68bn debt since last year, but had been expected to wait until the end of the conflict in Iraq before coming to market. Yesterday, however, a source close to the French company said bankers advised management at the weekend that they could sell the necessary shares more than five times over. "Management thought that that was a good indication that now is as good a time as any to go ahead with the fund raising." Investment bankers contrasted investor appetite for refinancing large European corporations which have fallen on hard times to the lack of interest in new issues. Companies intending a flotation will have to continue to wait. The fundraising is a vital component of a rescue plan which includes refinancing bank debt, job losses and disposals put in place recently by France Télécom's new chief executive Thierry Breton. He took over last year from Michel Bon, the architect of the acquisitions spree which turned France Télécom into one of Europe's most indebted companies. Mr Breton said yesterday that the cash raised will reduce the company's financing costs by €500m this year and €700m to €800m in 2004 and set the company on the road to recovery. "I would like France Télécom to become a normal company as quickly as possible," he said. But the fund raising, which dwarfs rescue refinancings by other European telecom companies including BT, is being carried out at a 28% discount to France Télécom's current share price in order to drum up interest. Under the terms of the rights issue the company is offering one free warrant for each France Télécom share held by the close of trading yesterday. Investors can then exchange 20 warrants - which can be traded just like a share - for 19 new shares at €14.50 each by April 4. The share issue is being underwritten by a syndicate of 21 banks lead by ABN Amro Rothschild, BNP Paribas, Crédit Agricole Indosuez Lazard, Crédit Lyonnais, Deutsche Bank, Goldman Sachs, Merrill Lynch and Morgan Stanley. Cash calls March 2003 Company: France Telecom Amount: €15bn Cash needed to pay down €68bn of debts amassed buying assets, including Freeserve and Orange, during boom years. May 2001 Company: BT Amount: €9bn Almost €35bn in the red after acquisitions spree. The company was forced to sell assets and launch UK's largest ever rescue rights issue. December 2001 Company: KPN Amount: €5bn The Dutch telecoms operator had debts well in excess of €20bn after building up a position in the mobile phone world by buying up stakes in other companies and picking up a handful of mobile phone licences. March 2003 Company: Allianz Amount: €3.5-€4bn The loss-making insurance firm needed to get its balance sheet back on an even keel after losses and a downturn in its investment banking arm. April 2002 Company: Ericsson Amount: €3.25bn The dramatic drop in demand for mobile phone network equipment plunged the Swedish company into a loss and forced it to make extensive job cuts and implement the raising of additional capital.

title: French weapons maker may halve its workforce
bodyText: Workers at France's state-owned weapons maker, Giat Industries, are braced for a new round of job cuts which could see more than half the 6,200-strong workforce lose their jobs over the next two years. Giat management is expected to make the announcement, which will involve the closure of two plants in central France, this morning. Union leaders said after a board meeting yesterday afternoon that Giat was planning to slash the size of the workforce to 2,500 as it battles with a falling order book and bleak international prospects. The plants to close are at Cusset and Saint-Chamond, according to André Golliard, an official from France's second largest trade union, the CFDT. Unions are already planning to demonstrate against the cuts in Paris later this month and are demanding that the government take action to provide an acceptable alternative. Workers have already staged protests at several of the company's nine plants, according to trade union sources. The government will provide the company with a €1bn cash injection as part of the restructuring programme, according to the French newspaper La Tribune. A spokeswoman at Giat's Paris headquarters confirmed that the company would be making an announcement this morning. Giat has been hit by poor sales of two of its main products - the Leclerc, which is France's main battle tank - and the Famas assault rifle. The Leclerc, for example, has won only one big export order - for 436 tanks worth some $3.6bn from the United Arab Emirates in 1993 and which is thought to be close to completion. As well as tanks, Giat makes artillery, armoured vehicles, guns, munitions and small calibre firearms and has a number of joint ventures with other European arms makers. Last year the group lost €118m. The latest restructuring plan is the sixth in a series implemented by the struggling arms maker since 1988. Over the last 12 years its workforce has slumped from 18,000 to its current level. Sales last year were €777m, down by almost a a quarter on 1998 while its order book has slumped from €3bn to €2.4bn over the same period. Under the terms of the restructuring programme, drawn up by Giat management and France's ministry of defence and presented to the Giat board by chairman and chief executive Luc Vigneron yesterday afternoon, Giat is expected to be split into two units, with all its unprofitable business being put into a special holding structure.

title: Corus turns to France in search for salvation
bodyText: The new French chief executive of Corus is to receive a pay package of about £1.5m in his first year in a desperate effort by the board to turn around the Anglo-Dutch steel group. Philippe Varin, a long-time executive of French group Pechiney, was the board's surprise choice to succeed Tony Pedder, who resigned last month after Corus came close to collapse. He takes over on May 1, immediately after the group's annual meeting is expected to be given details of boardroom plans to rescue the British business through plant closures and hundreds of job cuts. A month later Sir Brian Moffat, effectively running the business since Mr Pedder's departure and architect of its failed strategy, will step down as chairman, a post he has held since January 1999. Already a pensioner, he will receive no pay-off. Mr Varin, aged 50 and latterly a Pechiney senior vice-president, won a warm welcome from British unions, which had campaigned for Sir Brian's dismissal, and from investors, who marked the shares up more than 31% to close at 12.50p. But he ran into immediate flak from the Dutch supervisory board and works council over his reaffirmation of Corus's existing strategy of concentrating on carbon steel and selling off the profitable aluminium business. The Dutch, backed by the courts, vetoed the £543m sale of the aluminium business - ironically, to Pechiney - and triggered the ousting of Mr Pedder and the latest crisis at Corus. Mr Varin, who will be joined by Jim Leng moving up from deputy chairman to chairman, is to be paid €1m (£690,000) a year, compared with the £550,000 Mr Pedder received. He will also get a pension allowance of £207,000. For his first year he has been guaranteed a minimum bonus worth 20% of his salary or £138,000 but he could earn up to 60% or £414,000 if he turns the business around quickly. "He's expected to hit the ground running," one source said. Mr Varin, who will be on two years' notice in his first year, has agreed to buy 1.1m shares, matching an award from the company, and will be granted options for a further 3.3m exercisable in three tranches over the next five years. He is also being paid a one-off sum of €800,000 in exchange for giving up all of his Pechiney options, giving him a total package worth £1.59m to spur him towards rescuing Corus. Mr Varin, who said he decided to leave Pechiney some time ago, said his first priority was to stem Corus's British losses, which propelled the group last year into a net deficit of more than £450m. He also plans to sort out the group's refinancing, a crucial element of its plans to salvage the UK business, which has been hammered by the strong pound and declining manufacturing base. Mr Varin said it was too early to say how he would repair the rifts between the British and Dutch arms of the business but his aim was to produce a "united and unified company". He is expected to make his first plant visit in Holland early next month. Sir Brian, who was paid £245,000 last year and had initially delayed his departure, said he was "delighted to be moving to pastures new at long last". His upbeat comments were echoed by Mick Leahy, leader of the ISTC union, who saw the appointment as a vindication of the workforce's campaign for new blood and a greater sense of partnership.

title: France given EC deficit deadline
bodyText: France came under unprecedented pressure yesterday to stop flouting the rules that govern the euro after the European commission gave Paris less than six months to tackle its wayward budget deficit or face the consequences. In a humiliating swipe at the French government, the EC ordered Paris to take remedial action to bring its budget deficit below the maximum 3% threshold allowed under EU rules by October 3 at the latest. It also stipulated that any measures taken would have to bear fruit by 2004. France's deficit came in at 3.1% last year and the EC warned it was likely to swell to 3.7% this year as a result of generous tax cuts and heavy government spending. Under the EU's stability and growth pact, which underpins the single currency, budget deficits must not exceed 3%. Germany and Portugal have fallen foul of the pact in the past. The EC's ultimatum to France - the eurozone's second largest economy - will have to be approved by EU finance ministers including Gordon Brown at a meeting in Brussels next month, but that is seen as a formality. If France then fails to respect the deadlines it could be fined up to 0.5% of its gross domestic product. To make matters worse, the EC said yesterday that France's budget woes were entirely of its own making. There was no severe downturn nor, it added, was there any "unusual event outside the control of the French authorities". French debt levels were also a cause for concern. Brussels said they were poised to shatter the hallowed ceiling of 60% of GDP - a prerequisite to join the euro in the first place - and urged preventive action. France has already said it will freeze public spending next year at 2003 levels and ensure its budget deficit is reined in.

title: Air France on course for sell-off
bodyText: Air France said yesterday that it could receive clearance for privatisation this summer but was unlikely to be ready for take-off into the private sector at least until September. Europe's second largest airline by passenger numbers said that shareholders, including the French state with a 54% holding, could give the go-ahead at the annual meeting in July. However, chief operating officer Pierre-Henri Gourgeon cautioned: "Immediately [after the annual meeting] there is summer, which is hardly favourable. We are ready for a process which could be launched from the beginning of September, depending on market conditions." Air France reported net in come for the last financial year of €120m (£86m) - after exceptionals including a €59m provision linked to the suspension of Concorde. That compared with €157m in the year to the end of March 2002. The group said results in the fourth quarter had been affected by war in Iraq and that a delay in economic recovery and the Sars crisis was affecting the current performance. Capacity and spending had been cut to cope with the downturn, it said. But the last few weeks had seen an upturn in advance booking across its network - except for Asia routes. "If this continues, Air France aims to post a slightly positive operating income before aircraft disposals for the year," it said, adding that it was proposing a dividend of €6 a share.

title: French government attempts controversial pensions reforms
bodyText: This week France faces the possibility of an all-out transport strike. Unions are furious at the government's plans to reform the country's generous pay-as-you go pensions scheme. They have already fired a warning shot across the bows of the ship of state but captain Jacques Chirac and his first lieutenant Jean-Pierre Raffarin are steaming on regardless. The government's thinking is easy to understand. At present, retirement pensions are funded by today's workers, whose own pensions will in turn be paid for by the next generation of wage earners. It is supposed to be a pyramid - with more workers than pensioners. But demographics are changing the rules of the game. As the workforce shrinks and pensioners live longer, the pyramid, according to one French commentator, is in danger of turning into a mushroom - a top-heavy burden of liabilities supported by a slender stalk of resources. The proposed reforms will mean French workers working on beyond age 60. The government also wants to bring the public sector into line with the private sector by extending their pension contribution period from 37.5 years to 40. It's not the first time the French government has tried to reform the pensions system. It did so in the mid-1990s and sparked a storm of opposition. The protests effectively wrecked the administration of then prime minister Alain Juppé. This time Mr Raffarin looks to have more firepower. Opinion polls show the French are aware of the need for reform, while the socialists are still trying to regroup after the hammering handed out at the last election. One of the main union groups has acquiesced although that appears to have hardened the resolves of the other two main groups. Time is of the essence. As the postwar baby boomer generation moves closer to retirement, future administrations may find it harder to win political support for reform. Turkeys don't vote for Christmas. Nor is the battle in France a purely domestic affair. Germany and Italy are among those countries with looming pension problems. The German chancellor, Gerhard Schröder, yesterday managed to get the backing of his party for his Agenda 2010 reform programme - which includes changes to pension provision - but that does not mean opposition is at an end. Germany's trade unions may be looking to take a less confrontational approach than their French colleagues but they will want a say in what happens. And it would not take many from the left of Mr Schröder's party in parliament to vote against the proposals to leave the chancellor considering his future career options. A victory for the French unions would resonate powerfully on the other side of the Rhine. This week could be the beginning of a long hot summer. · Mark Milner is the Guardian's deputy financial editor

title: Bank of France chief cleared of fraud
bodyText: The head of France's central bank was today acquitted of fraud charges and moved a step closer to succeeding Wim Duisenberg as president of the European Central Bank. Jean-Claude Trichet diplomatically told reporters after the acquittal that the possibility of his appointment was a matter for EU leaders. But France's finance minister, Francis Mer, said France would push his case at the intergovernmental summit in Thessaloniki, Greece, later this week. Mr Trichet had long been expected to succeed Mr Duisenberg as the eurozone's equivalent of Alan Greenspan, the head of the US Federal Reserve, but his appointment was held up by the fraud trial. Mr Duisenberg planned to retire on July 9 on his 68th birthday but recently agreed to stay on at the request of European governments until Mr Trichet's fate in the French courts became clear. He had been charged with complicity in the publication of misleading accounts at Crédit Lyonnais a decade ago when he was director of the French treasury. The bank nearly collapsed under the weight of ill-advised loans to finance real estate and entertainment investments. "I'm touched by the justice system's decision," he said. "Three years and two months ago I said that I had confidence in the justice system and it has now just ruled. I am happy and that's all I have to say." But a degree of uncertainty remains because there is still the possibility of an appeal. Nine people in all - civil servants and the bank's managers - were tried in the case. The Paris public prosecutor's office, which had asked judge Olivier Perusset to give Mr Trichet a suspended jail sentence of at least 10 months, said it had not yet decided whether to appeal and had 10 days to weigh the options.

title: Trichet clears final hurdle for ECB role
bodyText: The final hurdle to Jean-Claude Trichet's appointment as the next head of the European Central Bank was removed yesterday as the Paris public prosecutor said he would not appeal against the Bank of France governor's acquittal on charges of complicity in the Credit Lyonnais scandal. Prosecutor Yves Bot had 10 days to lodge an appeal against Wednesday's ruling by a Paris court that Mr Trichet was not guilty in the alleged cover-up of huge losses at the then state-owned Credit Lyonnais in the early 1990s. But Mr Bot said yesterday he had already decided against an appeal. "Everything has been said - we must move on now." That leaves the path clear for Mr Trichet to take over from current president of the ECB, Wim Duisenberg, as early as August or September. Speaking at a financial conference in Venice, Mr Trichet declined to comment on the news from Paris. He said the central bank's aim was for a "strong and stable euro", and all the members of the ECB governing council agreed that the UK's early entry into the single currency was desirable. The French banker, one of nine people on trial in the Credit Lyonnais scandal, was placed under formal investigation in 2000. As the French finance ministry's treasury director at the time of the scandal, he was in charge of overseeing the accounts of state-owned companies. Credit Lyonnais came close to collapsing under the weight of ill-advised loans, requiring a €31bn (£21.6bn) government rescue.

title: Chirac threat to veto reform of farm subsidies
bodyText: Jacques Chirac pushed global trade talks to the brink of collapse yesterday by threatening to veto reform of Europe's wasteful farm subsidies. In what other diplomats described as the "nuclear option", Mr Chirac incurred the wrath of Brussels after warning he was prepared to scupper months of painstaking negotiations on the common agricultural policy by invoking French national interest. The French president called a halt to CAP talks by farm ministers in Luxembourg late on Thursday night in the hope that he could use his personal clout and detailed knowledge of agriculture to force an agreement at the EU summit in Salonika this weekend. He was blocked by Costas Simitis, the Greek prime minister, who insisted that farm ministers should reconvene their talks next week, despite the furore caused by Mr Chirac's late intervention. France, the largest recipient of farm subsidies, has waged a campaign of attrition against the European Commission's reform proposals, forcing agricultural commissioner, Franz Fischler, to water down the package several times. Trade analysts said the reform package was now so weak it would be impossible for Europe to meet the demands of its trading partners in the new round of global trade talks launched in Doha 18 months ago. Cuts to the west's subsidies are seen as essential to keeping developing countries at the negotiating table. "The writing is on the wall for the Doha round," said Kevin Watkins, head of research at Oxfam. "It's been kneecapped by Chirac." Another EU diplomat who asked not be named said the problem was that few people dared stand up to Mr Chirac. "Leaders don't have the guts to tell him that something needs to be done about farm reform," he said. "They are chickens and their lack of nerve lets Chirac run the whole show." Diplomats said Mr Chirac had told Mr Simitis that if a majority decision went against France, he would invoke national interest to force talks to continue until there was unanimous agreement. In Brussels negotiation-weary EU diplomats did not even bother to conceal their anger towards France. "We were quite close to a deal and then all of a sudden France hardened its position," said one, referring to long but ultimately fruitless negotiations between EU farm ministers earlier this week. "The French farm minister suddenly threw up new obstacles and demanded new concessions. It was essentially a French plot. The French have not been negotiating in good faith - they have been wasting all our time." The agriculture commissioner had wanted to break the link between subsidies and farm output which the CAP's many critics say encourages wasteful overproduction. Europe's surpluses are dumped at below cost prices in the developing world, ruining local farmers. But after France and Germany formed an anti-reform axis ahead of the meeting and won the support of several other member states, Mr Fischler was forced to make substantial concessions, including reestablishing the link to output for up to 35% of subsidies in sensitive areas. The 45-year-old policy costs more than €40bn euros a year - half the EU's budget - and cannot be sustained at current levels when 10 new members join the club next year.

title: Chirac and Jospin basked in his reflected glories ...
bodyText: Messier had long been at the forefront of the French business community. With the Seagram merger, he transcended the Place de Paris. In the days following the merger, his cherubic features graced the cover pages of all the international weeklies; Time, Newsweek, Fortune and BusinessWeek were just a few of the US magazines which attempted to interpret the significance of the 43-year-old Frenchman. Many depicted him as a symbol of the changing face of French capitalism and of a new, self-confident country liberated from a wearisome complex over its own relative decline. Messier incarnated "la France qui gagne", the France that wins. Nor was it just the in ternational media that puffed the new economy miracle boss du jour. Messier was an unbeatable advertisement for an economically vibrant France, one that was ready to lead Europe in the race to dominate the industries of the new millennium. Both the Gaullist president Jacques Chirac and his socialist prime minister, Lionel Jospin, raced to bask in his reflected glory. In the year that followed the announcement of the merger, Messier experienced the kind of flattery that would have turned the head of an Easter Island statue. At a ceremony to anoint Messier the Franco-American chamber of commerce's person of the year, the French ambassador to the United States, François Bujon de l'Estang, set the tone: "Jean-Marie Messier, you have shown truly exceptional leadership and vision in taking the helm of the prestigious but somewhat stodgy company, Générale des Eaux, a major actor in water services and construction activities, and changing it almost overnight - well, five years - into the service group of the 21st century. You have shown along the way a clear will to become a major actor of globalisation and seize the opportunities it offers, instead of concentrating on the threats that it can bring. "On a more personal note," the ambassador continued, "you are the prototype of a new breed of executives that dispels the traditional clichés about France. You have championed a new type of corporate governance, more in line with international and, yes, American standards, and more attuned to the expectations of investors worldwide. In you and with you, Jean-Marie Messier, the chamber recognises today the new French economy that has enjoyed a tremendously good coverage in the American press, an economy of entrepreneurs (reminding us all that this is a French word), of business creation, of internet start-ups that are the avant-garde of 'la France qui gagne'." "'We are the champions! We are the champions.' Once again this refrain is on everyone's lips," observed Erik Israelewicz in Les Echos. "Messier's landing in Hollywood is a symbol of a newfound French aggression." France, it seemed, was winning everything. Less than a fortnight after Messier bought up a chunk of Hollywood, Zinedine Zidane led the French football team to a brilliant victory in the European Championship, repeating its triumph at the 1998 World Cup. Israelewicz contrasted the euphoria with the sombre mood that had overshadowed France three years earlier: "The country was worrying about 'the end of work' and 'austerity without end'. Today, it sees only 'la vie en rose' and 'la France qui gagne'."

title: Alstom unearths €51m black hole
bodyText: Alstom, the troubled French engineering group, yesterday faced a fresh blow to its credibility when it was forced to suspend two executives after discovering a €51m (£35m) black hole at its US train-making unit. The group, which lost a record €1.38bn last year, said its losses had been "significantly underestimated" because of "accounting improprieties" at Alstom Transportation Inc. Stéphan Rambaud-Measson, ATI's senior vice-president, and Joe Janovek, vice-president of finance, were suspended pending completion of an internal review "assisted by external accountants and lawyers". Its move, triggered by an informal inquiry by the US securities and exchange commission and the FBI, came ahead of tomorrow's shareholders' meeting to approve a €600m rights issue to help cut its €4.9bn debt. Alstom officials said the aim was to be as transparent as possible while industry sources said the fact that the group had been able to quantify the size of the hit showed the internal inquiry had advanced significantly. It emerged that the real and forecast costs of a contract being carried out at Alstom's Hornell plant in New York were understated, partly by shifting some of these on to other contracts. The group faces an unprecedented protest outside tomorrow's annual meeting by workers from Britain, France and Germany who are angry about the scale of the job cuts implemented to halve its debts by 2005. British unions, taking 50 shop stewards to Paris, claim as many as 5,000 jobs face the axe over the next year, the bulk of them in Britain. There are fears the job losses could reach 10,000. Alstom, which employs 11,000 people in Britain, last month said it would cease production at its Washwood Heath train-making plant in Birmingham, putting 1,000 jobs at risk. The group, which has been building the Pendolino tilting trains for Virgin Rail in Birmingham, says its British operations will increasingly focus on service rather than manufacture, and trains for London Underground's Jubliee line will be built in France or Spain. Alstom, whose shares have lost 90% of their value in two years and took a severe hit yesterday, recently sold its gas turbines business - the immediate source of its crisis - to Germany's Siemens for £900m. It employs 2,500 people in Lincoln. It is also selling its core power transmission and distribution business, with recent reports suggesting that French nuclear energy services group Areva is the sole contender after the withdrawal of cable-maker Nexans. A price tag of €1.2bn-€1.7bn has been put on the business though sources said the lower estimate was likely to prove more accurate.

title: EU threatens France's plans to save Alstom
bodyText: The French government's plans to rescue Alstom by taking a 30% stake in the engineering group via a rights issue yesterday threatened to bring it into conflict with EU competition authorities. Alstom is expected to announce as early as today a refinancing deal - prompted by government intervention - with its banks over its €4.9bn (£3.5bn) debts, enabling its €600m capital increase to go ahead. The group's board, which has been selling off core businesses to stay afloat, met again last night after telling its bankers: "It's make your mind up time," sources said. The banks had refused to act without government guarantees. The deal, which would see the French state contribute up to half the rights issue, ran into trouble in Brussels where the European commission said it had serious doubts about its legality. Mario Monti, EU competition commissioner, even phoned French finance minister Francis Mer to demand that any rescue package be submitted for his scrutiny at the earliest opportunity. Mr Mer, ironically masterminding a series of state sell-offs to help finance France's heavy budget deficit, agreed to do so. Mr Monti can block any state aid to companies which he deems to be unfair and is already investigating a €9bn credit line to France Telecom. The commission was sceptical, a spokesman said, whether a profit-driven private investor would have behaved in the same way as the French state and wanted to establish whether the move constituted illegal state aid or whether the French government had "other motives". He said the test would be whether the French state expected a market return on its investment. Alstom has lost 90% of its value in the past two years. "The company is facing a liquidity issue and a strategic business issue in its transport operations which invites careful scrutiny of whether a private investor would have participated in such a capital increase," he said. Alstom, which sold its turbines business to Siemens this month for €950m in cash and is in talks to sell its power transmission/distribution business to state-owned nuclear power plant builder Areva for around €1bn, employs 30,000 in France. It is closing its Birmingham-based train-making unit with the loss of up to 1,400 jobs.

title: Alstom secures £1.9bn rescue deal
bodyText: Struggling engineering group Alstom today secured a €2.8bn (£1.9bn) rescue package that gives the French government a 31.5% stake in the company. The firm, which makes TGV high-speed trains, cruise ships and gas turbines, is getting a capital injection of €600m, half of which will come from the government. The rest of the bailout will come in the form of convertible bonds worth €900m and new loans worth €1.3bn. The package "represents a considerable and exceptional effort of our shareholders, our banks and the state", said the chief executive, Patrick Kron. Francis Mer, the French finance minister, expressed his confidence that the rescue plan, financed with the proceeds of the government's recent sale of shares in car-maker Renault, would win the approval of EU competition authorities in Brussels. However, the European commission yesterday said that it had serious doubts about the legality of the package, as European competition laws ban government aid for companies.
