The commission can block such aid, and is already investigating a €9bn credit line to France Telecom. Alstom, which employs 100,000 people worldwide - a fifth of them in France - said in a statement that it faced a difficult environment, notably because of a slump in the power generation market. Accounting irregularities at a US transport subsidiary have also come as a huge embarrassment, and Alstom said that an investigation had unearthed the need for more charges that would take €100m out of operating profit. Alstom, whose shares have plunged by 90% in the past year, said it had requested that its shares, which were suspended on Monday pending a statement on the plan, would restart trading today. The firm has sold core businesses, slashed jobs and issued shares to cut its €5bn debt, and expects to sell its electricity transmission and distribution unit by next month.

title: Raffarin expects leniency on deficits
bodyText: France will reduce its budget deficit and boost growth next year but the European commission must be "flexible" about strict eurozone deficit rules, the French prime minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin urged yesterday. Mr Raffarin flew to Brussels for the meeting of the commission to signal that Paris would do what it could in difficult circumstances. "Europe will do its job, the French government will do its job," the conservative leader said after talks with Romano Prodi and his colleagues. "My initial responsibility is to mobilise everything to increase growth and encourage employment." But the French prime minister made clear that he expected a lenient approach to the stability and growth pact, which requires eurozone members to keep their deficits to a maximum of 3% of GDP. Both France and Germany, with unemployment over 9%, have exceeded the limit for the past two years and could face massive fines if, as is almost certain, they do so again this year. Les Echos, the Paris business newspaper, reported yesterday that the French finance ministry feared a deficit of 3.9% this year, compared with a current official forecast of 3.6%. In Berlin, meanwhile, a German government source said the finance ministry expected the 2003 deficit to reach 3.8%. Mr Raffarin's call followed a controversial appeal last month by President Jacques Chirac for the terms of the pact to be temporarily "softened". That outraged smaller eurozone countries such as Austria and the Netherlands, which are struggling to balance their books. It also fuelled a campaign for the stability pact to be reformed. Any decision to impose fines rests with EU finance ministers and would be extremely sensitive at a time when the union is preparing to take in eight new eastern European members, many with large deficits. But Mr Prodi stuck carefully to the formal position that repeated deficits will be punished. "We have no choice but to apply the rules of the treaty," he said. "This has to be done with the maximum possible flexibility. The commission will apply the treaty for the common good but we need full cooperation." France has to come up with new spending plans by early October. Mr Raffarin also said France would be applying for money from the EU's solidarity fund, set up after the devastating flooding in Germany last year, to help pay for the damage done by this summer's forest fires and drought.

title: French poodle loses its cool in the heat
bodyText: It will take months for the French government to recover from the political fallout of its handling of August's heatwave which killed about 11,400 people. It will take far longer for politicians to come to terms with the new-found aggression of the French press. In the space of a few weeks, the press's historically subservient approach to the government has been replaced with a new tone of vitriol. The nation's Health Minister was hounded for his failure to avert the deaths. Even in the right-wing papers the headlines were stark. 'The heatwave is killing people,' Le Figaro announced on 11 August - long before the government accepted that it had a problem on its hands, and days before the Prime Minister and the Health Minister saw fit to abandon their holidays. 'A national healthcare catastrophe,' Le Parisien warned two days later. Judged by its own more forthright standards, the left-wing daily Libération was unrestrained in its criticism, covering its front page with the words, the 'Government Under the Grill', orange flames curling around each letter. The gravity of the situation combined with the sluggish official response triggered howls of fury from the media, which demanded an official inquiry. Media analysts have begun asking whether the troubled summer will mark a revolution in the traditionally restrained and complicit nature of the French media. Daniel Schneidermann, France's leading media commentator and a columnist for Le Monde, said: 'I can't remember an occasion on which the press responded in such a critical way. They were pugnacious to a degree that is extremely unusual in France, even if by the standards of the British press there was nothing remarkable about its coverage. I found it extremely surprising.' Despite the mounting death toll, President Jacques Chirac decided not to cut short his holiday in Canada. He expressed his sorrow at the deaths of UN workers in Iraq but made no comment on reports that thousands were dying in France. The newspapers responded with bemused outrage. Jacques Esperandieu, deputy editor of the mild-mannered mid-market tabloid Le Parisien, said the paper had no choice but to adopt a hard-hitting stance reflecting the public's response. 'Ours is not a political paper, and we didn't seize on this as an opportunity to criticise the President. However, we sensed that the country was shocked by his delay in even commenting on the crisis and we had to reflect that.' The way that the heatwave was handled laid bare the extent of the political power vacuum that reigns in France every August. Newspaper offices and television studios, obliged to continue reporting on a daily basis, saw the impending catastrophe long before any senior politician did, simply because almost all of the government was on holiday. Oliver Costemalle, media correspondent for Libération, explained: 'When the heatwave began to turn bad, newspapers devoted all their energy, staff, resources and space to covering it.' The turning point came with the broadcast interview with the Health Minister on 11 August, 10 days after the record-breaking temperatures began to sweep through France and just as news of the first deaths was emerging. Jean-François Mattei was shown in a T-shirt, in the garden of his villa in the south of France, flatly dismissing reports of an impending catastrophe. His words were transmitted immediately after reports showing doctors in Paris unable to cope with the sudden influx of heat victims and funeral workers complaining that they were having problems burying the bodies fast enough. Prime Minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin echoed this casual approach in a statement from his Alpine holiday home. Costemalle said: 'People were horrified at the contrast between the gravity of the situation unfolding in Paris and Mattei's relaxed air, in the garden, in his shirtsleeves, somewhere on the Cote d'Azur - this was what triggered the much tougher approach to the government.' Because the government's most senior representatives were all away, there was no rapid reaction unit on hand to respond to the flood of bad news. Newspapers and television reports had no choice but to report the government's vociferous critics without balancing their words with an official response. The negative headlines mounted. Significantly, the most powerful figures in the French media timed their holidays to coincide with the departure of the political elite, and this may also have led to a harder approach. France's newspaper editors have always enjoyed uncomfortably close ties with the political establishment; most are former political correspondents and have spent decades working alongside the people they write about. Schneidermann believes this helps explain the media's traditional reluctance to launch outright attacks on the government. 'Most of them use the familiar form "tu" when they address each other. These people lunch together, take holidays together, have affairs with each other. Once you've established this kind of relationship, it's much harder to be critical,' he said. The fact that the editors too were away meant the more junior reporters felt no constraints in their coverage. Costemalle agrees: 'There was no one with concerns about their personal relationship with the government to put the brake on. Journalists were much freer to be as critical as they wanted.' The French government's alarm at this new phenomenon of a ferocious media is evident in the defensive measures it has taken. One of the Prime Minister's media handlers took the unprecedented step of making a written complaint to Agence France Presse about its use of the expression 'power vacuum', complaining that this was plucked from the mouth of the socialist party leader. Another official tried in vain to force the head of France 2 to stop broadcasting footage of the Prime Minister running downstairs, pursued by journalists demanding to know why the official response had been so slow. 'For years the French media has been criticised for its reticence; this summer may mark the beginning of a shift towards the extremes of British newspapers,' Schneidermann said. 'They have a long way to go before they register anywhere near the British aggression on a Richter scale; but this summer we have seen the intensity of their attacks creep higher up the scale.'

title: New ECB chief Trichet attacks French profligacy
bodyText: French banker Jean-Claude Trichet yesterday used the hearing that confirmed him as president of the European Central Bank to castigate his country for budget profligacy. France is in breach of the rules which forbid eurozone countries to exceed a budget deficit of 3% of gross domestic product - rules that were unanimously agreed by all eurozone governments before the single currency's introduction in 1999, he noted. "It seems to me that it is up to all partners to be up to their responsibility," he told the hearing. "Given the current deficits that some member states have, to say that we can just continue like that is not appropriate from an economic point of view." Mr Trichet cleared the final hurdle to assuming the presidency of the ECB when the European parliament gave him its blessing. MEPs had vowed to give him a hard time at the crucial confirmation hearing, casting aspersions on his integrity and suitability for the job - despite the fact that he has been acquitted of complicity in a French banking scandal in the early 1990s. In the event, the parliament's powerful economic and monetary affairs committee voted by 20 votes to two to approve his appointment with only four abstentions. The full parliament is now expected to ratify the committee's decision this month. Mr Trichet will take over from Wim Duisenberg on November 1. Mr Trichet's criticisms of the French deficit came as the ECB warned France and Germany to get their finances in order. "Recent fiscal developments in the euro area are of great concern. There is growing evidence that most countries will miss their budgetary targets for 2003 by a significant margin," the bank said in its monthly health check on the eurozone economies, pointing at Germany, France, Italy and Portugal as particular causes of concern. The fresh round of criticism of Paris and Berlin comes as the debate about the EU's tattered stability and growth pact intensifies. A European commission idea to use a legal loophole to let the eurozone's two biggest budget transgressors, France and Germany, off the hook has caused widespread consternation. Senior Dutch government officials said yesterday they would sue the commission if it went through with the plan. MEP Theresa Villiers, one of the Tories who abstained in the vote, said the ECB head faced a difficult task. "Mr Trichet is clearly an able and intelligent banker. However, he had no solutions to offer to remedy the dismal economic performance of the eurozone. He will be taking on an impossible job - finding one interest rate to suit 12 different countries."

title: EU blocks France's Alstom rescue plan
bodyText: The European commission today blocked France from pumping state money into Alstom, the maker of TGV trains. However, the commission gave the French government breathing space to come up with an alternative plan. In making its unanimous decision, which will come into effect in five days, the EU executive also formally opened an investigation into the bailout plan for the engineering giant. The deal would give the French government ownership of almost one-third of the company and provide €3.4bn (£2.3bn) in a mix of private and public money. Alstom has been hit by cost overruns on key projects, accounting irregularities at its US operation, and the bankruptcy of a major client. The company's share price has plunged 90%, and it has shed thousands of jobs over the past two years. The European commission has already announced plans to take legal action, on October 1, forcing France to make troubled computer maker Bull repay a €450m rescue loan to the government. The commission is willing for France to make a small temporary loan to keep Alstom, which also manufactures ships and electric generation systems, afloat. However, Mario Monti, the competition commissioner, said that France would have to defer a major injection of funds until the commission could review the plans to ensure that they did not violate EU competition rules. The commission issued two suspension orders against France: one barring it from making a proposed €300m capital injection, and the other blocking a €300m loan, to be made on favourable terms. One impact of the order was immediately apparent, as Alstom's stock plunged 8.5% to €2.79 before the French bourse suspended trading. "We remain open to continued dialogue," Mr Monti said, adding he was confident of reaching a solution that will avoid competitive distortions. In Paris, government spokesman Jean-Francois Cope echoed that, stressing that many jobs depended on Alstom's survival. He said France sought "a solution that reconciles respect for EU rules [with] the need to do everything to preserve employment in an industrial group". Alstom, Mr Cope said, guaranteed "tens of thousands of jobs" in France, and elsewhere in the EU.

title: Gallic charm fades for bucket-shop brigade
bodyText: Ryanair suffered another defeat in the French courts yesterday over the subsidies it enjoys from publicly-owned airports across the continent. It lost an appeal over an earlier ruling that it must tear up its deal with Strasbourg airport; Ryanair flights to the city will be suspended while a full appeal is heard. That could take a year. Flights to another 18 publicly-owned airports across France now look under threat, while later this year the airline faces an altogether more significant court-room battle in Belgium, where the European commission is challenging the payments it receives from Charleroi - Ryanair's first continental airport hub. Michael O'Leary, the airline's chief executive, is likely to invent new expletives if what is alleged to be illegal state aid is removed from his business plan. Ryanair will be grounded financially. The French case has caused uproar amongst well-heeled British Francophiles, who have snapped up pretty retreats in obscure corners of the French countryside on the assumption they would be travelling there at bucket-shop prices. The row has a wonderful xenophobic lining, in that the French case has been brought by a subsidiary of Air France, a bloated state-owned airline which has relied on state handouts for its survival for as long as anyone can remember. But did it not occur to those buying second homes that the absurd prices they were paying for their airline tickets might prove unsustainable? Did they really think it might only cost £5, say, to transport a person to Carcassonne? O'Leary is not a financial magician. The transportation costs have been met by taxpayers across Europe. And the environmental costs associated with mass short-haul air travel are being left for future generations to pick up. If, in a past moment of stupidity, you bought shares in Ryanair, sell them now. And go to Brighton for the weekend. Portfolio for danger The blame game, following the collapse of global trade talks last weekend, is already souring the atmosphere ahead of next week's meetings of the World Bank and International Monetary Fund in Dubai. In the hours following the breakdown, Brussels and Washington lambasted developing countries for refusing to accept the modest concessions the two big trading powers were offering. Yesterday the sniping turned transatlantic when the US accused Europe of having brought the talks to the point of collapse with its demands for a new global investment and competition treaty. Finance ministers, generally, get along better than their colleagues in the trade world, if only because their meetings are occasions for exchanges of windy rhetoric rather than real negotiations involving national interests. But fine talk will not be enough to solve the challenges facing the world economy which the fund spelt out in its twice-yearly assessment of it yesterday. Real cooperation is necessary in at least three areas: realigning global currencies which have moved seriously out of kilter; designing a system for preventing a repetition of the financial crises which shook the global economy in the 1990s; and stumping up the cash for the ambitious agenda world leaders have set on global development. In the present atmosphere, a breakthrough on debt or aid in Dubai seems unlikely. Worryingly, a greater danger is that far from cooperating to solve the challenges, ministers could turn the blame game from trade to international finance and indulge in a dangerous bout of competitive devaluations to keep their own economies afloat at the expense of their neighbours. Everyone's business The Times and the Financial Times were at each other's throats yesterday after the publication of the British Business Survey, which, every three years, asks a defined universe of business people which publications they read. The FT, declaring itself to be "Britain's top business paper", told its readers that more chief executives read the FT than any other UK daily paper. The Times, meanwhile, said the survey showed it is read by 49% more business people than the FT and 29% more than the Daily Telegraph. The survey includes lots of other nuggets, such as the fact that National Geographic magazine has 30% more business readers in this country than the Economist. Oh, and Andrew Neil's The Business now has a market share of 1.1%, just beating the Daily Star Sunday. Thank you for reading Britain's fastest growing daily, quality business paper.

title: Alstom awaits bankers' decision
bodyText: The survival of French engineering firm Alstom lay in the hands of its creditors last night as Paris and Brussels appeared to be nearing consensus on a rescue plan. With the clock ticking towards an EU deadline at midnight tonight, Alstom's 32 banks have to decide whether to pay €2.4bn, on top of €800m from the French state, to avert a slide into backruptcy. The banks must give their verdict by lunchtime. If they back the plan, Paris will submit it to EC officials, who last week vetoed a €3.4bn state-backed rescue that would have given Paris 31.5% of Alstom's capital. "Tonight, I believe that a solution is possible," European trade commissioner Pascal Lamy told France's LCI TV yesterday. "That said, it is not over yet." At stake is the future of 110,000 employees and a French industrial powerhouse. Alstom equipment, including the turbine at St Alban nuclear plant, central France, above, generates a fifth of the world's electricity, and its shipyard is building the world's biggest cruise liner, the Queen Mary II. Alstom's woes grew acute with a record €1.38bn full-year net loss in May. The finance ministry declined to comment, but sources said the €3.2bn rescue plan involved the French state buying a €300m bond with a 20-year maturity convertible intoshares.

title: Alstom sells off high-voltage business
bodyText: Alstom, the troubled French engineering group, today announced the sale of its most profitable business as part of its recovery programme. The maker of TGV high-speed trains, power plants and luxury cruise ships agreed to sell its high-voltage equipment arm to nuclear power plant maker Areva. The transaction values the unit at around €950m (£657.5m). The sale of the transmission and distribution arm, which employs 25,000 people, including 3,500 in the UK, is also expected. Alstom said that the sale would help repay loans it received last month as part of a €3.2bn bailout by the French government. The engineering group, which is struggling with debts of €5bn, had originally hoped to sell the transmission and distribution unit for more than €1.5bn. EU antitrust officials have said that they have no problem with French state-owned Areva buying the unit, but wanted to ensure that the terms offered were similar to those in the private sector. The EU is also taking a close look at a broad rescue package put together by the French government and Alstom creditors to save the company, which employs around 110,000 people. The investigation could prompt the EU's executive commission to force Alstom to sell further assets if it rules that the bailout raises competition concerns. Patrick Kron, Alstom's chairman and chief executive, has indicated that he aims to plans to sell the company's marine division, which makes luxury cruise ships such as the Queen Mary 2, currently being built in northern France. However, he has ruled out the sale of the high-profile TGV train business. Siemens, the German engineering company, is a prime candidate to buy some of Alstom's assets. Alstom, battered by cost overruns on key projects, accounting irregularities at its US operation and the bankruptcy of a major client, has lost 90% of its market value and shed thousands of jobs over the past two years.

title: Alcan gets final approval to bid for Pechiney
bodyText: Canadian aluminium group Alcan cleared yesterday the final regulatory hurdle in the way of its agreed €4bn (£2.8bn) takeover of French rival Pechiney, enabling it formally to launch its offer later today. The French stock market regulator CMF said it had given final approval for Alcan to open its bid, which had already secured the green light from American, Canadian and European competition authorities and the French bourse watchdog COB. The Pechiney board, which finally succumbed to Alcan's advances in mid-September after twice rejecting bid approaches, is expected to recommend the bid officially tomorrow. Alcan hopes to complete the takeover before the end of next month, propelling it into front position in the global aluminium business by overtaking American rival Alco in terms of sales. The Canadian group, which initially offered €41 a share in early July and then raised its offer to €47-€48 two months later, has said it will pay up to €48.50 in cash and shares, including a €1 per share supplement if 95% of shareholders vote for the deal. It has now agreed to fix the share price five trading days before the offer closes, using the average of 10 random trading days within the 30 days that the offer stands. The closing date is likely to be November 20 or 21. The offer won the backing of French unions after Alcan undertook to make no further redundancies. The Pechiney board also backed the bid after the Canadians agreed to place several global business centres in France, including the core headquarters for aerospace, the fastest-growing segment of the aluminium industry. Alcan's takeover leaves unanswered the fate of the profitable aluminium business of Corus. The Anglo-Dutch steel group saw plans to sell its aluminium business to Pechiney scuppered by its Dutch supervisory board, works council and the courts.

title: Mild EU rebuke for French deficit
bodyText: France today earned little more than a slap on the wrist for failing to keep its budget deficit within the limits set by EU financial rules. The European commission in Brussels said France had not taken sufficient measures to rein in its budget deficit, but stopped short of a formal declaration that the French government was in breach of the rules governing the single currency, a move that could have led to hefty fines. France's budget deficit is running at 3.1% of national wealth, just above the 3% ceiling permitted under the stability and growth pact that forms the financial cornerstone of the eurozone countries. The commission says it looks as if the problem will get worse, with the deficit reaching at least 3.7% this year. But despite France's financial misdemeanour, the commission would find it politically unacceptable to start imposing fines, particularly in the wake of the Swedish referendum "no" to joining the euro. EU officials are hoping France will do enough in the next few weeks to justify avoiding an embarrassing clash, but European finance ministers admit there is nothing they can do to stop France breaching the stability and growth pact for a third successive year in 2004. The French finance minister, Francis Mer, however, is expected to extend an olive branch to Brussels by agreeing to commission requests to further cut France's underlying deficit and to make additional structural reforms. Meanwhile, Graham Watson, the Liberal Democrat leader in the European parliament, said Britain could not be expected to consider joining the euro if others were allowed to ignore the rules. The UK would be subject to the same strictures if it joined the euro, and the pressure on France is being seized on by eurosceptics who claim adopting the single currency would effectively hand control of the Treasury to Brussels. "Sweden's vote of no confidence last month proves that the EU still has some work to do in convincing the public of its most important projects. Going back on its word now will reduce Europe's credibility once again," Mr Watson said. The stability and growth pact was introduced, mainly at the behest of Germany, to discourage the likes of Italy from financial irresponsibility. But it has proved to be a straitjacket for economies plagued by slow growth or recession as in the case of France and Germany. Germany is also expected to exceed the 3% budget deficit target.

title: US gives French longer Life line
bodyText: The French government is understood to have won more time in its attempts to strike a deal with the US authorities over the failed Californian insurer Executive Life. Initially the French government had been given until today to reach a deal but is now reported to have been offered a four-week extension to the deadline by US prosecutors. The Executive Life affair dates back to the early 1990s. US prosecutors allege that the then state-owned French bank Crédit Lyonnais used other companies to acquire Executive Life assets at knock-down prices. As a state-owned bank Crédit Lyonnais was barred from owning Californian insurance assets. In September the two sides agreed a $585m (£345m) settlement - most of which would be funded by the French government - but France refused to ratify the agreement after US prosecutors sought to change the terms to allow them to take action against individuals allegedly involved in the affair. Finance minister Francis Mer reiterated that France wanted an agreement which settles all the issues: "I think we will find an acceptable deal. It is necessary and normal to include in this accord all the proceedings under way." If a deal is not reached Crédit Lyonnais, now owned by Crédit Agricole, could lose its US banking licence. Weekend press reports in France suggested that Mr Mer is seeking talks with US treasury secretary John Snow in an attempt to reach an agreement. No one at the US treasury could be reached for comment yesterday. But Mr Snow is expected to have talks with his French counterpart on the issue at the G-20 summit in Mexico later this week.

title: France and Germany to flout budget rules until 2006
bodyText: The economic fundamentals that underpin the eurozone looked shaky last night after the European commission warned that its two biggest economies - Germany and France - are on course to flout EU budget deficit limits for four years running. Despite the threat of punitive fines, Pedro Solbes, the EU monetary affairs commissioner, disclosed that both countries were likely to run budget deficits above the EU's maximum 3% of GDP ceiling until 2006. They could only avert such a situation, he added, if they took urgent remedial action. The news came as the German government yesterday approved the biggest budget deficit since the second world war. The cabinet agreed to a plan by the finance minister, Hans Eichel, that would see the country's debts rise to €43.4bn (£30bn) this year - twice the amount originally forecast and well above the 3% ceiling. Mr Solbes noted that three countries have broken the rules of the EU's stability and growth pact in the past (Germany, France and Portugal) and warned that the Netherlands, Italy and Greece were looking like they would follow suit. He said the UK, which is outside the eurozone, was also at risk. The commission estimated that the UK deficit would swell to 2.8% this year from 1.5% in 2002. Although the commission could not fine the UK if it broke the 3% limit it might rebuke it for sloppy handling of public finances.

title: France tests pact to breaking point
bodyText: The European Union's strict budgetary rules have their day of reckoning tomorrow as France continues to defy calls from Brussels to to cut its budget deficit. Eurozone Finance Ministers are expected to agree that France has failed to comply with orders to bring its deficit below the 3 per cent cap of the EU's Stability and Growth Pact. But France may offer enough last-minute concessions to further delay a vote by the Ecofin meeting of Europe's 15 Finance Ministers - including Chancellor Gordon Brown - on specific sanctions for the French government. Economists say that the European Commission, guardians of the Stability Pact, may struggle to regain its credibility for fiscal discipline. 'While the pact might have been followed to the letter by the Commission, after next week's vote its bark will have been shown to be worse than its bite,' says Michael Hume at Lehman Brothers. Smaller European countries, such as the Netherlands and Austria, that have adhered to the pact, have led the campaign to bring the French into line. Paris has drawn up a budget that forecasts a budget deficit of 3.6 per cent. The Commission has given France an extra year to make spending cuts or tax increases. But France insists it can do little more. Britain, which will have a vote in any final decision on possible fines, has been quietly pushing for reforms of the pact to bring it into line with the Treasury's own fiscal rules. Europe's major powers are thought to be sympathetic, but concerned about the damage to Europe's financial credibility that could be caused by any apparent loosening of the pact. Even the current wrangle has spooked financial markets. 'Whether this has any long-term implications for Europe's bond markets is not clear at this stage but, at the margin, it must diminish the probability of a quick return to a "tight" fiscal policy and so put upward pressure on bond yields,' says Hume. In a vital week for the future of the European economy, Frenchman Jean-Claude Trichet also takes over the helm at the European Central Bank with his first policymaking meeting as president, replacing Wim Duisenberg. ECB base rates are expected to be held at 2 per cent.

title: Former Elf oil chiefs jailed
bodyText: France's mammoth Elf corruption case, probably the biggest political and corporate sleaze scandal to hit a western democracy since the second world war, drew to a close yesterday as three key former executives of the oil giant were jailed for up to five years. Elf's former chairman, Loik Le Floch-Prigent, 60, was sentenced to five years in jail and fined €375,000 (£260,724); his principal bag-man, the former director Alfred Sirven, was given the same prison term and ordered to pay €1m. The company's "Mr Africa", André Tarallo, was jailed for four years and fined €2m. The judge, Michel Desplan, said Le Floch bore "the primary responsibility" for the Elf affair and was "personally behind a majority of the misappropriations". To Sirven, he said: "All this would not have happened without Le Floch, but it could not have existed without your help." The three were among 37 defendants on trial for illegally siphoning off an extraordinary €350m of the then state-owned company's funds, from 1989 to 1993, while Le Floch was chairman. The never-ending stream of cash was used to buy political favours at home and abroad, and to fund some extravagant lifestyles. But the four-month trial, which had France riveted with its tales of political graft and sumptuous living, was also that of a system of state-sanctioned sleaze that flourished in France for years: successive politicians saw the country's state-owned multinationals not just as undercover foreign policy tools, but as a convenient source of ready cash to keep friends happy and enemies quiet. Le Floch, whose lawyer said yesterday his client would not be appealing to the court, insisted throughout his trial that he was in "daily contact" with the Elysée palace, and that "all the presidents of France" had known of, and condoned, the company's illicit dealings. Elf, now privatised and part of the Total group, paid "at the very least" €5m a year to all of the main French political parties to buy their support, Le Floch told the court at one stage. Most of the money went to the centre-right RPR party founded by the present president, Jacques Chirac, until the socialist François Mitterrand, soon after his presidential election in 1982, demanded that the spoils be evenly spread In their 1,045-page indictment and a further 44,000 pages of documents, the investigating magistrates described in detail "a large number of operations carried out on the margins of normal functioning of the group's structures, and destined... to collect assets off the books". In addition to jail terms totalling 60 years, the public prosecutors sought a record €34.5m in fines against the 37 defendants, who included business associates of the company and executives' relatives accused of having benefited from illegal largesse. Among those sentenced yesterday was Le Floch's former wife, Fatima Belaid, found guilty of receiving €4.6m from Elf in exchange for her silence over the company's underhand dealings after the couple agreed to divorce. She was sentenced to three years in prison, of which two were suspended, and fined €1m. Many of the missing millions were paid out in illegal "royalties" to various African leaders and their families. Tarallo told the court in June that annual cash transfers totalling about £10m were made to Omar Bongo, Gabon's president, while other huge sums were paid to leaders in Angola, Cameroon and Congo-Brazzaville. The multi-million dollar payments were partly aimed at guaranteeing that it was Elf and not US or British firms that pumped the oil, but also to ensure the African leaders' continued allegiance to France. In Gabon, Elf was a veritable state within a state. France accounts for three-quarters of foreign investment in Gabon, and Gabon sometimes provided 75% of Elf's profits. In return for protection and sweeteners from Elf's coffers, France used the state as a base for military and espionage activities in west Africa. Illegal commissions were also paid to businessmen and third-party associates to smooth Elf's business dealings closer to home: about £30m was paid out "under the counter" in the company's 1992 acquisition of the Leuna refinery in east Germany. Among those sentenced yesterday were Nadhmi Auchi, an Iraqi-born British billionaire, who was fined €2m over a 1992 transaction with Elf, and Dieter Holzer, a German businessman, who was accused of taking kickbacks on the Leuna deal, and given 15 months in jail and a fine of €1.5m. But the bulk of yesterday's convictions focused on the way Elf's senior executives shamelessly enriched themselves. Michel Desplan, the judge, told Le Floch he was "the source of most of the misappropriations... carried out to enrich yourself personally". Le Floch was found guilty of personally misappropriating €180m, while Sirven and Tarallo, both now aged 76, were convicted of pocketing €106.7m and €25.9m respectively. Much of the money was spent on jewellery, artworks and sumptuous apartments: the magnificent villa that Tarallo bought on Corsica was estimated at €23.1m, while Sirven spent €1m a year for six years on jewels alone. Le Floch and Sirven are already serving jail terms after another Elf court case: the former socialist foreign minister Roland Dumas was convicted of accepting kickbacks but then acquitted when it was found he did not realise the gifts from his mistress - including £1,100 Italian shoes, and five antique statues worth £26,400 - were paid for by Elf. Landed: big fish who swam in murky waters Andre Tarallo Ex-head of hydrocarbons division, 76, (outside court, above) nicknamed 'Mr Africa' for friendships with African leaders. Brilliant mandarin; graduated alongside Chirac from elite ENA civil service college. Ensured up to $30m a year was paid into secret bank accounts of Gabon's Omar Bongo. Convicted of embezzling €30m. Four years jail and €2m fine. Loik Le Floch-Prigent Former Elf chairman, 60, appointed by Mitterrand after running state railway SNCF. Once one of France's best-known civil service high-flyers, claims he is a scapegoat for the French state's 'subtle game'. Convicted of pocketing €180m. Once bought £6,800 worth of CDs on company credit card in a single afternoon. Sentenced to five years and €375,000 fine. Alfred Sirven Le Floch's deputy, 60. Held purse strings of Elf's entire embezzlement operation, running secret bank accounts in Switzerland, Liechtenstein, Monaco, Luxembourg, Austria and Virgin Islands and personally carrying suitcases of cash around world. Four years on run before arrest in 2001. Misappropriated €107m; sentenced to five years and fined €1m. Fatima Belaid Le Floch's ex-wife and, according to judge Michel Desplans, a 'pernicious' influence on her husband. Managed to ensure Elf paid for divorce settlement; effectively received €4.6m of company money in exchange for keeping her mouth shut about what she knew (a great deal) about dodgy dealings. Sentenced to three years (two suspended) and fined €1m.

title: Eurozone boosted by French and German growth
bodyText: The French and German economies, which account for half the eurozone economy, are showing signs of flickering to life, according to official figures released today. Germany, Europe's biggest economy, pulled out of its second recession in two years in the third quarter, expanding by 0.2% between July and September. It was a feeble expansion compared to that of the turbo-charged US economy, which grew at an annual pace of 7.2% in the third quarter, but nevertheless boosted hopes that Europe will make some contribution to global growth. There was also a pickup in French economic activity. Having narrowly avoided recession in the first half of 2003, France expanded by 0.4% in the third quarter. Analysts also expect figures from Italy, which are due on Friday, to show a return to growth. "Germany's move out of recession is accompanied by a fall in unemployment of 479,000 on a year earlier, which will lift domestic demand in the German economy," Andrij Halushka, an economist with the Centre for Economics and Business Research, said. "Today's data are expected to support the European stock markets. It is also becoming even more likely that the eurozone rates will stay at 2% for now." The European Central Bank, which left rates unchanged at a record low of 2%, said there were growing signs that the eurozone economy was recovering. However, it added that inflation would not come down as swiftly as had been thought some months ago. The French and German GDP figures provided hard evidence of an upturn, particularly after the International Monetary Fund had yesterday rebuked the eurozone for not pulling its weight. "I see that business confidence seems to be mounting in Europe, but I don't see it being reflected in the actual numbers," Raghuram Rajan, IMF chief economist, told Reuters. "Confidence is a leading indicator - so you hope that might signal something about what's coming down the line - but the numbers have not been particularly encouraging." In September, the IMF had remarked that Europeans who wanted to see economic recovery would have to watch it on television. The eurozone economy is still likely to lag behind the world's other leading economies, and its interest rates are expected to stay unchanged until the middle of next year in order to foster recovery. In contrast, the Bank of England last week raised interest rates against a background of strong consumer demand and a frothy housing market. The eurozone's weak performance has led to criticism of the ECB for not pushing down interest rates far or fast enough, unlike the US Federal Reserve. Germany, which represents 30% of the eurozone economy, has been tagged the sick man of Europe because of its feeble economic performance over the past two years. The major drag on the region, it is still not expected to grow at anything like the rate of the US. Other figures revealed a boost in another part of the eurozone, with the Netherlands demonstrating growth in the third quarter after nine months of contraction. Gross domestic product expanded by 0.1% from the second quarter, but shrank 1.1% year on year after contracting 1.2% in the previous quarter. In its quarterly inflation report yesterday, the Bank of England said that it expected growth in the euro area to return to around trend (2.5%) next year, although prospects were somewhat weaker than in its last report in August.

title: France and Germany return to growth
bodyText: Hopes of economic recovery in the eurozone received a boost yesterday with news that the two powerhouse economies of the single currency bloc, Germany and France, have returned to growth. Stronger exports, particularly to the buoyant US, are believed to be the source of expansion that has followed a dismal year in which Germany has seen activity flat or falling for three successive quarters and France saw output decline in two out of three quarters. Figures released yesterday showed that Germany's economy grew by 0.2% in the third quarter of 2003, but output was still 0.2% lower than in the same quarter of 2002. France posted growth of 0.4%, but gross domestic product was also 0.2% weaker than a year earlier. Germany's federal statistical office said exports from Europe's biggest economy had grown strongly, helping to off set the continued softness of domestic demand. Figures from France showed a jump in the trade surplus between August and September from €686m (£480m) to €922m. Some City analysts warned that the recovery could be choked off by the rise in the value of the euro against the dollar, which makes exports from the eurozone more expensive. However, the data was welcomed by supporters of the euro in the UK, who have found it more difficult to make the case for British membership of the single currency while Germany and France have been struggling. Philippe Legrain, chief economist of Britain in Europe, said: "The economic recovery in Germany and France, the second and third biggest destinations for British exports, is good news for both the eurozone and Britain. It is powerful evidence that the pessimism about Europe is overdone: far from suffering from inexorable decline, it appears to be on the road to recovery. And as demand recovers, the benefits of the euro to long-term economic growth - increased trade, investment and productivity growth - will become more apparent." The flipside to export-led growth in France and Germany, however, was a deterioration in the already-huge US trade deficit. Record imports meant America's shortfall with the rest of the world rose for the first time in six months - from $39.2bn to $41.3bn. On the currency markets, news of the widening US deficit put downward pressure on the dollar, even though markets shrugged off a small increase in the number of jobless claims in the past week. Analysts said the increase of 13,000 to 366,000 still suggested the labour market was improving, helping to underpin the prospects of the US economic recovery taking root over the next few months.

title: EDF must repay state aid
bodyText: EDF group, the French company that provides a quarter of Britain's electricity needs, is about to be asked to return almost €1bn (£700m) in illegal state aid, the European commission disclosed yesterday. The state-owned firm, traditionally known as Electricité de France, has been accused by competitors in the past of funding overseas acquisitions with French taxpayers' money and yesterday's news is likely to be seen as fresh evidence of unfair play. EDF owns London Electricity, Seeboard and SWEB in Britain and has spent billions acquiring stakes in privatised utilities in Italy, Spain and Germany. Although energy markets in Britain and elsewhere in Europe are liberalised, the same has not been true in France and EDF has managed to expand abroad while retaining a virtual monopoly in France and remaining state-owned. The commission believes EDF has benefited from huge amounts of illegal state aid and officials have drawn up a decision designed to strip EDF of its unfair competitive advantage. A spokesman said yesterday EDF will be ordered to return €888m plus interest to the French state, which Brussels argues it received in illegal tax breaks between 1986 and 1997. That puts the commission on a collision course with the French government, which is on record as claiming that the tax breaks did not constitute illegal state aid. The commission will also demand EDF be stripped of its unlimited state guarantee, which means it is exempt from France's bankruptcy and insolvency laws. "France is now supposed to put an end to the unlimited guarantee and turn itself into a limited company," said the spokesman. The French government has already promised it will privatise EDF and is negotiating with trade unions about the likely impact on jobs.

title: Executive Life saga poised for court
bodyText: The French government is on course for a courtroom clash with authorities in the US over a failed Californian insurance group, Executive Life. The chances of the issue going to trial have risen dramatically this week after long running talks between French officials and US prosecutors broke down without a resolution. Yesterday the French government said it was still hoping for a negotiated settlement but spokesman Jean-Francois Cope said: "We are not afraid of a trial." If the legal battle were to go against the French side it would open the way for large civil claims against the French parties involved and might ultimately cost Crédit Lyonnais - now owned by Crédit Agricole - its banking licence in the US. Crédit Agricole has its own, separate banking licence in the US. The row dates back to 1991 when US authorities allege that the then state-owned French bank Crédit Lyonnais bought some of the assets, including the junk bond portfolio, of Executive Life. At the time banks were banned from owning insurers and California law barred foreign governments from owning insurers based in the state. Back in September France pulled out of a deal under which it offered a $585m (£350m) settlement on condition the US authorities agreed to drop all claims including those against former Crédit Lyonnais boss Jean Peyrelevade and Francois Pinault, a close friend of French president Jacques Chirac. Mr Pinault's Artemis company bought part of the Executive Life junk bond portfolio. The French finance ministry refused to comment yesterday on why the negotiations had broken down but it is understood that the two sides could not reach agreement on the inclusion of Mr Pinault and Mr Peyrelevade in the deal. "It would be dangerous not to get a settlement which is as wide as possible," said a French source. Mr Pinault, who is said to be one of the three richest men in France and head of the PPR retail chain, has denied any wrongdoing as has Mr Peyrelevade who became chairman of Crédit Lyonnais after the Executive Life deal had taken place.

title: Crash airline banned by Swiss a year ago
bodyText: The Egyptian charter airline whose Boeing 737 crashed on Saturday killing all 148 people on board, including 133 French tourists, has been banned from flying to one European country for more than a year because of safety concerns, it emerged yesterday. Swiss aviation authorities said Flash Airlines, whose flight FSH604 from the Egyptian resort of Sharm el-Sheikh to Paris plunged into the Red Sea within minutes of takeoff, had been barred from Swiss airspace since October 2002 after a spot check on one of its jets uncovered safety problems. "During a routine inspection at Zurich we discovered the airline was a danger to aviation security," said Celestine Perissinotto, a spokeswoman for the Swiss Federal Office for Civil Aviation. "It concerned violations of the rules of the International Civil Aviation Organisation." She declined to give details, but said the airline had not done enough to allow the ban to be lifted. If a company was banned from national airspace, it meant the problems were "serious", she added. The Egyptian aviation minister, Ahmed Shafiq, denounced the accusations as "baseless" and the chief pilot of Flash Airlines, Hassan Mounir, denied that the ban was due to safety concerns, saying it stemmed from "a financial problem". But he confirmed one of the airline's two Boeing 737s had caught fire while flying from Sharm el-Sheik to Bologna, Italy, during the same month as the Zurich airport inspection. The jet made an emergency landing at Athens airport with flames coming out of the starboard engine. "To say the plane was decrepit would be a compliment," one passenger, Eugenio Gedda, later told Italian television. Mr Mounir insisted, how ever, that the six-year-old company's planes were "very well maintained". Yesterday the grim search resumed for bodies and wreckage from the crash, one of the worst ever involving French passengers. Four aircraft and 40 boats quartered a 10 kilometre square expanse of sea, but rescue workers had so far found only small pieces of debris, mainly personal effects such as toys and papers, and "very few" body parts, officials said. The local governor, Mostafa Afifi, said the 11-year-old plane had hit the sea so hard that everything had smashed into fragments. "We can't say that we have found bodies as bodies," he said. After visiting a hospital morgue in Sharm el-Sheikh, some 300 miles from Cairo, the French foreign secretary, Renaud Muselier, said that the human remains were so badly shattered that only "scientific means" - DNA tests - would be able to identify them. France, declared "a nation in mourning" by the prime minister, Jean-Pierre Raffarin, dispatched a military surveillance plane, a C-130 military transport plane, the Tourville, a naval frigate with advanced sonar capabilities, and 16 divers from Djibouti in the Horn of Africa. It was was also sending the Achille, a robot submarine, to help search for the wreckage and remains, which are lying in up to 1,000 metres of water. Experts said only a robot would be able to recover the aircraft's two vital black box flight recorders, which could help explain what went wrong, since human divers can go down to depths of only around 250 metres. Radar images showed that the pilot tried to turn back shortly after takeoff and was making the turn when the plane went into the sea. Egyptian and French authorities have so far ruled out a terrorist attack and insist the cause was technical, but have given no detailed explanation for the crash. Most of the passengers were on a holiday organised by Fram, France's third largest tour operator. The company said it had 125 people, on the flight. The dead included seven members of the same family. France will fly relatives out to the crash zone in the middle of this week, according to the transport minister, Gilles de Robien. The cardinal of Paris, Jean-Marie Lustiger, celebrated mass at Notre Dame cathedral yesterday in memory of the victims.

title: Decision to let France and Germany off deficit hook was illegal, says EC
bodyText: The European commission threatened to reopen a poisonous dispute over the size of budget deficits yesterday, announcing it was considering taking EU governments to court for being far too soft on budget transgressors France and Germany. Clutching a legal opinion from its lawyers, the commission suggested that member states - including Britain - had acted illegally last November when they voted to reject tough disciplinary action against the eurozone's two largest economies. Under the EU's enfeebled stability and growth pact, member states are not allowed to run a budget deficit above 3% of gross domestic product. But France and Germany have done just that, and are likely to breach the hallowed 3% ceiling for the third year in 2004, hamstrung by a severe economic slowdown. The commission wanted EU finance ministers to order the two to cut their deficits by more than they had budgeted for this year and to get back under the 3% threshold by 2005. But Pedro Solbes, the commissioner for monetary af fairs, was humiliated last November when finance ministers voted to ignore his recommendation and decided to let France and Germany off the hook. Departing from the rules of the stability pact, they accepted a mere political commitment from the two to fall into line by 2005 and steered clear of prescribing tough measures on the grounds that it might imperil a fragile economic recovery. A furious Mr Solbes asked commission lawyers to study the legality of the move, and yesterday he revealed that they had concluded it had "no basis" in the EU treaty and could be challenged. The EC is due to make a final decision on the matter next Tuesday. But Mr Solbes made it clear he favoured dragging EU governments through the court of justice over the issue. "We are a community of law and procedures have to be re spected. It could be useful to clarify whether the procedures that have been applied are correct or not." But Romano Prodi, the EC president, is said to oppose legal action since he considers it politically injudicious - and the commission as a whole is said to be divided on the issue.

title: Live in France, work in Britain and help out Eurotunnel
bodyText: Eurotunnel is planning to run commuter trains from France and its own freight trains to and from mainland Europe to offset the fall in revenues brought on by a savage price war on cross-Channel routes. The Channel tunnel operator, which saw income from passengers fall 11% last year in the face of a 20% drop in prices, is also planning a further shake-up of rail services before the end of its guaranteed income from train operators in late 2006. Eurotunnel, due to confirm another year of losses when it reports last year's results on February 9, said its sales fell 5% from £597m to £566m as passenger insecurity driven by the conflict in Iraq and fears of terrorism took its toll. The operator, which still faces fierce competition from ferry operators offering bargain prices, said it carried 2.3 million cars last year. Its market share remained at 47% despite a 2% drop in overall numbers. With low-cost airlines offering cheaper flights to mainland Europe, the sea-crossing market fell by 5% in the final quarter and could fall further as short-stay shoppers are deterred by government-imposed increases in the price of alcohol and tobacco in France. The company, which increased its share of the declining coach market by four points to 36%, reported a 4% rise in truck traffic to 1.3m trucks or a 43% market share. Roger Burge, chief financial officer, cautioned against expectations of rises in prices and, hence, in company revenue this year. He saw, at most, a 2% rise in traffic. "Even with this it is unlikely to be a great year; it will be better than 2003 and much less volatile." Eurotunnel, whose managers face a vote of no confidence by investors in April, reduced its £6.1bn debt by £134m in the first nine months of 2003 but remains desperate to raise revenues before it starts to repay capital in 2007. One idea is to seize on the one-hour journey time from Calais to London when the Channel tunnel rail link is complete in 2007 to run commuter trains from northern France - filled with Brits escaping overcrowded, expensive London and the home counties. Another is to challenge EWS and French state-owned operator SNCF by using its own freight trains to carry retail goods and automotive components from mainland Europe to Britain.

title: Sanofi using bid to avoid takeover, says Aventis
bodyText: Aventis's board meets today to draw up a battle plan to repulse the €48bn (£30bn) hostile takeover bid by French drugs company Sanofi. "We don't need them," chairman and chief executive Igor Landau told investors in a defiant statement last night. He insisted that Sanofi had only moved to protect itself from becoming a bid target. "They want to buy a life insurance policy almost free of charge - but that's their problem, it is not our problem. We have the critical mass." He hinted that there were other firms Aventis believed would make better partners. Sanofi-Synthélabo made a swoop for Aventis on Monday, offering five of its shares and €69 for every six Aventis shares in a deal which would create the world's third largest drugs company. Sanofi chairman and chief executive Jean-François Dehecq said on Monday there was a "compelling strategic rationale" behind a deal, but Aventis board members reiterated their opposition yesterday. Aventis plans to highlight its pipeline of promising drugs when it announces full-year results next week in order to bolster its argument that Sanofi's offer values it too cheaply. "People are expecting an all-singing, all-dancing performance at the time of the results," said a source close to the firm. It has hinted that the €1.6bn a year savings Sanofi believes it could make might result in as many as 10,000 job losses. Unions fear the figure could be closer to 25,000. There is also concern that Sanofi's blockbuster anti-blood clot drug, Plavix, is facing a patent challenge in the American courts this year. The French government is enthusiastic about the bid, which would create a Paris-based global champion. But German finance minister Wolfgang Clement weighed in on the other side yesterday, warning that German jobs could be lost if the merger went ahead. "The German government has a strong interest in strengthening Aventis's position in Germany rather than weakening it," he was reported as saying. Sanofi plans to base a merged firm in Paris. Aventis was formed through a merger of German firm Hoechst and Rhône-Poulenc of France; it employs 9,000 German workers among its workforce of almost 80,000. With its board determined to reject Sanofi's advances, a tie-up between the two companies could take six months to finalise, and many analysts expect Sanofi to be forced to improve its offer.

title: Aircraft carrier deal will help heal Anglo-French rift
bodyText: Britain and France are set to share the costs of designing and building three aircraft carriers in a further rapprochement since the rows over the war in Iraq, political and industry sources said yesterday. The French government is understood to have rejected domestic pressures to build a nuclear-powered sister ship for the Charles de Gaulle and to have opted for conventional propulsion instead. Its decision, yet to be announced, paves the way for an historic work-share programme for British and French companies such as BAE Systems, Thales and Rolls-Royce on two British and one French carrier. The move, triggered by negotiations between Geoff Hoon, the defence secretary, and his French counterpart, Michèle Alliot-Marie, comes as Britain struggles to contain the costs of its two carriers within the Ministry of Defence's £3bn budget. A senior industry executive said after talks in Paris: "The French navy are happy to be part of the UK programme, and this could help the UK government out of the embarrassing dilemma that we believe the ships cannot be delivered with the required capability for less than £4bn." The British carriers, originally designed to be 290 metres long, were reduced to 245m to save costs but are now back to 285m, similar to the length of the proposed French carrier. All three vessels will weigh about 58,000 tonnes. One initial idea discussed would be to build two-thirds of the UK carriers in British yards, with the remainder going to French yards such as DCN's St-Nazaire, which built the Queen Mary 2 cruise ship. Denis Ranque, the chairman of Thales, has estimated that sharing the programme could cut the overall costs of the three carriers to €6bn (£4bn) - closer to the MoD budget. Whitehall insiders said Mr Hoon was prepared to reach agreement with Mrs Alliot-Marie. A French political source said: "It would be of great advantage to both countries and to the EU as a whole to share the two projects. It would bring economies of scale, and all three ships would be available to the EU's joint reaction force." But Whitehall officials said any deal must not jeopardise the planned entry into service of the British carriers in 2012 and 2015. The MoD has yet to overcome resistance among naval chiefs.

title: French losses addle Egg
bodyText: Egg, Britain's largest internet bank, which was put up for sale by its majority shareholder last month, yesterday forecast another year of strong growth as it announced a doubling of profits at its core UK operations. Despite recent warnings from senior officials at the Bank of England about rising levels of consumer debt, Paul Gratton, Egg's chief executive, said he was confident the booming market for credit cards and personal loans would continue. "Nobody is forecasting the sort of recessions we saw in the 1980s. Unemployment remains low and base rates are unlikely to go above 5%." Consumers were becoming increasingly intolerant of traditional banks and their opaque product offerings. In Britain, Egg saw operating profits double to £73m from £35m in the 12 months to end-December 2003 as it signed up 635,000 new customers and sales of personal loans started to take off, with the company claiming 12% of the market. Losses at its troublesome French operations ballooned from £46.7m to £89.1m during 2003, pushing the group-wide loss to £34.4m from £16.6m last time. The loss per share rose from 2.3p to 4p. Egg has struggled in France since launching La Carte Egg in 2002. Last October the company announced it would close down the operation if it could not find a partner to share the costs. That plan was put on ice when life assurer Prudential, Egg's majority shareholder, announced last month that it was in talks to sell its 79% holding. Placing a value on the French operation, which has 130,000 customers, is seen by analysts as crucial to the outcome of the auction. Two potential buyers, one of which was reported to be Royal Bank of Scotland, are thought to have withdrawn from the sale process. Mr Gratton, who is assisting the Prudential with the sale talks, said there was a place for a consumer friendly financial services business in France. "We were just not big enough to do it," he said, adding that Egg would concentrate on keeping the French business stable while the company awaited a buyer. Egg shares closed up 3p at 170p with investors impressed by the performance of the UK business.

title: French parent given EDF boost
bodyText: Yesterday the group, which includes the former London Electricity, Seeboard and Eastern, reported pre-tax earnings up 69% in 2003 to £640m as its parent said its operating profits had jumped from €5.2bn to €6.8bn. EDF Energy increased its contribution to its parent's earnings by 160% to €296m (£210m), making it one of the strongest-performing units of the group which is due for a partial float in 2005. Humphrey Cadoux-Hudson, chief financial officer, said that, after stripping out the effects of property sales and the 2002 acquisitions of Seeboard and Eastern, underlying earnings had increased 44% to £182m last year. EDF Energy, the first utility to join the Nectar loyalty card scheme, is raising its electricity prices by 6.9% and gas prices by 4.5% this spring after significant increases last year. But Vincent de Rivaz, chief executive, said that, unlike competitors raising their own prices, EDF had agreed to delay increases for its poorest customers, on pre-payment meters. These normally pay more and would now be paying the same as others among EDF's 5m retail customers. The group has won plaudits for setting up a £2m trust to help non-payers in debt. Mr Cadoux-Hudson said synergy savings from recent acquisitions would exceed £130m by 2006. The French parent, forced by the EU to pay €1.2bn in back taxes to the French exchequer, said it planned to raise gross profit margins to 26%.

title: Eurotunnel hopes to appease rebels with Club Med boss
bodyText: Eurotunnel is to appoint former Club Med boss Philippe Bourguignon as its new chairman, in an attempt to appease maverick French investors who are threatening to overthrow the entire board. Insiders say the Channel tunnel operator will announce this week that its chairman of seven years, Charles Mackay, will stand down at next month's annual meeting. The switch will provide more French influence on the bi-national board - a recognition of the fact that French retail investors hold more than 60% of the shares. Mr Bourguignon, 56, is chief executive of the World Economic Forum. In the early 1990s, he masterminded a turnaround at Euro Disney before joining Club Med in 1997. He had been expected to join Eurotunnel's board as a replacement for Michel Combes, chief financial officer of France Telecom. Rebel Eurotunnel shareholders have been complaining that the company has adopted an insufficiently aggressive approach to lowering its £6.4bn debt. They have tabled a motion of no confidence, proposing a new board which includes a rightwing French MP, Pierre Cardo, and the founder of travel agency Nouvelles Frontieres, Jacques Maillot. Eurotunnel's chief executive, Richard Shirrefs, has urged shareholders to vote. He admits that he is concerned about the possibility of defeat at the meeting, which will be held in Paris on April 7. Company insiders say the board is concerned about a lack of supporting institutions. Just 5% of the stock is in the hands of British retail investors, while some 15% is held by tracker funds and hedge funds, which rarely vote. The rebels have proposed seeking government aid to tackle the debt. Eurotunnel says this is unrealistic. It has proposed Project Galaxie, an alternative plan which would see the firm taking partial control of the Channel tunnel rail link.

title: Novartis tells Paris to back off
bodyText: Swiss drugs company Novartis yesterday issued a rebuke to the French government for playing politics with the pharmaceuticals industry, warning that it would make a "white knight" rescue bid for Aventis only if Paris stopped interfering. French prime minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin infuriated Novartis last week when he invoked France's "national interest" in the context of the deal, and suggested Aventis's vaccines business was important in protecting the country against a bioterrorism attack. Since Aventis first received a €47bn (£31bn) hostile bid from its rival Sanofi in January, Paris has made no secret of its enthusiasm for the marriage - which it hopes would create a Paris-based global drugs champion and keep highly paid research jobs in the country. Aventis's board has repeatedly rejected the deal, saying it would prefer to merge with a larger partner. It is thought to have been sympathetic to the overtures of Novartis's ambitious chief executive, Daniel Vasella, in recent weeks. Yesterday's statement from Novartis, released after pressure from the French market regulators to clarify its intentions, said it wanted reassurance from both the government and Aventis before making a formal proposal. "Although the business case looks viable, the negative attitude of the French government has influenced Novartis' considerations to a point that it will only enter into a negotiation phase if formally invited by the Aventis supervisory board and if the French government assumed a neutral position," it said. Some analysts believe Mr Vasella was hitting out at the French government to give himself an escape clause if he cannot persuade Novartis's board to rubber-stamp a formal merger proposal. But Aventis's overseas investors have been frustrated by the French government's insistence on involving itself in the bid process. Thomas Shrager, of US fund manager Tweedy Browne, ridiculed Mr Raffarin's approach as "misplaced national pride", and joked that with Basel right next door to France, "the military option is always available to them if they think Novartis is threatening their national interest". In yesterday's statement, Novartis said a "working hypothesis" was that it would spin off some of Aventis's drugs, together with some of its own, into a separate company, in a move parallel to the "carve-out" of its less-profitable products which Aventis has already put in motion. It said the new firm would "preserve jobs, specifically in France and Germany" - but would concentrate on developing and making drugs, rather than the prestigious cutting edge science the government would like to keep in France. A spokesman for Novartis last night denied that a merger with Aventis would mean giving up the firm's long-cherished ambition to swallow its Swiss neighbour, Roche, in which it has more than a 30% stake. "Our strategy has not changed," he said. Its share price closed up almost 2% as investors judged that yesterday's developments meant a potentially costly deal was less likely. Sources close to Sanofi denied it was facing overwhelming pressure to increase its offer, pointing out that analysts have been increasing their sales projections for its most promising product, the anti-obesity, anti-smoking drug rimonabant, after new evidence from clinical trials.

title: EADS pressed to sever French link
bodyText: EADS, the European aerospace and defence group, is under growing pressure from Wall Street investors to dilute or even scrap stakes held by the French government and its other two main shareholders. It is understood that both DaimlerChrysler, the German-US cars group, and Lagardère, the French media group, have been urged by US investors keen to buy into the majority-owner of Airbus to press Paris for a synchronised sell-down. Manfred Bischoff, EADS co-chairman and senior DC executive, warned President Jacques Chirac against turning the group into a purely French player. "In the long run, national interests would best be served if we keep EADS multinational," he told Defense News. Analysts said yesterday EADS "can kiss goodbye to ever having a substantial US business" if it handed over control to the French state which shares equally a 30.13% stake with Lagardère. DC holds a 33% share. The issue has come to a head because Arnaud Lagardère, son and successor to Jean-Luc at the eponymous group, has indicated his desire eventually to sell the EADS stake and focus on the media business. Analysts said both the French group and DC would eventually like to realise the value of their EADS holdings as cash for ei ther acquisition or, in Daimler's case, consolidation of a balance sheet put under pressure by Chrysler's losses. Mr Lagardère has said he will stick to his father's commitment to the EADS stake until the Airbus superjumbo, the 555-seater A380, has entered service in 2006 - and proven its commercial viability. The French state, which is considering a partial privatisation of the power groups Gaz de France and Electricité de France next year, has long held the view that aerospace and defence businesses are "strategic" interests. But Mr Bischoff told Defense News: "Often, the word 'strategic' can be replaced with 'not making money'." Analysts said it would require a substantial cultural change for Paris to allow a more distributed share ownership base. Mr Bischoff said: "They [French officials] are concerned about ownership and fear that the company will be managed only on quarterly results." With the European defence market worth $180bn (£100bn) compared with the $400bn-plus American market, EADS is keen to reach its target of 10% profit margins by taking on more US military business. Last year, with the A380's $1bn annual development costs eating into earnings, the group made €1.54bn (£1.03bn) pre-tax profits and says it will make €1.8bn this year.

title: Eurotunnel's entente discordante
bodyText: A new board, new directors and a new beginning for Eurotunnel. At least, that it is the way the rebels who ousted the company's management last week would like to see it. But the company is still in a financial pickle, and the new executives will have to work fast to come up with solutions and convince Eurotunnel's creditors, who are owed £6 billion, that they are people with whom they can do business. The price of failure for the incoming regime will be high. The company's finances are in such a parlous state that Eurotunnel could be seized by the banks as early as this autumn, perhaps sooner. If that happens, new chairman Jacques Maillot, founder of French tour operator Nouvelles Frontières, and Jean-Louis Raymond, a multi-millionaire businessman, risk losing everything for stockholders. Investors will get nothing if the creditors take control. And let's face it, things are looking bad for Eurotunnel. In 2006, the company will be hit by a double whammy. First, arrangements that guarantee the company a minimum level of income from passenger and freight trains will expire. That means income of over £200 million could be more than halved as normal commercial rates kick in. Second, Eurotunnel is faced with mandatory interest repayments for the first time - bad news as the company has recently not been making enough profit to service its massive debt. So when Maillot and his friends go and see the banks with their new business plan this October, it had better be good, because otherwise the banks will be able to resort to something called substitution, which enables them to run the company for cash, delist Eurotunnel's shares in London and Paris, and be free of any obligation to ever pay out dividends to investors. Eurotunnel, of course, has lurched from one crisis to another ever since work began on the tunnel in 1987. Consul tants told Margaret Thatcher, Prime Minister at the time, that the project would cost about £5bn. It ended up costing £12bn, and opened one year later than planned. But that wasn't the end of it. Almost all the other forecasts contained in the original business plan have turned out wrong. It was thought 15 million, even 20 million passengers would pass through the tunnel each year, but the number is nearer 6 million. It was estimated that freight tonnage would exceed 5 million tonnes, but it is barely a quarter of that. The result has been that Eurotunnel, which started life as a noble dream - to link Britain with the Continent for the first time since the end of the Ice Age - has been a financial nightmare. There have been countless restructurings, new share issues and interest payment holidays to try and put Eurotunnel on a sound financial footing, but to no avail. Christian Wolmar, a transport analyst, said: 'The tragedy is that Eurotunnel would be a perfectly viable company if it wasn't saddled with so much debt. But Mrs Thatcher and Francois Mitterand (the late French president) insisted that it be built without public subsidy. That's a tall order for such a project - in some ways, I am surprised that the banks have been so gullible and put up so much cash.' Without the debt, the tunnel could be making good money. Sir Alastair Morton, former Eurotunnel boss from 1989-96, said: 'It's a well-run company, cash-generative, and is running at 99 per cent reliability and punctuality. And the day-to-day running costs are not inordinately high.' Management at Eurotunnel, under former chief executive Richard Shirrefs, who was removed after a rebellion of French shareholders last week, had a plan that the banks liked the look of. It was designed to exploit the operational strengths that Morton always loves to highlight. Shirrefs planned to cut charges for users of the tunnel, especially for heavy freight operators, offering them discounts of up to 50 per cent. His other idea was to open new rail freight services - for example from Switzerland and Cologne to England - as part of a drive to lift volumes and income. Shirrefs' plan, codenamed Project Galaxy, looked like a good way to beat off the competition, which has been making Eurotunnel's life ever more difficult. Potential pas sengers have shunned the tunnel in favour of low-cost airlines (something not foreseen in the 1980s) while the ferries have proved formidable rivals for freight traffic. But by last summer, Shireffs and his chairman Charles Mackay began picking up intelligence of a campaign that was stirring in France, led by Nicolas Miguet, a former presidential candidate with far-right connections. At first Shirrefs dismissed the challenge, but then Miguet, who is a share tipsheet journalist, began his offensive. He galvanised French shareholders to buy the stock and asked them to consider the possibility of joining together to remove Shireffs. What the company needed, said Miguet, wasn't price cuts but price rises to lift profitability, and he dismissed the idea of extending Eurotunnel's freight operations as too much of a 'jam tomorrow' strategy. Most importantly, he wanted the French and British governments to write off or renegotiate the debt. Only by following his strategy, he said, would shareholders have any hope of seeing a decent return on their investment. As the campaign reached its height, there was a frenzy of speculative buying as the number of small French shareholders grew from 700,000 to more than 1 million. Miguet's army was beginning to take shape. Mackay and Shireffs were horrified; Miguet was rejecting Project Galaxy, which management and lenders had agreed together over months of tortuous negotiations. The creditors include the usual heavyweights: Credit Agricole, HSBC, US financial services giant MBIA, Citigroup, the European Investment Bank, and Soros Asset Management, run by the financier George Soros. No matter, it was Miguet who won the day, carrying 63 per cent of the vote at a noisy meeting in Paris last Wednesday. The lenders are worried: particularly about the individuals behind the campaign. Miguet has convictions for fraud and defamation, and cannot hold a company directorship as a result.
