Legal experts say there is little risk of a repeat of the judicial scandal of last year's paedophile court case in Outreau, in northern France, when a child sex ring trial collapsed after the admission by a witness that most of her accusations were untrue. In that case, which saw six of the 13 accused freed and the remainder given leave to appeal, a naive and inexperienced investigating magistrate is said to have given excessive credence to often confused and contradictory evidence from child victims. In this case most of the evidence is from adults.

title: France sets date for EU constitution vote
bodyText: The French president, Jacques Chirac, today called a referendum on the European Union constitution on one of the earliest dates available for the vote, amid concern that French support for ratification is falling. The referendum will take place on May 29, a statement from the president's office said. But Mr Chirac - one of the strongest supporters of the new constitution among EU leaders - risks a backlash from voters unimpressed by the government's cost-cutting reforms. It was originally thought Mr Chirac favoured a poll later in the summer, but decided on a May date to try to pre-empt any further fall in support for the constitution, which is designed to make the EU run more smoothly following its enlargement from 15 to 25 members last May. "The promoters of a short campaign argue that it is better to have a short campaign to prevent a crystallisation of dissatisfaction over domestic issues," Stephane Rozes, of the CSA polling group, explained. The French have traditionally been strong backers of the EU, but often express concern that the EU is not evolving as they would wish and that French influence is declining as the union grows. France will be only the second member state to hold a referendum on the treaty after Spain approved it in a vote last month. The Dutch will vote just three days after the French, and plebiscites will follow in around half of all member states. Others will ratify the constitution - which needs to be approved by all states to take effect - through their parliaments. Maastricht - the last treaty to require French ratification by referendum - was only just approved in 1992, partly due to discontent with the then government. "In France all referendum campaigns are a risk," a senior EU diplomat told Reuters. "It is always a difficult exercise, whatever the circumstances." Mr Chirac and the government of prime minister Jean-Pierre Raffarin can count the support of the opposition Socialist party, and opinion polls show that if a referendum were held today, around 60% of voters would back the constitution - well ahead of the simple majority required. But support has fallen markedly in the past few months, and the low turnout in the Spanish vote provided little comfort for the Yes camp. The drop in support has coincided with strikes and street protests against the government's policies on pay, cost-cutting and other reforms, and over its failure to reduce unemployment, which is at a five-year high. "The debate on the future of Europe is nobody's property. It goes beyond political splits, without removing them. It concerns, above all, future generations," Mr Raffarin wrote in Le Monde newspaper, urging voters not to throw the future of the EU into crisis by rejecting the constitution. However, left-wing critics argue that an alternative constitution to the one agreed last year by EU leaders should be negotiated and reject the "neo-liberal" economy they say is being imposed on member states. They argue that the proposed constitution does not enshrine equal rights or protect jobs, but attacks public services. Among them is the Communist party leader, Marie-George Buffet, who called on the French people "to reject this constitution which sets ultra-liberalism in stone for decades".

title: Chirac calls snap vote on EU constitution
bodyText: Jacques Chirac yesterday called a snap referendum on the European Union constitution amid increasing concern that France's voters will turn against the proposal and effectively kill the treaty. Setting May 29 as polling day - the earliest feasible date - the president's Elysée palace denied claims that his hand had been forced. But opinion polls have shown support for the treaty dwindling. A rejection in France, traditionally in the forefront of European integration, could deal a terminal blow to the constitution. As the only large founding member state to be holding a referendum, whose former president Valerie Giscard d'Estaing drew up the constitution, France wields an effective veto. The leaders of the ruling centre-right UMP party and opposition Socialists, whom Mr Chirac consulted this week, urged him to allow plenty of time for the Yes camp to convince France's voters. But the president wants to avoid a repeat of France's referendum on the Maastricht treaty in 1992, when his predecessor Francois Mitterrand saw a handsome lead in the polls gradually dwindle to almost nothing. France approved the treaty by just a couple of hundred thousand votes. A series of bank holidays, including Easter, meant that May 29 was the earliest Sunday that Mr Chirac could stage the poll. Late last year, some 65% of the French said they were in favour of the constitution. That figure has dwindled to less than 60%, with a corresponding rise in support for a No vote from 30% to more than 40%. A poll last month showed half of France's voters are now undecided on whether to vote Yes, and only 25% say they are certain to back the treaty. The No camp - made up of the far right, the far left, Eurosceptic sovereignists, Republicans, and a large minority of the Socialist party - has been airing its objections for months . Support has been fuelled by a growing belief that the consti tution embodies a Thatcherite view of the union, theatening France's emphasis on worker protection, public services and welfare guarantees. Aurore Wanlin, of the Centre for European Reform, said: "A French No vote would be a very big symbol of how much the EU has split away from its people. At this point European leaders would have to write another treaty or rethink the EU."

title: William Keegan: What if France says 'non' to Brussels?
bodyText: In February, there were faint signs that the previously confident position of President Chirac's government on the forthcoming referendum on the European constitution was starting to weaken. The balance of those saying they would vote 'yes' on May 29 was still strongly positive, at around 60 per cent, but it was beginning to fall. 'In the circumstances,' said one overseas observer, 'the French are right to be going ahead with the referendum soon, because who knows what lies ahead?' One significant point in Chirac's favour was that the opposition socialists, whatever political harm they might wish to inflict on him for other reasons, have done what was generally regarded as the decent thing and come out in favour of a 'yes' vote. For TS Eliot, April was 'the cruellest month'. And the big item of news in France last week (apart from the death of the Pope) was the dramatic rise in the 'no' vote in recent opinion polls. The number of respondents saying they would vote 'non' suddenly ranged from 52 to 55 per cent, depending on which poll you read. For some nervous supporters of the European Constitution, an erosion in prospective voting strength was threatening to become a rout. Hostility from the Front National, among other extreme groups, and from the 'anti-globalisation' movement, was to be expected. But the socialist consensus of February now appears to have been paper-thin. And opposition seems to be spreading among the general public. In addition to worries about the constitution, the desire of the French public to use the occasion of the referendum as a protest vote against Chirac and his government is manifest. According to the pollsters, the state of the economy - sluggish growth and high unemployment - emerges as the second factor, after the constitution itself, influencing the burgeoning strength of the 'no' vote. There are a number of paradoxes here - not least that while the French worry about the constitution, other countries are queuing up to join the European Union. And while the British 'no' campaigners are obsessed with the danger of ceding sovereignty to the continent, one of the principal sources of concern in France is that the economic aspects of the proposed constitution are too 'Anglo-Saxon'. In which context, a remark of President Chirac's at the last EU summit - 'ultra-liberalism is the communism of our age' - has struck a chord, even though, according to some reports, it was made jokingly. Desperate to prop up the potential 'yes' vote - almost 40 per cent of the French electorate is still said to be undecided - Chirac is resorting to traditional Gaullist wrecking tactics during routine EU business. Not only has he thrown a spanner in the works of long-standing proposals to liberalise the EU market in services, he has also taken a strongly hostile position on the infamous 'British rebate' from Brussels - a rebate originally agreed because the rules for financing the EU budget discriminated against the UK. This stand may or may not play well in the French referendum campaign; no member of the French public has ever raised the British rebate in conversations with your correspondent. But senior members of the French ruling elite privately acknowledge that the issue would be such a hot potato during a British referendum campaign that the French might in the end have to be 'understanding' about the rebate to assist a faltering British 'yes' campaign. Of course, if the proposed European constitution is rejected by the French, the British referendum may never come to pass - or to fail. This eventuality cannot now be ruled out. Although there is evidently a lot to play for among the don't-knows, after a fortnight travelling around France I am struck by the depth of concern. The optimistic view is that, in the end, the French did vote for the Maastricht Treaty (although only by decimal points above 50 per cent) and, in the words of one Parisian, 'none of the French want the rest of Europe to think they are capricious'. (After all, the proposed constitution was drawn up, albeit with interesting interventions from the British, by France's former president Valéry Giscard d'Estaing.) Yet it was quite a surprise to come across a copy of the constitution at the till counter in a cafe in the French Alps, alongside a copy of a 'no' pamphlet entitled 'When the European Union kills Europe'. No comparable 'yes' pamphlet was on display, but French schools are reportedly being issued only with 'yes' pamphlets. Be that as it may, three of the top five best-selling works in the non-fiction category in French bookshops are versions of or guides to the EU constitution, according to research group Ipsos. Now, your correspondent did not get where he is today by attempting to predict the results of French referendums, but it strikes me that Chirac is going to have his work cut out to achieve a 'yes' vote. And if the French, of all people, reject the constitution, everyone concerned will have to go back to the drawing board. Britain would be off one European hook (but, knowing our history with the rest of the EU, no doubt we will be impaled on another one sooner or later). The economic background to all this is very important, and, as noted, is most certainly contributing to the strength of the French 'no' campaign. While commentators go on and on about the need for 'structural reform' and further 'liberalisation' to galvanise the European economy, the elephant in the room remains the excessively deflationary policy adopted in the eurozone in the run-up to and after the birth of the single currency in 1999. The long-predicted improvement in the economic outlook for the core of the eurozone - France and Germany - keeps being postponed. And even as the European Commission and European Central Bank revise their forecasts for the economic outlook downwards, smoke signals emerge from Frankfurt suggesting that the ECB has its sights set on raising interest rates later in the year. The Stability and Growth Pact has broken down. This is lamented by some, not least the ECB, but it was inevitable at some stage, and forecast by many. Whereas the oil crisis of the mid-Seventies followed a period of very rapid economic growth, the concerns about a persistently high price of oil now follow a period when growth in the eurozone has been anaemic, to say the least. A further slowdown now could be serious - not least for Chirac's efforts on the referendum. Differences between US and eurozone rates of growth are smaller on a per capita basis because of the big impact of immigration on the size of the US workforce; also, expansionary policies in the US have been overindulgent, and a price will have to be paid at some stage. But this makes it all the more important that eurozone policymakers, even though the underlying long-term growth rate is not much more than 2 per cent, should do something about what economists call the 'slack' in the economy: in other words, pulling out the stops and making a concerted effort to boost demand. Such an approach would seem economically and socially necessary anyway, irrespective of the imminence of the referendum. Perhaps on that front, the best economic hope Chirac has is that next month is a seasonally good time for employment, and that things may look - temporarily - better. May is not usually the cruellest month.

title: William Keegan: Maybe the French will say 'oui' - just
bodyText: At the beginning of April, I reported in this column how opinion in France had shifted within a couple of months from being in favour of the proposed European Constitutional Treaty to being against. France of all countries, the historical driving force behind the European Union! In recent weeks the mood has altered several times but, with only a week to go until next Sunday's referendum, the Chirac government still faces powerful opposition, and there is much speculation about the consequences of a 'no' vote. This column did not get where it is today by predicting the results of French referendums; but it would be a quintessentially Gallic result if the 'yes' campaign managed to scrape a decimal point or so above 50 per cent - and very reminiscent of the French referendum on Maastricht. In my opinion, the economic troubles now worrying the French electorate were fomented by the restrictive economic policies adopted in the run-up to the Maastricht Treaty and the policy regime since then. For though referendums are supposed to be about a single issue (and historically they are seen as such) they are the occasion for all manner of protests about sources of discontent with governments and individual leaders, and the performance of the French economy, not least its high unemployment rate, has featured prominently in recent debates. Last week, even as Gordon Brown admitted to the Confederation of British Industry that his confident forecasts of economic growth would be affected by downward revisions to forecasts of growth in the eurozone, Le Figaro was proclaiming: 'The British miracle should serve us as an example, provided we have the courage to cross the Channel and seek answers ... to the anaemia of our economy.' Yes, the grass is always greener on the other side of the Channel. I wish I had Le Figaro's confidence in the durability of the British economic miracle: there have been disturbing echoes recently of the 'miracle' that Lord Lawson thought he had worked here in the late 1980s. Then as now we heard a lot about the flexibility of the British labour market after all those Thatcherite reforms that essentially amounted to strengthening the hand of employers against the employed. But that flexibility was not much consolation when disastrous macroeconomic policies brought us recession in the early 1990s. Of course, pace Figaro, you do not need courage to cross the Channel or take a train beneath it. London is teeming with French waiters escaping the high unemployment in France. Indeed, they add a touch of class to the rather dubious standards we take for granted in our so-called 'service economy'. But to return to the French economy. The great hope of the French during the work on the Delors report of the 1980s and the Maastricht Treaty was that the outcome would be an expansionary European economic policy, providing some insulation (as far as was possible) from the vagaries of a world economy dominated by the United States. What the French and the rest of the eurozone ended up with was subjection to a European Central Bank that seems to think Weimar inflation lurks just around the corner, plus fiscal rules that did not take account of the lessons of economic history and economic cycles - rules which have recently had to be stretched, inevitably, but whose framework was, and is, fundamentally inimical to the kind of 'dash for growth' that is required when unemployment is chronically high. With its 'opt out' from the Maastricht Treaty, Britain escaped this straitjacket, an escape which assisted the impressive economic performance recorded under the chancellorships of both Kenneth Clarke (why don't the Tories realise he is their best man?) and Brown. The British government did remarkably well in securing its opt-out from the eurozone while preserving membership of the EU. But Whitehall was determined that it should not again fall into the trap that ensnared Margaret Thatcher; she had signed up for the single market, without catching on to what it entailed (the single currency). Thus the British proceeded to play an active part in the negotiations for the Constitutional Treaty as is well brought out in the revised edition of Peter Norman's book The Accidental Constitution (Euro Comment, Brussels), which provides what The Economist has called 'the definitive account' of the making of the treaty on which France votes next Sunday. Le Monde commented: 'Whatever people say, this text remains a British victory', and a Belgian newspaper termed it 'a text in the service of Her Majesty'. The view that the treaty is too supportive of the 'Anglo-Saxon' approach to economic policy is one of the factors cited by Laurent Fabius, former French Socialist Prime Minister,in support of his campaign against the treaty (although the party itself officially voted in favour). But the treaty is such a hard-honed compromise that Chirac can himself promote it as a French bulwark against the British. The essential point about the treaty is that with the blessing of both Blair and Chirac it is aimed at enabling the enlarged EU to function more efficiently. The federalist scare is for the birds. The former French president Valéry Giscard d'Estaing, who was in charge of the convention that produced the treaty, was once of the Monnet school of 'ever closer union'. But Giscard's view became closer to the British concept. As Norman says: 'The union would instead develop as a hybrid, as a "union of states with federal competences".' (Trade negotiations for instance are already conducted at the federal level.) Norman, who has spoken to everyone who matters, including, it sometimes seems, celestial powers, is of the view that 'a rejection by France in its referendum would be a crisis for the union as a whole and could kill off the constitutional treaty'. He adds: 'A rejection by the UK in 2006, at the end of the ratification process and following approval by all or nearly all other member states, would be a crisis - but a crisis for Britain, and for its relations with the rest of Europe.' One of the treaty's proposals strongly pushed by Tony Blair was that the presidency of the European Council of Ministers should be in the same hands for two-and-a-half-years, and not rotate every six months. Whatever the outcome of the French referendum, and the Dutch one that is due to follow hot on its heels, Blair will be in charge of the still 'rotating' presidency from July to December. He will either be sorting out a mess caused by a French 'no' or he will himself be preparing for a British referendum. Whatever the French end up doing, those who admire the British economic model may have their eyes opened as the UK's housing and credit bubbles deflate, and the public sector ceases to create employment at such a breathless pace. It may well be a return to 'economic problems as usual'. Meanwhile, if the French say 'yes' and Britain does indeed go ahead with a referendum, then our Prime Minister, who is so obsessed with 'respect', must face the problem that there are few people left in this country who respect his judgment. Ironically, if Blair wants to go down in history as the man under whose premiership Britain's position in Europe was secured, he himself will have to take a back seat in the 'yes' campaign.

title: French want British 'miracle' as a model for change
bodyText: France's poor economic performance, outlined in yesterday's OECD report, is one of the major issues dominating the run-up to Sunday's referendum on the European constitution. Unemployment is 10.2%, more than double Britain's 4.7%. Almost a quarter of young French people, including graduates, are out of work. Britain has a record 75% of its adult population in jobs; France has 62%. "That is a tremendous waste of human capital. The French model is under threat because not enough jobs are being created. Britain is exploiting its full potential but France is not," says Raymond Torres, a labour market specialist at the OECD. He is not the only one to come to this conclusion. The daily newspaper Figaro recently said: "The British 'miracle' should be a model for us." France's high unemployment is not a result of economic stagnation - since 1998, the French economy has expanded by 13%, not much less than Britain's 17%. But France has grown more slowly than Britain in the past two or three years and the OECD forecasts its growth will remain weak for the next couple of years. Nor is it a result of other economic problems. Productivity, or output per worker, is higher in France than in Britain, as French firms have tended to employ machines rather than people. France's generous unemployment benefits, and the high costs that firms face if they hire anyone, have reduced the incentives both for the unemployed to work and for firms to hire people. Rod Mitchell, a Briton who has lived in France for 20 years and set up a mortgage broking business five years ago, says France is in decline. "There's not a work culture any more. People have become too dependent on the state. There would be a leisure culture but people don't even have the money to enjoy their leisure. This country used to be wealthy but will soon be bankrupt." Figures prove his point. Average earnings in France over the past decade have only just kept up with inflation, meaning the French have not got any wealthier. In Britain, earnings growth has comfortably outstripped inflation. Britain is getting richer but France is not; Britain is affording more spending on public services while France is cutting back. The British are buying homes in France. "France is starting to look like Britain in the 1970s. The two countries are moving in opposite directions. The French are petrified of the changes they need to make and are burying their heads in the sand," says Mr Mitchell. "The English success, built over 25 years, rests largely on the flexibility of the labour market - flexibility which is not incompatible, quite to the contrary, with high levels of social protection," says Arnaud Vaissié, the London-based chief executive of International SOS, who has formed a thinktank of French company bosses living in Britain. Flexibility, however, remains a dirty word in France, where labour market rigidities - high social insurance costs, the 35-hour week, the difficulty of making people redundant - abound. And the cost to France of its generous welfare system is enormous. The fundamental change France needs, say Mr Torres and Professor Richard Layard of the London School of Economics, is to reform its unemployment system radically so that it encourages people back into the job market with a carrot-and-stick approach. "The longer you pay people for doing nothing, the longer they will do just that," says Prof Layard.

title: Why 'non' may help the eurozone
bodyText: Financial markets across Europe and the world could be in for some turmoil today as they digest the potential impact of the French referendum on the European Union constitution. The currency markets closed on Friday with traders all but assuming that there would be a twin "no" vote from the French yesterday and the Dutch on Wednesday, dealing a further blow to an already sinking euro. The euro has fallen by about 7% against the dollar since the middle of March and is at about $1.257, although that has mainly been due to a re newed strengthening by the dollar more than to a weakening of the euro. But although London markets, where the bulk of European foreign exchange dealing is done, are closed today for the bank holiday, the euro could be sold off in New York later in the day or in other markets earlier on. Simon Hayley, an analyst with the consultancy Capital Economics, said: "A French 'no' vote is likely to see some knee-jerk selling in many markets. In particular, we would expect some rebound in sterling against the euro, since this would be assumed to save the UK government from having to hold its own referendum next year." Markets are unlikely to be perturbed in the longer run by "no" votes as they would essentially mean that the situation in the EU will continue much as it did before. And any falls in the euro in the short term are likely to be helpful to eurozone exporters who have been hit hard by the euro's appreciation against the falling dollar over the past couple of years. Bond markets could see some reaction today. Buying of eurozone debt last week pushed yields on 10-year Bunds to a record low of 3.27% as markets felt that the imminent rejection of the constitution by France could delay economic reforms in the bloc and keep growth sluggish. Dealers also expected the Turkish lira to be sold off in the wake of a French "no" vote. The issue of Turkey's accession to the European Union had been a factor in the run-up to the referendum and a "no" could indicate that Turkey's accession - which in any case would not happen for at least a decade - could be delayed even more.

title: Peter Preston: When word of mouth was mightier than the editor
bodyText: It was, you might have assumed, the power and glory of the British press what lost it for the French and the Dutch 'elites'. Two European referendums blowing mindless bureaucracies to smithereens so we could rejoice and pay off sundry old scores. 'Vive la France! Vive la libération!' cried the Mail Now Jacques Chirac was Le Worm, according to the Sun, a 'strutting charlatan' and 'busted flush' who might soon turn 'vain and vengeful' in a rich lather of adjectival incontinence. Now France's 'commies, fascists, racists, eco-nutters, anachronistic unionists, feather-bedded farmers, middle-aged students, Trot professors and Welfare queens' had proved Mark Steyn (and other Telegraph ranters against Eurosclerosis) right all along. Was this total victory? The Telegraph thought so. The French 'have removed the necessity for a British referendum'. The Mail thought not. 'Forget the French ... it is now more vital than ever that we British have OUR say'. First the Sun wanted 'to nail the coffin' shut, then it seemed to lose interest. Who, after all, could advocate an expensive UK referendum when there wasn't a treaty to left to vote on? By the time the Dutch had done their stake-through-the-heart bit, the Bun had moved on to chronicle 'Lusty Lesley's' year of sexual abstinence and other big-breasted brotherly issues But then, consistency didn't matter overmuch with victory to celebrate: victory over the 'unaccountable power junkies' of Brussels and Guardian/ FT/Indie-reading 'eurofetishists' closer to home. Wonderfully satisfactory! Everybody could plug the number they first thought of and claim total triumph. The Barclay Brothers, Viscount Rothermere and Rupert Murdoch were top peasants leading this revolt. But did any of the rejoicers (or, indeed, the lamenters on the other side of Fleet Street) notice something odd here? If the press is the true voice of democracy, bringing discredited elites to heel, why did nobody take any notice of it in Paris or Amsterdam? Pause, as we say, for more reflection. France's state broadcasting channels dutifully played all Chirac's best tunes. Nobody thought them even-handed. Not one major national paper - from Le Monde to Figaro to Libération - backed the 'no' campaign. The best they could do was print a few pages of countervailing arguments and, where necessary, editorials urging readers to make their own minds up. Le cop-out. And though Dutch TV worked rather harder at fairness and balance and interminable studio discussions, it still gave the Prime Minister his full quota of appearances on referendum eve. Just like France, the major parties of governance all wanted a 'yes' and all used their platforms in that cause. The no camp followers had to get a word in edgeways where they could. Every major Dutch newspaper, meanwhile, lined up to say 'yes'. Maybe no one quite campaigned in the English style via a barrage of awful threats and big headlines; the debate was more decorous, the space for opposing views scrupulously cleared. Nevertheless, the power of the Dutch press, just like that of the French press, was thrown overwhelmingly behind ratification. But editors everywhere got two fingers and a thick ear for their pains. What can we deduce from this shocking snub for media might? Nothing simple, of course. The European Union is a monster mash of countries doing different things for different reasons where mixed motives and hypocrisy rule OK! (See how the 'Polish plumbers' the Express and Mail didn't want within 1,000 miles of Dover last year have become symbols of brilliant British business acumen and French astigmatism this year.) You can, just about, construct a French case based on the relative feebleness and reach of the Parisian 'national' press. The capital and Lyons and Bordeaux all voted 'yes', for instance. It was the far south and the blue-collar boondocks where such papers barely reach that destroyed Chirac. But the Netherlands is a far different case: smaller, more homogeneous, with one of Europe's closest replications of a British-style national press - and that, too, was brushed aside. Big, influential papers such as Die Volkskrant voted for the treaty, shrugged, and openly acknowledged that nobody, for once, was paying attention. Dutch voters, gloomy about immigration and the euro and Brussels, did their own thing regardless, echoing the full Mail or Express agenda. They didn't, in short, need newspapers to egg them on. Word of mouth, coffee shop chat and school gate gossip, counted for much more than editorial opinion. So, perhaps, did the mushroom growth of blogs on the net. As the dust settles, in France especially, there's a good case seeing this as the first modern election where the internet (with 24 million French signed up) actually wrecked a campaign led by old politicians and traditional media. Naught there for the comfort of Mr Murdoch and friends, perhaps? Tea and buns in Downing Street doesn't fix anything any longer. The absurd courting of editors (as chronicled by Piers Morgan) seems even more absurd. The emperors have left their clothes in the changing room. Vive la libération! you might say (out of the other side of your mouth).

title: Reduce to expand
bodyText: The Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development this week prescribed a remedy for France's economy - privatisation, fiscal discipline and UK-style labour reforms - that the patient does not want. One can understand why, up to a point. To anyone who visits France regularly, the French still seem to have an enviable lifestyle and an efficient health service and transport system that remain years ahead of the UK. The unemployed 10% of the population are not visible to the casual visitor - and they are, in any case, fairly well looked after by the state. That, of course, is part of the problem. When jobseekers know that (unlike in the UK) they will continue to receive relatively high levels of unemployment pay for a long period, they are naturally reluctant to take jobs they don't like. Equally, employers - faced with the difficulty of laying off workers - are reluctant to hire them until strong demand for their products forces them too. This is one of the reasons why the introduction of a 35-hour week did not lead to companies hiring more people, as its protagonists had expected. What is the solution? While there is no doubt at all that France ought to reform its economy in the long run (starting with getting rid of most agriculture subsidies), that does not answer its short-term problems. As with the rest of the eurozone, the main restraint holding its economy back is shortage of demand. People need to spend more. If you compare the British and French economies over recent years, the biggest difference is that in Britain very high levels of public and consumer spending have brought about (temporarily at least) a virtuous circle in which higher economic growth increases real wages, house prices and living standards, thereby generating extra spending power to keep the boom going. It is true that spending is now slowing down rapidly and high levels of debt have in any case made the boom unsustainable, but Nemesis hasn't arrived yet. It may be that the British economic miracle turns sours just when European countries start to adopt it - but that hasn't happened yet, because the eurozone has yet to take any serious measures to boost demand. What is needed is a sharp cut in eurozone interest rates to stimulate consumer and capital spending. Yes, there are risks to stimulating inflation, but there are enough offsetting pressures around - such as globalisation and 10% unemployment rates - to reduce such risks. Europe has the huge, underexploited, advantage that it is largely a self-sufficient economy. When demand rises, countries mainly buy from each other. European countries should not even think about dropping the euro until they have exploited the potential of the economic union they have created. Cutting interest rates isn't a panacea, simply because people may be so worried about the future that they put any extra money they have into savings (as has happened in Japan). But no one will know whether it will work until it is tried.

title: French farmers brace for a last stand on the barricades
bodyText: JéRôME Regnault, president of France's national young farmers' union, surveyed the scene as 600 French farmers dressed in green T-shirts marched from the European Commission's Paris offices to Les Invalides, near the country's farms ministry, last week. Railing against low wheat prices and delays in receiving their subsidies from Europe, the farmers built a wall from 500 sacks of wheat in what has become a ritual Parisian set-piece. 'I hope this will be a strong message for the future of agriculture,' Regnault shouted. It is a message that the French political elite cannot ignore even if they want to. The French farming lobby holds a stranglehold over President Jacques Chirac. The veteran leader's domestic political career has been largely built on defending the interests of small farmers. 'He instinctively feels it necessary to do - and he is probably right,' says former UK Environment Secretary John Selwyn Gummer. Never mind that farmers' numbers are dwindling as they desert the countryside for towns and cities. In 1973, when the UK joined Europe, 11 per cent of the French labour force was in farming. Today that figure has more than halved, and some 40 per cent of French farmers have an annual income below the minimum salary. In France today it is the massive agribusinesses that soak up 80 per cent of France's €10.4 billion (£6.9bn) Common Agricultural Policy handout - companies such as sugar giant Beghin-Say, which receives annual payments from Europe of €236m. That money enables Beghin to sell sugar cheaply to developing countries, which ruins their fledgling industries and damages lives. France is easily the biggest CAP beneficiary in Europe. In 2003, its €10.4bn compared with €6bn for Germany and €4bn for Britain and Spain. In the nine years before that, French farmers received €154bn, compared with €64bn in Britain. And that's not the full extent of the handouts; French farmers also receive substantial support from its government. Increasingly, though, there are voices of dissent. Sweden has stated that the CAP is insupportable, and in France itself, Bénédicte Hermelin of Solagral, a French campaign group, has said: 'Most French civil society organisations, especially development NGOs, do not support the government position on CAP reform. Negative impacts of the CAP are well-known. The CAP is not working, especially when the consequence is poverty and food insecurity in developing countries.' Hermelin added that a new national alliance is forming to end France's reliance on the CAP and promote trade justice. The CAP was fundamentally reformed two years ago. No longer were cheques from Europe tied to how many acres were farmed. But French farmers, unlike their British counterparts, have been painfully slow at adapting to the new regime. The feeling is that when Chirac eventually leaves office, a new generation of politicians will not feel such a historical, emotional link to the scheme and will accept a phasing out of the CAP. They may opt to postpone the inevitable by doling out larger government handouts. After all, one fundamental reason why France voted 'no' in its constitution referendum earlier this month was to protect its national identity from the homogenising effects of unfettered free market capitalism. But one thing is clear: the CAP is entering its endgame. As European money moves east to accession countries, the question is only how quickly it will go. In the next six months, there will be enormous pressure for France to compromise. World trade talks in Hong Kong hinge on Europe and America offering major concessions by withdrawing generous handouts. Pressure will come from the developing countries, which have learnt that, by hanging together, they exert an effective veto on reaching solid agreement at the Doha round. Northern European countries appear prepared to accept compromise. It is France and Spain that are most protective of the status quo. They will be joined by America, which has increased subsidies to its own farmers. In March 1957, France was one of the signatories of the Treaty of Rome, which established the EEC, but in 1958 it seemed that France would not be able to provide it with the expected leadership. There was inflation and economic stagnation at home, and the insoluble Algerian problem across the Mediterranean. France appeared to be moving rapidly towards chaos. But the extension of the European Coal and Steel Community into the European Economic Community in 1958 set in motion the introduction of the CAP in 1960. What was meant to promote food security has become a security blanket. Separating the main beneficiary from its blanket will not be easy - but it has to be done.

title: Wheels come off the onion economy
bodyText: The following correction was printed in the Guardian's Corrections and clarifications column, Wednesday June 22 2005. In the following article, we gave the published employment rates from France and Britain as 62% and 75% respectively. However, once the figures are standardised by Eurostat, they come out at 63% and 72% respectively, a nine-point difference rather than 13. The new French prime minister, Dominique de Villepin, has declared he will start to tackle France's unemployment problem within his first 100 days in office. Joblessness, he told his compatriots, is the "true French disease". That is self-evident, but de Villepin's frankness shows that the recognition of the problem is becoming more and more widespread, especially since the French voted "non" in the referendum on the European constitution, at least in part because of malcontent over unemployment. For many years the French have looked down their Gallic noses at Britain's "Anglo-Saxon", hire-and-fire, liberal market economy with what they saw, wrongly, as its McJobs - low living standards and long hours. Indeed, de Villepin was careful in announcing some reforms to the labour market which he hopes, wrongly, might reduce France's army of jobless people, to emphasise that he was not abandoning France's cherished "social model" in favour of anything more "liberal". Meanwhile French newspapers, even left-leaning ones, are lining up to praise "Le Blairisme" while the country's interior minister and presidential hopeful, Nicolas Sarkozy, has declared that the French model is no longer the best. He's got that right. The figures show why. French unemployment is 10%, more than twice Britain's 4.7%. One in four young French people is out of a job, compared with one in 10 in Britain. Small wonder, then, that so many young French people are working in Britain, and often in highly skilled, high value-added jobs. Employment here is a record 75% of the workforce. In France it is a dreadful 62%. French average earnings (wages and salaries) have grown 2 to 2.5% a year over the past decade, roughly in line with inflation. That means people are not getting any richer in France. In Britain, by contrast, earnings have been growing by 4 to 4.5% for years, meaning a real-terms rise of more than 20% in less than a decade. That represents a big rise in wealth, the sort of rise the French were used to in earlier decades but are now having to forego. In short, the French social model is not making French people better off and is not offering them enough jobs. More of an anti-social model, I should have thought. None of this is to say that France is not a great country with beautiful countryside, fantastic infrastructure and many world-class companies. It is not for nothing that more than 60,000 British households have a second home in France. But the point is that growing prosperity means they can afford one. While France looks great on the surface, the economy is simply not working for its people. This does not mean Britain is perfect but at least strong economic growth is freeing up resources to pump into public services. The French health service, which many British holidaymakers think is wonderful, has run out of money. Nor is it the case that Britons are all working horribly long hours for low wages in Dickensian workhouses. Britain, like France, has a minimum wage, set at almost the same level. But more French people work for the minimum wage than British people do. So much for Britain's low-wage economy. Working hours are interesting too. We have all heard of the French 35-hour week while the French often think Britons all work more than 48 hours a week because of Tony Blair and Gordon Brown's insistence on keeping the opt-out of the EU's working-time directive. While it's true that about 20% of the British workforce work longer than 48 hours, the average full-time week is 37 hours, just above the French. It is simply not obvious that the British work a longer week than their cross-Channel neighbours. And nor does the McJobs claim (that British jobs are all low-grade services jobs rather than "proper" manufacturing jobs) stand up to scrutiny. As Arnaud Vaissié, a French chief executive working in London, points out, the size of the French manufacturing sector is slightly smaller than the British as a share of the economy. While Britain has an acknowledged problem with the quality of its education and skills levels, it is not clear that France is a shining beacon. "Actually the average educational attainment in France and Britain is very similar," says Raymond Torres, a labour market economist at the Paris-based Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development. In fact, Vaissié adds, the two economies are more striking for their similarities than their differences. The economies are a similar size, as are their populations. France, though, is more than twice as big as Britain, giving those big, empty spaces that holidaymakers like so much. Interestingly, a similarly low proportion (less than 5%) of each country's workforce is involved in agriculture. Economic growth has also been fairly similar. Britain's economy has grown by 17% since 1998 and France's by 13%, though France has grown more slowly than Britain in the past couple of years. What really sets the two apart are their labour markets. For France this is a real achilles heel and one which is holding back an economy that could otherwise really sizzle. France's keenness to provide protection for those in work and generous benefits for those out of work has suffered from the law of unintended consequences and sent unemployment up. Firms faced with an equation in which the cost of employing people outweighs the benefits, particularly for the low-skilled, have invested in machines instead. Thus productivity, or output per worker, is much higher in France than Britain. "In France, recruitment is the last resort. In Britain it is the first," says Vaissié, who has set up a thinktank to consider what France can learn from Britain. Flexibility in its labour market is the key, he concludes, but flexibility remains a dirty word in France. Rod Mitchell, an English mortgage broker in the south of France, is seeking to expand his business but says taking on French workers is hazardous and costly. "If I want to pay a part-time assistant €1,400 [£940] a month gross, I would actually pay €2,400 once all the social costs are added." If the person turned out to be unsuitable, sacking would be difficult and expensive. "So I haven't done it." Professor Richard Layard of the London School of Economics says France needs to reform its labour market to encourage the unemployed back to work as quickly as possible with a carrot-and-stick approach, rather than paying them a lot to do nothing for a long time. "Unemployment is one of the clearest sources of human misery which we know how to reduce. The key ... is to mobilise the unemployed to fill emerging vacancies as quickly as possible," he wrote in a recent paper. "The longer you pay people for doing nothing, the longer they will do just that." In France it is quite possible to receive several thousand euros a month for quite some time after you lose your job. In Britain it is a few hundred pounds and that can be quickly withdrawn if you don't accept one of the jobs on offer. Britain has one-stop job centres combining benefits payments with job searching and training. France has three different agencies doing those functions. France has a myriad of labour market policies in place but spends more than five times as much as Britain on them even though its unemployment is only twice as high. But the policies are misdirected and don't coax people back into work. Britain changed direction with its labour market policies in the late 1980s, with considerable success. So did countries such as Denmark, Sweden and the Netherlands, which have high levels of social protection and prove that it is not incompatible with high employment. Getting more people into work doesn't mean forcing people's wages down and hours up. Indeed, high unemployment in France has had the effect of depressing pay. It does involve regional flexibility of wages so that firms in different areas and industries can adjust their pay to local conditions. France is bizarre in that union membership is lowest in the industrial world, at 10% of the workforce, but union pay agreements are extended by law to cover 95% of all wage deals. In Britain, 30% of us are in unions and 30% of wage deals are set by agreements with unions. Torres and Layard say this is a significant rigidity keeping joblessness high in France. France does better than Britain in one area - disability benefit, although France has far fewer people on sick leave than Britain's 2.7 million. France's labour market problems can be fixed but de Villepin's response is not promising. Putting up the minimum wage and increasing unemployment benefit are not the way ahead. Nor is adding even more civil servants to an already bloated public sector and paying for them by scrapping a planned tax cut. Much bolder action is required but don't bank on it happening soon. De Villepin is a big fan of Napoleon. He may soon be reminded that his hero's own 100-day campaign ended at Waterloo. ashley.seager@theguardian.com

title: Hard-up French start sell-offs with Gaz stake
bodyText: The French government yesterday began a series of planned privatisations of state enterprises by launching the sale of up to 22% of Gaz de France in a move to shore up its finances. The €4bn (£2.6bn) sell-off at Europe's second biggest gas supplier is due to pave the way for the much trickier part-privatisation of power group Electricité de France in the autumn and of nuclear operator Areva next year amid protest strikes by unions. The offering came two days after Thierry Breton, finance minister, told the country that it was living beyond its means after running up substantial deficits for the past 25 years. France has been hit by an unemployment rate stuck at around 10% and declining consumer confidence, with Mr Breton cutting this year's official growth forecast to 2% from 2.5% and Insee, the leading forecaster, putting it at just 1.5%. Falling growth is putting severe pressure on the government's target of cutting the budget deficit below the 3% ceiling set by the EU's stability and growth pact. Similar pressure in Germany has prompted reports that the federal government could sell off its remaining stakes in Deutsche Telekom and Deutsche Post to help fill a new €10bn hole in its budget. The offering at GDF, which values the group at up to €23.6bn, is expected to prove popular with investors and could net the state between €2.5bn and €2.9bn, informed sources said. Global institutional investors are being offered the shares at a price range of €20.70 to €24, with French retail investors being offered them at between €20.50 and €23.80. Up to 15% of the shares on offer are reserved, partially discounted, for employees in an effort to win their confidence and head off the protest strikes. Unions yesterday shelved these until the autumn when up to 30% of EDF may be sold off. Industry sources said the real target of union ire was EDF which has amassed substantial debts through overseas acquisitions, including in Britain, but could raise more than €8bn through its planned IPO and be valued at around €50bn. The part-privatisation of Areva, originally planned for this year, could also raise substantial sums for the government, which said in late 2004 it could sell off between 35% and 40% but refused to confirm this yesterday. GDF will also net between €1.6bn and €1.9bn by issuing new shares in order to help fund its planned expansion at home and in the rest of Europe where the market is due to be wholly competitive by July 2007. It is only 8% self-sufficient in gas.

title: Gaz sale nets 2.5bn euros for France
bodyText: The French state made €2.5bn (£1.7bn) through the partial privatisation of Gaz de France (GDF) yesterday as fresh figures showed the country's budget deficit ballooning to more than €50bn in the year to May. In a prelude to the bigger sale of up to 30% of Electricité de France (EDF) in the autumn, GDF raised a further €2bn for its planned European expansion by issuing new shares. The stock was priced at €23.40 for institutional investors, towards the top end of the range, in an offering more than 30 times oversubscribed. It values GDF at €23bn. The EDF offer, due in October, is much more fraught but is expected to bring in substantially more cash for the government and to value the group close to €50bn. Thierry Breton, finance minister, said the government would invest the GDF cash in industrial innovation and jobs under a modernisation programme to drag the economy out of the doldrums. Mr Breton, who has begun freezing some state spending, insisted France would meet its target of a deficit below the 3% of GDP set by the EU stability pact for the first time in four years. In the first five months of 2005, lower tax revenues and higher spending sent the deficit soaring from €43.3bn in the same period of 2004 to €51.5bn, suggesting the deficit will be closer to 3.5%.

title: Big two face new budget deficit flak
bodyText: Germany and France, the eurozone's two biggest economies, could soon face renewed EU sanctions because of their failure to bring their budget deficits under control. Yesterday EU finance ministers confirmed they had cut the eurozone's growth forecast from 1.6% to 1.3% because of the impact of rising oil prices as the OECD signalled it is likely to cut its 1.2% forecast in September. Germany and France have been warned that, without further savings, they will breach the 3% deficit limit set by the EU's stability pact again this year, with Germany heading for 3.7% in 2005 and 3.4% in 2006. Hans Eichel, German finance minister, who presents his proposed 2006 budget today, appealed to the European commission to take the country's anaemic economic performance into account when deciding whether to reopen infringement proceedings, suspended against both countries in 2003.

title: Germany and France fight deficit
bodyText: Germany and France, the eurozone's two biggest economies, yesterday stepped up privatisation programmes to curtail their burgeoning budget deficits as their economic growth slowed. As Germany's central bank, the Bundesbank, said the economy stagnated in the second quarter, the government sold its remaining stake in Deutsche Post and a tranche of Deutsche Telekom. The French government, which netted some €2.5bn (£1.72bn) this month when it sold off 22% of Gaz de France, hopes to raise a further €11bn from the sale of its final stakes in three motorway companies. It launched a tender competition yesterday. The German government parked a final 7.3% state holding in Deutsche Post and a similar stake in Deutsche Telekom with state bank KfW, raising €5bn. The bank is free to sell the shares on.

title: French market watchdog may investigate Danone
bodyText: The French stock market regulator, AMF, is considering a full-scale inquiry into this month's surge in the share price of Danone, the food group, and alleged manipulation regarding a putative takeover by PepsiCo. AMF confirmed yesterday PepsiCo had told it that it was not planning a takeover bid for Danone, sending the French group's shares down as much as 9%. The shares had risen up to 27% since the beginning of this month as the political establishment in France mounted an unprecedented campaign to keep Danone, the owner of Evian water, out of overseas hands. It emerged that Gérard Rameix, the regulator's senior official, had been pressing the American soft drinks group for several days to declare its intentions and had been warning Danone, investors and other players in the market of rules banning the spreading of "inexact, imprecise and deceptive" information. An AMF spokeswoman said the regulator had been carrying out systematic surveillance of the buying and selling of Danone shares during recent weeks and technical inquiries were continuing. "We are in a pre-investigation phase," she said. "It does not mean that there will be an investigation and it could take several days or even weeks to decide. And we may not make it public." Colette Neuville, the leader of the minority shareholders' defence group, Adam, has demanded a full-scale investigation to determine whether the rumours about Danone were the result of market manipulation and insider trading. A series of senior politicians had publicly called for measures to defend Danone against a likely hostile bid from PepsiCo while the French media reported rumours that one was imminent - and even, wrongly, that the American group had already bought a 3% stake. Ms Neuville said the ferocious media campaign had relied entirely on rumours but allowed some investors to make big profits. Thierry Breton, the French finance minister, told Le Monde that the mobilisation around Danone, which brought in President Jacques Chirac and Dominique de Villepin, the prime minister, was "very good news." "It's the first time that the French have shown such passion for their own companies. One says too often that they are angry with their firms. "I want to capitalise on this mobilisation and take it forward," he said. Mr Breton, a former chief of France Télécom, urged French and other European firms to see off predators with sound business plans and close links with their shareholders. But he said: "All capital is welcome. France has no future in protectionism and turning in on itself ... It's not the nationality of the capital that counts, it's the nationality of the business plan."

title: Suez sales hit 20bn euros as prices soar
bodyText: Soaring electricity and gas prices drove sales at Suez, the French utilities group, 8% higher to more than €20bn (£13.8bn) in the first half, it reported yesterday. Turnover rose €1.5bn, with higher natural gas prices alone contributing €320m. In Europe, which accounts for 79% of sales, increased wholesale electricity and gas sales added €143m. Suez, which used to own Northumbrian Water, claims to be Britain's leading provider of recycling and waste management services through its Sita subsidiary. Sita, which employs 5,000 in the UK, increased sales by 4.2%, partly due to a new incinerator on the Isle of Man. The group boosted its sales in France by more than €500m and, through the opening of the country's energy market for industrial consumers, now claims an 8.5% share of the electricity and 5% share of the natural gas markets. Suez, which is threatening to quit Argentina, said its international division boosted sales by 16% to €2.8bn, largely thanks to increased demand in the US. It aims to grow overall turnover organically by up to 7% this year.

title: Gloom spreads across eurozone
bodyText: France reported slower than expected economic growth for the second quarter yesterday, ending a week of disappointing data from the eurozone and casting doubt on the European Central Bank's optimistic forecasts for the rest of the year. France's growth in domestic product slowed to 0.1% in the three months to June, down from 0.4% in the first quarter of the year. Most analysts had expected growth to be higher and blamed France's woes on sluggish consumer demand. "The momentum in the French economy has dried up," said Michael Hume at Lehman Brothers. "The days of France outperforming the rest of the euro area are now over." Unemployment in France is wavering near a five-year high and analysts said concerns over job opportunities, as well as rising petrol and energy prices, are keeping consumers out of the shops. Jean-Louis Mourier, of Aurel Leven Securities, said: "The labour market is not improving enough and there is little job creation. For consumer morale to improve, households would have to see a real improvement in the job market." The slowdown in the eurozone's second largest economy will have repercussions for all of Europe. France's strong performance last year, mainly driven by lower household saving and increased spending, is widely credited with helping to prop up the rest of mainland Europe. Kenneth Wattret, a senior economist at BNP Paribas, said: "It was the French economy out of the major economies of the eurozone which was supplying most of the stimulus to growth over the last 12 to 18 months." Germany's economy suffered even more than France, with growth stagnating. The German statistics agency said earlier this week that it had revised the first-quarter German growth figure to 0.8%, down from an earlier estimate of 1.0%. The one bright spot in the eurozone appears to be that this week Italy, its third-largest economy, is reporting second-quarter growth of 0.7%, double the level of analysts' expectations. But reports of an Italian "rebound" may prove to be misleading. Italy's economy contracted in both the first quarter of 2005 and in the fourth quarter of last year, and its year-on-year growth is only 0.1%. Analysts at Capital Economics suspect that the data has been insufficiently corrected for seasonal factors and anticipate a substantial downward revision in future reports. "This does not reduce our serious concerns about the Italian outlook. The prospects for Italy are still bleak" said Julien Seetharamdoo at Capital Economics. The Netherlands has posted the largest recent increase in output. Dutch gross domestic product growth was 1.2% in the second quarter, a turnround from -0.8% in the first quarter and higher than the United States. Analysts suspect similar seasonal data distortions to Italy's and believe these countries are unlikely to maintain such high growth. As a whole, the eurozone suffered a sluggish second quarter in the light of these mixed growth reports. The European Central Bank this week announced modest 0.3% growth, down from 0.5% in the first quarter. Despite the weak report, the ECB remained upbeat about the remainder of the year and forecast a substantial growth pick-up. Growth in the third quarter is expected to be between 0.3% and 0.6% and in the fourth quarter 0.4% to 0.8%. City analysts concurred with the likelihood of a turnround in the eurozone's fortunes as second-quarter growth was better than forecast. "We expect the second quarter to be as bad as it gets for the eurozone this year," said Mr Seetharamdoo. The ECB also signalled that increases in interest rates are unlikely: "There is no significant evidence of underlying domestic inflationary pressures building up in the euro area." The ECB expects the euro's 8% decline against the dollar to dramatically improve European export potential, but the ECB estimates might be over-ambitious. After a three-month rally, the dollar has begun to fall. Last year, the euro's appreciation against the dollar and other foreign currencies subtracted almost half a percentage point of growth for the year, according to calculations from the International Monetary Fund. The rocketing price of oil this spring - with new highs of more than $65 a barrel this week - could cause a worldwide slowdown and further depress low consumer demand across Europe. Forthcoming elections in Germany this year and in Italy next year could also cause uncertainty in the markets and renew concerns about the stability of pensions and the troubled outlook for many government budgets. An International Monetary Fund report published earlier this month claims that the eurozone may face deeper structural problems which will continue to stump its growth potential. The fund's report described the eurozone recovery as "low-flying". It cited the chronic high unemployment in Europe and the need for "product market reforms". According to the IMF analysis, the eurozone still has significant red tape problems that prevent European businesses from working together and taking advantage of the full benefits of a single currency and easily migrating workforce. Attempts by the eurozone to reach an agreement on a centralised budget for the member states in June ended in fierce debate, frustrating hopes for long-term structural reforms.

title: French dig in over China clothes row
bodyText: Peter Mandelson, the EU trade commissioner, yesterday won broad backing from the 25 member states for a rapid solution to the textiles crisis that would see millions of Chinese-made clothes released from warehouses at European ports. Mr Mandelson made a dramatic plea at a meeting with the 25 ambassadors to the EU for all 80m items blocked at the ports to be released, to avoid shortages and higher prices in shops this autumn. The clothes have been warehoused since early August. But it emerged that he still faces a struggle to free all the goods because of continuing resistance from southern European countries such as France, Italy and Spain, which are demanding concessions from the Chinese in return. The trade commissioner, who was given strong support by Downing Street yesterday, wants to allow all the blocked items to be released first. Talks with the Chinese about the impact on agreed quotas would follow, in proposals he will put to his 24 fellow commissioners this morning. While the French and others said they were prepared to let large amounts of clothes go free, they demanded that Beijing agree to cut its quotas for next year. So far Beijing has refused. Tonnes of Chinese-made clothes, including sweaters, trousers and bras, flooded into the EU after a three-year agreement to limit growth in imports was signed on June 10, but before the agreement came into force on July 12. These licensed goods and other unlicensed goods are now blocked at the ports. French diplomats indicated that Paris now favoured a solution based on "equity and fairness" to allow importers who ordered goods from China "in good faith" before July 12 to get immediate access to them. The remaining items would be handled differently. France has come under pressure from Germany, its traditional ally, and, recently, retailers and consumer groups, to enable goods blocked at its own ports to be released. France, Italy and Spain are among the more protectionist-minded countries but also those with the biggest backlog of warehoused clothes. Britain, which holds the EU presidency, and the commission said all 25 countries at yesterday's talks had agreed on the need for a rapid solution to the crisis, although no vote was taken. Tony Blair's official spokesman said the prime minister "fully supports" Mr Mandelson in his efforts to resolve the crisis which, he added, showed the need for the EU to modernise if it is to cope with the challenges from emerging economies. Meanwhile talks between the US and China on the same issue broke up in Beijing with President Bush ready to impose emergency restrictions on Chinese-made clothing.

title: French flunk labour reforms
bodyText: 'Tries hard but could do better.' This was the remark, French prime minister Dominique de Villepin admitted last week, that his teacher used to write regularly on his school work. That may be the conclusion that could still be drawn today as Mr de Villepin celebrates his 100th day in the job, a period that he launched promising to tackle France's chronic unemployment problem. He started with an "urgent plan for jobs" and rounded it off last week with a second phase aimed at boosting "social growth" and the purchasing power of the French. But his plan was, he said, "a modernisation" of France's cherished social model rather than a new model. On the surface at least, he appears to have had some success. Unemployment has unexpectedly fallen from the five-year high of 10.2% that Mr de Villepin inherited to 9.9%, but is still twice as high as in Britain, Japan or the United States. "These first results are proof that we can win the battle for jobs. But we must do better," he told the French last week. Opinions on the streets of Paris are mixed. "They all make promises these guys but never deliver. My son has been out of work for a year and can't find a job, especially one that matches his qualifications," said taxi driver Pierre Danese. His words reflect the fact that almost 25% of young French people are out of work. Others are more enthusiastic. "He's a tough politician that De Villepin. I think the economy will get going now and unemployment is already falling," said Hassim, working in a cafe. But with the French economy all but grinding to a halt in the second quarter of the year and consumer spending shrinking even before the latest surge in oil and petrol prices, economists are warning that Mr de Villepin may be counting his chickens too early. Most forecasters, including the government, expect the French economy to grow little more than 1.5% this year, far too slow to encourage genuine, self-sustaining job creation by the private sector. The fall in unemployment was likely to be a fluke related to public-sector jobs or apprenticeships paid for by the state, say economists. High unemployment Unemployment in Britain has started to rise while economic growth is about 2% this year, so France, with its slower growth, is likely to suffer renewed rises in unemployment rather than further falls. High unemployment has kept wage growth down for many years, leading to a fading in purchasing power and consumer demand in spite of the 2% interest rates set by the European Central Bank. This lack of demand, in turn, is now restricting growth. "Consumers are becoming much more cautious. This is disappointing because France had been the best performer of the big three eurozone economies in recent years," says Gwyn Hacche, European economist at HSBC. France's problem for many years has been that even when the economy has grown steadily, as it did between 1997 and 2004, it has barely created any jobs. As non-wage costs are so high and job protection very strong, firms have tended to employ machines rather than people. In Britain, where growth was similar over the period, unemployment fell sharply, helped by a flexible labour market. What is needed, agree labour market experts, is not only stronger economic growth but a wholesale reform of France's complex and over-regulated labour market, making it more attractive for firms to hire people and giving people a greater incentive to work than live on the dole. That would produce a virtuous circle of growth creating more jobs and more employment helping to boost growth. Mr de Villepin knows this. "We must make it easier and more interesting for people to work than live on benefits," he said last week, to the fury of France's notoriously truculent trade unions. His key labour market measures, however, do not yet add up to a radical programme. The changes include extending a probationary period for new workers to two years from six months - but only for firms with fewer than 20 employees. He is increasing an in-work tax credit and allowing it to be paid monthly rather than annually. He will also give a €1,000 (£680) bonus to any long-term jobless person who takes a job. He announced other measures but there was no real reduction of unemployment benefits, which can stretch to thousands of euros a month. He also promised a small income tax cut to boost purchasing power for the middle classes - but not until 2007. There will also be some more TGV tracks and motorways, areas that France traditionally excels in. In all the package is worth a modest €5bn, of which the lion's share will go on the 2007 tax cut. France has a big public deficit, so big tax cuts are out of the question. Experts remain to be convinced the package will work, especially as it adds to, rather than replaces, France's mountain of job creation measures that have had little success in recent years. "He has made some steps in the right direction but creating a specific new employment contract works against one of the other priorities that they should have which is to cut red tape," says Paul O'Brien, senior economist responsible for France at the Paris-based Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development. "But at least they have begun to recognise the problem. I hope the extension of probationary periods to two years is the start of a more comprehensive reform," he said, adding that previous governments' attempts at reform have often come to nought or led to rejection by voters at general elections. Mr Hacche at HSBC puts it more bluntly. "I don't think anything has changed. The French are in a muddle about economic reform. They don't want to take the Anglo-Saxon route but don't seem to know what else to do." He points to a recent announcement from the government of a list of so-called "strategic" industries that it intends to protect from takeover by foreign firms, in defiance of EU law. Mr de Villepin declared himself ready, over the summer, to defend food group Danone from a potential takeover by PepsiCo. At the same time French companies are active in other countries, owning large chunks of Britain's electricity and water provision, for example. Other governments have learned that increased competition improves corporate efficiency and lowers prices for consumers. So Mr de Villepin is talking the talk on reform but has yet to prove he can walk the walk.

title: France hints at tax on oil firms
bodyText: France today raised the prospect of a tax on oil companies to ease the pressure on consumers and businesses from higher energy prices. Thierry Breton, the French finance minister, put forward the idea as EU finance ministers met in Manchester to discuss the impact of higher oil prices. Crude oil price have risen by more than 50% in the past year, and hit a record $70 (£38) a barrel after Hurricane Katrina damaged production and refining capacity in the Gulf of Mexico. The Austrian finance minister, Karl-Heinz Grasser, said he expected his colleagues to issue a statement urging the US to make more effort to cut its oil consumption, which is by far the world's biggest. "If the US had the same energy efficiency as Europe, we could save 8m barrels of oil a day," Mr Grasser said. He added that he thought the European ministers should make that point in a communique to a meeting of the G7 industrialised countries later this month. Joaquin Almunia, the European economic and monetary affairs commissioner, who is also attending the talks, said he feared bigger fuel bills would dent confidence, spur inflation, reduce consumer spending and hit a potential economic recovery in the eurozone. EU officials said the finance ministers would discuss the measures they had in mind and avoid action putting other governments under excessive pressure to cut taxes. Several ministers said cutting fuel tax was not the answer. "We will discuss this here but ... no tax cuts - I don't believe in that," Antti Kalliomaki, the Finnish finance minister, said. In 2000, truckers across Europe staged protests demanding subsidies and tax cuts after France infuriated Britain and other countries by caving in and offering financial relief to lorry drivers who had blockaded motorways. Protest has been simmering since world oil prices reached recent highs, but the unrest has not yet reached the level of five years ago. The rise in fuel prices has also boosted government petrol tax revenues. Both Belgium and France are to hand back some of the windfall receipts by sending a cheque for €75 (£50) to households. Before heading to Manchester, the chancellor, Gordon Brown, officially launched a new scheme to raise an extra $4bn worth of vaccines for children in poor countries. Mr Brown also invited several economic experts and CEOs for a brainstorming session on the challenges of globalisation - among them the chief executives of Unilever, Vodafone and Reckitt Benckiser.

title: French take a gamble on part-privatisation of EDF
bodyText: The French government yesterday took a huge financial and political gamble by launching the part-privatisation of state-owned EDF, Europe's second-largest power group, in the face of investor uncertainty and union fury. Ministers ran into immediate resistance from investors when they set a price range of €29.5 to €34.1 per share - higher than the €29 expected - for selling off 15% of EDF in a move designed to raise €7bn for the group's expansion programme in Europe. The sale is the world's biggest initial public offering for more than four years, since Kraft Foods' market debut in the US in 2001, and values EDF at between €55bn and €63.5bn, enabling it to overtake Germany's Eon as Europe's biggest utility. The launch came just a day after Dominique de Villepin, the French premier, pulled the plug on a long-planned float of Areva, the nuclear power-plant maker, and coincided with the scrapping of the flotation of Eutelsat, the European satellite operator, because of poor demand among investors. Amid growing evidence that the government would have to lower the price-range to get the share sale under way to meet the proposed listing from November 21, Thierry Breton, finance minister, raised hackles further by slashing a proposed price hike by GDF, the gas supplier, to 3.8%. GDF, part-privatised in July after initial problems, wanted to raise domestic prices 12% because of booming oil prices and investors saw the government-imposed cut as another reason to be wary of EDF. The French group has no such problems in Britain's fully competitive energy market, where it owns substantial generation, distribution and supply businesses, including the former London Electricity and Seeboard. It provides 7% of UK power and supplies more than 5 million customers. Unions opposed to the EDF sell-off have called a nationwide strike for November 8, while the socialist opposition has said it would renationalise the group if it wins power in 2007. The unions lost a court battle on Thursday to prevent the sell-off. But the government is pressing ahead with selling its stakes in key motorway operators, raising €11bn to help ease rampant state debts. As European bourses fell to a two-month low, fund managers such as Jacques-Antoine Bretteil, of International Capital Gestion, said the EDF price range was too expensive. "It's way over the top in the current climate; I expect them to cut it a bit," he told Reuters. But, with EDF certain to enter into the CAC-40 index, others indicated that the offering could not be ignored - depending on the price. Foreign investors could prove the most reluctant buyers, it was said, because of the unions' influence on the group. The government, which is offering retail investors a €1 discount and has reserved part of the offering for EDF employees, said it would sell a maximum of 206.6m new EDF shares, with a further over-allotment of 31m shares. The final price will be decided on November 20. Pierre Gadonneix, EDF chief executive, said the €7bn would help fund the group's plans to invest €40bn over the next five years, half of it in France, where the energy market is due to be fully liberalised by 2007. Rivals such as Suez are targeting a 10-15% share.

title: The new misérables of France
bodyText: Curfews, deportations and macho language from Paris may have quelled the unrest in France's troubled suburbs, but the rioting teenagers and burning cars exposed deep divisions in the nation's economy. It was a French sociologist, Emile Durkheim, who coined the term anomie for the malaise and alienation that result from radical social change. For him, writing in the 19th century, it was the Industrial Revolution that had shifted social boundaries and caused suffering and dissent. Today, as youngsters from the banlieues take to the streets, some experts argue it is globalisation that has caused society's norms to break down. Unlike their counterparts in the agricultural sector, who are generously subsidised, unskilled industrial workers are heavily exposed to low-cost competition from fast-growing economies such as India, China and the former communist countries. While farmers quietly plough their furrows and hi-tech national champions thrive (again, sometimes, with a little assistance from the state), factory workers are struggling to survive.
