'France is good at using technology at the high end,' says Michael Dicks of Lehman Brothers. The problem lies with 'the basic manufacturing-type jobs, which you can do outside the country'. Charles Dumas of Lombard Street Research believes the riots are a nasty side-effect of globalisation, which benefits the world as a whole but creates losers, many already at the bottom of society. 'Globalisation adds hugely to world welfare and sharply reduces global inequalities in the distribution of income, but not everybody is a winner,' he says, adding that if you bolt on two billion or more Chinese and Indian workers to the global labour market, the bargaining power of low-skilled workers in other parts of the world is bound to be reduced. It doesn't help that, as with many of its eurozone partners, France's long-awaited economic recovery has failed to materialise. 'Consumption has lost its way; industrial production is lower than it was a year ago. Wherever you look, you're seeing a loss of momentum,' says Dicks. Income inequality - the gap between the haves and the have-nots - has not increased in France in recent years as it has in the US and UK. But a toxic combination of poor education in poor areas, and in some cases sheer prejudice, creates a vicious circle which means that those from ethnic minorities, heavily concentrated in large estates on the outskirts of cities, can become trapped outside the job market. Muslim fundamentalism, poor parental discipline and the ghettoisation of minorities probably all had their part to play in causing the riots, but economists say chronic unemployment certainly doesn't help. Part of the blame can be laid on the French labour market itself, which is frequently criticised as heavily protected and excessively rigid. That means people who are already in jobs are well looked after; it's difficult to fire them and they receive generous benefits. But, paradoxically, those very protections can mean that fewer new jobs are created. When the Paris-based Organisation of Economic Co-operation and Development surveyed the economy earlier this year, one chapter was headed 'Why does unemployment remain so high, and participation so low?' and began: 'The unemployment rate is currently 10 per cent, and has not been below 8 per cent for 20 years'. (In Britain, the unemployment rate is less than 5 per cent.) Prime minister Dominique de Villepin recently introduced a package of measures to kick-start job creation, aimed particularly at small firms, but economists say they are unlikely to make much difference. 'The huge difference between the UK and France is that the UK is really a market economy,' says French economist Yves Zenou, professor of economics at the Institute of Industrial Economics in Stockholm. 'You have a very low unemployment rate, but lots of bad jobs. In France, there is a higher minimum wage, but a lot of people have been out of the labour market for a long time. The difference is flexibility.' There is evidence that some groups come off especially badly. More than a fifth of youths are unemployed, twice as high as the average rate for adults. For foreign-born workers, it's even worse. Almost one in four is out of work, and in the districts the government has designated 'zones urbaines sensibles' - the most troublesome city areas - more than a third have no jobs. Education levels for foreign-born workers are far lower than those for French natives and the gap is wider than in many other countries. Some of last week's rioters said they were third-generation unemployed. Those divisions are reinforced by a streak of prejudice. Cécile Deer, a French economist at Oxford University, says the attitude of employers exacerbates the problem. 'The employers are biased - not all of them, but some. Studies show that if you say your name is Mohammed and you come from Clichy-sous-Bois, you're less likely to get a job than if you say your name is François and you come from Paris,' she says. Just how bad things are for ethnic minorities in France is impossible to say - because it's illegal to try to find out. 'There is no ethnic monitoring in France, because it is forbidden by the constitution,' Deer explains. 'It's a deeply republican, ingrained thing: all citizens should be regarded as equal.' And, just as in the US, social programmes targeting minorities have been strongly resisted: money is distributed to particular areas of the city, rather than channelled to the most deprived social groups. When the elite Parisian university Sciences Po began an outreach programme to schools in poor areas, some of the objections came from students themselves, who said the value of their own achievement in winning a place at the college would be devalued. Britain has looked smugly across the Channel over the past fortnight or so, but Dumas says it has little reason to expect to avoid the tensions France is facing over the coming years. The violent clashes and suffering of the miners' strike in the early Eighties showed how furiously workers react when their livelihood is swept away by the tides of global competition. Preparing Europe to cope with the exigencies of globalisation - the 'China Challenge', as Gordon Brown likes to call it - was at the top of the agenda when European leaders met at Hampton Court this month. Sweeping away agricultural protection and using the cash to increase skills, invest in technology and so on may be part of the solution, as Britain argues. But the teenagers from the Parisian banlieues were sending a powerful reminder that European governments will also have to think about those who are left behind.

title: Millions of French investors buy shares in €7bn flotation of EDF
bodyText: The French flocked in record numbers yesterday to buy shares in EDF, the state-owned energy group that is raising €7bn (£4.8bn) through an initial float in the Paris market's biggest ever operation. More than 4.85 million small investors, enticed by the prospect of rapid gains when EDF stock begins trading on Monday, ordered shares - more than the 4.5 million British "Sids" who bought into British Gas in 1986, the 3.9 million who took part in France Telecom's float in 1997, and the 3.3 million who invested in the French gas group GDF earlier this year. But analysts warned that the government, which is selling off 15% of EDF's equity, had priced the shares too high, at €33 for institutions and €32 for retail investors and EDF's current and former workforce. Thierry Breton, finance minister, who set the final share price towards the upper end of the initial range of €29.50 to €34.10, was forced to raise the allocation of shares for small investors to 60% in the face of wary interest among institutions. But he said: "With current and retired employees, we think that there are more than 5 million French citizens who will be owners of part of the capital of EDF. This is a very big, popular success." Analysts said shares in EDF would fare worse than those in GDF, which rose 23% on its first day of trading in July but institutions are expected to buy the stock as the French energy group will automatically join the CAC40 index of top shares. They are, nevertheless, suspicious of the influence the French government will retain over EDF's business, pointing to the decision to slash a proposed price rise by GDF from 12% to 3.8%. They are also concerned about the power exercised within the group by unions, which have staged a series of strikes against the part-privatisation and have threatened to paralyse the French rail network on Monday in protest at supposed plans to float part of SNCF. Ministers deny that any such plans exist. The float values EDF, which has extensive interests in Britain, including some 5 million domestic customers, at about €60bn, enabling it to overtake Germany's E.ON as Europe's biggest energy company. The French group plans to use the €7bn raised to finance the €40bn it is investing in Europe, particularly France, over the next few years. Part of this is likely to be spent on acquisitions within the EU as the power market becomes fully liberalised.

title: Brown blames France for the deadlock in world trade talks
bodyText: Gordon Brown last night launched a fresh attempt to revive struggling global trade liberalisation talks with a strong attack on France for its refusal to accept greater reform of the common agricultural policy as a way to end the deadlock between rich and poor countries. The chancellor said France was wrong to argue that a solution to world poverty would not be helped by big cuts in Europe's support for its farmers and warned that the CAP presented a barrier to faster European growth. At the start of a week of talks that will shape the outcome of a ministerial meeting of the World Trade Organisation in Hong Kong next month, Mr Brown used an awards ceremony in London to say that the willingness to reform agriculture was a test of Europe's appetite for modernisation "across the board". "It is simply wrong to say that tariffs are essential to advanced industrial societies and wrong to say that big cuts in farming tariffs would not help a solution to poverty," Mr Brown said in direct response to an article in yesterday's Financial Times from France's trade minister, Christine Lagarde. France is threatening to veto any deal in Hong Kong that goes beyond the already-agreed cuts in the CAP, and EU foreign ministers said yesterday said they would closely monitor the performance of Europe's trade commissioner, Peter Mandelson, at the WTO summit. They plan to meet him in a "special session" during the summit and offer him "necessary guidance". Talks are taking place in Brussels and Geneva this week in the hope that concessions from rich countries on agriculture will lead to moves from the leading developing countries, such as Brazil, on manufactured trade. The chancellor added that "failing to break the deadlock will mean a huge price - for reform of Europe, for prices for consumers, for our competitiveness, and for the world's poor." A comprehensive trade deal in Hong Kong is seen by Britain as a significant objective for its twin presidencies of the G8 and EU in 2005. He said Britain would use a meeting of G7 finance ministers early next month to press for ambitious agricultural reform. "The EU has the power to act. Our presidency of the G7 and the EU gives us the chance to help move that reform forward." With Jack Straw engaged in discussions over the EU's budget, Mr Brown made it clear that there could be no concessions on Britain's rebate without more radical reform of the CAP. "What better signal could Europe send of its commitment to wider economic reform and our willingness to rise to the challenges of globalisation than tackling wasteful subsidies that consume 40% of the European budget even at a time when agriculture accounts for just 2% of the European economy?" Mr Mandelson was accused yesterday of practising "deceit" and of being a "liar" for promoting development in public as at the heart of the current round of trade talks while promoting corporate interests in the actual WTO negotiations. The allegations were strongly denied by Mr Mandelson's aides, who said he was committed to a deal in Hong Kong that would help poor countries. Seattle to Brussels, a pan-European network of non-governmental organisations active in trade issues, said in a 36-page report that corporate lobbyists and business groups had extraordinary, hidden and unregulated access to the European commission - and dictated its policy on agriculture, industry and services. John Hilary, the head of campaigns and policy at War on Want, said that Mr Mandelson's proposals on opening up markets would wipe out companies in many poor countries. "Our message to him is that he's talked a lot about a development agenda but all of his actions are a complete deceit and lie."

title: Comet owner offers cold comfort for Christmas
bodyText: Falling prices and tough competition in France will hit this year's profits at the electrical chain Kesa, which also owns Comet in Britain. Kesa, which was spun out of Kingfisher two years ago, yesterday described market conditions as "very difficult" and the chief executive, Jean-Noël Labroue, said it was impossible to predict how Christmas sales might turn out. He said: "Any sales growth will be in flat-screen televisions and new technologies rather than traditional white, brown and furniture goods." But flat-screen TVs and items such as portable music players generate lower profit margins than other products. Analysts, who have already shaved their profits forecasts for the group several times this year, cut back their expectations again. David Stoddart, at Teather & Greenwood, reduced his forecast from £176m to £156m while Rhys Williams, at Seymour Pierce, cut his from £169m to £154m. It was the fourth time the broker has trimmed its forecasts after originally expecting more than £200m. The shares - which were more than 320p at the start of the year - fell 5.25p to 245.25p. Kesa's biggest problem is in its French businesses - the electrical chain Darty and furniture business BUT. In a third-quarter trading update, Kesa said like-for-like sales at Darty were ahead just 0.2%. That represents an abrupt slowdown from the 1.8% growth of the first half - and Kesa confirmed the growth was from lower margin new technology. The group's other French unit, the electrical and furniture chain BUT, had what one analyst called "a disastrous period" with sales growth of 3.1% in the first half becoming a 4.3% downturn in the three months to the end of October. Kesa said BUT would make lower second-half profits than last year, blaming tough market conditions together with the recent French riots and "a more aggressive competitive environment". At Comet, sales were down 3.1% but that was an improvement on first-half trade. Its performance is in line with its rival DSG, which this week announced a 3% drop at Currys and a 2% dip at Dixons. Meanwhile, one of the few retailers defying the gloomy high street conditions - the car parts and bikes chain Halfords - yesterday produced an increase in first-half profits built on rising sales. The chain had posted a 2.6% increase in first-half sales in an update last month, and yesterday said trade had since "strengthened further". Profit rose to £40.4m in the 26 weeks to the end of September, up from £33.5m a year earlier.

title: France and Poland lead assault on Blair EU budget plan
bodyText: France and Poland spearheaded an assault last night on Britain's plans to cut the EU budget by £16bn as part of a deal that would preserve most of the British rebate and slash spending in eastern Europe. Setting the scene for a bruising 10-day battle before next week's EU summit, the two countries on either side of Europe branded the British budget proposal as "unacceptable". Philippe Douste-Blazy, the French foreign minister, accused Britain of failing to offer "substantial and lasting reform" of its rebate. "These proposals do not seem to be of a nature to lead to the agreement for which we all wish," he said. Kazimierz Marcinkiewicz, the Polish prime minister, underlined the deep unease in eastern Europe at Britain's plan to slash their structural funds by £9.3bn. "The proposal is not based on solidarity," he said. "In this form it is unacceptable." In a sign of Britain's isolation, the European commission's president, Jose Manuel Barroso, added his voice."This proposal amounts to a budget for a 'mini-Europe', not the strong Europe we need." The attacks were launched minutes after Jack Straw, the foreign secretary, unveiled Britain's plans to trim the EU budget for 2007-13 from £580bn to £564bn. In a move which enraged the French, Britain proposed cutting its own rebate by £5.33bn over the seven-year budget period. Under the last budget proposal, which France accepted in June, the rebate would have been cut by £12bn. Britain proposes to make the more modest cuts in one of two ways: increasing Britain's VAT contributions to the EU or removing the rebate from the calculations of a proportion of non-farm spending in the new member states. With the overall budget increasing - after the addition of a total of 12 new members by the start of 2007 - this will mean Britain's rebate will actually increase by 40% from an average of £3.3bn to £4.6bn a year. France took exception to the more modest cuts because of the second major plank of the British proposal: that structural spending should be cut in the new member states by £9.3bn. France accused Britain of failing to pay for the EU's enlargement. Britain hopes to win round the new member states by proposing four measures that will make it easier for them to spend the £100bn they will be offered in structural funds. These are: cutting the level of cofinancing the new members will have to provide from 20% to 15%; allowing private funds to be included in co-financing; giving an extra year to spend the money; and allowing inner-city housing to be funded. Mr Straw said: "This is an enormous amount. The total funds for the new member states is twice the amount of the Marshall Fund that rebuilt western Europe 60 years ago." Tackling head-on the French claim that Britain is not paying its fair share of enlargement, he added: "The UK government recognises its responsibility... We are prepared to pay our fair share but no more than a fair share." Britain, which had braced itself for a hostile reaction, hopes to secure a deal by pledging to cut the contributions of rich countries, such as Germany, and by ensuring that countries of a similar size pay about the same amount. Britain would pay about 0.41% of its annual income in net contributions to the EU compared with 0.4% by France. Under the June proposal Britain would have paid 0.5%, while France paid 0.39%. The Tories last night attacked the government for abandoning part of the rebate without securing a cut in farm subsidies. But Britain is proposing a major review of EU spending in 2008. The reaction was not universally hostile. Jean-Claude Juncker, the Luxembourg prime minister who led the last round of budget negotiations, said the proposals could form the basis of a deal if Britain moved further on the rebate. Jiri Paroubek, the Czech prime minister, described the proposals as realistic and worth discussing.

title: Blair risks French anger on EU budget deal
bodyText: Tony Blair will today seek to pull off one of the biggest negotiating deals of his premiership as he tries to persuade the European summit in Brussels to back a revised compromise on the stalled EU budget that would spread a little more pain and gain among the member states. Downing Street admits that what it is proposing is not "everyone's ideal deal". But it is realistic - "not never never land" said one official - and far more likely to deliver "faster and more effective funds" to hard-pressed communities than more utopian options. Initial reaction was hostile to the proposals, with the French government and the European Commission president, Jose Manuel Barroso, calling for more concessions and the Polish prime minister threatening to use his veto. The personal animosity which flares between Mr Blair and the French president, Jacques Chirac, could tilt the summit either way, with both men hoping that Germany's new conservative chancellor, Angela Merkel, will side with them at the crucial moment. That moment could come over Britain's demand for a "review" of farm subsidies paid under the Common Agricultural Policy in the middle of the 2007-13 budget period, almost certainly to France's detriment, as a crucial trade-off to placate British Eurosceptics. The power of the EU, US and Japanese farm lobbies, which also threaten this week's world trade talks in Hong Kong, are as symbolically important to France as the 1984 established rebate is to Britain. After Whitehall published the UK presidency's latest formula yesterday, complete with a modest £1bn concession to promote economic development among the EU's new East European partners, most initial reaction was predictably hostile ahead of the endgame.The French government and Mr Barroso signalled that Britain would have to make further concessions on the UK's rebate when the summit convenes this evening. Kazimierz Marcinkiewicz, Poland's new rightwing prime minister, who leads a Eurosceptic government, went further. "The proposal, if it remains as it is now, will be vetoed by Poland. It does not satisfy us in any way," he said. But it was an open secret that yesterday's negotiating concessions to the new member states - and technical changes to ease the burden on rich states such as Germany, notably on the way VAT payments are calculated - would be matched by further British movement on the UK rebate in the Belgian capital. France remains adamant that the UK rebate must be cut by £9.3bn over seven years, rather than the £5.3bn offered by Britain. It is also saying that the changes to the rebate must be made permanent, which Mr Blair will probably accept along with a rebate cut of £6.6 bn, but only in return for a review that will cut bloated farm subsidies before 2013. Yet senior officials in key states remain optimistic that a deal can be achieved by as early as tomorrow night. While protesting that London's latest offer was "insufficient" to clinch the "knife-edge" summit - despite saving Berlin €7bn (more than £4bn) in VAT payments - German officials refused to be gloomy. The mood among EU members is "far more positive" than when the June summit collapsed amid a row between Britain and France. One official said: "We are not just acting in Germany's interests. We are trying to reach a consensus among all states. It isn't clear whether they can all live with it." It was confirmed last night in Paris that Mr Blair will meet Mr Chirac and Mrs Merkel separately after going to Brussels via a session at the EU parliament in Strasbourg this afternoon. France made clear yesterday that Britain had failed to go far enough when Jack Straw, the foreign secretary, unveiled a small increase in the budget, from £564bn to £566bn. The French foreign minister, Philippe Douste-Blazy, told the senate in Paris: "The negotiations will be difficult. The latest British propositions unfortunately do not create a basis for an agreement acceptable by everyone." Warsaw's harsh response leaves Britain battling on two fronts: trying to win back traditional allies in the east, and dealing with the rebate and farm subsidies. Britain says the consequences of failure will hurt everyone. "You could have paralysis for at least a year," one official said. What the UK is offering · An EU budget totalling €849bn (around £500bn) over seven years from 2007-13 · A ceiling on the EU budget of 1.01% of the 25 countries' total income, below the 1.06% proposed by the Luxembourg presidency in June, and the 1.24% sought by the European commission · A rise in Britain's net contribution of €58bn over seven years as the EU economy expands, including an €8bn (£5bn) cut in the UK rebate to help modernise east European states

title: Surge in Germany and France points to eurozone fightback
bodyText: German investor confidence has hit its highest level for two years while French industrial production has surged at its fastest pace in six years, according to figures yesterday that highlighted the growing recovery in the 12-member eurozone. Germany has benefited from a strong world economy which has helped exports. The ZEW institute reported that as a result of this and growing signs that the domestic economy may be picking up, its investor confidence index jumped by 9.4 points this month to 71.0, much higher than economists expected and the second big monthly rise in a row. The result will please new Chancellor Angela Merkel who will be looking for a further boost from this year's World Cup in Germany. The figures increased expectations that the European Central Bank, which raised interest rates last month for the first time in five years, would do so again, though not at its meeting this week. "The ZEW survey was most probably buoyed by strong rises in German equities but probably also reflects rising business confidence and the associated improved investment outlook. [It] will help boost expectations of ECB tightening," said Tom Levinson of ING financial markets. In spite of the growing recovery, ZEW chief Wolfgang Franz, one of the government's so-called Five Wise Men, forecast that the German economy - the eurozone's largest - would grow only about 1.5% this year, though that was up from the 1% growth the institute predicted back in November. Separately, the French statistics office said that industrial production had leapt 3.1% in November from the month before, its best performance since September 1999 and much stronger than expected, helped by a rebound in car production. November's rise left production only 0.1% higher in the three months to November from the same period a year earlier, suggesting it is making little contribution to overall economic growth which the French government is hoping will top 2% this year and help reduce the country's high unemployment rate of nearly 10%. Other data showed the French budget deficit running at €58.6bn (£40bn) in the first 11 months of last year, virtually identical to the same period of 2004 in spite of the government's pledge to reduce its deficit below the bloc's limit of 3% of gross domestic product. Economists say Paris is not serious enough about cutting spending to reduce the deficit.

title: Sans courage
bodyText: The riots in the French suburbs late last year, mainly among disaffected and unemployed second generation immigrant youth, helped bring a new focus to the government of Dominique de Villepin in the run-up to next year's presidential election. "Rupture", or breaking with the past - the buzzword of de Villepin's arch-rival on the right, Nicholas Sarkozy, the interior minister and leader of the UMP ruling party - is the new rallying cry. This week de Villepin called for a radical overhaul of France's labour market in an effort to drag the country into the 21st century and tackle persistently high levels of unemployment, currently at just under 10%, or more than twice the UK level, but falling. Specifically, he set out new plans to combat youth unemployment - 23% among people under 26 - and encourage workers over the age 57 to stay in the labour market. His plans, predictably, have unleashed a ferocious debate and fierce reaction among trade unions, with the communist-led CGT organising a "mobilisation" on January 31. Even this tentative step towards "flexibility" has provoked outrage at an alien "Anglo-Saxon" concept. On the same night as de Villepin set out his plans in the company of five senior cabinet ministers, Ségolène Royal - the governor of Poitou-Charentes region and the likely socialist candidate to replace Jacques Chirac denounced their effect of raising "précarité" - precariousness or insecurity - among the workforce. Her live-in partner and rival candidate of the left, Francois Hollande, did likewise. France, a deeply conservative country in the throes of a collective identity crisis, appears incapable of anticipating change without screaming about insecurity. Yet de Villepin, who has made reducing unemployment the central task of his government, was among the first to introduce the Nordic concept of "flexicurity" into the French lexicon, and French ministers will be among the 25 EU employment ministers meeting in Austria this weekend to exchange views on how to dovetail the social safety net with the flexibility required by businesses under globalisation. Flexicurity derives from the labour market reforms undertaken in Denmark in the 1990s that halved unemployment in five years. These combine high mobility between jobs, with a third of the workforce switching jobs each year, low job security and high rates of unemployment benefit. Danish ministers have called them a "golden triangle" of labour market flexibility - easy hire and fire, high social security and active employment policies including education and training. De Villepin's version of flexicurity is to extend an existing programme to encourage small firms with fewer than 20 employees to take on more staff to all companies, and to limit the new rules to the young unemployed. The initial scheme (CNE) has seen 280,000 people taken on under a two-year "contract" that allows employers to fire them at will, but forces them to offer long-term employment after two years. The new scheme (CPE) applies to the jobless under 26 who take between eight and 11 years in France to find a permanent job, compared with three to five years in most of Europe. Firms offering long-term positions after two years will be given three-year tax breaks, while companies that employ more than 250 people will have to make participants in scheme 1% of their workforce from January next year rising to 3% by the start of 2008. Companies are also being encouraged to take on people aged 57 or over and offer them 18-month contracts that can be renewed once. De Villepin has not dismissed out of hand suggestions that such measures, which are common fodder in British (and Nordic) corporate life, could foster endemic "fears" in French society, but, he says, such fears of "précarité" are "not always real but fed" and he has signalled further changes to France's labour laws. Bernard Thibault, the CGT leader, has labelled the plans "a new year's gift" to the employer's body, Medef, and an assault on long-standing collective rights in favour of "a fools' market". Unions as a whole are demanding more active macro-economic policies - code for extra state spending, and "rupture" is proving as tough to achieve for de Villepin as he probably imagined. But his plans prompted Le Figaro, the conservative daily, to quote Sir William Beveridge, architect of Britain's welfare state, on how the great evil of unemployment can generate hatred and fear - precisely the experience of Aulnay-sous-Bois and other French suburbs. France, a home of revolution, is being offered a timid reform, and its running scared. · David Gow is the Guardian's European business editor, based in Brussels

title: French agree to share aircraft carrier costs
bodyText: Britain and France agreed last night to share the initial costs of new aircraft carriers in a move described by defence secretary John Reid as "hugely significant". The agreement, whereby France would pay up to a third of the £300m design assessment costs for the ships, was also heralded by the French defence minister, Michèle Alliot-Marie, as a symbolic step that was "essential for Europe". France wants to build one carrier, Britain two. The French firm Thales will work on both projects, with British arms manufacturer BAE Systems a partner in the British programme.

title: France ferments wine revolution
bodyText: Some time this year, wine consumers in the UK will come across a new French label that will directly challenge the new world wines that have lured consumers away from old Europe. Chamarré - French for "bursting with colours" - is the product of a company called OVS that has taken on board the success of wines from Australia, Latin America and the US. For OVS president Pascal Renaudat, Charmarré is France's riposte to new world brands such as Jacob's Creek and Hardy's. Mr Renaudat, who has had 20 years in the wine business, told Guardian Unlimited: "We have to simplify our product and reject an arrogant approach that was perhaps natural to us. It is important to produce wine that corresponds to what people want to drink and at a good price." With that in mind Chamarré wines will sell for between £4.99 and £6.99, the price range where new world wines have made the biggest inroads. This is the mass market where French wines have felt the squeeze in the UK in recent years. The fact is, France still holds the largest share of UK wine imports. According to figures from HM Revenue & Customs, France in 2004 took 20.6% compared with Australia's 17.3%, Italy's 11.1%, America's 10.7%, South Africa's 8.3%, Spain's 8.3% and Chile's 6.1%. But the problem for France is that it is losing ground in a growing market. Apart from Spain, all of France's competitors increased sales in 2004. OVS hopes to reverse the trend by ditching the French love affair with the "appellation" system, with its separate label requirements and vinification rules for each of nearly 450 wine-growing regions. The appellation system is baffling to most French people, never mind foreigners. "This is not wine for connoisseurs. It is for pleasure," Mr Renaudat explained. Seeking to demystify and simplify complex wine labels, the French government agencies already encourage producers of non-AOC (vins d'appellation d'origine controllee - quality wines produced under strictly controlled regional designations) wines to mention the name of the varietals (for instance, shiraz and sauvignon blanc), a common practice among their new world counterparts. Chamarré takes the process of simplification further. For the moment, the brand will produce three lines. The first blends two varietals based around one dominant type, the second is a line of blends from several French regions and finally there is an AOC line. "It's time to get rid of the stuffy pretentiousness that surrounds French wine," said Renaud Rosari, Chamarré's master wine maker. "Chamarre´ is about bringing our wines to life for the consumers - the brand is lively, uncomplicated and approachable and means consistently high quality wines, with the fresh easy drinking style customers are looking for." To get Chamarré off the ground, OVS had to persuade seven of France's largest wine cooperatives to join forces - not an easy task. But they will benefit from the combined marketing clout of OVS pushing one single brand, which Mr Renaudat believes is the key to success in a brand-dominated market. A lot is at stake for the French wine industry. The sector employs 300,000 people and is worth €5.7bn (£3.9bn) a year. Last year, for the first time, Brussels agreed to a French demand to convert 1.5m hectolitres of quality wine into industrial alcohol in response to a large drop in wine prices - up to 30% in some cases - caused, in large part, by overproduction. Unlike previous years when "crisis distillation" was used to lower the European lake of cheap table wine, this time AOC wines were boiled into industrial alcohol. Part of the problem is declining domestic consumption, which has forced producers to turn increasingly outwards. Jamie Goode of wineanorak.com credits OVS for taking a brave commercial decision and believes that the company could make a go of it - provided quality is good and reasonably consistent. "The trouble is that everybody is doing it," Mr Goode said. "Access to market is key. You need to get into the supermarkets, but you need to have a strong brand with which to negotiate or else they will savage you on price." OVS is currently negotiating with the supermarkets and expects to have Chamarré on the shelves in the next few months. That will be the first big test for Chamarré. If the brand proves to be a success in the UK, other French producers will be clambering to come under the OVS umbrella.

title: Chirac denies racism charge over Mittal
bodyText: The French president, Jacques Chirac, rejected charges today of racism in European reaction to Mittal Steel's bid for its Luxembourg-based rival, Arcelor. "In principle, we have absolutely nothing against a non-European taking over a European company," Mr Chirac said, during a visit to India to boost economic cooperation between Paris and Delhi. The French leader told reporters that European officials were still waiting for full details of the bid before making any final decisions. "We were given no reasons for this bid, we have no notions of what intentions, or what lies ahead for the company in question," he said at a joint press conference with the Indian prime minister, Manmohan Singh. "We're simply standing by waiting for explanations to be given." The governments of Luxembourg and Spain have opposed the €18.6bn (£12.7bn) bid - which would create the world's largest steel firm - while several top French officials have raised fears of job losses in France and elsewhere in Europe. Arcelor's French chief executive Guy Dolle, has compared his company's steel to perfume and Mittal's to mere eau de cologne, raising hackles in India. The Indian trade minister, Kamal Nath, has described European reaction to the Mittal bid as "xenophobia". In a letter to the European trade commissioner, Peter Mandelson, he said the reaction of certain countries against the Mittal bid went against the spirit of the World Trade Organisation. Mittal, the world's largest steel maker, is run by Indian-born Lakshmi Mittal, one of the world's richest men. Born in India, Mr Mittal, 55, now lives in Britain. His company has its headquarters in the Netherlands and operations spanning the world, from Indonesia and Kazakhstan to Trinidad and Tobago. Arcelor dismissed a call by a hedge fund known for its active participation in bid situations to open talks with Mittal. Atticus Capital said it had written to Arcelor's chief executive to express dissatisfaction with the company's refusal to consider Mittal's bid. "For us nothing has changed. There is not a good industrial plan, and our board of directors will only be in a position to consider a bid by Mittal if it is made in cash only," Arcelor said of the letter. During his visit, Mr Chirac signed an agreement on future civilian nuclear cooperation, and a state-owned Indian airline finalised a deal to buy 43 Airbus jets for €2bn.

title: Chirac denies steel bid racism
bodyText: Jacques Chirac, the French president, yesterday denied allegations of racism surrounding the hostile £13bn bid by the world's largest steelmaker Mittal for European rival Arcelor. Manmohan Singh, the Indian prime minister, has strongly condemned some of the criticism of the deal as "xenophobic". Asked about the allegations of racism Mr Chirac, who is in the Indian capital to sign trade and defence deals, said his country had "absolutely nothing against a non-European taking over a European company". Although France is not a shareholder in Arcelor, the company has 30,000 French employees. Chairman Lakshmi Mittal has an Indian passport, but his company operates from London. He has seen his empire spread across the world - from Indonesia and Kazakhstan to Trinidad and Tobago. With China's insatiable demand pushing up steel prices, Mr Mittal is now worth £15bn. Mr Mittal, in an interview with India's Economic Times, pointed out that India has welcomed foreign capital and contrasted this with France's attitude to his bid. "If this deal faces roadblocks, it will frustrate many Indian businessmen, especially those looking at Europe to invest." Mr Mittal has played down opposition to the deal, telling New Delhi TV that he "personally had not faced any racism. The way it has been portrayed and the way comments have been made by politicians, it can be construed as a racist feeling ... I am sad about that."

title: Suez boasts big jump in profit as Enel gathers forces for bid
bodyText: Suez, the French energy and water group planning a controversial €70bn (£48bn) merger with Gaz de France, yesterday criticised a possible counter-bid from Enel, accusing the Italian group of wanting to dismantle its prey. Italian ministers and banks lined up behind an all-out hostile bid worth €50bn if Suez refused to sell Electrabel, its Belgian electricity and gas unit. Gérard Mestrallet, Suez's chief executive, said Electrabel was not for sale. Armed with a 48% jump in net profits last year to €2.5bn, Mr Mestrallet made plain that the two French groups saw huge growth and earnings potential in their merger but accepted the Suez board and shareholders would have to consider an alternative approach. Insiders claimed Enel would be prepared to break up the entire group and put at risk 140,000 jobs and millions of customers for the sake of acquiring Electrabel and its 20,000 staff. They said an Enel bid would be financially risky. Other sources argued that the markets, which marked down Suez shares after a bull run, considered an Italian counter-proposal neither feasible nor desirable. Asked if he had had even informal approaches from Fulvio Conti, Enel's chief executive, Mr Mestrallet told the Guardian: "I don't know him and I've never heard from him. I've never seen him at any of the international energy meetings we all attend." EU competition commissioner Neelie Kroes indicated in Brussels that, following a spate of energy mergers, she wanted radical changes to break up their control over the supply chain. Mr Mestrallet said: "Suez and Gaz de France will be the most European and international utility of all the EU players ... France represents only 25% of our business while Italy is 95% of Enel's." He plans to meet Ms Kroes this month to argue for the merger to go ahead in mid-April. He indicated Suez would be willing to make disposals, notably in Belgium, to secure approval. Presenting a 10% rise in pre-tax profits to €6.5bn and a 25% dividend rise with a promise of special payouts if the merger goes ahead, he said Suez had no plans to dispose of core operations such as water. He rejected Italian complaints that the French state had breached EU rules in engineering the deal. "I first discussed the deal two years ago and the French government announced it because it has to change the law [requiring its holding in GDF to be no less than 70%]." Separately, E.ON, the German utility which launched a €23bn bid for Spain's Endesa last month, declared an 8% rise in 2005 pre-tax profits to €7.3bn.

title: Enel cools on Gaz de France bid
bodyText: Enel, the Italian energy group, is preparing to withdraw from its planned €50bn (£35bn) bid for the French utility group Suez. This would break up the proposed marriage of Suez with Gaz de France, a deal that has provoked a political row over protectionism. Sources close to Enel in Rome indicated yesterday that the launch of the long-mooted counter-bid, initially planned for this weekend, had been postponed ahead of talks with the EU competition commissioner, Neelie Kroes, next week. She is to see Fulvio Conti, Enel chief executive, on Tuesday and the heads of Suez and Gaz de France on Monday. Enel could be offered wider access to the French power market, including parts of the state-owned Electricité de France's business, ahead of full-scale liberalisation in mid-2007 as Ms Kroes threatens to "unbundle" vertically integrated energy groups. She said this week she favoured European-wide competitors rather than dominant national players. "They don't want to clash with the French government and are trying to find a solution that avoids this," the Rome sources said. Enel, which presents its annual results in London on Thursday, has also been caught up in the Italian general election campaign. Other sources suggested that Enel was still looking for a partner to snap up Suez's extensive water and waste interests, worth up to €20bn, before launching a bid. Veolia, the French water group, pulled out of a joint bid under direct pressure from President Jacques Chirac.

title: Dole blues
bodyText: Up to 300,000 students demonstrated throughout France yesterday against a government measure designed to tackle the biggest scourge of the national economy - the 23% unemployment rate among young people, the highest in Europe. And that's the official rate; in the "banlieues" that rioters set on fire late last year, it is 50%. It's like a reprise of the May 1968 protests. The CRS riot police cruise the streets of the Latin Quarter in Paris, breaking up groups of protesters, and storm the Sorbonne where students staged a three-day occupation last week. Between 51 and 64 of France's 82 universities are now disrupted by the wave of angry protests. Dominique de Villepin, the increasingly unpopular prime minister in a stand-off with Nicholas Sarkozy, the interior minister, over the rightwing candidacy for next year's presidential elections, has nailed his colours to the mast. He plans to see off the youthful demonstrators and avoid the fate of one of his predecessors, Alain Juppé, whose government fell in 1995 when street protests undermined his economic reforms. De Villepin told Paris Match his sole regret in introducing the "first employment contract" (CPE) - the decree at the centre of the protests - was that he had forced the issue too fast, without consultation with either unions or employers. But he added: "Now, we have to explain and convince. And I will do it right to the end because I believe in this measure." The CPE, based on a similar scheme among small and medium-sized firms (SMEs) that was introduced seamlessly, allows all companies to hire employees under 26 for a trial two-year period, during which they can be fired at any time. To sweeten the pill, former employees can claim unemployment benefit after four months rather than on the current basis of working for six out of 22 months. The government has drawn on the successful Danish model of "flexicurity" for its own scheme - one that combines high levels of unemployment benefit (90% of previous pay) with ease of hire and fire. And it is easy to see why the model is attractive to a country in which the powerful trade union movement, although dwindling, has succeeded in protecting those in work at the expense of those kept outside the labour market. French unemployment overall is 9.6% but this official figure masks a much deeper problem. Only a third of people aged 50 or more are in full-time employment so "work" has become the preserve of the 30- to 50-year-olds. De Villepin sees his scheme as a first step to solving this conundrum, pointing out that young French people take twice as long to find a stable job on graduating - eight to 11 years - as those elsewhere in the EU. He insists, moreover, that it has little or none of the punitive aspects of labour reforms in neighbouring Germany which have cut unemployment and social security benefits and forced people into accepting low-paid jobs. The French scheme at least guarantees pay levels agreed through collective bargaining. But his problem is that in France, as in Germany, companies have been making record earnings (up 30% last year) while cutting labour costs. Helmut Panke, BMW chief executive, said this week that the top German companies in the Dax-30 had shed 300,000 jobs in the last five years - mainly to recover the competitiveness lost when the Deutsche Mark entered the euro at an over-valued rate and to restore their position as the world's leading exporter. BMW has, meanwhile, taken on 12,000 staff in Germany in the same period - largely through measures which have driven up productivity and flexibility in return for security of employment. German companies have also circumvented national collective bargaining agreements and secured deals with workforce and unions tailored to their local or regional needs. This option is less readily available in France, where the code du travail, or employment code, weighs 2.5 kilos and has grown by more than 800 pages in 40 years, according to Laurence Parisot, the woman heading the employers' body, the Medef. She has been a stern critic of the CPE even though the Medef backs it overall, her reserves about the scheme arising largely because it covers all youth, regardless of professional qualification or educational attainment. De Villepin, whose ministers have proven reluctant to back his scheme in public, refusing to go on primetime current affairs TV programmes, for instance, has hit the enduring paradox of French politics: the popular demand for change without change, or revolution without reform. The reaction to his scheme, mirroring the populist hostility to foreign takeovers in a country where one in seven works for an overseas firm and almost half the capital tied up in the Cac-40 index of leading companies is foreign, is profoundly conservative. There is another problem. French growth rates - only 1.4% last year compared with the government target of 2.5% - are too low to create employment in the wake of improved investment and increased consumer spending: an even deeper problem across the Rhine. Only by encouraging companies to take on more labour can this poor level of growth be improved. But the prospects for resumed growth have been rendered more insecure because of the decisions of another French leader - Jean-Claude Trichet, president of the European Central Bank - who is set on a course of raising the cost of borrowing to 3% by the end of the year to stamp out a nonexistent inflationary risk. If de Villepin stays the course and sees his scheme bear fruit, he may well find its harvest diminished by the monetarists in Frankfurt, over whom he has no control.

title: French hopping mad over lazy frog jibe
bodyText: A sense of humour was holding - just - on both sides of the Channel yesterday, after a budget airline from Yorkshire posted a website cartoon of a French frog blocking a runway with a placard reading "I am lazy". The jibe was prompted by widespread disruption of British flights in France, culminating in British passengers chanting "Rosbifs want to go home" for two hours when their plane was marooned at Chambéry in the Alps. "It seems to me that either the air traffic controllers or the students run France at the moment," said Philip Meeson, chief executive of Jet2.Com, which has played on a no-nonsense, plain-spoken local image to build a customer base at Leeds-Bradford airport. "The lazy frog is a bit of fun, but I am very disappointed that the French police stood by and let the students disrupt this flight by blocking the runway, and force another to divert because it couldn't land." Mr Meeson let fly on his website with a lecture on industrial relations when the student protest on new youth employment laws coincided with a traffic controllers' dispute. He told the warring parties: "After a token stoppage, why can't you just sort the matter out amicably without bringing thousands of people around the world (who, I would like to add, give your country huge economic wealth) into the argument?" He added: "Pouvez-vous nous expliquer pourquoi exactement êtes-vous en grève ...?" [Could you explain to us exactly why you are on strike?] He said yesterday: "It's not a diplomatic incident, it's the roast beefs and the frogs having our natural little bit of sparring. Our passengers are being inconvenienced and we are entitled to have a little go." Mr Meeson's comments and cartoon, subtitled "A picture speaks a thousand words", drew a dusty response from the French controllers' union, the CGT, whose spokesman said: "It is easy for them to ask some people to go back to work but if people in France do not agree with the government it is a very good reason to strike." But criticism was stronger from Mr Meeson's fellow Brits. Chris Davies, leader of Britain's Liberal Democrat MEPs, said: "This is a stupid and crass remark." A spokeswoman for Jet2.Com said that Mr Meeson loved France and had a holiday home there, although its whereabouts were not being advertised - for obvious reasons.

title: The French go marching into the past
bodyText: Quite why, you might wonder, is a country with wonderful infrastructure, beautiful towns and countryside, world-class companies and highly productive workers tearing itself apart again? Over the past couple of weeks, we've seen millions of French people - students, trade unionists and pretty much any other group you care to mention - protesting on the streets against a small change in labour laws. The protests have been bigger, though less violent, than last summer's riots in many of France's poor suburbs, home to many of its immigrants, which sent TV pictures of burning cars beaming around the world. The reason can be summed up in one word: jobs. It is a problem that has existed for two decades and one that France's ruling elite, to its shame, has failed to do anything about. Mind you, given the scale of the protests whenever they do, maybe that is no surprise. The protesters have been angered by a law introducing a "first job contract" or CPE, which makes it easier for French employers to fire a young worker within his or her first two years. The idea is to make employers less reluctant to hire young people. The protesters see it as an increase in job insecurity. They are also unhappy at the way the prime minister, Dominique de Villepin, forced the law on to the statute books last week without any real discussion. His motivation was clear: to try to do something about France's chronic youth unemployment, which is running at 23%, rising to 50% among the children of immigrants in the suburbs that burnt last summer. Unemployment among the whole population is 9.2%, almost double the rate in Britain. Employment levels are also very low in France, at 64% of the workforce, compared with a record high of 72% in Britain, on internationally standardised numbers. Rigid labour market Why is unemployment so high in France? There are a number of reasons, mostly related to rigidities in the labour market. Non-wage costs are very high, making people expensive to hire. Firing people is difficult and costly. This has made firms over the years more and more reluctant to take people on, preferring instead to hire more machines. This is where the higher productivity of French workers compared with their British counterparts comes from. The French have 80% more capital employed for each worker than the British do. So in many ways high French productivity is the flipside of high unemployment. The French say it is right to have high protection for workers. The trouble with that, says labour market economist John Philpott, is that it leads to a classic "insider-outsider" problem. "There is lots of protection for prime working age males but this tends to exclude other groups, particularly the young," he says. Once on the inside, which is where all the protesting youths want to be, jobs are very comfortable, with a 35-hour week and lots of computers and other capital to work with. These are typically the people who defend the French "social model", looking down on what they see as Britain and America's "Anglo-Saxon" model with its "hire-and-fire" jobs market and long working hours. In fact, Britain's average work week is 37 hours, according to the ONS, the government statistician - that is little different to France's. At the aggregate level, France's model is simply not working. Unemployment is so high that it has depressed wage growth to well below British rates. A social model that provides neither jobs nor rising living standards is not easy to defend. Nor has the French economy grown as fast as Britain's over the past decade, allowing national income per head to slip behind Britain's. As the Guardian revealed on Saturday, the French model has so excluded many young French people that they have come across the channel in huge numbers in recent years looking for work in an economy that has created 2.5 million jobs over the past decade. They have got good jobs and like living in London. The money is better too, they say. They may not have the same level of job security as back home but they have jobs, and that, they say, is what matters. The French are valued workers in London. Many are highly qualified in maths and engineering and have easily found work in the City of London. Thus they contribute to the British economy and pay taxes here rather than at home. It is unfortunate for France that its sclerotic labour market encourages its keen, educated young workers to go abroad in such numbers. Eurostar exodus The flows of French coming to Britain each year outweigh those British people, mostly older or retired, going in the opposite direction. It seems there are as many, if not more, French people living in London than there are British in France. For anyone living in London, this will not come as a surprise. Nearly three-quarters of the 300,000 to 400,000 now estimated to be living here are in Greater London. And most are under 35. It is reminiscent of Norman Tebbit's advice to the unemployed in the 1980s - "On your bike!" The modern equivalent would be "On the Eurostar!", except no one has had to urge these young French people to do it, they have made their own decision to come and look for work, and good for them. This is, after all, what the European Union is about. It is strange, though, how the myth persists in France that the British economy is made up of low-paid, insecure, low-quality jobs. In fact, figures show that more French people work on the minimum wage than British people do. The two are set at broadly similar levels although a key difference is that the French do not have a lower rate for young workers, as we do. Adair Turner, who has just completed a pensions review for the government but was until recently head of its Low Pay Commission, says this has been crucial in keeping youth unemployment high in France. Attempts to introduce a lower rate in the mid-1990s led to riots similar to those we are seeing in France at the moment. The French like to point out that their workers are more productive than the British, but this is not really an answer. There are plenty of economies, especially the United States, but also the Scandinavian countries, that have managed the dream combination of high employment and high productivity. Realising this, Gordon Brown has geared British policy, now that full employment has been more or less achieved, to raising the productivity of the British workforce by raising spending on education and skills and other measures. There is little sign of it yet, unfortunately, but you wouldn't bet against it happening suddenly, rather as it did in the US in the mid-1990s. France's challenge, to boost employment, is a more difficult problem, especially given the mass opposition to the slightest change. It is depressing that the very group that M de Villepin is trying to help are the ones protesting loudest. Clearly he and his predecessors should have explained more clearly what was needed - and the French also need to wake up to what is going on - yet these are the politicians who are now trying to shut out foreign companies from takeovers in France, even though French companies are active in foreign markets, making the most of the opportunities globalisation offers them. But it is not only the politicians who have an attitude problem. Two-thirds of French people in a recent poll said their ambition was to become a civil servant. And only a third of the population think the free market system is the best way for the world in the future, compared with two-thirds in most other countries, including Russia. These are disturbing facts and show that France's problems will not be solved any time soon.

title: Rebels without a job
bodyText: What happens to the French economy now that the government has abandoned the reforms it was proposing to help young people into work? The interesting thing is that the economy was on an upward trajectory before the riots got underway. Consumer spending was increasing quite significantly (by 4.5% over the year) and employment was rising - albeit slowly - while unemployment was declining (though still unacceptably high at 9.6%). At the same time, a buoyant housing market was adding to consumer confidence in a way that could have encouraged more consumer spending. In other words, if the riots hadn't happened, stronger growth might well have led to companies taking on more young people. The government could then have attributed this to its labour reforms, even if it had nothing to do with them. The unanswered question now is whether the outbreak of violence will stunt the economic growth in the pipeline. This was an unusual strike because young people - one in four of whom is unemployed - were voicing opposition to measures that would almost certainly have given them more jobs. The new legislation probably would not have increased economic growth overall, or the total amount of permanent jobs, because it would take an injection of demand for those things to happen. But it would have encouraged employers to take on more youngsters, secure in the knowledge that if lack of growth had made their retention unjustified two years down the line, they would not have been forced to keep them on. When similar labour reforms were introduced in the UK there were comparable fears about sackings two years later. But they haven't materialised - at least not yet - because economic growth increased the demand for labour. Britain is now in a position where many companies are reluctant to get rid of youngsters, especially the more skilled ones, in case they can't rehire them later in a tight labour market. France, make no mistake, is still a successful economy despite lacklustre growth in recent years. It is still wealthier than Britain and has higher productivity. But there is a paradox here. France's higher productivity may be largely explained by the fact that it has more people unemployed so figures about output per person may exaggerate the underlying situation. Britain may have lower productivity than France, but it also has far more people in work - and its average earnings are rising faster. France clearly has a serious cultural problem since, if opinion polls are to be believed, two-thirds would like to be civil servants (where their contribution to the wealth-creation process is debatable) and only a third appear to believe in a market economy. This is not an obvious model for a successful economy in a globalised era when companies are competing against low-wage economies in Asia. It is only common sense that any new company starting up would need to be extra-confident about its prospects in order to employ people who may then be on the payroll for a lifetime. The cultural problem is a long-term one. In the short-term, the problem is that the economy has been suffering from a shortage of demand. The best thing that could happen is that people spend more of their incomes (or else the government does it for them). This would boost demand leading companies to take on more staff - yes, even young people - who in turn would spend more giving companies more incentive to invest for the future. Before the riots reached their height it was - just - possible to see the beginnings of a virtuous circle developing in France. It remains to be seen whether the riots will snuff out the recovery in its infancy. The answer lies in the minds - and pockets - of every French person.

title: Pain and progression
bodyText: "We are presently engaged in a struggle for Europe's soul. That struggle is about whether the (European) commission wins the battle for an open Europe or whether Europe gives into the forces of protectionism, populism and at times xenophobia - which, if they succeed, could undermine the most fundamental achievements of European integration itself." This was Peter Mandelson, EU trade commissioner, ex-cabinet minister and continued Blair confidant, speaking in Brussels the day after the French government humiliatingly withdrew its labour market reforms and Italians voted by the narrowest of possible political margins for a break with the stagnant economy and public debt bequeathed by five years of government under Silvio Berlusconi. Superficially, the events in Paris and Rome point in different directions, with the French proving once more that "manifs" (demos) on the street can defeat liberal economic reforms promoted by a centre-right government and the Italians expressing an incoherent and faltering wish for dragging their society out of the mire. But there are grounds for believing that both these pillars of "old" Europe are embarked on a process of reform and of adaptation to the convulsive changes wrought by globalisation and domestic demography - despite the well-founded basis for Mandy's pessimism. For reasons to do with the sclerotic French political process and his own isolation, Dominique de Villepin, the prime minister, failed in his attempt to bring in a Thatcherite reform of the labour market. The CPE or first job contract was the centre-right's belated answer to the swingeing reforms the former Tory leader began in 1983-84 - albeit tempered with an effort to balance "flexibility" with "security" or an end to the "precariousness" of unemployment and social exclusion suffered by young French people, especially in the suburbs. Barring a miraculous recovery, de Villepin's ambition to succeed Jacques Chirac as president is doomed. But Nicholas Sarkozy, his arch-rival and not only interior minister but leader of the UMP, the presidential majority party, has emerged strengthened from the two-month battle of the streets. Most pertinently, he has signalled that, despite the CPE setback, he wants to press ahead with reforms or "rupture" with the failed past. Under his tutelage, the UMP last night tabled a series of planned measures to tackle the problems, most notably youth unemployment stuck at 24%, that have bedevilled France's recent economic performance. "If we wish to respond to the hopes of the French people, great changes are indispensable," he told Le Figaro. "The CPE gave the idea that young people were being stigmatised. I would not like now that the idea of reform be lost with this unhappy affair." "Sarko" not only wants to continue to address the acute problems of young people with no qualification or skills or with qualifications ill-suited to the modern labour market but to form a legal basis for the "social partners" - unions and employers - to be given a central role in social change before the government decides. Another post-Thatcherite reform would force striking students, employees or public servants to hold secret ballots a week after action began. He also proposes to replace only one out of two retiring civil servants in order to cut public debt. The French economy, despite budget deficit and debt problems, has proved pretty robust, attracting high levels of foreign investment while its own companies expand overseas, whereas the Italian economy expected to be inherited by Romano Prodi has taken over the mantle of "sick man of Europe" from a semi-resurgent Germany. Last year it was in recession, public debt rose to 106.4% of GDP (compared with the eurozone limit of 60%) and the deficit was 4.1% amid warnings it could breach 5percent (compared with the eurozone limit of 3%) this year. Italy has under-performed the EU average growth-rate for 15 years but a series of labour-market reforms under Berlusconi, inspired by Thatcher-Blair, have reduced unemployment - making temporary or part-time jobs available. Its enduring problem, however, is - apart from the dire state of the public finances - that its small and medium-sized firms, the source of innovation and growth, have been hammered by the emerging economies and its biggest firms - think Fiat - have been in a state of semi-collapse with the exception of Finmeccanica, the aerospace giant, Generali, the insurer, and Unicredito bank. Its financial sector has, until recently, been jealously protected from overseas takeovers. Prodi, an economics professor before he became president of the EU commission, has said he will spur reforms by encouraging more mergers and acquisitions and (vaguely) tackling the public finances. But he will undoubtedly be hampered by the unstable nature of his coalition and majority, forcing him during the campaign to concede plans to make it harder for firms to hire temporary staff or put on hold the raising of the retirement age to 60 (sic) in 2008. Nevertheless, his "European" stance is likely to carry him towards a more open economy and, under the pressure of events, reforms. These uneven developments in both France and Italy indicate that Europe, however much one wants to attach the epithets sclerotic or protectionist, is in the throes of change. Taken with the still-incomplete reforms in Germany, the biggest of the "Old Europe" triumvirate, they indicate that Mandy's battle for its soul is far from lost. The populist sirens will scream louder but the gales of change will continue to howl more fiercely.

title: William Keegan: Why French law isn't working
bodyText: Paradoxically, the law that provoked millions of French into protesting on the streets was intended to create employment. Unfortunately for the government in general and prime minister Dominique de Villepin in particular, it was interpreted as having the opposite effect. This is not altogether surprising because, in order to introduce greater 'flexibility' into the labour market and encourage employers to take on more young people, the law was designed to make it easier to fire people. It was to apply to the under-26 age group, among whom unemployment is over 20 per cent, and was seen as having particular urgency after the riots last November in the deprived suburbs of Paris and other cities, where youth unemployment runs at up to 50 per cent. So, a measure devised in response to localised demonstrations led to mass demonstrations all over France. Joseph Heller, of Catch-22 fame, would have been proud of the now-defunct law: 'In order to create employment, we must make it easier to fire people.' The thinking is that employers are reluctant to take on recruits because France's strict employment laws render it a costly and arduous process to make people redundant. The system, it is said, has evolved from one of well-intentioned employees' rights into a cocoon that gives excessive protection to existing employees, systematises inefficiency and effectively discriminates against young, would-be entrants to the labour market. Whatever the theoretical merits of de Villepin's proposal, its attempted execution, without much consultation, was desperately ill-conceived. Far from being accepted as a partial solution, it has focused attention on France's chronic unemployment problem and aggravated discontents. Thus the law, by definition, would have done nothing about the huge degree of protection afforded to employees aged over 26. It added insult to injury for younger members of the workforce in a country where, as the Canadian academic, Timothy B Smith, has written, 'every year, over 500,000 young workers are hired as stagiaires [interns], paid nothing or perhaps up to €200-€300 per month and then dispensed with after a year of full-time labour has been extracted from them.' Smith points out that, since the early 1980s, French labour law, taxation policy, family policy and monetary policy 'have worked to the detriment of young French women and men'. As European economist John Gillingham notes, the 'social exclusion' of (especially) young people, 'far from being either necessary or inevitable', resulted from 'two reversible decisions: to defend the over-valued franc and to protect labour markets. Not globalisation, but rigid protectionist policy- making is what gave rise to les exclus.' Resistance to globalisation has been much cited as a factor behind the dramatic referendum result a year ago, and the recent disturbances. Yet French firms are a major presence on the international business stage and, contrary to the general impression, there is plenty of foreign investment in France, as a recent IMF study noted. Alain Minc, the leading French business consultant, points out that more than half of blue-chip companies on the Paris stock market are foreign-owned. This is not to say France is immune to the 'outsourcing' of manufacturing jobs to developing countries. But, as George Irvin points out, as a counterpart of losses of manufacturing jobs, the growth of services has been constrained in core eurozone countries both by a lack of consumer confidence and by over-regulation and the high cost of low-skilled labour. This said, Irvin rightly emphasises that 'supply-side rigidities such as inflexible labour markets do not appear to be nearly as important as the Keynesian notion of insufficient aggregate demand in explaining the eurozone's inability to increase income and employment'. This accords with a theme of this column over many years. We have followed the 'franc fort' economic policy instituted after the collapse of François Mitterrand's experiments (1981-83) with 'Keynesianism in one country' and the way that there grew up a powerful political consensus that the solution to all difficulties was (in Mitterrand's slogan) 'always more Europe'. More Europe meant, alas, the restrictive economic policies of 'le franc fort', the Delors report and the single currency itself. The struggle between 'insiders' and 'outsiders' in the labour market is heavily influenced by over two decades of economic policies that have been in strong contrast to the more expansionist policies in the US and UK. Nevertheless, as Smith damningly concludes: 'The French model itself is the leading cause of the current economic and social crisis. It has served not to diminish inequality and unemployment but, rather, to institutionalise these scourges on both class and racial lines.' Incidentally, while witnessing the demonstrations, I have been reliving the three-day week and the British industrial troubles of the 1970s in an advance copy of Challenge to Democracy by Ronald McIntosh. As director-general of the National Economic Development Office, he had a ringside seat when many in the economic establishment were near to despair. There is a myth that the economy was rescued by Thatcherism and one hears talk that France needs its own Thatcher. But hers was a political creed that brought problems of its own. It has been sad to see de Villepin mishandle this crisis so badly. Former president Valery Giscard d'Estaing was scathing: 'The enemies of France have viewed these images with delight and her friends with consternation. It is high time to get out of this quagmire.' One thing we know for certain: there is no French saviour waiting, like De Gaulle, to be called from Colombey-les-Deux Eglises.

title: 'When the plant was built  there really was no debate'
bodyText: David Lasalle chuckled as he reeled in his fishing line after lunch yesterday and tossed a chunky sea bass into his coolbox. "Grilled sea bass for dinner," he boasted to fellow fishermen. But his favourite meal comes with a twist - Mr Lasalle fishes outside France's largest civil nuclear power plant, where warm water from the cooling towers attracts a steady supply of fat and sleepy bass. "This is a great place to fish," he said as he cast his line into the waters running into the English Channel at Gravelines, half way between Calais and Dunkirk. "The water is 10C degrees warmer than normal, which means there is a great supply of sea bass." He dismissed fears about fishing in potentially irradiated waters. "I have been fishing here for 10 years and catch excellent sea bass. I am absolutely confident that this is safe." Mr Lasalle epitomises France's attitude to nuclear power. Unlike Britain, where Tony Blair faces major opposition to the extension of nuclear power, France veers between indifference and enthusiasm for the world's most controversial source of energy. With few natural resources of its own, France decided after the 1973 oil crisis to embark on the world's biggest programme of civil nuclear power plants. France now has 56 nuclear reactors. Built by Areva, the state utility company which is offering to come to Britain's rescue, the plants generate up to 80% of France's electricity supplies. In the early 1970s, the government slogan "France doesn't have oil, but it has ideas," persuaded the French that nuclear power would make the country less dependent on foreign energy. People living in the shadow of the Gravelines plant, whose six reactors generate about 8% of France's electricity needs, still accept this bargain. "Why should we be scared?" asked Patrick Farasyn, as he pushed his children on the swing in his garden a few hundred metres from the plant. "There are risks wherever you live." His son, Jeremy, 11, enjoys the cachet that goes with living in the house that is closest to the plant. "There might be an explosion," he joked as he played football with a friend. The plant dominates the picturesque town, whose cobbled streets and 18th-century walls make it a popular tourist destination. "The first thing visitors want to see are the walls and then they ask about the nuclear plant," said Dolores Thery, who runs the town's tourist office. "Sadly, they can't visit it any longer because the plant was closed to visitors for security reasons after the September 11 attacks in New York." The open-door policy of the French nuclear plants before 9/11 is still remembered by locals. "I remember when the plant was built and I have totally accepted it," said Rosita Dewitte as she walked her dog on the beach in front of the plant. "I do not worry at all. But then, if anything went wrong it would be too late to do anything." Mme Dewitte, whose husband used to work at the plant, says French people accept the need for nuclear energy. "There is no debate in France. When the plant was built there really was no debate. We are confident that French technology is good and we will not have a Chernobyl-style accident here." Locals demonstrate their confidence by barely batting an eyelid at a lucrative offshoot of the power plant. Water from the cooling towers is diverted into rows of tanks at a fish farm in the grounds of the nuclear plant run by Aquanord. "The warm waters are perfect for rearing sea bream," a worker said.

title: EU to investigate Suez-Gaz de France deal
bodyText: The controversial €70bn (£48bn) merger between French energy groups Suez and Gaz de France hit the buffers on Thursday when the EU indicated it would mount a four-month investigation and leading politicians in Paris sought to derail the deal. Neelie Kroes, the European competition commissioner, is due to rule on Monday that the merger, which was sharply criticised this week in parliamentary debates by French deputies and senators, will require an in-depth inquiry because of its adverse impact on consumers. According to Reuters news agency, the decision has already been taken. EU officials denied that was the case but it is clear that Ms Kroes, who has set her sights on controlling dominant energy groups accused of abusing consumers, will act. Her concerns centre on Belgium, where Suez owns the quasi-monopoly electricity supplier Electrabel as well as Distrigas, the dominant gas supplier, gas pipeline operator Fluxys and power grid operator Elia. Gaz de France has a 25% stake in rival Belgian power supplier SPE - the same stake as Britain's Centrica, owner of British Gas and the most outspoken critic of the Suez-Gaz de France merger. Ms Kroes has come under sustained political pressure from Italy, where both Romano Prodi, the new premier, and Silvio Berlusconi, his predecessor, have accused French president Jacques Chirac's government of engineering the merger to block a takeover of Suez by Enel, Italy's leading utility. Mr Prodi, who met Mr Chirac in Paris earlier this week, is to press the issue later on Thursday at the EU summit in Brussels. Enel, which has kept silent on its intentions in recent weeks, is said by industry sources to be keen on acquiring all or parts of Electrabel and/or of Suez's gas operations in Belgium. Separately, Centrica wants to win control of SPE to gain serious access to the continental market.
