Gérard Mestrallet, Suez chief executive, told French financial daily Les Echos: "If Enel wants to develop in the north of Europe then it should allow us to do the same in the south. It's a region that interests us." Defending the strategic value of the merger, he said: "By merging, Suez and Gaz de France would dispose of an unparalleled purchasing power in gas in Europe and would thus be in a pole position to negotiate with suppliers. Not only would consumers benefit but France would gain the best imaginable tool for ensuring its security of supply." Faced with a clear majority opposed to the merger within his ruling UMP centre-right party, Mr Chirac and his premier, Dominique de Villepin, have summoned the cabinet to a crisis session this weekend to consider alternatives. The UMP chairman and interior minister, Nicholas Sarkozy, who plans to replace the president next year, favours a deal that guarantees continued state control of Gaz de France and a three-way tie-up with Enel. The UMP opposes planned legislation to reduce the state's holding below the legally-binding 70% under the merger while Ségolène Royal, the likely socialist presidential candidate in May 2007, would renationalise both Gaz de France and EDF, its electricity equivalent.

title: Letter from... Paris
bodyText: The defeat of buccaneering French businessman Vincent Bolloré by UK institutional investors last week provoked a curious glow of satisfaction here. French popular dislike of big-shot business leaders surpasses even the ancient sense of rivalry with the British. Bolloré has spent the best part of £400m building up a 29 per cent stake in Aegis, a London-based media buying group. He is now by far the biggest shareholder. Last Wednesday, his demand for seats on the board for two placemen was voted down by shareholders, who agreed with chairman Colin Sharman that it would cause an irreconcilable conflict of interest. That conflict arises because Bolloré is also chairman of Havas, France's second-largest advertising agency, a position he gained after acquiring 25 per cent of its shares and demanding seats on the board. Naively, Havas shareholders, fed up with poor performance under Bolloré's predecessor, backed him. He has since renewed the management almost entirely, yet Havas seems as devoid of direction as ever. British fund managers repelled French boarders for the simple reason that they weren't playing fair. In London, pretty much everything is for sale, but you have to bid for it - and that includes control of companies. But, to plagiarise LP Hartley, France is a foreign country; they do things differently there. Or they used to. When Bolloré boasts that his Groupe Bolloré, with annual revenues of €6bn, has a 184-year history, he's right. But 25 years ago the family had all but lost control of their Brittany paper business to Kimberly Clark. Edmond de Rothschild, of the celebrated banking clan, helped Bolloré get it back. Having learnt the art of the squeeze, Bolloré applied similar techniques, successively, to a bunch of French family-controlled businesses. He could carry out such raids because for half a century or more, many French companies have been controlled by family dynasties - think Peugeot, Ricard, Taittinger, Michelin and Lagardère - or by the state. And for state read graduates of the ultra-elite Ecole Nationale d'Administration, who also dominate the government. Call it crony-capitalism if you will. Shareholder democracy is still a fancy new import in France, but foreign funds and home-grown activists are beginning to provide its teeth, swimming on a tide of change unleashed by globalisation, competition and technology change. Just as Edmond backed Bolloré, so another Rothschild financier, Edouard, is today making waves in the French newspaper world. Eighteen months ago, he invested €20m in the left-leaning paper Libération, battling to reverse losses and a circulation slide under founder editor Serge July. With the money all but gone, Edouard, who has 39 per cent of the equity, wants July and chief executive Louis Dreyfus to leave before he will recapitalise it again. He apparently wants to make a name for himself by building an internet-era publishing empire, with the iconic Libé as his launchpad. So what does all this add up to? A lesson in shareholder democracy for a country that needs more, and a lesson in business reality for a newspaper that has lost its way. Change is in the wind in corporate France, and it's long overdue.

title: Drowning in Europe's wine lake
bodyText: Out of the scorching sunlight baking the vineyards of Languedoc, Pierre and Juliette Comps, both 83, sat in their ancient stone house learning from the television how Brussels would dictate their fate. Mr Comps has a metal hook for a hand after he blew off his arm in a hunting accident, but that hadn't stopped him and his wife working the family's vineyards since the 1940s. Deep in Hérault, France's oldest and biggest wine-producing region, the family's high-quality reds still brought French locals and tourists to their door in the village of Puisserguier. But from this week, they will begin ripping up some of their vines as part of the EU's attempt to drain a surplus "wine lake" that is out of control. Yesterday Brussels decided that the €1.3bn (£895m) it spends each year subsidising the EU's wine sector could no longer counter competition from the New World. Already hundreds of millions of bottles of wine from France, Spain and Italy are distilled into industrial alcohol or fuel in "crisis" measures each year. Now Brussels has decided that the industry, which has not been reformed since 1999, must be reshaped. Winegrowers will be paid to rip up their vines to try to stem the tide. "We are producing too much wine for which there is no market," Mariann Fischer Boel, the EU's agriculture commissioner, said yesterday. "Stocks are already the equivalent of a year's production ... The time has come to stop simply relieving the symptoms of our problem, year after year. The time has come to find a cure." The Languedoc-Roussillon region - described in tourist guides as "France's land of plonk" - is the biggest contributor to the wine lake and its increasing numbers of impoverished producers are dependent on Brussels' plans. But it is also the heartland of "wine-terrorism", where for several years, balaclava-clad winegrowers have torched cars and buses, dynamited agriculture ministry buildings, hijacked foreign wine tankers and planted explosives outside businesses suspected of unpatriotically importing foreign wines. Activists from Crav or Comité Regional d'Action Viticole, have plastered graffiti over agricultural banks and poured gallons of wine down local drains to express their plight. In the Puisserguier region, the centre of Crav's operations, some feared yestserday they could mount a fresh campaign if Brussels and Paris did not come to their aid. The tradition of winegrowers' uprising dates back to 1907 when protests led to the army killing six people. Now, as France suffers from overproduction, falling cost prices, a slump in exports, and the shrinking drinking habits of the French, many in an industry which employs 300,000 people and is worth around £5bn a year, feel desperate again. While some winegrowers continue to flourish, there are an increasing number of winegrowers in Languedoc who claim state benefits. Earlier this year, thousands of vineyard workers took to the streets of local towns such as Narbonne and Béziers. Among the Occitane flags of this region, a woman held a placard written in the Occitane language: "Abere tant de boun bi et pas pourre mangea de pan"(we have so much good wine and yet we don't have bread to eat). In the Comps' kitchen, Pierre, 30, the couple's grandson, who maintains their Saint-Chinian label, was planning which vines to plough up. "My cousin, wisely, has focused on marketing," he said. "We have four reds, a white and a rosé, we're Apellation d'Origine Contrôlée [high quality], we're in the wine guides and appreciated by the critics. We sell from our door - so comparatively, we're well off, but we'll have to start destroying vines anyway." While local producers say they are receiving less from distributors than it takes to produce the wine, they complain that supermarket prices for wine are the same. "Someone somewhere is making a margin of profit from the growers' plight," said Pierre Comps. Many complain the French government has done nothing to help. But Brussels feels it is time to clamp down. "Today we spend as much as half a billion euros a year to distil wine, store it and turn it into ethanol for cars and factories," Ms Fisher Boel said yesterday. "This is a ridiculous way to use taxpayers' money. We must spend our wine budget more intelligently." Her plans include proposals to give cash to winegrowers to quit the market or "grub up" older vines. In the Puisserguier region some of the older winemakers plan to take the money but the younger ones worry about how to survive. Some have tried to branch out into olives. But there is steep competition from Spain. One local winemaker, Jacques Sabarthes, 46, has seized another opportunity. As EasyJet prepares to start direct flights to a nearby airport, he has started working as an estate agent on the side. Britons have already seen the potential of "lovingly doing up wrecks", as one local French estate agent put it. He had 20 hectares (50 acres) but sold off 13. "The market in France is terrible," he said. "A litre of fuel costs five times the price of a litre of wine and frankly the French prefer to heat their homes." Back in the Comps' kitchen, Juliette felt her history was slipping away."Vines are like shy young women," she said, "they need to be coaxed, protected and tendered. You can't bring back what has been destroyed." · Additional reporting by David Gow in Brussels

title: Dogfight  that could  bring  down Airbus
bodyText: When Jacques Chirac, Tony Blair, Gerhard Schroder and Jose Luis Rodriguez Zapatero came together in Toulouse 18 months ago, they were gathered in praise of one of Europe's most ambitious technological collaborations: the Airbus A380 superjumbo. Playing host, Chirac pointed to the 555-seat double-decker and waxed philosophical: here was a metaphor for a successful European industrial policy, propelling Airbus past Boeing as the world's leading aircraft maker, embodying European integration. A year and a half later, things could not have turned out more differently. Far from establishing Airbus's dominance over its US rival Boeing, orders for the A380 have stalled at just over 150. Against this came a bombshell that could wreck the company and even threaten the French government. On 13 June, Airbus announced a €2bn (£1.37bn) profits warning. The reason: the A380. The problem: technical difficulties with its electrical systems. The result: Airbus's parent company, EADS, lost a quarter of its value in a day. But this was not all. Attention focused on EADS co-chief executive, Noel Forgeard, who had made €1.5m for himself by selling EADS shares in March. Did he know something? If he was looking for support from EADS shareholders he did not get it. Arnaud Lagardere, Co-EADS chairman and head of the Lagardere group, a 7.5 per cent shareholder, called for the affair to be investigated. French and German financial authorities are looking into the share sale. The ripples do not stop there. Forgeard has powerful friends in France's leading right-of centre UMP party, notably Chirac himself, for whom Forgeard has acted as industrial adviser, and his Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin. De Villepin was asked by Francois Hollande, leader of the French socialists in the French national assembly earlier this week if he still supported Forgeard. De Villepin, himself under pressure over a number of other industrial issues, reacted furiously calling Holland a coward. In January 2005, Forgeard, then head of Airbus, had basked in the glory of the A380 launch. Now, after promotion to the top of its parent, many inside and close to the company are calling for his head in the wake of the fiasco. Far from sealing European integration, the A380 has precipitated an internecine war. What Forgeard knew and when will be decided by the French authorities (problems over the electrical systems were known about well before April, when current Airbus CEO Gustav Humbert initiated an investigation into them). But Airbus sources make another point: Tom Enders, the German co-chief executive did not sell shares because he considered it inappropriate to do so at the time. Hardly a ringing endorsement for Forgeard, regardless of what regulators find. In fact, the man who more than anyone personifies the A380 is the object of deep enmity in both Airbus and EADS. From an EADS perspective, this partly relates to how he ran Airbus before he was promoted last year. One observer says: 'There has always been tension between the two and between the nationalities. The A380 is a symbol of what Airbus was - it believed it was the asset, and it delivered the profits, and so it did not think it had to listen to the centre. That's not just a reflection on Forgeard, it poses questions about management of the whole group.' In contrast to Airbus, EADS's management structure is weak, with co-chairmen and chief executives and a series of rules balancing French and German interests that date back to the foundation of the company from a consortium of national aerospace companies in 2000. But these national rivalries have been resurrected by the rise of Forgeard. This dates back to Autumn 2004, when, as head of Airbus, he launched a campaign to win the top job. He argued that his stewardship of the €12bn A380 project and the then Airbus supremacy made him the natural choice, and that he had great plans for EADS. But those close to him at Airbus tell another story. Forgeard lobbied shareholders - Lagardere, the French government and Daimler Chrysler - against the then French co-CEO, Philippe Camus. He argued he should be sole CEO. The Germans refused, and insisted that Enders, EADS defence head and a close ally of Daimler chairman Jurgen Schrempp, be appointed. Then Forgeard argued for control of Airbus. This again was refused, and Humbert, a German - who as former Airbus chief operating officer is not clear of blame for the A380 crisis - was later installed. Forgeard replaced Camus, but his manoeuvrings left bad blood. An Airbus insider says: 'This was a coup by Forgeard, who wanted to be CEO. He got it through support from the French government. Lagardere had his arm twisted by Chirac. He wanted the job himself, he wanted control of Airbus, but he failed. It has left a very bad taste.' The bitterness has increased since 13 June. Forgeard blamed the problems on factories, singling out Hamburg, where fuselage work is undertaken. He added that under his stewardship, no targets had been missed. This caused fury, not only among German workers but also in Toulouse. 'It was a lie to say he missed no targets - the first delay was under him, and he announced a €1.5bn budget overspend,' says a Toulouse source. With many in the company now openly briefing against him, even the political dimension may have turned against Forgeard. De Villepin has been under pressure over the Clearstream scandal in which senior members of the UMP, including his arch rival for the presidency, Nicolas Sarkozy, were implicated in fraudulent share dealings via the Luxembourg bank. Senior EADS and Airbus officials including Forgeard's right-hand man Philippe Delmas, were also fingered. The documents on which the allegations were made turned out to be fakes. After Hollande's remarks, De Villepin indicated the French government should play a role in sorting out EADS. Lagardere warned him off, reminding them of a pact which imposed strict conditions on government involvement. Lagardere himself is reportedly no supporter of Forgeard. His reaction to De Villepin is echoed inside EADS. A senior official says: 'The only role the government should play is consulting with their co-shareholder (Lagardere) about who should replace Forgeard.' The problems for Airbus, however, are broader than immediate questions over who should be CEO. Airbus bet that flying people in large planes to hub airports before they transferred to their destinations would be the future. Boeing gambled that smaller, efficient planes flying direct would be the answer. The A380 has stalled on its orders, the International Lease Financing Corporation is considering cancellations, Malaysian Airlines said to be doing the same and Qantas demanding compensation. Airbus has run into difficulty with the A350, with some airlines demanding its return to the drawing board. But the business problem may have less to do with the engineering and more to do with the internal affairs, as one observer noted: 'There is huge reputational damage here. Would you buy an aircraft from these people today? The answer is probably no. You'd go to Boeing.' The men taking the flak Noel Forgeard The co-chief executive of EADS is under investigation by stock market regulators into share sales in March, weeks ahead of the emergence of problems at Airbus. He has also stirred up anger with remarks blaming workers for problems with the A380, and claiming nothing had gone wrong when he was running Airbus. Dominique de Villepin The French prime minister is beset by industrial problems from the merger of utilities Suez and Gaz de France to the Clearstream scandal. He was forced to apologise after calling the leader of France's Socialist party a coward when questioning whether he supported Forgeard, who got the top job at EADS through the patronage of President Chirac. Gustav Humbert The German chief executive of Airbus had to front up the profits warning and promise an action plan to remedy things. He ordered the investigation into the A380's problems in April. He has not suffered the same opprobrium as Forgeard. But he was promoted from within so he is certainly not immune from criticism over the A380. Arnaud Lagardere Co-chairman of EADS. Heads the Lagardere conglomerate started by his father as a defence business. He has refocused it on media and halved its EADS stake to 7.5 per cent. He was angered by the profits warning and has ordered an internal investigation. He has pointedly refused to stand behind Forgeard. The British connection The problems at EADS have angered BAE Systems, which owns 20 per cent of Airbus: it could lose tens of millions after the profits warning. When France's Aerospatiale, Germany's Daimler Chrysler Defence and British Aerospace failed to merge at the end of the 1990s, the UK company decided to merge with the defence interests of Lord Weinstock's GEC Marconi, forming BAE Systems, while the French and German groups merged with Casa of Spain to form EADS. BAE wants to sell its Airbus stake to EADS for £3.5bn, but EADS won't pay that much. At the beginning of June, BAE exercised a put option to sell the stake to EADS. Both companies appointed investment bankers. When this failed, Rothschild was appointed to conclude negotiations. Some UK defence sources believe the timing of the profits warning is suspicious: the value of BAE's share of Airbus may have dropped dramatically thanks to the news that it will see profits cut by €2bn over the next four years. Others say both sides will have known about the problems before the put option was exercised and, if not, should have asked more questions.

title: Crisis exposes crippling cronyism at heart of the French state
bodyText: Another week, another case of French government impotence in the face of globalisation. The strategic and production disasters at Airbus, whose parent, EADS, is 15 per cent owned by the French taxpayer, have rebounded straight back into the French Parliament. There, on Tuesday afternoon, Prime Minister Dominique de Villepin turned on Francois Hollande, leader of the socialist opposition, and accused Hollande of a 'cowardly' attitude for suggesting that De Villepin's centre-right government had been inept and slow in handling the crisis. The insult was so virulent that De Villepin had to be led away by stewards for his own protection. He was obliged to make a public apology the next day. France, an architect of the European Union, has responded to the Union's increasingly liberal economic bent by creating European industrial champions - EADS, Airbus, Arcelor, Air France-KLM and Euronext among them, and part-privatising others under pressure from the European Commission. All too often the state retained an interest, either through equity holdings or crony capitalism. Meantime, the 35-hour week, introduced by the previous socialist government, has stymied French economic growth by restricting working hours and bolstering production costs, limiting the potential to reduce unemployment that had been brushing 10 per cent for two decades. De Villepin promised change. His unpopular first move, to make it easier to hire and fire workers via the New Hiring Contract, scraped through in the teeth of parliamentary opposition. His second, to make it easier to hire and fire college-leavers, was defeated this spring after street protests. Bruised from that defeat, De Villepin - catchphrase 'economic patriotism' - revealed a new project, a €64bn (£44bn) merger of state-controlled Gaz de France and Suez, a quoted company built upon a former state water contractor. The idea was to create another French champion, which would also impede progress by Italian energy group Enel into French markets. Better to have a French-run colossus than real competition that might benefit French industrial and commercial customers. Enel and European competition authorities are unimpressed, but that's nothing compared to the reaction of MPs of the majority UMP party. De Villepin was also obliged to put off a vote last week on the privatisation of GDF until the autumn. Not because they care about competition, but because many of them oppose surrender of the government's controlling stake. Why? In the words of Bernard Marois, professor at the HEC business school, 'France is the only industrialised country where the market economy is not considered legitimate'. He blames the Catholic anti-profit philosophy, allied to post-war socialism for the popular distrust of capitalism. Cronyism between government and business continues. It was EADS vice-president Jean-Louis Gergorin, a chum of De Villepin's, who provided him with a list of politicians who supposedly held accounts at scandal-rocked Luxembourg clearing house Clearstream, a list which De Villepin allegedly used to seek dirt on ambitious interior minister Nicolas Sarkozy. Chirac personally backed the appointment last year of former Airbus chairman Noel Forgeard as the French co-chief executive of EADS. Yet having been largely sleeping partners in EADS while it erred for want of governance, De Villepin and crew have been abruptly awakened to their responsibility as shareholders. De Villepin promised a 'complete overhaul' of EADS management last week, despite discovering a previous socialist government had assigned rights to nominate EADS French bosses to private sector partner Lagardere. While finance minister Thierry Breton held meetings with Lagardere boss and EADS French co-chairman Arnaud Lagardere and his German counterpart, transport minister Dominique Perben would not quash rumours that the government wanted to replace Forgeard with SNCF chairman Louis Gallois, who represents the government on the EADS board. Gallois is an old aerospace hand, having piloted former state aerospace company Aerospatiale, now absorbed by EADS. But that's only part of his appeal. 'Louis Gallois and I graduated together from ENA [the elite Ecole Nationale d'Administration] which makes it easier to exchange ideas,' said Perben. So long as a tiny elite controls the levers of power in France, so long as the government tries to control business, so long as it flies in the face of markets, then industrial and economic policy will remain a cause of crisis and scandal.

title: French shareholders launch class action lawsuit against EADS
bodyText: EADS, the majority owner of Airbus, hit new turbulence yesterday when a French shareholder group launched a class action suit over last month's collapse in the company's shares and the European plane-maker reported a steep fall in orders so far this year. The Franco-German-Spanish group also came under renewed pressure to decide swiftly on a complete redesign of the Airbus A350 mid-size airliner because of declining orders. These reached just 117 in the first half, compared with 276 last year - and 441 for arch-rival Boeing. But it could be forced to renege on its promise to order a new A350 design in time for next week's Farnborough air show because Christian Streiff, the new Airbus chief, has only just been confirmed in his job, as has Louis Gallois, the new co-chief executive of the parent group. The new executive team, installed after the enforced resignations of Noël Forgeard from EADS and Gustav Humbert from Airbus, is also grappling with the technical problems afflicting the A380 superjumbo, which is now a year behind its delivery schedule, and with the fall-out of the decision by BAE Systems to demand a full audit of Airbus's value. BAE, which planned to sell its 20% stake to EADS, is incensed at the €2.75bn (£1.9bn) value placed on it by Rothschild bank, the independent arbitrator. EADS, in turn, is angry that the audit, lasting to the end of August, will take up management time, distracting the new team from tackling production problems. The A350, Airbus's belated answer to Boeing's best-selling 787 Dreamliner, is being shunned by airlines which see it as merely an upgraded A330, and it is thought that Mr Gallois, a long-standing EADS board member, and Tom Enders, his co-chief, could override Mr Streiff and order the redesign, leaving some of the details to him. That could cost between €7bn and €10bn - up to double the initial price - sources said, and trigger a demand for even more launch aid from the four governments supporting Airbus. The UK has signed up to lend €550m. If ministers gave the go-ahead for the repayable loans, that would buttress Boeing's case before the World Trade Organisation alleging unlawful state aid. The myriad problems at Airbus, which has outsold Boeing since 2001 and delivered 219 aircraft against 155 for its rival in the first six months of 2006, are mirrored at its parent, EADS. Its shares dropped 26% on June 13, when it announced the latest A380 delays, and fell 1% yesterday. Frederik-Karel Canoy, a lawyer and head of the Association of Active Shareholders (AAA), confirmed he had launched his lawsuit in the Netherlands, where EADS is registered, because class actions are not allowed in France, although the French government plans to make them legal. The suit, lodged in Haarlem, alleges "disastrous financial consequences" for shareholders and follows the decision by the French financial regulator, AMF, to investigate dealings in EADS shares made just before the A380 announcement.

title: The flavours of stagnation
bodyText: On holiday in France, one is reminded what a richly endowed country it is, with beautiful countryside, fine wines, advanced transport infrastructure, nationwide art galleries and a coastline to die for. Pity about the food, though. The food? What do I mean, surely France is the gastronomic centre of the world? Well, the food is as good as it ever has been - and that's the problem. Like some other areas of the economy, it simply hasn't moved on. While Britain has been wallowing in a gastronomic revolution brought about by an explosion of good restaurants and unprecedented improvement in cooking at home, France has just stood still. When we first went to Provence over 30 years ago there were three or four restaurants with the coveted three Michelin stars against none in Britain. Now there are none in the vast area of Provence, stretching from Italy to the Camargue, while a single British village (Bray) boasts two of these beasts (though few people in France would believe it). You can, of course, still eat superbly in France's best restaurants, but the run-of-the-mill eateries have hardly changed their menus. The main courses have a boring sameness about them and the deserts are the same everywhere, as though there has been a ministerial decree that they are only allowed to serve tarte aux pommes, iles flottantes, gateaux maison, mousse au chocolat, creme caramel and one or two variations. More worrying for France is the possibility that gastronomic attitudes are merely a symptom of a more widespread conservatism that is preventing the economy from exploiting its full potential. France's refusal to remove its agriculture subsidies - on the grounds that they protect a way of life - was one of the main reasons for the recent failure of the international trade talks. The country's reluctance to change its labour laws (to make it easier to hire and fire) is commonly quoted as being one of the main reasons behind its very high unemployment rate. The nation's determination to preserve high social benefits including public pensions and public employment would be admirable if the economy could generate enough growth to provide the taxes to pay for them. But so far the economy has shown no sign of pulling itself out of the mediocre growth rates that have characterised most of the past decade. It is in this context that the latest figures - revealing unexpectedly high growth of 1.1% in the second quarter of 2006 - deserve special scrutiny. Is this a sign that France has turned the corner, or is it just a reflection of the prolonged revival of world trade (that could end suddenly if, as expected, the US economy comes off the boil later this year), boosted by a temporary rise in spending associated with the World Cup? It will, of course, need a prolonged revival if growth is to bring about a serious reduction in France's 10% unemployment problem. This is not impossible. In recent years, while France's politicians have been talking nationalistically, many of its big companies have quietly internationalised (including adopting English as the corporate language) and are very competitive. What France needs (along with Germany and Italy) is a prolonged revival of consumer spending to offset the threat of a US slowdown and to bring about a big reduction in unemployment. The best way for Europe to achieve this is not to rely all the time on US growth, but to act as the dynamic economic unit it was supposed to have become when the single currency was adopted. Europe as a whole is remarkably self-sufficient. The vast majority of any increases in income are spent on European goods. A sustained rise in consumer spending would have a self-reinforcing effect without necessarily leading to a significant increase in inflation. France would inevitably benefit from rising European demand. Who knows, it may even lead to a more cosmopolitan attitude to food.

title: Suez and GDF ready to sell power plants to win merger approval from Brussels
bodyText: Suez, the French energy group, is ready to sell or swap power plants and other assets to win the approval of the European commission for its planned €72bn (£49bn) merger with Gaz de France (GDF). Industry sources indicated yesterday that the two groups would swiftly propose such "remedies" to Brussels after the commission sent an interim "statement of objections" about the merger to the two companies because of competition problems in Belgium and France. The disposals could open the way for rivals such as Centrica, owner of British Gas, to secure a stronger foothold in the mainland European energy market and pre-empt moves by Neelie Kroes, the EU competition commissioner, to enforce an opening up of the market. The commission, in documents running to more than 200 pages, told Suez and GDF that their proposed merger raised concerns about the gas and electricity markets in Belgium, where Suez is the dominant player through its ownership of Electrabel, the country's main power supplier. GDF also owns 25% of SPE, Electrabel's rival in the Liège region. Centrica also has a 25% stake in SPE and is known to be keen to acquire GDF's stake, helping it to expand beyond Britain. The combined Suez/GDF entity would control 96% of the Belgian gas market, where Suez owns 57% of Fluxys, a gas pipelines business, and the same amount of Distrigas, a gas-trading company. It has said it will also sell off part of its stake in the Elia power grid business and reduce its 90% control of the electricity market. Late last week Electrabel sold a site for a new power station in Belgium to Germany's E.ON in a move that sources said could be replicated elsewhere. "We could sell or exchange assets, whether plants or installed capacity measured in megawatts, in a way that could help create a single market in Europe," one said. Brussels has also raised concerns about the impact of the merger on the gas market in France, including trading and distribution to different types of customers. It said it would issue a final ruling on October 25. The French companies are optimistic that they can win commission approval. Of 30 in-depth investigations into mergers launched by Brussels between January 2003 and June 2006, nine were cleared outright, 20 approved with "remedies" and only one was blocked. But the merger faces formidable political obstacles in France, where Thierry Breton, finance and economics minister, plans to seek parliamentary approval next month for legislation cutting the state's holding in GDF from 80% to 34%. The partial privatisation of GDF faces furious opposition from unions, leftwing politicians and even from within the ranks of the ruling UMP party. Mr Breton, who said last week he was "very confident" the two companies and the commission would reach a deal, has finally overcome resistance to the plan from Nicolas Sarkozy, the interior minister who heads the ruling UMP party and is likely to stand for president next year. Patrick Devedjian, a close Sarkozy confidant, said yesterday that the merger was important for France's energy independence by creating a powerful actor on the world gas market and one capable of diversifying supplies, notably through nuclear power. He suggested the government could invoke a clause in the constitution to force through the legislation if the opposition persisted with its threat to block it by adding amendments.

title: Fighting us on the beaches: Ryanair's cheap Normandy landings enrage chic Deauville
bodyText: At the half-timbered clubhouse of a Normandy golf course, Christiane Célice looked up from her lunch and stared across the green towards the Channel. "Isn't it beautiful?" she sighed. "Now imagine if a Boeing 737 thundered past, it would ruin the whole effect. We must fight to the end." From her 19th century cottage, the former antiques dealer is leading a battle against the imminent arrival of Ryanair in Deauville, the ultimate chic beach resort on the Normandy Riviera. The Irish low-budget airline next month starts three flights a week from Stansted to the Calvados town after striking a deal with the local mayor. In a standard transaction by small towns keen to attract low-cost flights from Britain, Deauville town hall itself will meet the costs for advertising the new route in England, paying €170,000 a year (£114,000) in monthly instalments. To Madame Célice and her growing protest group it is unthinkable to spend French taxpayers' money promoting a non-French company. She believes the new route will damage the tranquillity of an area once beloved of Gustave Flaubert and Marcel Proust, lower the price of homes and crowd the roads. "We're considering legal action," she said. "Not against Ryanair, but against our local representatives. There was no consultation. Why should French taxpayers' money be spent on this?" Network Picturesque Deauville is nicknamed the "21st arrondissement" for the Parisians who swell the population of 4,500 to 30,000 in the summer. Coco Chanel opened her first boutique here and Yves Saint-Laurent owns a house nearby. The town's film festival, casino, polo matches and thoroughbred horse fairs attract celebrities and foreign visitors on private jets, but to Madame Célice, regular Ryanair flights are a step too far. Her group recently held its first public meeting. Those who spoke included the TV presenter Frédéric Mitterrand, nephew of the former president. Some feared the worst. The owner of the smart restaurant currently occupying the second floor of the tiny airport fears he could be shut down for a snack bar. "We're not anti-English," Madame Célice said. "From William the Conqueror to the Normandy landings and the liberation, there has always been a link across the Channel here - we get on exceptionally well with the English. But you can't build an airport for every house or village where English, German or Italian people are." The debate on Deauville reflects the growing network of low-cost flights between the UK and France. Until now, Normandy, where at least 9,000 British people own homes, was the only French region without a budget route to Britain. Over the past six years, dozens of small French towns have made deals with budget airlines, acknowledging that a cheap "commuter" flight to Britain can boost tourism, business and property sales. Some new routes have been followed by an influx of British people buying second homes or moving to France. Ryanair already has agreements with 19 state-owned airports in France to transport what its founder Michael O'Leary calls "the chateaux-owners of the shires". In the south-west, Bergerac is an example of how a region can be transformed. In 2001, before the first Buzz flights from Stansted, the airport handled 16,000 passengers a year. In 2005, 250,000 passengers passed through on several new routes as the Dordogne enjoyed a boom in British house buyers. A new book analysing the effect of low-cost routes on the region is called Help, The English are Invading Us! Paradox Its author, José-Alain Fralon, says: "There is a certain amount of envy. Why have low-cost flights for the English when we don't have them for ourselves? There are currently five or six different flights between Bergerac and England, but none between Bergerac and Paris. Some people question why France gives money to foreign companies which will compete with our TGV [highspeed trains]. But the paradox is that France has the greatest number of small airports of any European country and in a way the British have aided decentralisation with these flights." On the beach at Deauville, Christian Fougeray, head of the chamber of commerce and the syndicate that owns the airport, said the protesters were in the minority. He said the €600,000 that local authorities will spend improving the airport would have been spent regardless. The airline would bring 40,000 passengers a year, creating jobs. "It's fantastic for tourism, brilliant for business and it makes perfect sense," he said. Building a sandcastle with his son, one Londoner who took seven hours to drive his family over on the ferry said the flights would transform the journey. At the picturesque neo-Norman town hall, the mayor, Philippe Augier, brushed aside the suggestion that budget flights from Britain would cheapen the resort's image. "Yes, Deauville is in many ways a celebrity town, but everyone is welcome here," he said. "The very rich don't keep the small businesses open here," observed one local restaurant owner. In the Jardin des Délices shop where British tourists buy Calvados, the owner Pascal Bultez was checking the Ryanair website. "A return flight for €33? Can it be true? I haven't flown to London before, but I'll definitely be doing it now."

title: Moves to bar French gas takeover
bodyText: The French opposition yesterday vowed to wreck a planned €70bn (£48bn) merger between energy group Suez and Gaz de France as parliament began a tumultuous debate on privatisation of the gas group. Socialist and communist MPs have tabled a record 137,449 amendments to the privatisation bill with paperwork calculated to stand almost a kilometre high. Ségolène Royal, likely socialist presidential candidate in May, said: "This law must not be passed and I think it will not be between now and the presidential election." Other socialist leaders favouring a tie-up between GdF and state-owned Eléctricité de France demanded a referendum. The bill will cut the state's GdF holding from 70% to 34%. Ministers are under pressure from some of their national assembly deputies to scrap it ahead of the elections or enforce it by guillotining debate. Gérard Mestrallet, Suez chief executive, predicted that the merger, promoted by prime minister Dominique de Villepin to block a takeover by Italy's Enel, was on track for completion by December. Announcing a 40% jump in first-half net income to €2.2bn, he said: "Europe is living through a third energy shock. The landscape will be concentrated on a few big players of a global dimension and we want to be one of those." His team yesterday began negotiations with the European commission on a series of disposals Brussels is demanding to reduce Suez/GDF's post-merger dominance in France and Belgium where the group would control more than 90% of the gas and electricity markets. Mr Mestrallet insisted Suez/GDF would seek to compensate for disposals by acquisitions in other European countries, though Britain is ruled out for the time being. Suez increased first-half sales and pre-tax profits 10% to €22.4bn and €3.7bn.

title: Suez and GDF agree roles in 72bn merger
bodyText: The French energy companies Suez and Gaz de France cleared one of the last significant hurdles to their €72bn (£48bn) merger yesterday after ending months of disagreement about executive roles. Gérard Mestrallet, Suez's chief executive, will become the boss of the new company with Jean-François Cirelli, his GDF counterpart, who threatened to resign over a preponderance of top posts for Suez managers, lined up as his deputy. The controversial merger, approved by France's assembly in a bill that cuts the French state's holding in GDF to 34%, is expected to be given the go-ahead by the European commission on November 14. This follows a series of concessions, mainly asset disposals in Belgium and France, made in negotiations with the Belgian government and Neelie Kroes, EU competition commissioner, designed to create more open energy markets. Suez is expected to sweeten the deal for shareholders by raising a special dividend from €1 to €3. Talks on the final terms are due to be completed next month. Mr Mestrallet and Mr Cirelli refused to comment on the terms at the announcement in Paris of the new management and organisation - only agreed after months of negotiations likened to a "white-water ride". But the pair insisted the merger was on track to be completed in December. Mr Mestrallet also categorically ruled out any sale of Suez's environmental business, courted by the billionaire entrepreneur Bernard Pinault, by the combined group. Ms Kroes, meanwhile, warned that the commission would break up "vertically integrated" power groups such as Germany's E.ON and France's EDF by forcing the sale of infrastructure. She said it was vital to restore "credibility" in the market.

title: French presidential runners split over economic problems
bodyText: Segolene Royal, the new socialist candidate for next spring's French presidential election, and Nicolas Sarkozy, her likely opponent, are sharply divided over how to confront the country's deep-seated economic problems. More than one in five French youngsters are unemployed, a fact brutally exposed by riots in Paris over the past 12 months. Despite the eurozone-wide upturn which has helped to boost the economy in 2005, and seen GDP growth in France outpace Germany and Italy, analysts say a new President will be faced with stubborn challenges of low workforce participation and high unemployment. 'There's a major problem in France: we have a stable unemployment rate that has not been below 8 per cent for about 20 years,' says Gilles Moec, a French economist at Bank of America, who describes the situation across the Channel as 'boom and gloom'. French workers are more productive than those in the US, but fewer work. Those who do, work shorter hours than almost anywhere else in Europe, and much less than in South Korea, the hard-work capital of the world. The main reason is the mandatory 35-hour week, introduced in the hope of sharing jobs around. 'Sego', as Royal is known, advocates increasing the minimum wage, and making it more expensive for employers to hire workers on precarious short-term contracts, by pushing up payroll taxes for them. She has also questioned the sacred 35-hour week. 'Sarko', by contrast, has advocated gradually phasing out some of the protections offered to workers on long-term contracts, closing the divide between short-term employment, which carries few rights, and heavily regulated permanent jobs.

title: Saudis' threat to jet order puts British jobs on the line
bodyText: There was growing alarm in Whitehall and at the aerospace manufacturer BAE Systems last night that Saudi Arabia might abandon a multibillion pound purchase of British jets due to anger over allegations that payments to the Saudi royal family were bribes. Saudi diplomats are understood to have made representations in recent days to the British government over a long-running Serious Fraud Office investigation into allegations that kickbacks were paid to members of the ruling family in order to secure an earlier series of lucrative arms contracts. The Saudis are reportedly threatening to ditch plans to purchase 72 Eurofighter jets from Britain in favour of buying French planes instead. Under the negotiations with the British government - said to be in their final stages - the Eurofighter Typhoon jets would be built under contract by BAE at its Warton plant in Lancashire, which employs some 9,000 people. The overall price of the deal is estimated at more than £75bn. British officials were yesterday trying to reassure the Saudis and save the Eurofighter deal while also making it clear they could not intervene to stop the fraud investigation. Officials confirmed that the Saudis were 'very angry' over the continuing fraud probe. An official at the Foreign and Commonwealth Office said: 'The SFO is an independent government department and part of the criminal justice system. Other parts of government cannot and would not interfere in or prejudice its investigations.' But she added: 'Equally, we are committed to ensuring that existing bilateral trade agreements with other countries including Saudi Arabia are implemented effectively and smoothly.' For the Conservatives, the shadow Defence Secretary Liam Fox also stressed that it was up to the SFO to decide the future of the investigation, but added: 'They have decided whether there's a genuine case to answer. If not, there's no point in pursuing a process clearly disadvantageous to British interests.' Rumours last week that the Saudis had opened talks with the French government on a possible deal caused a fall in BAE Systems' share price. Industry sources told The Observer last night that the French were pressing hard for an entry into the hugely lucrative Saudi defence market and had been pressing influential members of the royal family, headed by King Abdullah bin Abdul Aziz al-Saud to abandon the purchase of the British-made planes. An industry source said: 'We take the threat seriously. The French are working very hard at a very high political level to try to persuade the Saudis. The Saudis are extremely frustrated by the length of time the SFO investigation is taking.' BAE won the earlier large-scale Saudi arms deal - known as al-Yamama - two decades ago at the eleventh hour, snatching victory from under the nose of the French aerospace company Dassault. Industry sources said the British company's fear was that Saudi negotiators might go ahead with their threat, and make a similar last-minute change - this time, in favour of the French. Since the original deal was signed, BAE has supplied 96 ground attack Tornado aircraft, 48 air-defence versions, as well as 50 jets and 50 turbo-prop trainers. The aircraft were delivered in two tranches between 1989 and the mid-1990s. France has had difficulty finding export customers for its new fighter jet, which it has developed since pulling out of the pan-European collaboration behind Eurofighter. A deal with Saudi Arabia might not only provide a high-profile launch customer, but could also provide a long term source of jobs and cash. But the anticipated Eurofighter sale is also seen as crucial to BAE. Along with supplying the Typhoon aircraft under the earlier arrangement, BAE has some 4,500 employees based in Saudi Arabia to maintain and upgrade the aircraft.

title: France seeks power partner  to rescue its Suez masterplan
bodyText: The French government was yesterday scrambling to save its scheme to create a national champion power group by engineering an €80bn (£54bn) merger between Suez and Gaz de France. Amid evidence that the two groups had given up speaking to each other, the government, which brokered their engagement in February, was reported to be seeking alternative French partners to prevent Suez becoming a takeover target for a foreign group. Ministers in the outgoing centre-right government of president Jacques Chirac had, according to market rumour in Paris, approached oil company Total to take over Suez but their overtures were rebuffed. Meanwhile the merger plans were further upset by reports that billionaire entrepreneur Francois Pinault had launched a €60bn rival bid to acquire Suez and take control of its water and waste arm. Mr Pinault, owner of luxury retail goods company PPR, was reported by French daily Le Figaro to have discussed with Dominique de Villepin, the French premier, plans to acquire Suez for €60bn, a 25% premium to the market capitalisation. He would then sell its energy assets to GDF for €40bn and retain its water and waste business. Mr Pinault refused to comment. Mr de Villepin, who is said to be "unenthusiastic" about the plans, announced the Suez-GDF merger earlier this year to head off a proposed takeover approach for Suez from Italian group Enel. The Italians, who have since said they have lost interest, are known to have worked closely with both Veolia Environment, the French water business, and Mr Pinault, who abandoned earlier plans to buy Suez's water assets for €18bn in October. Gérard Mestrallet, Suez chief executive, yesterday insisted the energy and water and waste assets of his group were complementary as he set out recent multi-billion contract wins for both wings, including in Britain. He refused to comment on Mr Pinault's reported approach, which sent Suez shares, already up 47% this year, to fresh highs, valuing the group at €48bn. Colette Neuville, influential head of small shareholders' action group Adam, said a Pinault takeover would be "an ideal solution," enabling France to realise its dream goal of a combined energy group. The all-French merger between Suez and GDF, which remains 80% in state hands, has hit the rocks since the country's constitutional court ruled it could not go ahead until July 1 next year - the date when the EU's energy market is due to be wholly competitive. But, by then, Mr Chirac will have been replaced in spring elections, probably by either centre-right leader Nicholas Sarkozy or socialist Ségolène Royal. Ms Royal has said she would scrap legislation to privatise GDF, leaving just 34% in state hands, while Mr Sarkozy is notably lukewarm.

title: Pinault may jump into Suez deal
bodyText: The French billionaire François Pinault headed into a political storm yesterday when he refused to rule out gatecrashing the planned €80bn (£54bn) merger of the utilities Suez and Gaz de France. In a statement issued through his holding company Artemis, Mr Pinault said he had yet to decide on whether to bid for Suez but said "all options remain open". He was forced to make a public statement by the French markets regulator after widespread speculation about a possible bid gathered pace at the end of last week. An infuriated Suez complained that the financier's intent was still unclear. The "vague and imprecise statement gives no clear indication of [Pinault's] intentions," it said. "For Suez shareholders it encourages speculation and brings about uncertainty." Suez asked the regulator to press for clarification under new takeover rules introduced last year. It is the first time the "put up or shut up" clause has been used. The French government controversially brokered the merger between Suez and GDF almost a year ago. The deal to create a "national champion" was designed to fend off a proposed takeover approach for Suez from the Italian group Enel. But the Suez/GDF merger has been put on hold after the French constitutional court ruled that it could not go ahead until July 1, when the energy market is fully opened for competition. French presidential elections in May present further uncertainty as the Socialist candidate Ségolène Royal could renationalise energy assets if she wins. Mr Pinault wants hopes to buy Suez to dismantle it, according to reports.

title: French regulator pushes Pinault to decide on Suez
bodyText: The French financial regulator has given François Pinault until February 4 to make an offer for Suez, the French utility that is due to join Gaz de France (GdF) in an €80bn (£54bn) merger. The AMF said Mr Pinault should either announce a bid or reveal the details of a takeover plan. Mr Pinault, through Artemis, his holding company has said he is keeping his options open. The proposed merger between Suez and GdF was announced last February to head off a takeover approach for Suez by Italy's Enel. But France's constitutional court has said the merger cannot go ahead until July 1 this year.

title: Can the French carry on living like this for ever?
bodyText: 'Sego' and 'Sarko' are squaring up in the battle for the French presidency, and presenting voters with two starkly contrasting visions of France's future. Socialist candidate Segolene Royal is promising to plump up the cushions of the social model and shut out the chill winds of globalisation; her centre-right rival, Nicolas Sarkozy, wants to lead a 'veritable economic revolution' to snatch his country from what he describes as a 'moral crisis'. As in Britain, French voters are sceptically chewing over the implications of globalisation. They are nervous about the perceived threat to their livelihoods, and to the long-established social compact which provides them with deluxe public services and a generous welfare system, in exchange for relatively high taxes. There has been much talk about precarite - the insecurity and riskiness of life in today's economy - and many analysts took the resounding Non in the referendum on the European Union constitution in 2005 as a rejection of the Anglo-Saxon free-market model. France has benefited from the upturn across the eurozone, and GDP grew by a relatively healthy 2 per cent last year; but some analysts warn that the traditionally dynamic economy is losing its competitive edge. 'I think they have some real strengths, and I don't think that they face a crisis, but at the same time, their growth potential is eroding,' says Simon Tilford, of the London-based Centre for European Reform. Early in the campaign, Royal sought to portray herself as a centrist, Blairite figure, reaching out to the electoral middle-ground; but in her 100-point pacte presidentiel, published last week, she struck a resolutely left-wing note. She promised to renationalise power company EDF, crack down on bank charges, establish a national agency for 'reindustrialisation', and introduce a range of other measures which would look positively retro on this side of the Channel. Some French business leaders responded by threatening to flee the country. Her chief economic advisor, Eric Besson, who was wheeled out to the press to explain how she would pay for her proposals, resigned. 'She was losing support both from the establishment of the party and from the left, so she's going back to the socialist orthodoxy,' says Gilles Saint-Paul, a French economist at the Centre for European Policy and Research. Tim Smith, of Queen's University in Ontario, author of France in Crisis, says that the 100-point plan failed to confront France's long-term challenges - in particular, paying for generous social services and pensions in an ageing society and beating youth unemployment. 'Royal's manifesto is remarkable for what is absent,' he says. 'There is precious little talk about unemployment. There are plenty of provisions to protect against the ravages of the market, but little is said about preparing the nation to compete in the European market.' He adds that the proposals will increase the risk that young French entrepreneurs will choose to go abroad. 'It's a recipe for brain-drain, further tax flight, and rising property values in swanky areas of London, as French tax exiles continue to pour into town,' he says. (There are already up to 300,000 French expatriates living in Britain.) While Sego is determined to cling to the comforts of the social model, Sarkozy says that he will gradually shrink the size of the state, replacing only one in two retiring civil servants, for example. However, he has shied away from a full-frontal attack on government spending, and focused most of his reforming zeal on the labour market. 'The French moral crisis has a name: it's the crisis of work,' he tells voters on his website, which is counting down to election day in milliseconds. Sarkozy has challenged the orthodoxy of the 35-hour week and promises to break down the barriers which separate cossetted permanent workers and the insecure temporary staff employed on short-term contracts. 'The 35-hour week was not that popular, even among those people it was meant to help: the working class,' says Gilles Moec, an economist at Bank of America. 'They want their overtime.' Economists have long argued that the rigidity in employment laws makes companies reluctant to create new jobs. Youth unemployment is more than 20 per cent, which is more than twice the rate in the workforce as a whole. 'There is a real problem of labour market dualism in France' says Moec. However, as last year's enormous demonstrations over ill-fated proposals for a new temporary contract for young workers (the contrat premiere embauche, CPE) demonstrated, forcing pro-market reforms on the French workforce is unlikely to be easy. 'People said, look, the government should explain things better, be more pedagogical, build more consensus; but the unions were willing to blockade the country for a month over the CPE,' says Saint-Paul. Sarkozy's policies may be well to the right of Royal's, but he is not a French version of Margaret Thatcher, waiting to handbag the trade unions. His election slogan is, 'Together, everything becomes possible'. When he was finance minister, he called retailers in and demanded that they cut their prices - hardly the behaviour of a rampant free marketeer - and much of his rhetoric is soothingly collectivist. 'Rightly or wrongly, the politicians believe that the people are scared by markets, and that adopting the recipes of the economists is the perfect way to lose the election - and they may be right about that,' says St-Paul. He argues that the two candidates' policy platforms are as interesting for what they miss out as for their concrete policies. In particular, for a country with relatively high public debt, there is little talk of fiscal rectitude or putting the books back in order - themes which have been critical to British general elections, where 'iron chancellors' are much admired. 'We are one of the countries in the world with the highest share of public spending in GDP, and the highest tax rate, and none of the candidates really wants to tackle that - all we've got is more spending,' says Saint-Paul. Voters have until April to decide whether Sego or Sarko is offering the most palatable plan for France's future. But with the battle lines already drawn, it is clear that Royal and Sarkozy are involved in a good old-fashioned clash of ideologies, the result of which could send France veering in dramatically different directions. Sego v Sarko: The policies Segolene Royal's 'pacte presidentiel' promises to: Prepare for 'l'apres petrole' - the world after petrol - by generating 20 per cent of France's energy from renewable sources by 2020. Raise the minimum wage to €1500 a month (currently €1254). Create 500,000 'springboard jobs' for the young unemployed. Encourage investment with a lower tax rate on dividends if they are reinvested, instead of distributed to shareholders. Create a 'national agency for re-industrialisation' to prevent jobs being outsourced. Tackle the high cost of buying a home by building 120,000 social houses, and allowing local governments to buy up empty homes. Invest 'massively' in innovation by raising spending on R&D by 10 per cent. Raise the state pension by 5 per cent. Tax the advertising revenues of private TV to pay for public broadcasting. Renationalise Electricite de France, and combine it with Gaz de France to create a national energy champion. Nicolas Sarkozy has promised to: Return €2,000 to every family in tax cuts. Be 'the President who values work' by giving people the right to work as much as they like (longer than the statutory 35 hours a week), and cutting taxes on overtime. Allow mothers to return to work by helping them to find childcare. Give workers the right to choose their retirement age. Abolish inheritance tax on all but the largest estates. Make it easier for French people to own their own homes by giving tenants in social housing the right to buy, and creating a tax credit on mortgage interest to help first-time buyers. Tackle climate change by investing in research and innovation. Create a 'minister for production' to help prepare France for globalisation. Campaign for a Europe-wide tax on speculative hedge funds. Reduce the size of the state by replacing only half the civil servants who reach retirement age.

title: French toast the return of a rock dinosaur
bodyText: The shades. The hair. And the buttocks. Who could say that French rock'n'roll has lost its touch? Johnny Hallyday, France's favourite but ageing rock star, may have just become a tax exile in Switzerland, but another Gallic dinosaur from the Sixties has lurched in to make a comeback - and has sold out stadiums across a nostalgic nation. Just as there were the Beatles and the Rolling Stones, in France there was Hallyday and Michel Polnareff. And now Polnareff, 63, is back. He last appeared live in his homeland 34 years ago before leaving for California and disappearing, wreathed in his frizzy blond mop, into a cloud of tax trouble, rumoured depression, health problems, myopia and the outrage sparked by a controversial promotional poster featuring his naked bottom. 'He left showing his bum and left France on its derriere. She never got up again,' said Liberation, the newspaper, hours before the curtain went up at the first of his shows. 'But he has kept the myth going. And he can prove it.' According to Christophe Lauga, author of Polnareffmania, the singer had wanted to return to his homeland for a long time. 'Michel loves his public and particularly his French fans, but there were lots of things that kept him away, mainly contracts,' he said. 'Now that everything is back in order, here he is!' For all Polnareff's fears - 'I'm scared about my voice,' he told reporters before Friday night's concert in Paris - those who had paid hundreds of euros for prized tickets were not disappointed. Though they have may have struggled to recognise the androgynous youth in the muscular, gym-built, elderly man, the classically trained artist ran through much of his repertoire of 200-odd songs, including about 60 hits, with very little trouble. He did not bare his derriere. After two weeks of dates in Paris, Polnareff will tour the provinces. Every gig is sold out. Those with long memories speak breathlessly of 'the return of a god'. It is certain that Polnareff, despite or because of his absence, has kept the mythic aura usually awarded to those rock stars who die young. The precociously gifted musician who attended some of the best French conservatories in his youth was the 'anti-Johnny Hallyday', said rock journalist Benoit Cachin. 'Not very pretty, too thin...and singing songs in which he complained all the time.' Polnareff was also a rival to the late Serge Gainsbourg. Both men mixed French traditional chanson with rock, pop and jazz. Both were sexually provocative, suffered from depression and had cult followings. 'Polnareff has one advantage over Serge,' said Liberation yesterday, however. 'He is alive.'

title: Chirac bows out amid new French revolution
bodyText: The curtain call will be brief. But Jacques Chirac's 10-minute televised speech tonight - officially slated as the 74-year-old President's announcement that he will not run for a third term - marks a historic generational, class and gender shift in French politics. The past week's opinion polls show the three front-runners for the forthcoming presidential election - all modernisers under 60 and including France's first woman candidate, Socialist Segolene Royal - neck and neck. With six weeks to go to the 22 April first round, Le Monde said yesterday that the election was the most unpredictable since 1969. It already looks likely that France's political rejuvenation will see the forced retirement, at 78, of veteran Front National leader Jean-Marie Le Pen. He is spending this weekend in a frantic battle to stay in the hunt as he has until Friday to secure the Constitutional Council's requirement from all candidates of 500 endorsements from mayors and other elected officials. He last failed to obtain them in 1981 and admits he is 70 signatures short. 'We're hard at it. We need to sign up 10 a day,' said an aide. Even if the FN leader qualifies for the race, he already looks outpaced as third man by the centre-right former Education Minister, Francois Bayrou, 56, whose down-to-earth policies appear to appeal both to Royal's potential voters and to right-wingers who are put off by the brashness of the Interior Minister, Nicolas Sarkozy, 51. The change is, as ever in French politics, subtle. But observers point to key recent events - including the No-vote to the European constitution in May 2005, the riots in French suburbs that autumn and the student protests in the spring of 2006 against the Prime Minister, Dominique de Villepin, and his 'first employment contract' - as signals from the electorate of a desperate cry for new dynamism. 'The crisis France is in is centred on the complacency of its politicians,' said political scientist Emmanuel Giannesini. 'They are complacent in the face of issues that are visible every day - corruption, short cuts and shoddiness - and which have stripped the country of the notion of what the republic stands for.' Long before its youth became restive, the French had demonstrated that they wanted a break from the ways of an ageing, elitist and distant political class, top-heavy with graduates, like Chirac, of the Ecole Nationale d'Administration. In the first round of the 2002 presidential elections, voters showed their desperation rather clumsily by voting for the only alternative to hand - Le Pen - rather than for Socialist candidate Lionel Jospin. That year it was only Le Pen's presence in the second round that secured Chirac's landslide to a second term. Even then, Chirac understood he was out of step with the people. Within weeks of his re-election, he sent politicians on to the ground to make contact with grassroots France. But the move never translated into a palpable sense that la classe politique was in touch with la France d'en bas Yesterday's Ipsos opinion poll gave Sarkozy a second-round victory against Royal of 53.5 per cent. His first-round score was put at 32.5 per cent and Royal's at 26. Bayrou was placed third on 20.5 per cent and Le Pen trailed on 12.5. However, polls earlier last week showed Bayrou's score reaching 24 per cent - making the centrist gentleman farmer a real threat to both main candidates. 'If there are as many of us here,' he told a rally of 3,000 in Perpignan on Friday, 'it's that something fundamental is happening in this country.' He ascribed his growing popularity to his closeness to French people and thrifty policies to tackle France's debt. 'The main parties are rushing around, pulling out the big guns against me. They are targeting me, but what they do not understand is that it's the French people who will decide.' However, Bayrou may yet fall foul to criticism - such as that from Sarkozy - that he is too wishy-washy. 'He told me he ran the Education Ministry in consultation with the trade unions,' said Sarkozy. 'That's why no one remembers him doing anything when he was there.' Bayrou has also come under fire for evasion about the Prime Minister he would appoint, promising only to 'pool the best talent from the right and left'. But if he maintains his presence in the polls - and wins round a sizeable share of the 16 per cent of voters who told Ipsos yesterday that they remain undecided - he represents a far greater threat to Royal, 53, than he does to Sarkozy. Royal's campaign hit a high point 10 days ago when she used the plight of the soon-to-be-jobless 10,000 European Airbus employees to illustrate the failure of recent industrial policies. Her outburst prompted a response from Sarkozy - once Chirac's Finance Minister - in which he had to admit that an injection of government cash would not prevent redundancies and that there was a need for the state to maintain a share in large enterprises. But in other respects Royal's campaign is weakened by its dependence on the Socialist Party machine. After some internal spats this year, she sidelined several younger members of her team, falling back on old-timers - such as former Mitterrandist minister Dominique Strauss-Kahn and former Prime Minister Laurent Fabius - who may be just the chip-off-the-block personalities voters want to get away from. Yesterday's Le Monde suggested that, unless Royal revamps her campaign and makes it look more dynamic, France could face the same scenario as in 1969, which saw two right-wing candidates in the run-off, Alain Poher and the ultimately victorious Georges Pompidou. In that election, the divided left garnished only 31 per cent of the first-round vote. Bayrou's opinion poll rise - combined with tempered rhetoric from Le Pen - has put wind in Sarkozy's sails. After weeks of maintaining a nice-guy image and refraining from evictions of squatters or comments about clamping down on crime, the Union pour la Majorite Parlementaire candidate on Thursday talked tough. Sarkozy's call on France 2 television for the creation of a 'ministry for immigration and national identity' was immediately denounced by the Socialists as 'a flirt with the Front National' electorate. Bayrou said: 'When you lump immigration and identity together, you're crossing a dangerous line.' But political scientist Loic Blondiaux warns against hasty predictions of a right-right run-off on 6 May: 'We only have opinion polls to go by, and it's crucial to remember that voters who today say they will support Bayrou are much less certain of their choice than the majority of those opting for Royal or Sarkozy.' According to Brice Teinturier of the TNS-Sofres polling institute, voters are in 'the temptation phase' and this will gave way to 'the decision stage': 'The Bayrou phenomenon is interesting because he seems to appeal to the educated voters who are not traditionally waverers. The race is wide open.' Even though for three years Chirac has been reported as detesting Sarkozy - whose boisterous style jars with his own regal stance - the President is likely to endorse the Interior Minister. But according to observers close to him, the President will not use tonight's speech 'which he has spent several weeks perfecting' to direct his supporters to vote for Sarkozy. Even the Interior Minister himself has admitted that support from Chirac at this stage would be 'helpful but against all probability'. Instead, as Chirac enters the last weeks of a presidency he inaugurated in 1995 with a return to nuclear testing, moving on to the introduction of the euro and his outspoken opposition to the invasion of Iraq, he now looks above all mindful of leaving at the end a good impression. The run-up to tonight has been marked by several 'on the couch' TV interviews with him and his wife, Bernadette, and much stress on Chirac's desire to be remembered for his commitment to France. 'You can approve or disapprove of my actions. I have always attempted to act in the best interests of the French people,' he told television interviewer Michel Drucker last month. On Friday in Brussels, he appeared to wish to make peace with his European partners, eating humble pie on the 2005 referendum result. 'We failed on the referendum. We let the French believe there was a Plan B we could reach for.' Frederic Salat-Baroux, secretary-general of the Elysee Palace, said that tonight's speech 'is a key moment for the President. The speech will be 100 per cent personal and focused on the future. He will express his view of the issues and challenges ahead - his passion for France.' Chirac's life and times Born: 29 November 1932 in Paris. President: from 17 May 1995. Prime Minister: from 1974 to 1976 and from 1986 to 1988. Mayor of Paris from 1976 to 1995. Little known qualification: Prince of Andorra since 1995. CV highlights best kept quiet: was briefly a Communist. Is a freemason of the 'Alpina' lodge. Volunteer: for the war in Algeria where he served 18 months (1957-58). Married: in 1956 to Bernadette Chodron de Courcel. Two daughters, Laurence and Claude. Favourite dish: tete de veau (calves head). Favourite drink: lager. Controversies: Chirac admitted the 'collective fault' of France in collaborating with Nazi Germany in 1995. He opposed the invasion of Iraq in 2002. Drama: survived a right-wing assassination bid on Bastille Day, 2002. Biggest mistake: decided to stage a referendum on the European constitution in May 2005, when 54.8 per cent of French people voted Non. Popularity: in a 2005 survey just 1 per cent of French people wanted him to run in for President in 2007.

title: Chirac says farewell to 40 years in politics
bodyText: Jacques Chirac said farewell to an uncertain and divided France last night, which his critics accused him of tipping into decline. But after 40 years in politics, the president once described as a "bulldozer" and "killer" of rivals left France in suspense as to whether he would eventually give his support to his party opponent, Nicolas Sarkozy, who hopes to take over his job. In a televised address, Mr Chirac, 74, who boasts one of the longest continuous political careers in Europe - twice president, twice prime minister and 18 years as mayor of Paris - said he was proud of what he had achieved but would have liked to have modernised France more rapidly. "At the end of my mandate, the moment will have come for me to serve you otherwise. I will not seek your backing for a new mandate," he said in a sombre, 10-minute speech where he laid out some of the major issues facing France. Voters are preparing to replace him with a president from a new generation in six weeks. All the three main contenders to succeed him - Mr Sarkozy of the ruling UMP party, Socialist Ségolène Royal and centrist Francois Bayrou - are in their 50s and all have pledged to break with the politics of the past 25 years. Styling himself as an affable rogue, Mr Chirac for decades charmed the public with his handshaking, patting of cows and shaking of dogs' paws on his tours round France - a man of the people who was able to eat five lunches in one afternoon on the election trail through kitchens. Nicknamed the "weathervane" for his ability to shift as it suited him - he went from championing state control in the 1970s to Ronald Reagan's free-market liberalism in the 1980s, from nuclear testing to eco-champion, eurosceptic to euro-defender. He was dogged by the sleaze scandals from his days at Paris city hall, which saw some of his closest associates convicted of corruption. He claimed immunity as president, but even after he leaves the Élysée palace, he is unlikely ever to see serious sanctions. Mr Chirac's popularity rose over his opposition to the war in Iraq and he will be remembered for his quest to reconcile France with its history, the first French head of state to recognise the Vichy regime's collaboration in the Holocaust and to apologise to the Jewish people. But over the past year, the country has seen a series of brutal appraisals of what critics term his dithering, broken promises and wasted 12 years as president. When he came to power in 1995, after two failed attempts, he promised to heal the "social fracture", crippling unemployment and inequality, but many suggest he is leaving France worse off, with social unrest on impoverished housing estates and a large public debt. Mr Chirac is most criticised for failing to steward change in France and for calling a referendum on the EU constitution in 2005, then failing to sell the idea to the electorate, who voted no. Highs and lows 2003 Opinion polls showed that approval for Jacques Chirac's anti- war in Iraq policy reached 90% 2002 Mr Chirac agreed common agricultural policy payments with Germany, securing his popularity 1995 Mr Chirac apologised for Vichy collaboration in the deportation of Jews 1997 In May his austerity programme cost the centre-right its majority 2005 France voted no to the EU constitution. In November he was accused of weak leadership following riots over high unemployment Isabelle Chevallot

title: Le Pen clings to heartland support
bodyText: Lucien Ruty, a retired French naval officer, proudly surveyed the living room he had turned into a shrine to Napoleon. Hundreds of model soldiers filled cabinets on the walls, urns held dust from the emperor's burial site on St Helena and soil from the battlefield at Waterloo. His wife wore a gold Napoleon pendant at all times and her paintings of soldiers jostled for wall space with Bonaparte busts and a giant French tricolour. The only item that could compete with this patriotic showcase to the emperor was a photo of the Rutys' clinking champagne glasses with their modern-day French saviour, the far-right leader Jean-Marie Le Pen. "Victory will be ours," said a note to them from the man himself. Tucked away behind the Roman amphitheatre in the bullfighting city of Nîmes, the Rutys' flat is the engine room of Mr Le Pen's National Front southern heartland. In the picturesque southern Rhône valley where Languedoc meets Provence, there are a selection of beautiful but fading rural towns with immigrant North African communities, retired soldiers from colonial wars and worrying youth unemployment where many feel under siege and disillusioned with mainstream politics. Some towns here handed the extreme right a vote of about 40% in 2002, prompting Mr Le Pen's shock appearance in the second round run-off of the presidential election.
